3.14: ehyeh asher ehyeh (ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh): 'I AM WHO I AM' or 'I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE.' The divine name is derived from the verb hayah (to be). The Tetragrammaton (YHWH) is likely the Qal imperfect form of hayah — 'He is' or 'He will be.' The name declares YHWH's self-existence and sovereign freedom: he does not derive his existence from anything outside himself; his being is its own definition. John's seven I AM sayings (John 6:35; 8:12; 10:9, 11; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1) and the absolute 'I am' (John 8:58; 18:5-6) are deliberate invocations of the Exodus 3:14 name, claiming for the Son the same underived self-existence.
3.1: uMosheh hayah ro'eh et-tson Yitro chotno kohen Midyan (ûmōšeh hāyāh rōʿeh ʾet-ṣōʾn yitrô ḥōtnô kōhēn midyān): 'Moses was shepherding the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian.' The great deliverer of Israel is introduced as a shepherd — the paradigmatic leadership role in the biblical imagination. Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, David, and Christ (John 10:11) are all shepherds. Kohen Midyan (priest of Midian) — Jethro as a Gentile priest suggests that knowledge of the divine was not limited to Israel, though the definitive self-disclosure comes through Moses and at Sinai.
3.2: vayera malach YHWH elav belabat-esh mitoch hasneh (wayyērāʾ malʾaḵ Yhwh ʾēlāw bĕlabbat-ʾēš mittôḵ hassĕneh): 'The angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from within the thornbush.' Malach YHWH (H4397 + H3068, messenger/angel of the LORD): the divine messenger who is functionally identified with YHWH himself throughout the OT (as here — v.4: YHWH called to him; v.6: I am the God of your father). The sneh (H5572, thornbush): a common, scrubby desert plant — the divine glory dwelling in the ordinary, the humble location of the holiest revelation. The fire that does not consume the bush is the presence that dwells in the lowly without destroying it.
3.3: vayomer Mosheh asura na ve'er'eh et-hama're haGadol hazeh madua lo-yivar hasneh: 'Moses said: Let me turn aside and see this great sight — why is the bush not burned?' Asura na (let me turn aside): Moses's voluntary turning — the pivot that leads to encounter. The question madua (why?) marks Moses as one whose curiosity opens him to revelation. Heraclitus said that the unexamined life is not worth living; the biblical corollary is that the uncurious eye misses divine disclosure. Moses's willingness to leave his path and investigate is what brings him into YHWH's presence.
3.4: vayar YHWH ki sar lir'ot vayiqra elav Elohim mitoch hasneh vayomer Mosheh Mosheh vayomer hineni: 'When YHWH saw that he turned aside to see, God called to him out of the bush: Moses! Moses! And he said: Here I am.' The doubled name Mosheh Mosheh is the address of urgency and intimacy — only used for figures in decisive covenant-encounter (Abraham in Gen 22:11; Jacob in Gen 46:2; Samuel in 1 Sam 3:10). Hineni (H2009, here I am): the covenantal response of complete availability — the same response that Abraham (Gen 22:1) and Isaiah (Isa 6:8) gave at their defining moments of commission.
3.5: al-tiqrav halom shal-ne'alecha me'al raglecha ki hamakom asher ata omed alav admat-qodesh hu: 'Do not come near here; remove your sandals from your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.' Admat qodesh (H127 + H6944, holy ground/soil): the ground becomes holy by the divine presence — holiness is not a property of places but of YHWH's dwelling. Joshua receives the same command before Jericho (Josh 5:15: remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy). The bare foot on holy ground signals that the human approaches naked — without any human covering or pretension.
3.6: anochi Elohei avicha Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchak ve'Elohei Yaakov (ʾānōkî ʾĕlōhê ʾābîkā ʾĕlōhê ʾabrāhām ʾĕlōhê yiṣḥāq wĕʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb): 'I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' The four-fold divine identification grounds the Sinai revelation in the patriarchal covenant — the God now speaking is not a new deity but the same covenant God of three generations. Jesus cites this verse in the resurrection debate (Mark 12:26-27): God said I am (not I was) the God of Abraham — YHWH identifies himself as God of those who are alive, not dead. The present tense of divine self-identification reveals the resurrection.
3.7: ra'o raiti et-oni ammi asher beMitsrayim (rāʾōh rāʾîtî ʾet-ʿŏnî ʿammî ʾăšer bĕmiṣrāyim): 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt.' Ra'o raiti: infinitive absolute + perfect — the most emphatic Hebrew construction (truly seen, seen with seeing). The doubled seeing is YHWH's emphasis that his attention has not been absent or inattentive. Oni (H6040, affliction/poverty/misery): the same root as the oppression of v.1:11-12 (anah). Ammi (my people): YHWH claims Israel as his own — not merely a people he created but a people he owns in covenant. The possessive ammi is the ground of the Exodus.
3.8: vaered lehatsilo miyad Mitsrayim ulehaaloto min-ha'aretz hahi el-eretz tova verechava el-eretz zavat chalav udevash: 'I have come down to deliver him from the hand of Egypt and to bring him up out of that land to a good and broad land, a land flowing with milk and honey.' The two movements of divine rescue: yered (come down, descend) to deliver, then alah (bring up, ascend) to the promised land. YHWH's descending is the incarnational pattern of divine rescue. Eretz zavat chalav udevash (land flowing with milk and honey): the standard covenantal description of Canaan (used 20+ times); milk and honey represent pastoral abundance (livestock) and agricultural sweetness.
3.9: gam ra'iti et-halacatz asher Mitsrayim lochatzim otam: 'I have also seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.' Lachatz (H3906, oppression/pressure) — the crushing pressure of empire on the covenant people. The doubled seeing (v.7 and v.9) intensifies the theological claim: YHWH has seen both the suffering of his people and the active oppression of their oppressors. The Exodus is both a rescue and a judgment — YHWH acting as both savior and judge simultaneously.
3.10: ve'atah lecha ve'eshlachacha el-Paroh vehotse et-ami bnei Yisrael miMitsrayim: 'And now go, and I will send you to Pharaoh that you may bring my people, the children of Israel, out of Egypt.' The commission: YHWH's seeing becomes YHWH's sending. The pattern of prophetic commission throughout the OT (Isaiah in 6:8-9; Jeremiah in 1:7-8; Ezekiel in 2:3-4): the one who has seen the divine glory is sent to the people. The divine imperative (go) is backed by the divine resource (I will send you — the verb shalach guarantees the sender's authority stands behind the sent one).
3.11: mi anochi ki elech el-Paroh: 'Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh?' Moses's first objection. Mi anochi (who am I?): the rhetorical question of inadequacy — the same that Gideon asks (Judg 6:15), Saul asks (1 Sam 9:21), and David asks (2 Sam 7:18). The question is not false modesty but a real reckoning with human limitation before divine commission. The answer is not a reassurance of Moses's adequacy but a promise of divine presence: ki ehyeh imach (for I will be with you) — the commission rests on YHWH's presence, not Moses's competence.
3.12: vayomer ki ehyeh imach (wayyōʾmer kî-ʾehyeh ʿimmāḵ): 'He said: for I will be with you.' Ki ehyeh imach — the promise of divine presence that answers every human inadequacy. Ehyeh (H1961, I will be): the first-person imperfect of hayah (to be) — the same root that becomes the divine name in v.14. YHWH's self-revelation as I AM is grounded in his promise of I-will-be-with-you: the divine name announces the covenantal presence. The sign given is counter-intuitive: this is the sign — after you bring the people out you will worship on this mountain. The proof of the commission comes at its completion, not before.
3.13: veyamru li mah shmo mah omar aleihem: 'They will say to me: What is his name? What shall I say to them?' Moses's second objection — anticipating the question of authority. In the ancient world, knowing a divine name was knowing the basis for appeal. The Israelites ask not merely for information but for a ground of trust: which god has sent you, and can we count on him? The answer Moses receives exceeds all expectation — not a name that places YHWH among other gods but a name that transcends the category.
3.15: YHWH Elohei avoteichem Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchak ve'Elohei Yaakov shlachani aleiichem zeh shmi le'olam vezeh zikri ledor dor (Yhwh ʾĕlōhê ʾăbōtêkem ʾĕlōhê ʾabrāhām ʾĕlōhê yiṣḥāq wĕʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb šĕlāḥanî ʾălêkem zeh šĕmî lĕʿōlām wĕzeh zikĕrî lĕdōr dōr): 'YHWH, God of your fathers, God of Abraham, God of Isaac, and God of Jacob, has sent me to you. This is my name forever, and this is my memorial for all generations.' The Tetragrammaton established as the permanent covenant name: le'olam (forever) and ledor dor (generation to generation). The name revealed in v.14 (ehyeh asher ehyeh) is now given its public form as YHWH — the third-person form of the same hayah root (He who IS/causes to be). Every generation of the covenant community calls on the same name.
3.16: leich ve'asafta et-ziknei Yisrael: 'Go and gather the elders of Israel.' The community-structure through which the divine word is mediated: the elders (zeqenim, H2205) are the representative leadership of the tribal covenant community. Before Moses addresses Pharaoh, he must address Israel's own leaders. The divine commission is not a solo heroic act but a communal call: the deliverer must first secure the covenant community's participation in their own liberation.
3.17: va'omar alachem me'oni Mitsrayim: 'I will bring you up from the affliction of Egypt.' The divine announcement before the event: YHWH promises the Exodus before it happens, establishing faith as the anticipation of fulfilled promise. The pattern of promise-then-fulfillment is the structure of all biblical covenant: what God says he will do becomes the ground of present trust even before the evidence is visible.
3.18: veshamu leqolecha: 'They will listen to your voice.' YHWH's assurance that the elders will hear — but the assurance is immediately followed by the warning that Pharaoh will not (v.19: I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go). The contrast structures the Exodus: Israel will hear; Pharaoh will not. The capacity to hear is a covenant gift — those within the covenant community have ears to hear what Pharaoh's hardened heart cannot receive.
3.19: va'ani yodea ki lo-yiten etchem melek Mitsrayim lahaloch: 'I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless compelled by a mighty hand.' YHWH's foreknowledge of Pharaoh's refusal is not the cause of it — Pharaoh will harden his own heart first (Exod 7:13-14) before YHWH confirms the hardening. The mighty hand (yad chazaqah) that overcomes the refusal is the same mighty hand by which YHWH brought Israel out of Egypt — the Exodus formula for divine power.
3.20: veshalach et-yadi vehiketi et-Mitsrayim bechol nifle'otai: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders.' Nifle'otai (H6381, my wonders/marvelous acts): the plagues are YHWH's wonders — acts that exceed natural causation and display divine power. Nifla'ot is cognate with pele (wonder, H6382) — used for YHWH's incomprehensible acts (Ps 77:14; 78:11-12; 105:5) and for the name of the angel of the LORD (Judg 13:18). The plagues are not arbitrary punishment but the pedagogical display of YHWH's incomparable power.
3.21: venatati et-chen ha'am hazeh be'einei Mitsrayim: 'I will give this people favor in the sight of the Egyptians.' Chen (H2580, favor/grace): YHWH sovereignly disposing the Egyptians to give generously to their departing slaves. The plundering of Egypt (v.22) is not theft but the providential reversal of the oppression — the labor never compensated is now paid in kind. Paul cites a similar providential generosity in 2 Cor 8:21: we aim at what is honorable.
3.22: ve'nitzaltem et-Mitsrayim (wĕniṣṣaltem ʾet-miṣrāyim): 'You shall plunder Egypt.' Nitzal (H5337, plunder/strip) — the same root used for rescue/deliverance elsewhere (YHWH nitzal Israel from enemies). The plunder of Egypt is the material counterpart to the spiritual deliverance: the covenant people leave with both freedom and provision, equipped for the wilderness ahead. The pattern of deliverance that includes material provision recurs in Christ's commission to the disciples: he sends them out with provision (Luke 10:7 — the laborer deserves his wages).

12.5: seh tamim (śeh tāmîm): 'a lamb without blemish' — the Passover lamb must be tamim (perfect, without defect), the sacrificial standard that applies to all Levitical offerings (Lev 22:19-20) and is applied to Christ in 1 Pet 1:19: 'without blemish or spot.' The lamb is also ben shanah (in its first year), symbolically in the prime of life — not old and worn out. The NT applies the typology systematically: Christ is the Passover lamb (1 Cor 5:7), his bones were not broken per Exod 12:46 (John 19:36), and his blood marks and protects the new covenant community.
12.1: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe veEl-Aharon beEretz Mitzrayim: 'YHWH said to Moses and Aaron in Egypt.' The address to both Moses and Aaron (rather than Moses alone relaying to Aaron) elevates the Passover instruction to the highest level of direct divine command. The phrase beEretz Mitzrayim ('in Egypt') is emphasized — this command is given while Israel is still in the land of bondage, before the exodus. The Passover ritual is thus defined as an act performed within the context of redemption-in-progress, not retrospectively from safety.
12.2: hachodesh hazeh lachem rosh chadashim: 'this month shall be for you the beginning of months.' YHWH resets the calendar: the month of the Passover (Nisan/Abib, spring) becomes the first month of Israel's sacred year. The calendar reset is a statement of sovereignty: YHWH defines time itself for his covenant people. The phrase lachem ('for you') is emphatic — this new calendar belongs to Israel specifically, as a testimony of their redemption. The year now begins with the Passover, encoding the memory of redemption into the very structure of time.
12.3: seh l'vet avot: 'a lamb/kid per household' (seh, H7716, small cattle — either sheep or goat, as v. 5 clarifies). The Passover is organized by households (beit avot, ancestral households) — not individuals, not the national assembly, but family units. The Passover is fundamentally a family meal, a household ceremony. This household structure is fundamental to the transmission of the covenant narrative to each generation (v. 26-27) and becomes foundational to the NT's household-church model (Acts 16:31-34: “believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved, you and your household”).
12.4: ufikku ish lefal ochlo: 'you shall count/divide up according to each person's eating.' The calculation principle (puk, to calculate, count) requires each household to estimate how many people can consume a whole lamb and share with neighbors if needed. The quantitative precision — every person counted, nothing wasted — reflects the covenantal seriousness of the Passover. Lifal ochlo (lit. 'according to the mouth of eating') uses the body-part metonymy peh (mouth) for eating. The communal sharing principle ensures no one is excluded for practical reasons.
12.6: ben haarbayim: 'between the two evenings' — a precise but debated temporal notation. The Pharisees understood it as between sunset (the first evening, when the sun goes down) and nightfall (the second evening, when darkness is complete) — roughly 3:00–5:00 PM. The Sadducees took it as between afternoon and sunset. The Synoptic Gospels place the Last Supper on the Passover night; John places the crucifixion on the day of Passover preparation (John 19:14) — Jesus dying as the lambs were being slaughtered. The timing of Christ's death aligns with the ben haarbayim slaughter of the Passover lamb.
12.7: velaqchu min-hadam venatenu al-shtei hamezuzot veal-hamashqof: 'they shall take some of the blood and put it on the two doorposts and the lintel.' The mezuzot (doorposts, from mezuzah, pillar/post) and mashqof (lintel, top of the doorframe) become the site of the blood application. The hyssop applicator (v. 22) and the blood-on-wood pattern anticipate the cross's blood-application to the wooden frame. The verb natan (to give/place) used for putting blood on the doorposts is the same verb used for YHWH's giving of signs and covenants — the blood-sign is an act of covenant protection.
12.8: kal-maror (bitter herbs): maror (H4844, bitterness) — the bitter herbs of the Passover meal encode the memory of slavery's bitterness (vaymareiru et-chayeihem, 'they made their lives bitter,' Exod 1:14). Matzot (unleavened bread, H4682): bread made without leaven (chametz), baked in haste. Paul explains the significance in 1 Cor 5:7-8: 'chametz' (leaven) = malice and evil; matzot (unleavened bread) = sincerity and truth. The Passover meal contains its own theological interpretation encoded in its ingredients: the bitterness of slavery, the haste of redemption, the purity required for the new beginning.
12.9: tzali-esh: 'roasted over fire' — not raw (na) and not boiled in water (mevushal bamayim). The specific requirement for fire-roasting distinguishes the Passover preparation from the standard Israelite cooking method (boiling) and from raw consumption. Fire in the OT is associated with divine holiness and judgment (Gen 3:24; Exod 3:2; 19:18). The fire-roasted lamb is thus associated with YHWH's presence and glory. The specific prohibition against boiling may have also distinguished Israel's practice from Egyptian or Canaanite religious cooking customs.
12.10: lo totiru mimenu ad-boker: 'you shall not leave any of it until morning.' The Passover lamb must be consumed entirely in a single night — nothing stored, nothing saved. The remainder must be burned (ushvarti ba'esh). The prohibition against leftovers has two functions: (1) the lamb is a sacred meal, not ordinary food to be treated casually; (2) the meal commemorates the haste of the exodus — they would leave by morning. The fire-disposal of the remainder also ensures no carcass remains to be desecrated. The totality of consumption pictures the completeness of Christ's atoning work: nothing left incomplete.
12.11: pesach hu l'YHWH: 'it is the Passover of YHWH.' The noun pesach (H6453) is derived from the verb pasach (v. 13, 23: 'to pass over/protect'). The traditional interpretation: YHWH “passed over” the blood-marked houses. More nuanced readings suggest pasach means to “protect” or “hover over” (cf. Isa 31:5: 'like birds hovering, so YHWH of hosts will protect Jerusalem') rather than merely “skip past.” Bechipazon (in haste, H2649) — the posture of readiness: loins girded, sandals on feet, staff in hand. The Passover meal is eaten as people on the move, not at leisure. The eschatological dimension: the meal anticipates departure from the present age.
12.12: veavarti beEretz Mitzrayim balaylah hazeh: 'I will pass through Egypt on this night.' YHWH acts in the first person — no angel is mentioned as the agent (contrast the “destroyer” of v. 23). The Piel of avar (to pass through, cross over) with YHWH as subject is the most direct form of divine personal action in the plague narrative. Ubechol-elohei Mitzrayim e'eseh shefatim: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments.' The plagues are explicitly identified as judgments against Egypt's gods — each plague targets a deity of the Egyptian pantheon: the Nile (Hapi), the sun (Ra/Khepri), life-force (various animal deities). The tenth plague kills the Pharaoh's heir — the incarnation of the divine Horus. YHWH alone is Elohim.
12.13: vehayah hadam lachem l'ot: 'the blood shall be a sign for you on the houses.' Ot (H226, sign, token) is the same word used for the covenant signs of rainbow (Gen 9:12-13) and circumcision (Gen 17:11). The blood-sign on the doorposts is the Passover covenant-sign — the external mark of covenant protection. Ufasachti aleichem: 'I will pass over/protect you' — the verb pasach (H6452) used in the Qal perfect. YHWH sees the blood and acts protectively. The NT parallel: the blood of Christ applied to the believer is the new covenant sign of protection from divine judgment (Rom 3:25: hilasterion; 1 Cor 5:7: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed).
12.14: zikaron (H2146, memorial, remembrance): 'this day shall be a memorial for you.' The Passover is designed as a zikaron — an act of sacred memory that makes the past event present for each generation. Deuteronomy 16's Passover instructions emphasize zachor (remember) as the primary obligation. The Last Supper's command touto poieite eis ten emen anamnesin ('do this in remembrance of me,' Luke 22:19; 1 Cor 11:24-25) uses the Greek anamnesis, the exact LXX translation of zikaron. The Eucharist is the new Passover zikaron — the memorial that makes the new exodus present for the covenant community in each generation.
12.15: chametz (H2557, leaven, leavened bread): to be removed from houses for seven days. The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins the day after Passover and continues for seven days (vv. 15-20). Chametz is the fermenting agent — it causes bread to rise through a process of decomposition. Paul's interpretation (1 Cor 5:6-8) applies chametz as a metaphor for moral corruption: 'a little leaven leavens the whole lump; therefore cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. For Christ our Passover has been sacrificed.' The seven-day removal of leaven pictures the complete moral renewal that accompanies redemption.
12.16: miqra-kodesh (H4744, sacred assembly, holy convocation): 'a holy convocation on the first and seventh days.' Miqra is derived from qara (to call, summon) — the assembly is called by YHWH. The sacred assemblies frame the week-long festival. Lo-ta'asu vo melacha ('no work shall be done') — the restriction on labor marks the day as set apart for YHWH's purposes, not human labor. The exception: food preparation (asher ye'achel lechol nefesh) is permitted, grounding the sacred in the physical necessity of eating — the festival is embodied, not merely spiritual.
12.17: ushemartem et-hamatzot: 'you shall guard the unleavened bread / observe the Festival of Unleavened Bread.' The Qal perfect of shamar (H8104, to keep, guard, observe) — the same verb used for keeping the Sabbath and the covenant. Kiy beetzem hayom hazeh hotzeti et-tzivoteichem: 'on this very day I brought your armies out of Egypt.' Tzivot (armies, hosts) — Israel in the exodus is framed as YHWH's military force. The same language — Tzivot YHWH (the armies of YHWH) — appears in v. 41. The exodus is not a refugee escape but a military march of YHWH's forces.
12.18: beavirim ad-esrim veechad lachodesh: 'from the evening of the fourteenth to the twenty-first.' The Feast of Unleavened Bread begins on the evening of the fourteenth (when the Passover lamb is eaten) and continues until the twenty-first — seven full days. The precision of the calendar dates reflects the covenantal seriousness: the times are YHWH's appointed times (moedim), not flexible traditions. Erev ad-erev (evening to evening) reflects the Hebrew reckoning of days from sunset to sunset (Gen 1:5 pattern), the same principle underlying the Sabbath.
12.19: kol-se'or lo yimmatze bevatechem: 'no leaven shall be found in your houses' — the command escalates from removing leaven (v. 15) to ensuring none is even present. The thoroughness of the pre-Passover leaven search (bedikat chametz) became one of Judaism's most detailed ritual observances. Ger u-vezrach ha'aretz ('both for the foreigner and for the native of the land'): the rule applies equally to resident aliens and native Israelites. The equal application to gerim (resident foreigners) anticipates the NT abolition of ethnic distinctions within the covenant community.
12.20: kol-machmat lo tochelu: 'eat nothing leavened.' The prohibition is absolute and repeated for emphasis throughout the chapter. The seven-day period of unleavened bread was so central to Israelite identity that chametz became a theological symbol. The Qumran community's strict unleavened-bread practice and Paul's application to moral purity (1 Cor 5:7-8) both reflect the deep symbolic resonance of the chametz/matzot opposition — corruption vs. purity, old age vs. new creation.
12.21: mishchu uqechu lachem seh lemishpechotechem: 'draw/select and take a lamb for your families.' Moses now communicates YHWH's instructions to the elders of Israel — the chain of authority flows: YHWH → Moses → elders → people. The Qal imperative mishchu (draw out, select) specifically means to draw/choose from the flock — a deliberate act of selection. The communal family-centered instruction reinforces the household structure of the Passover. Moses instructs Israel to “slaughter the Passover lamb” (vesachatu hapasach) — the lamb's slaughter is identified with the feast itself.
12.22: ezov (H231, hyssop): a small bushy plant with absorbent properties used in purification rituals throughout the OT (Lev 14:4-6, 49-52; Num 19:6, 18; Ps 51:7). The hyssop branch dipped in the basin of blood and applied to the doorframes is the original ritual use of this plant. Its later purification associations (Ps 51:7: 'purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean') ground the Passover blood-application in the broader purity theology. John 19:29 places a hyssop branch in the scene of Jesus's crucifixion — a deliberate echo of the Passover hyssop application.
12.23: hamashchit (H4889, the destroyer): 'YHWH will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses.' The mashchit (destroyer, from shachat, to destroy, corrupt) appears here as the agent of the final plague — distinct from YHWH himself acting directly (v. 12). Hebrews 11:28 summarizes: 'By faith he kept the Passover and sprinkled the blood, so that the Destroyer of the firstborn might not touch them.' The mashchit is the same figure as the 'destroying angel' in later texts (2 Sam 24:16; 1 Chr 21:15). The blood on the doorpost stops the destroyer — it is the visible covenant sign that marks the household as YHWH's.
12.24: ushemertem et-hadavar hazeh lechok lecha ulvaneicha ad-olam: 'keep this word as a statute for you and your children forever.' The Passover is not merely historical commemoration but perpetual covenant obligation — lechok olam (an eternal statute). The generational instruction follows in vv. 26-27: the children's question and the parent's answer are built into the ritual, ensuring the narrative is transmitted. The question-and-answer structure of the Passover became the template for catechetical instruction throughout Israel's covenant history.
12.25: vahayah ki tavo'u el-ha'aretz: 'when you enter the land.' The Passover instruction looks forward to the land — it is given in Egypt but oriented toward Canaan. The future tense (tavo'u) frames the current captivity within a trajectory of promise and fulfillment. Ushmartem et-ha'avoda hazot ('you shall keep this service/worship') — the word avoda (H5656, service, work, worship) was used for Israel's slavery in Egypt and is now redirected: the same word describes Israel's worship-service to YHWH. Redemption from slavery transforms avoda from oppression to devotion.
12.26: vehayah ki yomeru aleichem bneichem: 'when your children say to you...' The intergenerational transmission of the Passover narrative is built into the covenant structure: children will ask, parents will answer. The Haggadah's four sons (the wise, the wicked, the simple, and the one who does not know how to ask) are rabbinic elaborations of this built-in catechetical question. The Passover's narrative structure — question, answer, story — becomes the template for Christian kerygmatic preaching: the gospel is proclaimed in response to questions generated by covenant signs (baptism, Eucharist).
12.27: zevach pesach hu l'YHWH: 'it is the Passover sacrifice to YHWH.' The answer to the children's question names the meal as a zevach (sacrifice, offering) — the Passover is not merely a family dinner but a sacrificial act. Asher pasach al-batei vnei Yisrael beMitzrayim ('who passed over the houses of Israel in Egypt') — the explanation links the meal to the original event it commemorates. Vayiqod ha'am vayishtachavu ('the people bowed down and worshiped') — the response to the instruction is worship, not merely compliance. The Passover narrative evokes awe and gratitude before it demands observance.
12.28: vayelechu vaya'asu benei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH: 'the Israelites went and did exactly as YHWH had commanded.' The compliance formula (ka'asher tzivah YHWH, 'as YHWH commanded') appears repeatedly throughout Exodus (cf. chs. 39-40 re. the tabernacle). The formula marks covenant faithfulness: Israel's obedience to the precise Passover instructions is the covenant act that activates the divine protection. The collective obedience of the community (benei Yisrael, sons of Israel) stands as a model of covenant response — hearing and doing.
12.29: vayehiy bechatzot halaylah: 'at midnight' — the fulfillment of Moses's prophetic announcement (11:4). The precision of the fulfillment — at the exact time YHWH announced — validates the prophetic word. Vayak YHWH kol-bechor beeretz Mitzrayim: 'YHWH struck every firstborn in Egypt' — the Hiphil of nakah (H5221, to strike, smite) with YHWH as subject. The tenth plague requires no naturalistic explanation: it is direct divine action at the announced time. The scope — from Pharaoh's throne to the prisoner in the dungeon to the livestock — demonstrates YHWH's sovereignty over every social stratum of Egypt.
12.30: vataqom hu vechol-avadav vechol-Mitzrayim laylah: 'he arose in the night, he and all his officials and all Egypt.' The national night-rising echoes the earlier plagues (8:20: Pharaoh arose early) but this time there is no negotiation — only tzeaqah gedolah (great cry). Ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met: 'for there was not a house where there was no one dead' — the totality of the judgment is emphasized. The Egyptian household cry mirrors Israel's cry under slavery (Exod 2:23; 3:7). YHWH has heard the cry of his people and now Egypt experiences the cry of the bereaved.
12.31: qumu tze'u mitoch ammi: 'rise up, go out from among my people.' Pharaoh's night summons to Moses and Aaron ends with what he refused for ten plagues: 'go, serve YHWH as you have said.' Gam-tzoneichem gam-biqrechem qechu ka'asher dibartem: 'even your flocks and your herds, take as you have spoken' — every condition Moses demanded is conceded in a single sentence. Uvelachtem gam oti: 'and bless me also' — Pharaoh now asks a blessing from the man he commanded to leave his presence never to return (10:28). The reversal is complete: the oppressor becomes the petitioner.
12.32: uvelachtem gam oti: 'bless me also.' Pharaoh's request for blessing from Moses — whom he had threatened with death on sight (10:28) — is the ultimate reversal of the power dynamic. Varets vatekeved al-ha'am ('the Egyptians urged the people, pressing them urgently') — the very Egyptians who enslaved Israel now cannot expel them fast enough. The verb chazaq (to be strong, urge strongly) used of the Egyptians' urging echoes the chazaq of Pharaoh's hardened heart — the strength that held Israel captive is now directed toward releasing them.
12.33: vatechez Mitzrayim al-ha'am lemahher leshalcham min-ha'aretz: 'Egypt urged the people to hurry, to send them out of the land.' Ki amru kulanu meitim: 'for they said, “we are all dying.”' The Egyptians' fear of total annihilation drives the urgency. The reversal is stark: Israel came to Egypt as seventy people fleeing famine (Gen 46:27); they leave as a nation of 600,000 men (v. 37), bearing Egypt's wealth (vv. 35-36), with Egypt itself begging them to leave. The Exodus narrative's dramatic reversal — from humiliation to exaltation, from few to many, from poor to wealthy — is a pattern that the NT applies to Christ's death and resurrection.
12.34: vayisa ha'am et-beseto: 'the people carried their dough on their shoulders.' The kneading troughs (mishtarotam) were wrapped in clothing and carried — because they could not delay to leaven the bread. This is the factual origin of the matzot that would be baked at Sukkoth: the unleavened bread is not merely commanded but physically produced by the urgency of the departure. The hasty exit embeds the theology into the material circumstance: redemption comes without delay and cannot wait for the ordinary processes of life to complete themselves.
12.35: uvenei Yisrael asu kidvar Moshe vayish'alu meiMitzrayim: 'the Israelites did as Moses instructed and asked of the Egyptians.' The plundering of Egypt fulfills YHWH's promise to Abraham in Gen 15:14: 'afterward they shall come out with great possessions (rechush gadol).' Israel takes keli-keseph u-chelei zahav u-semalot (articles of silver, gold, and clothing) — the material wealth of the ancient world's most prosperous nation. Paul interprets this in the Spirit: the spoiling of Egypt is a type of all truth belonging to the church and being reclaimed from the “Egypt” of pagan culture (the Alexandrian tradition of theological use of pagan philosophy drew on this).
12.36: vaYHWH natan et-chen ha'am beynei Mitzrayim vayash'ilum: 'YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of Egypt, and they gave them what they asked.' The chen YHWH sovereignly provided (cf. 11:3) was the instrument of the plundering. Vayenatzelu et-Mitzrayim: 'they plundered Egypt' — the Piel of natsal (to strip, rescue) used in a plundering context. The same verb is used in 3:22 and 12:35 — YHWH promised the spoiling, Moses announced it, and now it occurs. The plundering of Egypt is the material accompaniment of the spiritual liberation: the enslaved gain not only freedom but the material means to build the tabernacle in the wilderness.
12.37: shesh meot elef ragley hagevarin: 'about 600,000 men on foot.' The number has generated enormous scholarly discussion: if 600,000 men (adult males), the total population including women and children would be 2-3 million — an enormous number for the Sinai wilderness. Some scholars read eleph (H505) not as 1,000 but as a military unit of variable size (suggesting ~5,550-6,000 men total), following George Mendenhall's analysis. The narrative's point is clear regardless of the exact count: Israel left Egypt as a nation, not a family. The 600,000 figure also connects to the census in Numbers 1-2 (603,550 men), creating a coherent numerology for the wilderness generation.
12.38: veGam erev rav ala itam: 'a mixed multitude also went up with them.' The erev rav (mixed multitude, H6154) accompanying Israel is the first indication that the Exodus was not ethnically exclusive: non-Israelites joined the departure. The same group is later blamed for the craving of meat (Num 11:4: ve'asafsuf asher beqirbo). The erev rav may have included other Semitic slaves, Egyptians married to Israelites, or people drawn by YHWH's power. The principle of the mixed multitude joining Israel prefigures Ruth, Rahab, and the Gentile inclusion in the covenant community.
12.39: ki lo chametz ki-gerushim miMitzrayim velo yachlu lehitmahmeah: 'for it was not leavened because they were expelled from Egypt and could not delay.' The physical reason for the unleavened bread is provided: there was no time to leaven it. The haste of the departure is the empirical origin of the theological requirement. The connection between urgency and unleavened bread is made explicit: the matzot that Israel eats at Passover every year is the bread of urgent departure — the bread of those who cannot linger in the old life because the new creation calls them forward.
12.40: umoshav benei Yisrael asher yashvu beMitzrayim shloshim shanah ve'arba meot shanah: '430 years.' The 430-year sojourn in Egypt (from the LXX version, extended to include time in Canaan: 'the sojourn ... in Egypt and Canaan') is cited by Paul in Gal 3:17: 'the law, which came 430 years afterward, does not annul a covenant previously ratified by God.' Paul's use confirms the 430-year figure and connects it to the Abrahamic covenant timeline: the Sinai law came 430 years after Abraham, not 430 years after the entry to Egypt (supporting the LXX's longer reckoning). The precision of the period — vayehiy mikets shloshim shanah ve'arba meot shanah (at the end of 430 years, to the very day) — emphasizes YHWH's absolute control over the timing of the Exodus.
12.41: tzivot YHWH (armies of YHWH): Israel leaves Egypt not as a refugee crowd but as tzivot YHWH — YHWH's military forces. The same term (tzva'ot, hosts/armies) is the root of the divine name YHWH Tzvaot (YHWH of Hosts/Armies), one of the most frequent divine names in the OT prophets. The exodus is a military event: YHWH is the commander, Israel is the army, and the exit from Egypt is a march. This militarized framing of the Exodus is the background for Ephesians 6's armor of God — the Christian life is framed within the same exodus-march-warfare structure.
12.42: leil shimmurim hu l'YHWH: 'a night of keeping/vigil for YHWH' — shimmurim (H8107, from shamar, to keep, guard) describes the night as one of active divine vigilance. YHWH keeps watch over his people on this night of all nights — the very night he acts to deliver them. The double use of shimmurim ('a night of vigil for YHWH to bring them out ... this same night is a night of vigil for YHWH for all the Israelites throughout their generations') frames both the original event and its annual commemoration as acts of reciprocal watching: YHWH watches over Israel, Israel watches through the night. The NT's night-vigil eschatology (Matt 25:13: keep watch; Luke 12:37: blessed are those servants whom the master finds awake) draws on the leil shimmurim pattern.
12.43: zot chuqat hapesach: 'this is the regulation of the Passover.' The Passover regulations (vv. 43-50) address participation eligibility: the central criterion is circumcision (mila). Kol-ben-nechar lo-yochal bo: 'no foreigner (lit. son of a foreign people) shall eat of it.' The restriction is covenant-based, not ethnic: the ben-nechar is distinguished from the ger (resident alien, v. 48) who may eat if circumcised. The circumcision requirement makes the covenant sign the entry point for Passover participation — covenant membership precedes covenant meal.
12.44: ve'eved ish miqnat-keseph: 'a slave purchased with money' — a permanent household slave (eved) who has been circumcised may eat the Passover. The contrast with the hired worker (toshav ve'sachir, v. 45) who may not eat reflects the distinction between full household membership (the slave belongs to the household permanently) and transient engagement. The slave's circumcision integrates him into the covenant community. Paul's use of slave imagery for the Christian's relationship to Christ (Rom 6:18-22; 1 Cor 7:22) draws on this covenant-household logic: belonging to Christ means full participation in the covenant meal.
12.45: toshav vesachir lo-yochal bo: 'a temporary resident and a hired worker may not eat of it.' The toshav (sojourner, resident alien without permanent status) and the sachir (hired worker, day laborer) are excluded — their relationship to the household is temporary and transactional, not covenantal. This verse creates the distinction that v. 48's exception clarifies: a resident foreigner who submits to circumcision transitions from toshav to full covenant member. The line between inclusion and exclusion is the covenant sign, not ethnicity.
12.46: veetzem lo tishberu-bo: 'you shall not break a bone of it.' The prohibition against breaking the Passover lamb's bones (also in Num 9:12) is cited in John 19:36 as fulfilled when the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs: 'these things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled: not one of his bones will be broken.' The unbroken-bone requirement for the Passover lamb is one of the most precise typological fulfillments in the NT. The physical integrity of the lamb corresponds to the physical integrity of Christ's body — the true Passover lamb. Ps 34:20 ('he keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken') applies the bone-protection language to the righteous man, which John also sees fulfilled in Jesus.
12.47: kol-adat Yisrael ya'asu oto: 'the whole congregation of Israel shall keep it.' The Passover is a community-wide obligation — not optional, not individual. The edah (congregation, assembly) is the technical term for the covenant community gathered. The communal nature of the Passover meal is its fundamental character: no individual participates alone; the whole covenant community celebrates together. This community-wide structure is the background for Paul's 'the cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ? Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body' (1 Cor 10:16-17).
12.48: vechi-yagur itcha ger ve'asa pesach l'YHWH himol lo chol-zachur: 'if a stranger dwells with you and wants to keep the Passover to YHWH, he must have all the males circumcised.' The inclusion of the circumcised foreigner (ger) in the Passover community is the proto-Gentile inclusion: the covenant meal is open to those who receive the covenant sign. This verse becomes a NT proof-text for Gentile inclusion: Paul argues in Gal 3:8 that Abraham's covenant was always designed to include the nations, and the circumcision requirement has been replaced by faith in Christ (Gal 3:26-29: 'in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith ... neither Jew nor Greek').
12.49: torah achat yihyeh laezrach velager: 'the same law shall apply to the native and to the stranger.' The principle of equal legal standing for the native and the resident alien is stated explicitly — one law (torah achat), not a two-tier system. This principle appears repeatedly in the Torah (Lev 24:22; Num 9:14; 15:15-16) and is foundational to the NT abolition of Jewish-Gentile distinction in the church: 'for there is no distinction between Jew and Greek' (Rom 10:12); 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28).
12.50: vaya'asu kol-benei Yisrael ka'asher tzivah YHWH: 'all the Israelites did just as YHWH had commanded.' The compliance formula closes the Passover instruction section. The emphasis on full obedience (kol-benei Yisrael, all Israel) and precision (ka'asher tzivah YHWH, exactly as YHWH commanded) frames the Exodus as a covenant-keeping event: Israel's obedience to the Passover instructions is the faith-act that receives YHWH's protection. The same pattern — hearing the word, doing it precisely — underlies the NT's 'faith that works' (James 2:17-18) and Jesus's parable of the two builders (Matt 7:24-27).
12.51: vayehiy betzem hayom hazeh hotzi YHWH et-benei Yisrael mei'eretz Mitzrayim al-tzivotam: 'on that very day YHWH brought the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions.' Betzem hayom hazeh ('on this very day') — the phrase used throughout the Exodus for the precision of divine timing (also in v. 17, 41). The book of Exodus closes its narrative of the departure with this summary: YHWH is the subject, Israel is the object, and the movement is out from Egypt. The phrase al-tzivotam ('by their divisions/armies') repeats the military framing of v. 41 — Israel leaves as YHWH's organized military force, not as a chaotic mass. The Exodus is a completed, organized, divinely orchestrated event.

20.2: anochi YHWH eloheicha asher hotzeiticha meeretz mitzraim mibeit avadim: 'I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The Decalogue opens not with a command but with a redemption narrative — the commands follow from the prior act of grace. YHWH does not say 'obey me so that I will be your God and bring you out' but 'I brought you out; therefore I am your God; therefore obey.' The evangelical-imperative structure of the Decalogue is: grace, then obligation. Paul's ethical sections follow the same pattern: Romans 1-11 (gospel), then 12-16 (imperatives); Galatians 1-4 (grace), then 5-6 (walk).
20.1: vayedaber Elohim et kol-hadevarim ha'eleh lemor (waydabbēr ʾĕlōhîm ʾēt kol-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh lēʾmōr): 'God spoke all these words, saying.' The direct divine speech of the Decalogue is unique in the OT — YHWH speaking directly to the entire assembled people, not through a prophet. Kol-hadevarim ha'eleh (all these words): the Decalogue is presented as divine speech, not human legislation. The authority of the Ten Commandments derives entirely from their speaker, not from their reasonableness or social utility.
20.3: lo yihyeh lecha elohim acherim al panai (lōʾ yihyeh-lĕkā ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ʿal-pānāy): 'You shall have no other gods before my face.' Al panai (before my face/in my presence): the commandment is spatially framed — in YHWH's presence there is no room for other allegiances. The command is not primarily a philosophical monotheism but a covenant loyalty: YHWH's face defines the space of relationship, and in that space the exclusive claim holds. Elohim acherim (other gods): the commandment does not deny the existence of other supernatural powers but prohibits their veneration in YHWH's presence.
20.4: lo ta'aseh lecha pesel vechol-temunah asher bashamayim mima'al (lōʾ taʿăśeh-lĕkā pesel wĕḵol-tĕmûnāh ʾăšer baššāmāyim mimmaʿal): 'You shall not make for yourself an image/idol or any likeness of what is in heaven above.' Pesel (H6459, idol/image): from pasal (to cut/chisel) — a carved representation. The prohibition covers all representations of the divine in material form: sky, earth, or sea. The rationale emerges from the Sinai theophany: Israel heard a voice but saw no form (Deut 4:12 — you heard a voice but saw no form). YHWH's invisibility is theologically determinative: to make an image is to assign a form to what has no form, to domesticate what is beyond representation.
20.5: lo tishtachaveh lahem velo ta'avdem ki anochi YHWH Eloheicha El qanna (lōʾ-ṯiŝtaḥăweh lāhem wĕlōʾ ṯoʿoḇdēm kî ʾānōkî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾēl qannāʾ): 'You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God.' El qanna (H7072, jealous God): qanna from qana (H7065, be jealous/zealous) — used exclusively of YHWH in the OT (Exod 34:14; Deut 4:24; 5:9; 6:15; Josh 24:19; Nah 1:2). Divine jealousy is not pettiness but the passion of a covenant Lord who has committed himself exclusively to his people and therefore cannot tolerate their divided loyalty. The jealousy of YHWH corresponds to the covenant's exclusive demand: monogamy and monotheism have the same structure. Poked avon avot al-banim: visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children — the generational transmission of covenant violation (not arbitrary punishment but the natural consequence of covenantal patterns passing down).
20.6: ve'oseh chesed la'alafim le'ohavai uleshomrei mitzvotai: 'Showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.' The asymmetry: iniquity to the third and fourth generation; chesed (H2617, steadfast love/covenant faithfulness) to alafim (thousands of generations). The divine mercy vastly exceeds the divine judgment in scope. The judgment extends three or four generations; the chesed extends to thousands. YHWH's primary orientation is mercy, not wrath; judgment is the reluctant instrument, love is the determinative character.
20.7: lo tissa et-shem YHWH Eloheicha lashav (lōʾ tiśśāʾ ʾet-šēm Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā laššāwĕʾ): 'You shall not lift/bear the name of YHWH your God for emptiness/vanity.' Tissa (H5375, lift/carry/bear): the verb is not about swearing but bearing — the name of YHWH is something carried. Shav (H7723, emptiness/vanity/worthlessness): not merely false oaths but any use of the divine name for trivial, empty, or purposeless ends. To bear the divine name (as Israel does as a covenant people) for emptiness is to misuse the covenant identity — whether in false oaths, magic, manipulation, or casual irreverence. The commandment protects the weight of the divine name.
20.8: zachor et-yom haShabbat lekadsho (zāḵôr ʾet-yôm haššabbāt lĕqaddĕšô): 'Remember the Sabbath day to keep it holy.' Zachor (H2142, remember): the covenantal memory-word — remember is not a passive mental recollection but an active bringing-into-the-present (as in YHWH remembering the covenant in Exod 2:24). Lekadsho (to sanctify/set it apart): the Sabbath's holiness is not intrinsic to the day but given by covenantal act — the same sanctification word used for Sinai (v.8-10). Time is made holy by separation and divine assignment, just as space is made holy.
20.9: sheshet yamim ta'avod ve'asita kol-melachtecha: 'Six days you shall labor and do all your work.' The positive command precedes the prohibition: six days of productive work are commanded before the seventh of rest. The Sabbath is not the negation of labor but its covenantal framing: human creativity and productivity are honored in six days; the seventh day declares that human work is embedded in a larger divine rhythm and does not define existence. Work receives its meaning from the rest that frames it.
20.10: veyom hashvii Shabbat laYHWH Eloheicha lo ta'aseh khol-melacha atah uvinkha uvitecha avdecha va'amatcha uvhemtecha: 'But the seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH your God. You shall not do any work, you or your son or your daughter, your servant or your maidservant, your livestock, or the stranger within your gates.' The comprehensive scope of the Sabbath rest extends to every level of the social hierarchy: children, servants, animals, foreigners — the Sabbath creates a covenantal equality of rest. Even the animals and the alien resident participate in the covenant's rhythm of rest. No social hierarchy survives the Sabbath day.
20.11: ki sheshet yamim asah YHWH et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz vayinafash (kî šēšet-yāmîm ʿāśāh Yhwh ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ wayyinnāpaš): 'For in six days YHWH made the heavens and earth, and rested (nefesh) on the seventh day.' Vayinafash (H5314): from nefesh (soul/breath/life) — YHWH breathed/refreshed/took breath. The anthropomorphic language is striking: YHWH's rest on the seventh day is described as the refreshment of a living being. Human Sabbath rest participates in the divine rest-pattern embedded in creation — the weekly rhythm is cosmologically grounded, not merely socially convenient.
20.12: kabed et-avicha ve'et-imecha (kabbēd ʾet-ʾābîkā wĕʾet-ʾimmekā): 'Honor your father and your mother.' Kabed (H3513, honor/be heavy): from the root meaning heavy/weighty — to honor is to give weight to, to treat as consequential. The commandment grounds social order in the family: the first four commandments address the vertical relationship (Israel to YHWH); this commandment transitions to the horizontal (Israelite to Israelite) by beginning with the foundational human relationship. The promise attached — that your days may be long in the land — ties individual obedience to national covenantal blessing.
20.13: lo tirtzach (lōʾ tirṣāḥ): 'You shall not murder.' Ratzach (H7523, murder): specifically premeditated, willful killing — not the same as harag (H2026, kill generally) or hemit (H4191, put to death). The commandment prohibits intentional, unauthorized taking of human life — a prohibition grounded in the image of God (Gen 9:6: whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own image). Human life is inviolable because the image of God makes each person a representative of the Creator.
20.14: lo tin'af (lōʾ tinʾāp): 'You shall not commit adultery.' Na'af (H5003, commit adultery): the sexual corollary of the first commandment's covenant-loyalty requirement. Marriage is the human covenant that structurally mirrors the divine covenant; adultery is the human betrayal that structurally mirrors idolatry. The prophets consistently use adultery as the metaphor for Israel's covenant-breaking (Jer 3:8; Ezek 16; Hos 1-3). The prohibition protects both the marriage covenant and the community's covenantal integrity.
20.15: lo tignov (lōʾ tiganob): 'You shall not steal.' Ganav (H1589, steal): the taking of what belongs to another — whether property, persons (kidnapping is also covered under this root, Exod 21:16), or anything belonging to another. Respect for the neighbor's possessions is grounded in respect for the neighbor as a fellow covenant member with God-given rights. The command extends the image-of-God protection from life (v.13) to dignity (v.14) to property (v.15).
20.16: lo ta'aneh vere'acha ed shav (lōʾ-taʿăneh bĕrēʿăkā ʿēd šāwĕʾ): 'You shall not testify against your neighbor as a false witness.' Ed shav (witness of emptiness/vanity): shav is the same word as in v.7 — the emptiness that misuses the divine name is the same emptiness that perverts testimony. The legal system depends on truthful testimony; false witness destroys the possibility of justice. The commandment protects the covenantal community's capacity to adjudicate rightly — the court as the arena of covenant justice.
20.17: lo tachmod beit re'echa lo tachmod eshet re'echa ve'avdo va'amato veshoro vachamoro (lōʾ taḥmōd bêt rēʿăkā lōʾ taḥmōd ʾēšet rēʿăkā wĕʿaḇdô wăʾămāṯô wĕšôrô waḥămōrô): 'You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your neighbor's wife or his servant or his maidservant or his ox or his donkey or anything that belongs to your neighbor.' Chamad (H2530, covet/desire intensely): the desire for what belongs to another, named for the attractive pull it exerts. Unique among the commandments in targeting inner desire rather than external action — the only commandment that can only be violated before the act. Paul cites this commandment as the one that awakened his awareness of sin (Rom 7:7: I would not have known covetousness if the law had not said, you shall not covet).
20.18: vekhol-ha'am ro'im et-haqolot ve'et-halappidim ve'et qol hashofar ve'et-hahar ashen vayar ha'am vayanu'u vaya'amdu merachok: 'All the people saw the thunderings and the flashes and the sound of the trumpet and the mountain smoking. When the people saw it, they trembled and stood far off.' The synesthetic description — seeing thunder and lightning, the visible and audible blended — is the phenomenology of theophany. The people's trembling response and their standing far off is the appropriate creaturely response to unconditioned divine presence.
20.19: vayomeru el-Mosheh daber-atah imanu venishma'ah ve'al-yedaber imanu Elohim pen-namut: 'They said to Moses: You speak to us and we will listen, but let not God speak to us lest we die.' The people's request for a human mediator is not disobedience but honest self-knowledge: they cannot sustain the direct encounter with YHWH. Their fear validates the necessity of the Mosaic mediatorial office. The pattern repeats: Israel needs a mediator between YHWH's holiness and their creaturely limitation. Moses is the type; Christ is the antitype — the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5) who resolves the mediatorial need permanently.
20.20: vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am al-tira'u ki livliti nassot etchem ba ha'Elohim uvavur tihyeh yirato al-peneichem: 'Moses said to the people: Do not be afraid, for God has come to test you, that the fear of him may be before you, that you may not sin.' The purpose of the theophanic terror: livliti nassot etchem (to test you) and yirato al-peneichem (his fear before your face). The terrifying encounter is not punishment but pedagogy — the fear generated is the appropriate orientation toward YHWH that prevents sin. The theology of fear and trembling (Phil 2:12 — work out your salvation with fear and trembling) is rooted in this Sinai principle.
20.21: vaya'amod ha'am merachok veMosheh nigash el-ha'arafel: 'The people stood far off, and Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was.' Arafel (H6205, thick darkness/deep darkness): the darkness that surrounds the divine light — not the absence of YHWH but his concealment within an overwhelming cloud. The people stand far off; Moses enters the darkness alone. The covenant mediator goes where the people cannot follow — into the thick of divine presence. Hebrews uses this pattern: Christ entered not a human sanctuary but heaven itself (Heb 9:24), the true most holy place where we cannot go except through him.
20.22: vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ko tomar el-bnei Yisrael atem re'item ki min-hashamayim dibarti imachem: 'YHWH said to Moses: Thus you shall say to the children of Israel: You yourselves have seen that I have spoken with you from heaven.' The direct appeal to witnessed experience: atem re'item (you yourselves have seen). The covenant is grounded not in tradition alone but in the community's own witnessed encounter with the divine voice. The appeal to testimony-based faith is the OT foundation for the NT's eyewitness theology (1 John 1:1-3: what we have seen and heard we proclaim to you).
20.23: lo ta'asun iti elohei kesef ve'elohei zahav lo ta'asun lachem: 'You shall not make alongside me gods of silver and of gold; you shall not make such for yourselves.' The prohibition immediately follows the theophany — the community that has just heard YHWH's voice directly is immediately warned against the idol-substitute. The golden calf (Exod 32) will violate this commandment within weeks. The speed of Israel's violation underscores the commandment's necessity: human inclination toward tangible gods is powerful and persistent.
20.24: mizbach adamah ta'aseh li vezavachta alav et-oloteicha ve'et-shlameicha et-tsoncha ve'et-beqarecha: 'An altar of earth you shall make for me and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep and your oxen.' The simple altar permitted after the Decalogue — earth or unhewn stone (v.25) — sets the minimalist principle for early Israelite worship: the altar's simplicity declares YHWH's sufficiency. No human craftsmanship adorns the first altar; the only material is what the earth already provides. The elaborate tabernacle and temple are later, more specific instructions; this is the baseline.
20.25: ve'im-mizbach avanim ta'aseh-li lo-tivneh ethen gazit ki charbecha haneifta aleiha vatechaleleiha: 'And if you make me a stone altar, you shall not build it of cut stones, for if you wield your tool on it you profane it.' The prohibition of dressed stone for the altar: human tool-work profanes the altar — the natural, uncut stone preserves its integrity. The rationale is contested (the stones' natural completeness, or the avoidance of phallic symbols carved on stones). The principle: human craftsmanship is not required to make the altar acceptable to YHWH; what YHWH provides is sufficient for approach to him.
20.26: velo ta'aleh bema'alot al-mizbechi asher lo-tigaleh ervatecha alav: 'You shall not go up by steps to my altar so that your nakedness is not uncovered on it.' The altar's access must not involve raised stairs that expose the priest's nakedness — a combination of modesty requirement and anti-Canaanite contrast (Canaanite altar worship involved fertility rites). The practical regulation shapes the covenantal aesthetics of worship: the holy space requires an approach that maintains the dignity and integrity of those who serve in it.

25.9: kekol asher ani mareeh otcha et tabnit hamishkan veet tabnit kol kelav veken taashu: 'Exactly as I show you concerning the pattern [tabnit] of the tabernacle, and of all its furniture, so you shall make it.' The Hebrew tabnit (pattern/model) implies the earthly tabernacle is a copy of a heavenly original. Hebrews (8:5) quotes this verse explicitly (using LXX typos — pattern, type) to argue that the Levitical priests serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. The tabernacle's typological function is not a later Christian imposition but is built into the original instructions: Moses saw the heavenly original; Israel built the earthly copy.
25.1: Standard commission formula: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe lemor (vay-yeh-dah-BER YHWH el-Mo-SHEH le-MOR): 'And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying.' The Sinai legislation now pivots from the Decalogue and mishpatim (civil law, chs. 21-23) to the construction of the divine dwelling — miqdash and mishkan — as the covenant's physical expression.
25.2: terumah (teh-roo-MAH): 'contribution/heave offering.' H8641, from rum (to lift/raise — H7311). The offering is lifted toward God. Critical qualifier: nediv libbo (neh-DEEV lib-BO), 'whose heart moves him willingly' — H5068 from nadav (to give freely/volunteer). The tabernacle is built by freewill generosity, not conscription. Same root as the nedivim (noble/generous ones) throughout the Psalms. Paul echoes this principle in 2 Cor 9:7.
25.3: Materials in descending value: zahav, kesef, nechoshet (zah-HAV, KEH-sef, neh-KHO-shet): 'gold, silver, bronze.' These three metals map spatially onto the tabernacle zones — gold in the most holy place and holy place, silver as socket-foundations, bronze in the outer court. Sacred space is marked materially: proximity to divine presence corresponds to purity of metal.
25.4: tekhelet (teh-kheh-LET): 'blue' (H8504, possibly from Murex snail dye — same source as the tzitzit blue thread, Num 15:38). argaman (ar-gah-MAN): 'purple' (H713, a prestige color in the ancient world, associated with royalty). tola'at shani (toh-lah-AT shah-NEE): 'scarlet worm-dye' (H8438/H8144, from the kermes scale insect). shesh (shesh): 'fine linen' (H8336, Egyptian). These four materials are the signature of sacred space throughout the tabernacle, priestly garments, and the high priest's vestments — a visual theology of holiness.
25.5: ore tachash (oh-ROT tah-KHASH): 'tachash skins' (H8476 — the animal is unidentified; candidates include dugong, porpoise, or a now-extinct land animal). atzei shittim (ah-TZEI shit-TEEM): 'acacia wood' (H7848 H6086). Acacia is a desert-resistant hardwood — extremely dense, resistant to rot and insects. Its use for the ark, altar, and table is theologically significant: the incorruptible wood that bears the holy furnishings anticipates the incorruptible flesh that bears the divine presence (cf. Ps 16:10, Acts 2:27).
25.6: shemen hammishchah (sheh-MEN ha-MISH-chah): 'the anointing oil' (H8081 H4888, from mashach — to anoint, the root of mashiach/Messiah). qetoret hasamim (qeh-TOH-ret ha-sam-MEEM): 'incense of spices' (H7004 H1314). Both items define the priestly/cultic ministry: anointing consecrates, incense represents the ascending prayers of the people before God (cf. Rev 8:3-4).
25.7: avnei shoham (av-NEI sho-HAM): 'shoham stones' (H7718 — possibly onyx or a precious stone; identification uncertain). avnei milu'im (av-NEI mih-loo-EEM): 'setting/filling stones' (H4394, from male H4390 — to fill). The word for gem settings (milu) echoes the filling/fulfillment language of creation (Gen 1:28 umilu) — the sacred garments are filled/completed with precious stones.
25.8: miqdash (mik-DASH): 'sanctuary' (H4720, from qadash — to be holy/set apart). mishkan (mish-KAN): 'tabernacle/dwelling' (H4908, from shakan H7931 — to dwell/settle/tabernacle). The purpose statement — veshakhanti betokham, 'that I may dwell among them' — is one of the most theologically loaded sentences in the Torah. The mission of the tabernacle is divine indwelling in the midst of the people. John 1:14 employs the same Greek root (eskenos = tabernacled) to describe the Incarnation: the eternal Word pitched his tabernacle among us.
25.10: aron (ah-RON): 'ark/chest/box' (H727). The same word is used for Joseph's coffin in Gen 50:26 — the only two uses in the Pentateuch of aron for a chest. The ark contains the tablets of the covenant — it is the treasure chest of the divine word. Dimensions: 2.5 cubits long, 1.5 wide, 1.5 high. atzei shittim — acacia wood again — the incorruptible wood at the heart of the sanctuary.
25.11: zahav tahor (zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'pure gold' (H2091 H2889). The ark is overlaid with pure gold inside and out — total consecration, every surface. zar zahav (zehr zah-HAV): 'crown/molding of gold' (H2213), surrounding the top of the ark — a royal crown befitting the footstool of the King of the universe.
25.12: arba tabbaot zahav (ar-BAH tab-ah-OT zah-HAV): 'four gold rings' (H702 H2885 H2091). The rings are the interface between the holy ark and the carrying poles — the only means by which human hands can transport the divine presence without touching it directly. Placed at the four feet, or at the four lower corners, of the ark.
25.13: baddim (bad-DEEM): 'poles/staves' (H905, plural of bad). The root bad can mean separation, isolation, or the pole itself. The carrying poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold are the instruments by which the presence of God travels with the people. The baddim make the ark portable — YHWH is not bound to a fixed place but travels with his covenant community.
25.14: The poles are inserted into the rings on the sides of the ark — the ark's carrying system. The instruction anticipates the wilderness journey: the divine presence must move with Israel through the desert. The danger of touching the ark directly is illustrated later in the narrative of Uzzah (2 Sam 6:6-7), where even well-intentioned contact without the prescribed mediation proves fatal — holiness requires mediation.
25.15: lo yasuru mimmenu (lo yah-SOO-roo mim-ME-noo): 'they shall not be removed from it.' The poles are permanently in place — the ark is always in a state of readiness for movement. This permanence of the carrying apparatus reflects the pilgrim nature of the divine dwelling: even when Israel is at rest, God's presence is ready to move. The command distinguishes the ark from other sacred furniture whose poles could be removed.
25.16: haedut (hah-ay-DOOT): 'the testimony/witness tablets' (H5715, from ud — to testify/repeat). The tablets of the Decalogue placed inside the ark are haedut — the divine testimony, the witness of covenant. This names the ark: aron haedut = ark of the testimony. The tablets inside the ark function as the covenant document at the heart of the divine dwelling, like a suzerainty treaty stored in the sanctuary.
25.17: kapporet (kap-PO-ret): 'atonement cover/mercy seat' (H3727, from kapar H3722 — to cover, to atone, to make propitiation). This is the most theologically concentrated piece of furniture in the tabernacle. On Yom Kippur (Lev 16), the high priest sprinkles the blood of the sin offering on the kapporet — the blood of atonement applied directly to the cover of the covenant tablets, between the cherubim, in the divine presence. The LXX renders it hilasterion, the same word Paul uses in Rom 3:25 for what God put forward in Christ as the place of atonement. Dimensions: 2.5 cubits × 1.5 cubits — exact match to the top of the ark.
25.18: shenei cheruvim zahav (she-NEI kheh-roo-VEEM zah-HAV): 'two gold cherubim.' mikshah (mik-SHAH): 'hammered/beaten work' (H4749, from naqash? or kasha = beaten into shape). The cherubim are not cast separately and mounted — they are beaten out of the same gold as the kapporet. Cherubim (H3742) are the guardians of sacred divine space throughout Scripture, from Eden (Gen 3:24) to the heavenly throne room (Ezek 1, Rev 4).
25.19: miqqetseh hakapporet (mik-KET-zeh hah-kap-PO-ret): 'from the end of the atonement cover.' The two cherubim emerge from the two ends of the kapporet — they are one piece with it. The divine meeting place is flanked by the guardians of divine holiness. The unity of material (one piece of hammered gold) signals that access to the divine presence is inseparable from the atoning mediation of the kapporet.
25.20: porsim kanfeihem (po-REEM kan-fei-HEM): 'spreading their wings' (H6566/H3671, paras/kanaph). The wings of the cherubim shelter or overshadow the kapporet from above. upeneihem ish el achiv: 'their faces toward one another' — facing each other, looking down toward the kapporet. This posture defines the divine throne: YHWH is enthroned above the cherubim (1 Sam 4:4, Ps 80:1), in the space between their overshadowing wings.
25.21: The kapporet is placed on top of the ark, with the edut (testimony tablets) inside. The sequence — covenant document inside, atonement cover on top — is architecturally theological: the covenant law is covered by (and accessible only through) the atonement of divine mercy. The tablets demand; the kapporet answers.
25.22: veno'adti lekha sham (ve-no-AD-ti lekh-AH SHAM): 'and I will meet with you there' (H3259, from ya'ad — to appoint, to meet at an appointed time). The word for meeting (mo'ed) comes from this root — the Tent of Meeting (ohel mo'ed) is the place of divine appointments with Israel. mi'bayn shnei hakeruvim: 'from between the two cherubim' — the precise location of YHWH's speech: the mercy seat between the cherubim is the interface between heaven and earth, the place where divine communication enters human history.
25.23: shulhan (shool-KHAN): 'table' (H7979). The table for the bread of the presence stands in the holy place outside the veil. Acacia wood, overlaid with gold. The table is the second piece of holy furniture — after the ark, the table. Israel's covenant with YHWH is structured as a meal before him.
25.24: Pure gold overlay for the table; zar zahav (crown/molding of gold) surrounding it — matching the ark's crown. The gold crown or rim may have served a practical function (keeping items in place) while symbolically marking the table as royal furnishing in the divine throne room.
25.25: misgereth (mis-GEH-ret): 'border/frame/rim' (H4526, from sagar — to close/shut H5462). A handsbreadth-wide border surrounding the table. zar zahav again — the crown molding appears on both the ark and the table, marking them as the two central furnishings of the divine dwelling (covenant and provision).
25.26: Four gold rings for the table, one at each corner — the same carrying system as the ark. The rings placed at the four feet enable the table to travel with Israel. The table of the divine presence is not a fixed monument but a traveling provision for the pilgrim people.
25.27: The rings are close to the misgereth (border/rim) to hold the carrying poles. Precise engineering — the rings must be positioned to keep the poles level with the table surface during transport, preventing the sacred bread and vessels from sliding.
25.28: baddei shittim (bad-DEI shit-TEEM): 'acacia poles' — same material and principle as the ark's poles. The table travels on the same wood as the ark: acacia (incorruptible desert hardwood) overlaid with gold. The table is as portable as the covenant — divine provision travels wherever the covenant people travel.
25.29: qe'arotav (ke-ah-ro-TAV): 'its plates' (H7086); kappotav (kap-po-TAV): 'its pans/dishes' (H3709 — same word as palms of hands, the cupped shape); qeshavot (ke-sha-VOT): 'its pitchers/flagons' (H7184); menaqiyyot (me-nah-ki-YOT): 'its bowls' (H4518). All for the nesekh (NEH-sekh): 'libation/drink offering' (H5262). The table is fully equipped for a sacred meal service — vessels for bread, wine, and offering. Divine hospitality is not sparse.
25.30: lehem hapanim (LEH-khem hah-pah-NEEM): 'bread of the face/presence' (H3899 H6440). Literally: face-bread, or presence-bread — twelve loaves set before the face of YHWH perpetually (tamid). The twelve loaves represent the twelve tribes in constant presentation before God. In 1 Sam 21:1-6 David eats the sacred showbread in extremity, and Jesus appeals to this incident to teach that the sabbath/cultic law serves human need (Matt 12:3-4). The lehem hapanim anticipates the eschatological banquet where the redeemed eat at the divine table.
25.31: menorah (me-no-RAH): 'lampstand' (H4501, from root nwr/nyr — light, lamp H5216). zahav tahor — pure gold. mikshah: hammered out of one piece — not assembled from parts but beaten into its branching form from a single talent of pure gold. The menorah is the light of the divine house. Zechariah 4 interprets the menorah as the light of YHWH's Spirit (not by might nor by power). John 8:12 applies the light-language to Jesus as the light of the world.
25.32: shisha qanim (shee-SHA kah-NEEM): 'six branches' (H8337 H7070) — three from each side of the central shaft. yerech (YEH-rekh): 'thigh/central shaft' (H3409 — the same word used for the thigh/base of the body). The lamp's central pillar is described with the human body word for the thigh — architectural anthropomorphism, the living lamp with a spine and branches.
25.33: gevi'im meshukadim (ge-vi-EEM me-shoo-KAH-deem): 'almond-shaped cups' (H1375 H8247). The menorah's ornamental cups are shaped like almond blossoms — the shaqed (almond tree H8247) is the first tree to blossom in Israel (January), its name from shaqad = to wake/be alert/watch. Aaron's rod that budded was an almond branch (Num 17:8). kaftor (kaf-TOR): 'knob/capital' (H3730); perach (PEH-rakh): 'flower/blossom' (H6525). The lamp is adorned with imagery of new life, watchfulness, and blossoming.
25.34: The central shaft has four cups with knobs and blossoms — more ornamentation than the six branches (which have three each). The central trunk of the menorah is the most elaborate — the source from which the branches radiate. This structural emphasis on the central shaft anticipates the menorah's interpretation in Zechariah 4 and John 15 (the vine and branches).
25.35: The structural specification: a knob beneath each pair of branches — there are three pairs of branches, so three knobs on the central shaft where branches emerge. The total structure has a mathematical harmony: 7 lamps, 6 branches, specific cup/knob/blossom counts at each joint. This precision is not mere aesthetics but reflects the orderliness of creation and divine wisdom.
25.36: mikshah echad zahav tahor (mik-SHAH eh-KHAD zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'one hammered piece of pure gold.' The entire menorah — shaft, branches, cups, knobs, blossoms — is beaten from one talent of gold. No joints, no assembly, no seams. This unity of material reflects the oneness of the divine light that illuminates Israel. The menorah cannot be disassembled into parts; its light is whole and indivisible.
25.37: shiv'at neroteyha (shiv-AT ne-ro-TEI-hah): 'its seven lamps' (H7651 H5216). Seven is the number of covenantal completeness throughout Scripture. The seven lamps are to be arranged to illuminate the space in front of the lampstand — el ever paneyha: toward its face. The menorah faces the table of the bread of the presence — the light illuminates the divine provision, and both face the entrance, available to approach.
25.38: melqachayyim (mel-kah-KHI-yim): 'tongs/snuffers' (H4457, from laqach — to take); machtot (makh-TOT): 'trays/fire pans' (H4289). All pure gold. The tongs for trimming the wicks and the trays for carrying the ash are as sacred as the lamp itself — every element of worship is consecrated. Even the maintenance tools bear the mark of holiness.
25.39: kikar zahav tahor (ki-KAR zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'a talent of pure gold' (H3603 H2091 H2889). A kikar is approximately 34 kg / 75 lbs. The entire menorah, including all its vessels, is hammered from one talent of pure gold. This is one of the largest single-material sacred objects — the light of the divine house is costly, the most expensive furnishing in terms of raw gold weight.
25.40: ure'eh va'aseh betavnitam (oo-re-EH va-ah-SEH be-tav-ni-TAM): 'and see and make according to their pattern' (H7200/H6213/H8403). The closing command echoes v.9 — both the tabernacle and the menorah are built from the tabnit revealed on the mountain. asher-attah mar'eh bahar: 'which you are shown on the mountain.' Heb 8:5 and 9:23 apply this principle broadly: the entire Levitical system is a copy (hupodeigma) and shadow (skia) of the heavenly realities. The earthly tabernacle does not merely house God — it images the heavenly sanctuary where Christ now ministers.

34.6: YHWH YHWH el rachum vechanun erech apayim verav chesed veemet: 'YHWH YHWH, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love [chesed] and faithfulness [emet].' The thirteen attributes of divine mercy (mid-dot harachamim) became a cornerstone of Jewish liturgical theology, recited on fast days and festivals. Chesed (covenant love, steadfast love, lovingkindness) is perhaps the OT's richest theological term — it combines loyalty, love, mercy, and covenant-faithfulness into a single word. John's 'grace and truth' (John 1:14, 17) is likely a translation of chesed veemet, applying Exod 34:6's divine attributes to the incarnate Word.
34.1: pesol lĕkā šĕnê lûḥōt ʾăbānîm kāriʾšōnîm: 'Cut for yourself two tablets of stone like the first ones.' The new tablets require Moses's own labor (pesol lĕkā = cut for yourself) — unlike the first tablets, which were entirely YHWH's work (32:16: 'the work of God'). The writing is still YHWH's (wĕkātabtî ʾal-halûḥōt = I will write on the tablets), but the preparation of the material is now Moses's task. This shift in the covenant renewal: the second covenant involves human participation in preparation even while the content remains divine.
34.2: Moses is commanded to be ready in the morning (wĕhāyîtā nākôn labbōqer) and come up to Mount Sinai. The morning ascent pattern — the same time when the manna was gathered and the Sabbath morning designated for rest — carries the rhythm of covenant renewal into the liturgical cycle. Moses ascends alone (wĕʾîš lōʾ yiʿăleh ʿimmāk); the covenant renewal is a private encounter before its public declaration.
34.3: wĕʾîš lōʾ-yirʾeh bĕkol-hāhār gam-haṣṣōʾn wĕhabbāqār ʾal-yirʿû ʾel-mûl hāhār hahûʾ: 'no one shall appear in all the mountain; neither flocks nor herds shall graze before that mountain.' The comprehensive exclusion — no human, no animal — recreates the holiness boundary of the original Sinai theophany (19:12-13). The renewal demands the same awe as the original institution. The covenant is not a casual recondition; it requires the full formal apparatus of holy encounter.
34.4: Moses cuts the two tablets, rises early in the morning, and ascends Sinai with the two tablets in hand (bĕyādô). The combination of obedience (cuts as commanded, rises early) and preparation (brings the tablets) enacts Moses as the faithful mediator. His actions model the covenant response: doing exactly as YHWH commanded, without delay or modification.
34.5: wayyērēd YHWH beʿānān wayyityaṣṣēb ʿimmô šām wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH: 'YHWH descended in the cloud and stood with him there, and proclaimed the name of YHWH.' The cloud descent is the theophanic vehicle; the proclamation of the divine name is the content. The phrase wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH is ambiguous — it could be YHWH proclaiming his own name, or Moses calling on YHWH's name. The context favors YHWH's self-proclamation: what follows in v.6 is the definitive self-disclosure of the divine character.
34.7: nōṣēr ḥesed lāʾlāpîm nōśēʾ ʿāwōn wāpeša wĕḥaṭṭāʾāh wĕnaqqēh lōʾ yĕnaqqeh pōqēd ʿăwōn ʾābōt ʿal-bānîm: 'keeping steadfast love for thousands, bearing iniquity and transgression and sin, and by no means clearing the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation.' The asymmetry is deliberate and theologically crucial: mercy extends to thousands (lāʾlāpîm = to the thousandth generation), but punishment only to the third and fourth generation. The three sin-terms — ʿāwōn (iniquity, moral distortion), peša (transgression, rebellion), ḥaṭṭāʾāh (sin, missing the mark) — together cover the full spectrum of human wrongdoing.
