YHWH does not simply set aside guilt (wĕnaqqēh lōʾ yĕnaqqeh = 'by no means clearing/acquitting') — this tension between mercy and justice reaches its resolution only in the atonement.
34.8: Moses immediately bows and worships (wayyiqqōd mōšeh ʾarṣāh wayyištaḥû). The response to the proclamation of the divine character is prostration — the knowledge of YHWH's mercy and holiness together produces not familiarity but awe. The thirteen attributes (vv.6-7) are not abstract propositions but the living character of the God before whom Moses falls.
34.9: Moses intercedes immediately: if I have found favor, let YHWH go in Israel's midst, even though they are stiff-necked (ʿam qĕšēh ʾōrep). The appeal is to the very mercy just proclaimed: YHWH has declared himself forgiving of iniquity and transgression — Moses calls YHWH to act in accordance with his own character. Wĕsālaḥtā lăʿăwōnēnû ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾtēnû ûnĕḥaltānû: 'forgive our iniquity and sin and take us for your inheritance.' The three petitions correspond to the three sin-terms of v.7 — Moses is asking YHWH to exercise toward Israel exactly the mercy he has just claimed as his character.
34.10: hinnēh ʾānōkî kōrēt bĕrît: 'Behold, I am making a covenant.' YHWH responds to Moses's intercession with covenant renewal. The covenant is not a renegotiated form but the same covenant on new tablets — the content of YHWH's commitment is unchanged; the relationship is restored. Niplāʾōt ʾăšer lōʾ-nibĕrĕʾû bĕkol-hāʾāreṣ: 'wonders that have not been created in all the earth' — the renewed covenant is accompanied by miraculous signs that distinguish Israel from all other nations. The covenant is the source of Israel's unique history.
34.11: The covenant stipulations begin with expulsion of the Canaanite nations. Šĕmor lĕkā ʾēt ʾăšer ʾānōkî mĕṣawwĕkā hayyôm: 'Observe what I command you today.' The renewed covenant has the same stipulation structure as the original: grace, then obligation. YHWH will drive out the nations; Israel must not make covenants with them or intermarry. The covenant relationship demands exclusive allegiance.
34.12: hišāmer lĕkā pen-tikkĕrōt bĕrît lĕyôšēb hāʾāreṣ: 'Take care, lest you make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land.' The danger of covenant entanglement with Canaan is the specific warning: their altars must be torn down, their pillars broken, their Asherah poles cut down. The exclusivity of the covenant with YHWH requires the destruction of all alternatives — not toleration, not negotiation, but dismantling.
34.13: mīzbĕḥōtēhem tiṯṯōṣûn ûmaṣṣĕbōtām tĕšabbērûn wĕʾăšērāw tikrōtûn: three verbs of destruction targeting three types of pagan installation: altars (mīzbĕḥōt), sacred pillars (maṣṣĕbôt), and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm). Each type targets a different aspect of Canaanite worship: the altar (sacrifice site), the standing stone (deity's presence marker), the wooden pole (fertility goddess symbol). The comprehensive dismantling leaves no residue of alternative worship systems.
34.14: kî YHWH qannāʾ šĕmô ʾēl qannāʾ hûʾ: 'For YHWH, whose name is Jealous (qannāʾ), is a jealous God.' The divine name 'Jealous' appears only here and in 20:5. Qannāʾ (from qānāʾ, to be jealous/zealous) describes YHWH's passionate, exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance — the covenantal love that cannot tolerate a rival. The use of qannāʾ as a divine name (not merely an attribute) indicates this is constitutive of who YHWH is, not a mood he occasionally enters. NT application: Christ claims the same exclusivity ('no one comes to the Father except through me,' John 14:6).
34.15: The specific danger of covenant with Canaanite peoples: they will invite Israel to eat their sacrifices, and Israel's sons will take their daughters as wives. The warning traces the path of apostasy: shared meals at pagan altars → intermarriage → idolatry through the spouse's influence. The same concern appears in Solomon's apostasy (1 Kgs 11:1-13) and the Ezra-Nehemiah crisis (Ezra 9-10) — the historical record validates the prophylactic warning.
34.16: wĕlāqaḥtā mibĕnōtāw lĕbānêkā wĕzānû bĕnōtāw ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhêhen wĕhiznû ʾet-bānêkā ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhêhen: 'and when you take their daughters for your sons, and their daughters prostitute themselves after their gods, they will make your sons prostitute themselves after their gods.' The verb zānāh (to prostitute/play the harlot) applied to apostasy is the dominant metaphor of Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel — Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH is a marriage, so other-god worship is adultery.
34.17: ʾĕlōhê massēkāh lōʾ taʿăśeh lāk: 'You shall not make cast idols for yourself.' The prohibition directly references the golden calf incident (massēkāh = cast/poured metal image, the same word used in 32:4). The renewed covenant is a covenant for a people who have already violated this command and been forgiven — the repetition is a reminder in the context of grace, not merely legal reiteration.
34.18: The Festival of Unleavened Bread (ḥag hammaṣṣôt) and its seven-day observance in the month of Abib. The calendar of Israel's worship is embedded in the covenant renewal: the festivals that commemorate YHWH's saving acts are part of the covenant stipulations. ʾĕt ḥōdeš hāʾābîb (in the month of Abib — the spring month of the Exodus) — the Passover month anchors the entire liturgical year in the formative redemptive event.
34.19: The firstborn law: all that opens the womb belongs to YHWH. The principle of consecration of first fruits — the first of everything belongs to the One who gives everything — applies to livestock as well as people. Bekôr (firstborn) is the theological category established in the Exodus itself: YHWH struck Egypt's firstborn to redeem Israel his firstborn son (4:22-23; 11:5; 13:2).
34.20: The firstborn donkey redeemed with a lamb; if not redeemed, its neck is broken. The human firstborn must be redeemed. Wĕlōʾ-yērāʾû pānay rêqām: 'and they shall not appear before me empty-handed.' The covenant community comes before YHWH bringing something — the firstfruits that acknowledge his ownership. Appearing empty-handed (without offering) is the refusal to acknowledge covenant relationship. The offering is not earning favor but expressing dependence.
34.21: šēšet yāmîm taʿăbōd ûbayyôm haššĕbîʿî tišbōt bĕḥāriš ûbaqāṣîr tišbōt: 'Six days you shall work, but on the seventh day you shall rest; in plowing time and in harvest you shall rest.' The Sabbath law includes the most demanding seasons of agricultural labor — plowing and harvest. The Sabbath is not a convenience but a challenge: rest even when work is most urgent, trusting YHWH's provision over human effort. The sabbatical principle reshapes the agricultural economy around covenant time rather than production demand.
34.22: Three harvest festivals: ḥag šābuʿōt (Festival of Weeks, seven weeks after Passover = Pentecost), ḥag hāʾāsîp (Festival of Ingathering = Tabernacles), and the transition of the year (tĕqûpat haššānāh). The three pilgrimage festivals structure Israel's entire agricultural year as a series of appearances before YHWH. The calendar of Israel is not merely a timekeeping system but a theology of providence — each harvest is a gift to be acknowledged before the Giver.
34.23: Three times a year all males shall appear before YHWH, the God of Israel. The pilgrimage obligation — leaving home, going to the sanctuary — is the embodied enactment of covenant priority. YHWH is called hāʾādôn YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl (the Lord YHWH God of Israel) — the full covenant identification. The triannual appearance is the covenant community's repeated acknowledgment of who their Lord is.
34.24: The promise accompanying the pilgrimage obligation: YHWH will drive out the nations before Israel and enlarge its territory. The vulnerability of pilgrimage — the land unattended while everyone goes to the sanctuary — is met with a divine security guarantee: wĕlōʾ yaḥmod ʾîš ʾet-ʾarṣĕkā bĕʿālōtĕkā ('no one shall covet your land when you go up'). YHWH's provision during human absence is the structural expression of covenant trust: appearing before YHWH creates the condition for YHWH to protect what is entrusted to him.
34.25: lōʾ-tišḥaṭ ʿal-ḥāmēṣ dam zibḥî: 'You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leaven.' The purity requirements for sacrifice: no leaven mixed with the blood offering (cf. 12:15-20 — leaven is excluded from Passover). The separation of the sacred from ordinary bread (leaven is part of daily life) maintains the distinctiveness of covenant worship. The same principle of separation underlies the NT's 'purge out the old leaven' (1 Cor 5:7-8).
34.26: The first of the firstfruits of the ground shall be brought to the house of YHWH. Lōʾ tĕbaššēl gĕdî baḥălēb ʾimmô: 'You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.' The prohibition — which appears three times (13:19; 23:19; here) — protects against the use of the mother's milk (which is for sustaining life) as the medium of the offspring's death. It may also distinguish Israelite practice from Canaanite sacrificial customs. The triple repetition in the Torah signals its importance for covenant identity.
34.27: kĕtāb lĕkā ʾet-hadĕbārîm hāʾēlleh kî ʿal-pî hadĕbārîm hāʾēlleh kāraʾtî ʾittĕkā bĕrît wĕʾet-yiśrāʾēl: 'Write these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel.' YHWH commands Moses to write — the covenant is a written document, not merely oral tradition. The written word is the enduring form of the covenant. The parallel to the Sinai tablets: YHWH wrote on stone, Moses writes these words; the covenant has both divine inscription and human transcription.
34.28: wayhî šām ʿim YHWH ʾarbāʿîm yôm wĕʾarbāʿîm laylāh leḥem lōʾ ʾākal ûmayim lōʾ šātāh: 'He was there with YHWH forty days and forty nights; he neither ate bread nor drank water.' The second forty-day fast mirrors the first (24:18) — both covenant encounters require the same duration of concentrated divine presence. The abstention from food and water is not ascetic virtue but the physical sign of being entirely sustained by the divine presence. Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matt 4:2) recapitulates Moses's Sinai fasts — the new covenant mediator undergoes the same probationary discipline as the old.
34.29: wĕlōʾ yādaʿ mōšeh kî qāran ʿôr pĕnê mōšeh bĕdabbĕrô ʾittô: 'Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone (qāran) because he had been speaking with him.' The verb qāran (to emit rays, to shine) derives from qeren (horn/ray) — Moses's face emitted rays of light after the divine encounter. The unconscious nature of the transformation (lōʾ yādaʿ) is significant: the glory is not Moses's achievement but an effect of contact with YHWH's presence. Paul's extended meditation on this verse in 2 Cor 3:7-18 contrasts the fading glory (the veil hides what is passing away) with the permanent, unveiled glory of the new covenant.
34.30: Aaron and all the children of Israel saw Moses: wĕhinnēh qāran ʿôr pānāyw wayyîrĕʾû miggešet ʾēlāyw — 'his face was shining, and they were afraid to come near him.' The radiance creates a distance — the reflected glory makes Moses approachable only with preparation. The fear response is the human reaction to mediated divine holiness: even the derivative glory (Moses reflecting YHWH's glory) is beyond ordinary human comfort.
34.31: Moses calls Aaron and the leaders, and they return to him. He gives them the commandments just received. The structured order of proclamation — leaders first, then the whole community — establishes the pattern of covenant transmission: from the mediator to the elders to the people. The same pattern governs the delivery of the law throughout Deuteronomy.
34.32: After that, all the children of Israel approach and Moses commands them all that YHWH had spoken with him on Sinai. The completeness of transmission is emphasized: Moses passes on everything, not a selection. Kol ʾăšer dibbēr YHWH ʾittô bĕhar Sînāy — 'all that YHWH had spoken with him on Mount Sinai.' The chain of revelation: YHWH → Moses → leaders → people. Each link transmits without loss.
34.33: ûkĕkallōt mōšeh lĕdabbēr ʾittām nātan ʿal-pānāyw masweh: 'when Moses had finished speaking with them, he put a veil (masweh) on his face.' The word masweh (veil/covering) is a hapax legomenon in the OT — this is its only occurrence. The veil is put on after the speaking, not before — during proclamation Moses is unveiled, but between sessions of covenant instruction his face is covered. Paul's reading of the veil in 2 Cor 3:13-18 interprets it as hiding the fading glory; but textually, it also protects the people from the unmediated radiance that frightened them (v.30).
34.34: ûbĕbōʾ mōšeh lipnê YHWH lĕdabbēr ʾittô yāsîr ʾet-hammasweh ʿad ṣēʾtô: 'when Moses went in before YHWH to speak with him, he took off the veil until he came out.' The veil protocol: Moses removes it when entering YHWH's presence (face-to-face access to the divine), keeps it on when among the people between instruction sessions. The veil marks the boundary between the two registers of Moses's existence — priestly access to YHWH and mediatorial communication to Israel.
34.35: wĕrāʾû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet-pĕnê mōšeh kî qāran ʿôr pĕnê mōšeh: 'the children of Israel saw the face of Moses, that the skin of Moses's face shone.' The pattern repeats each time Moses emerges from the divine presence — the shining is renewed with each encounter. The glory is not permanent on Moses; it requires repeated visits. Paul's contrast: the new covenant's glory is doxa ek doxes, glory from glory, permanently transforming (2 Cor 3:18: 'beholding the glory of the Lord, we are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another').

1.1: ve'eleh shemot bnei Yisrael (wĕʾēlleh šĕmôt bĕnê yiśrāʾēl): 'And these are the names of the sons of Israel.' The Hebrew title of the book is Shemot (Names), not Exodus — names signal covenantal identity, and the whole book is about the people named by YHWH. The opening ve'eleh (and these) links to Genesis's ending, continuing the one narrative of YHWH's covenantal family. Each name listed is a covenant heir whose descendants YHWH will redeem.
1.2: Reuven Shimon Levi viYehudah: The listing of the twelve sons mirrors the tribal registers of Genesis 46 and Numbers 1. The genealogical record is a covenant document — listing those to whom YHWH made promises (Gen 12; 15; 17; 26; 28). Legal genealogies in the ancient Near East established inheritance rights; this genealogy establishes the covenant heirs who have a claim on YHWH's promise to the patriarchs.
1.3: Yissachar Zevulun uVinyamin: The continuation of the tribal list through the Leah-sons and Benjamin. Each name embeds a genealogical story from Genesis — Issachar (reward, Gen 30:18), Zebulun (honor/dwell, Gen 30:20), Benjamin (son of the right hand, Gen 35:18). The compression of the patriarchal narratives into a bare list at the Exodus opening shows that the Exodus is the continuation and fulfillment of what God began with the patriarchs.
1.4: Dan veNaftali Gad ve'Asher: The sons of Bilhah and Zilpah complete the twelve. The full tribal catalog before the oppression narrative establishes that all twelve are equally heirs of the covenant — there is no partial Israel. YHWH's redemption will not leave any tribe behind; the Exodus is for the whole covenant people, not a favored subset.
1.5: vayhi kol-nefesh yotzei yerech-Yaakov shivim nefesh (wayhî kol-nepeš yōṣĕʾê yereḵ-yaʿăqōb šibʿîm nāpeš): 'All the persons who came from Jacob's loins were seventy souls.' Nefesh (H5315, soul/person/life-force) — each member of the covenant community is a nefesh, a living being bearing the image of God (Gen 2:7 — man became a living nefesh). Seventy is a significant symbolic number in the OT (seventy nations in Gen 10; seventy elders of Israel in Exod 24:1; the seventy years of exile in Jer 29:10).
1.6: vayamot Yosef vekhol-echav vekhol hador hahu: 'Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation.' The transition from the patriarchal era to the oppression: the generation that knew Joseph and his saving role dies. The forgetting of Joseph by the new Pharaoh (v.8) is thus not merely political ingratitude but the erasure of covenantal memory. When the people who witnessed God's saving acts die without passing on the memory, the covenant community becomes vulnerable — a warning for every generation.
1.7: vayifru vayishretzu vayirbu vayaattsemu bim'od m'od (wayyipĕrû wayyišrĕṣû wayyirbû wayyaʿaṣmû bimĕʾōd mĕʾōd): 'The Israelites were fruitful and swarmed and multiplied and grew exceedingly strong.' Six verbs in rapid succession — the most concentrated description of population growth in the OT. Sharatz (H8317, swarm/teem) is the word used in Gen 1:20 for the swarming of creatures in the sea — Israel's multiplication is deliberately compared to the original creation-flourishing. The Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:2 — I will multiply you exceedingly) is fulfilled against the odds: vatimale ha'aretz otam (the land was filled with them) echoes Gen 1:28 (fill the earth).
1.8: vayaqam melek chadash al-Mitsrayim asher lo-yada et-Yosef (wayyāqom melek ḥādāš ʿal-miṣrāyim ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾet-yôsēp): 'A new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.' Lo-yada (did not know): yada (H3045) is covenantal intimacy and relational recognition, not merely factual awareness. The new Pharaoh does not merely lack information about Joseph — he refuses to recognize the covenantal reality that Joseph represented. Political amnesia as a form of covenant-blindness: deliberately not-knowing what connects the powerful to their obligations.
1.9: vayomer el-ammo hinei am bnei Yisrael rav ve'atsum mimenu: 'He said to his people: the people of the children of Israel are too numerous and too mighty for us.' Rav ve'atsum (numerous and mighty): the same terms used in v.7 for Israel's growth — Pharaoh's fear response perfectly mirrors what YHWH's blessing produced. The blessing of the covenant people becomes the pretext for their oppression. This is the recurring OT pattern: the flourishing of YHWH's covenant community provokes imperial anxiety.
1.10: hava nithachkema lo (hābāh niṯḥakkĕmāh-llô): 'Come, let us deal wisely/shrewdly with them.' Nithachkema (Hitpael of chakam, H2449, be wise): the deliberative reflexive — the council of empire deliberating cunning strategy against the covenant people. The supreme irony: Pharaoh's wisdom (chakam) is the anti-type of true wisdom (Prov 9:10: the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom). The wisest nation on earth devises a plan that ultimately destroys itself, while the ones it tries to suppress multiply further (v.12).
1.11: vayasimu alav sarei missim (wayyāśîmû ʿālāw śārê missîm): 'They appointed over them forced-labor taskmasters.' Missim (H4522, forced labor/tribute levies) is the specific term for the corvee system of imperial labor conscription. Sarei missim (supervisors of forced labor) are the administrative machinery of oppression. Building Pitom and Ra'amses (store-cities) — the wealth of empire built on the bodies of covenant people. The counter-image is Paul's church-as-temple being built with living stones (1 Pet 2:5) through willing participation.
1.12: ka'asher ye'anu oto ken yirbeh viken yifrotz (kaʾăšer yĕʿannû ʾōtô kēn yirbeh wĕḵēn yiproṣ): 'The more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and spread.' Anah (H6031, afflict/humble/oppress) — the same root used for the affliction YHWH predicted to Abraham (Gen 15:13: your descendants will be ani in a land not theirs). The covenant curse of oppression paradoxically fuels the covenant blessing of multiplication. Yifrotz (spread, H6555) — breaking out/spreading rapidly — the same root used when Jacob's prosperity broke out (Gen 30:43) and when the Davidic covenant promises spread (2 Sam 5:20).
1.13: vaya'avidu Mitsrayim et-bnei Yisrael befarech (wayyaʿăbidû miṣrāyim ʾet-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bĕpāreḵ): 'Egypt made the Israelites serve with crushing labor.' Farech (H6531, crushing labor/harshness): the specific term for the severity of forced labor — occurring only here and in Lev 25:43, 46, 53, where YHWH explicitly forbids Israelites from imposing farech on one another, because they know what it is like. The Exodus becomes the ethical foundation for Israel's labor laws.
1.14: vayemareru et-chayyeihem ba'avoda kasha (waymārărû ʾet-ḥayyêhem bāʿăbōdāh qāšāh): 'They made their lives bitter with hard labor.' Marar (H4843, bitter/make bitter): the same root as the bitter waters of Marah (Exod 15:23), as Naomi's name-change to Mara (Ruth 1:20), and as the bitter herbs of the Passover (Exod 12:8). The bitterness of Egypt is ritually remembered in every Passover celebration through the maror — the physical taste of oppression kept alive in covenant memory.
1.15: vayomer melek Mitsrayim lameyaldot ha'ivriyot: 'The king of Egypt said to the Hebrew midwives.' The first named women in the Exodus narrative: Shiphrah and Puah — their names preserved while Pharaoh's is not. Meyaldot (midwives, from yalad H3205, bear/give birth): women who stand at the boundary between life and death, facilitating birth against the decree of death. The politics of empire confronts the politics of the womb.
1.16: biyaldechem et-haivriyot ure'iten al-ha'avnayim: 'When you deliver the Hebrew women, look at the birthstool.' Ha'avnayim (H70, birthstool/two stones): possibly a dual form of even (stone), referring to the two stones or bricks on which a woman crouched for birth in the ancient Near East — attested in Egyptian birth scenes. The intimate setting of delivery is where Pharaoh demands that his death-decree be executed: infanticide disguised as midwifery.
1.17: vatirena hameyaldot et-haElohim velo asu ka'asher diber aleihen melek Mitsrayim: 'The midwives feared God and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded.' Yirat haElohim (fear of God) vs. compliance with Pharaoh's command — the foundational ethical conflict of the Exodus. The midwives' yirat Elohim is the same phrase used for Abraham's faith (Gen 22:12: now I know you fear God) and the ethic of the stranger (Gen 20:11: there is no fear of God in this place). To fear God is to resist idolatrous human power.
1.18: vayiqra melek Mitsrayim lameyaldot vayomer lahen madua asitem hadavar hazeh: 'The king of Egypt called the midwives and said: why have you done this thing?' The imperial interrogation of those who defied the decree. The midwives' answer — the Hebrew women are vigorous and give birth before we arrive (v.19) — is a deflection that preserves life. Civil disobedience that protects the innocent and deceives the oppressor is honored here: God blessed the midwives (v.20-21).
1.19: vatomernu hameyaldot el-Paroh ki lo kaNashim haMitsriyot haivriyot ki chayyot henah: 'The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women; they are vigorous (chayyot) and give birth before the midwife comes.' Chayyot (H2422, lively/vigorous, or possibly: like animals — they give birth easily): the midwives' response uses the vitality of the covenant people as their defense. The word chayyot is from chayyah (living/life) — the Hebrews' vitality is a mark of divine blessing.
1.20: vayyeitav Elohim lameyaldot: 'God dealt well with the midwives.' The divine reward for those who fear God over imperial power: vayyeitav (was good to, from tov) — the goodness of God expressed in covenant blessing. The midwives received batim (households/families) — a covenantal blessing of progeny and stability, the very thing they had protected for others. Those who protect life receive life.
1.21: vayhi ki-yareyu hameyaldot et-haElohim vayaas lahem batim: 'Because the midwives feared God, he gave them households.' The bayit (H1004, house/household) given to the midwives is simultaneously a domestic family unit and a dynastic legacy — the same word used for the house of David (2 Sam 7:11: YHWH will make you a house). Covenant faithfulness under persecution generates covenantal legacy. The midwives who preserved Israel's houses received houses of their own.
1.22: kol-haben hayilod hayorah tashlichuhu: 'Every son who is born, throw him into the Nile.' Pharaoh's final decree — a public, universal death-sentence on Hebrew male children. The Nile, Egypt's source of life, is made the instrument of death. The anti-creation decree (Gen 1:28: be fruitful and multiply) is reversed: all that is born (yilod) is cast away. The decree anticipates and inverts the Passover: Egypt will lose its firstborn as it tried to kill Israel's sons. The measure used will be measured back (Matt 7:2).

2.1: vayeikh ish mibet Levi vayiqach et-bat Levi (wayyēleḵ ʾîš mibbet lēwî wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-bat lēwî): 'A man from the house of Levi went and married a daughter of Levi.' The anonymity of Moses's parents — they are not named until Exod 6:20 (Amram and Jochebed) — focuses attention on the child rather than the lineage. Moses is from the tribe designated for priestly service (Levi), establishing his priestly identity before his prophetic commission. The double Levi (from Levi, daughter of Levi) underscores the covenantal continuity from the patriarchs.
2.2: vatare oto ki-tov hu (watteʾreʾ ʾōtô kî-ṭôb hûʾ): 'She saw that he was good.' Ki-tov (that it was good): the precise phrase from the creation narrative (Gen 1:4, 10, 12, 18, 21, 25, 31 — God saw that it was good). Moses's mother recognizes in her infant the same goodness that YHWH recognized in creation. Stephen in Acts 7:20 uses asteios (beautiful/fair before God), and Hebrews 11:23 calls him asteion — the parents saw something God-given in the child. The creation-goodness language signals that Moses's birth is a new creative act.
2.3: teva gome vatachmeraha bachemor uvazafet (tēbat gōme wattaḥmĕrāh baḥēmār ûbazzāpet): 'A basket of bulrushes/papyrus, she plastered it with bitumen and pitch.' Teva (H8392, ark/chest): the precise word used for Noah's ark (Gen 6:14-16) — only these two occurrences in the entire OT. Moses's basket is a miniature ark: like Noah, he is preserved in a teva through water while death surrounds. The pitch-and-bitumen coating echoes Noah's ark (Gen 6:14 — cover it with pitch inside and out). Both arks carry the seed of a new beginning through the waters of judgment.
2.4: vateytsatazev achoto merachok lede'ah mah-ye'aseh lo: 'His sister stood at a distance to know what would be done for him.' Moses's unnamed sister (later Miriam, Exod 15:20) watches from a distance — a combination of care and prudential distance. The word lede'ah (to know, from yada) — she watches to know what will happen. Covenant protection often works through attentive human witness, the small acts of faithfulness that shelter the vulnerable.
2.5: vateredu bat-Paroh lirchots al-hayeor: 'The daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the Nile.' Pharaoh's own daughter, drawing from the Nile to which Hebrew sons were condemned, discovers and rescues the condemned child. The irony is structural: the instrument of death (the Nile, Pharaoh's decree) becomes the location of rescue, and the agent of rescue is from within the house of the oppressor. YHWH's providential sovereignty uses the empire's own household against its own decree.
2.6: vatiftech vatirehu et-hayeled vehinei naar boche vatachemel alav: 'She opened it and saw the child — a boy crying; and she had compassion on him.' Vatachemel alav (she had compassion/pity on him): from chamal (H2550, have compassion/spare) — the spontaneous movement of the heart toward the suffering vulnerable. This compassion from within Pharaoh's own household is the surprise of grace: deliverance comes from unexpected places. The compassion of the divine is here mediated through a pagan princess.
2.7: vatomer achoto el-bat Paroh haelich veqarati lach isha meineket min-haivriyot: 'His sister said to Pharaoh's daughter: Shall I go and call you a nurse from the Hebrew women?' The sister's intervention brings the child back to his own mother — he is nursed by his biological mother at Pharaoh's expense. Providence arranges that Moses receives his Hebrew identity at his mother's breast, even while living in Pharaoh's household. The covenant heritage is transmitted through the most intimate possible means.
2.8: vateleich ha'alma vatiqra et-em hayaled: 'The young woman went and called the child's mother.' The chain of protection — mother, sister, princess — is the web of feminine courage that saves Moses. Multiple women act against the death-decree of a single man: the midwives (ch.1), Moses's mother, his sister, Pharaoh's daughter. The masculine power of empire is subverted by the feminine power of compassion. The Exodus begins with the courage of women.
2.9: vatomer bat-Paroh helikhihu et-hayeled hazeh vehenikiehu li vaani etten et-secharch: 'Take this child and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages.' Moses's mother is paid to nurse her own son. The irony of empire: Pharaoh's household funds the formation of its own deliverer. YHWH consistently uses the resources of the oppressor to equip those who will liberate the oppressed (cf. Israel's plundering of Egypt in Exod 12:35-36).
2.10: vatiqra shemo Mosheh vatomer ki min-hamayim meshitihu (wattiqrāʾ šĕmô mōšeh wattōʾmer kî-min-hammayim mĕšîtîhû): 'She called his name Moses, saying: for I drew him out of the water.' Mosheh (H4872) from mashah (H4871, draw out): the name-etymology given by Pharaoh's daughter — the Egyptian princess naming the Hebrew child after the act of rescue. A Hebrew passive form would mean one who is drawn out; an active form, one who draws out. Both work: Moses is drawn out of the Nile and will draw Israel out of Egypt. The name is a vocation compressed into a sound.
2.11: vayehi bayamim hahem vayigdal Mosheh vayetze el-echav vayar besivolotam: 'In those days, when Moses had grown up, he went out to his brothers and looked at their burdens.' Vayar besivolotam (he saw their burdens): Moses's seeing echoes YHWH's seeing in 3:7 (ra'o raiti et-oni ammi). The prophet who will mediate YHWH's deliverance first personally sees what YHWH has seen: the suffering of the covenant people. Stephen in Acts 7:23-24 identifies this as Moses's deliberate identification with his people, parallel to Christ's incarnational identification with human suffering (Heb 2:17-18).
2.12: vayifen ko vako vayar ki ein ish vayak et-haMitsri: 'He looked this way and that, and seeing no one, he struck the Egyptian.' The furtive glance before action — the consciousness of risk even in righteous response to injustice. Moses acts to defend the oppressed but through human agency that will need to be transformed. His zeal is real but his method (secret violence) is insufficient for the scale of liberation required. The Exodus will not be achieved by a single man's sword but by YHWH's mighty hand.
2.13: vayetze bayom hasheni vehinei shnei anashim ivrim nitsim: 'On the second day he went out and saw two Hebrew men quarreling.' The internal conflict within the oppressed community — the violence of empire internalized among its victims. The divided Hebrew community that rejects its own deliverer anticipates the pattern of Israel's repeated rejection of Moses in the wilderness (Exod 16-17; Num 14) and ultimately of the prophet like Moses (Acts 7:35-39).
2.14: mi samcha le'ish sar veshofet aleinu (mî śāmĕkā lĕʾîš śar wĕšōpēṭ ʿālênû): 'Who made you a prince and a judge over us?' The Hebrew slave's challenge to Moses's authority is quoted by Stephen in Acts 7:35 as typological of Israel's rejection of Christ: this Moses, whom they rejected saying who made you a ruler and judge? — this man God sent as both ruler and redeemer. The rejection of Moses the deliverer prefigures the rejection of the deliverer Moses pointed to.
2.15: vayishma Paroh et-hadavar hazeh vayevaqesh lahrog et-Mosheh vayivarch Mosheh mipnei Paroh: 'Pharaoh heard this thing and sought to kill Moses, and Moses fled from Pharaoh's face.' The exile of Moses to Midian is both flight from imperial violence and the necessary preparation for the burning-bush commission. The wilderness period (forty years, Acts 7:30) shapes the deliverer before deployment. Jesus's forty days in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11) compresses the Mosaic forty-year preparation into forty days of tested faithfulness.
2.16: ulekhohen Midyan sheva banot: 'The priest of Midian had seven daughters.' Moses encounters the daughters of Jethro (Reuel) at the well — the third great well-meeting in Genesis-Exodus (Isaac-Rebekah, Gen 24; Jacob-Rachel, Gen 29; now Moses-Zipporah). Wells are covenant meeting places in the OT: the place where the wandering stranger finds the one who will become the link to his future. Moses is now a stranger without a people — the well scene marks his transition from Egyptian prince to Midianite sojourner.
2.17: vayaqom Mosheh vayoshien vayashq et-tsonam: 'Moses stood up and helped them and watered their flock.' Moses's defense of the women against the hostile shepherds reveals his character: he uses his strength on behalf of the vulnerable. The shepherd who defends his flock — against hostile shepherds, against beasts — is the role Moses will now learn by tending Jethro's flock and that he will exercise for Israel for forty years in the wilderness. The Good Shepherd's character is forged in Midian.
2.18: madu'a mihartem bo hayom: 'Why have you come home so early today?' Jethro's question to his daughters reveals the normal situation: the well is contested, the women are routinely driven away. Moses's intervention was exceptional — not merely polite but decisive. The same word madua (why) opens the commission narrative in 3:3 (madua lo-yivar hasneh, why is the bush not burning up) — the question that opens perception to the unexpected.
2.19: ish Mitsri hitstsileinu miyad haro'im: 'An Egyptian man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds.' The daughters identify Moses as Egyptian — he looks Egyptian, speaks Egyptian, acts with Egyptian authority. Moses is a man without a clear identity: Hebrew by birth, Egyptian by formation, stranger in Midian. The deliverer is always the outsider within — Joseph in Egypt, Daniel in Babylon, Christ in Judea (John 1:11: he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him).
2.20: ve'ayeh lamdua zeh azavtem et-ha'ish: 'Where is he? Why did you leave the man?' Jethro's characteristic hospitality — the expectation that a stranger who acts with such character should be invited to share the table. Kireu lo ve'yochal lachem (call him to eat bread): table fellowship as covenant-initiation. The shared meal is always a covenant act in the OT (cf. Gen 31:54; Exod 18:12). Moses enters Jethro's household through bread.
2.21: vayoel Mosheh lashevet et-ha'ish vayiten et-Tziporah bito leMosheh: 'Moses agreed to stay with the man, and he gave Moses his daughter Zipporah.' Vayoel (H2974, agreed/consented): the verb suggests a deliberate choice — Moses makes Midian his home. The marriage to Zipporah grounds Moses in a new family before he returns to his first family. The name Tziporah (H6855) means bird — perhaps signaling freedom or swift movement. Moses's Midianite period is not wasted exile but essential formation.
2.22: vateled ben vayiqra et-shemo Gershom ki amar ger hayiti be'eretz nochriya: 'She bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, saying: I have been a stranger in a foreign land.' Gershom (H1648): word-play on ger (H1616, stranger/sojourner) + sham (there) — stranger-there. Moses names his son after his own identity crisis: the deliverer of Israel is himself a displaced person, a stranger. The ger identity will shape Israel's ethics: you shall not oppress a stranger, for you know the soul of the stranger, having been strangers in Egypt (Exod 23:9).
2.23: vayehi vayamim harabim hahem vayamot melek Mitsrayim vayane'anchu bnei Yisrael min-ha'avoda vayizaqu: 'During those many days the king of Egypt died, and the Israelites groaned because of their slavery and cried out.' Na'aqah (H5009, groan/cry out): the deep involuntary cry of those crushed beyond endurance. Tzaaq (H6818, cry out): the urgent legal cry to a superior for relief. Both words together emphasize the totality of Israel's anguish — involuntary suffering-cry and deliberate appeal. The Exodus begins with a groan that ascends to heaven.
2.24: vayishma Elohim et-na'aqatam vayizkor Elohim et-berito et-Avraham et-Yitzchak ve'et-Yaakov (wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-naʾăqātām wayyizĕkor ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-bĕrîtô ʾet-ʾabrāhām ʾet-yiṣḥāq wĕʾet-yaʿăqōb): 'God heard their groaning and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.' The four verbs of divine response in vv.24-25: heard (shama), remembered (zakar), saw (ra'ah), knew/took note (yada). Zakar (H2142, remember) is not mere mental recall but performative memory — God remembering the covenant is God activating it. The covenant made with three patriarchs is invoked with all three names: no generation of covenant is excluded.
2.25: vayar Elohim et-bnei Yisrael vayeda Elohim (wayyarʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl wayyēdaʿ ʾĕlōhîm): 'God saw the children of Israel and God knew.' The verse ends simply: vayeda Elohim — and God knew. Yada (H3045, know/take note of) as covenantal recognition: YHWH does not merely observe Israel's condition but enters into it — knowing as intimate concern and committed response. The brevity of the verse amplifies its weight: the entire Exodus is set in motion by these four words. God's knowing is God's acting.

4.1: Moses's first objection (hen lo ya'aminu li, "but they will not believe me") focuses on credibility, not capability. Aman (H539, believe/trust) — the same root as amen; his concern is that the nation will not extend covenantal trust to his claim of divine appointment. This is a practical objection, not theological rebellion: he has seen the bush but the elders have not; how does testimony without proof carry weight in a community that has been crushed into skepticism?
4.2: What is in your hand? — YHWH's response begins not with an argument but with an inventory of what is already present. The matteh (H4294, staff/rod) is the shepherd's basic tool — for guiding, defending, testing terrain. YHWH consistently works with what the servant already has rather than providing entirely new instruments. The question redirects attention from the impossible ("they won't believe me") to the available ("what do you already hold?").
4.3: Throw it on the ground — the staff becomes a nachash (H5175, serpent). The same word as the serpent of Eden (Gen 3:1); the wilderness is full of serpents (Num 21:6); the Egyptian deity Wadjet was depicted as a uraeus (cobra). In context, the sign has anti-Egyptian symbolic resonance: the serpent that threatens Israel (Egypt) can be grasped and controlled by the one who serves YHWH. Moses "ran from it" — appropriate fear, even from the instrument of his own wonder-working.
4.4: Grab it by the tail — contrary to safe snake-handling, which requires grasping just behind the head. The command requires trust: Moses must grasp the snake where it can still bite him, relying on YHWH's protection rather than his own technique. The serpent becomes a staff again (matteh, H4294) "in his palm" — the instrument of power is restored by obedience to the dangerous command.
4.5: The purpose statement for the signs: "so that they may believe that YHWH the God of their fathers — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — appeared to you." The signs are epistemological tools, not mere spectacle; they function to create the emunah (covenantal trust) that Moses feared would be absent. The covenantal name "God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob" (cf. Exod 3:6) anchors the commission in the patriarchal covenant.
4.6: Put your hand in your cloak — the second sign: the hand emerges leprous (tsara'at, H6879). Leprosy in the OT was associated with divine judgment (Num 12:10, Miriam; 2 Kgs 5, Naaman; 2 Chr 26:19-20, Uzziah), but also with divine power to afflict and restore. The cloak (cheq, H2436, bosom/chest fold) is where valuables were kept — Moses puts his hand into the place of security and it emerges defiled; then back again and it is restored "like the rest of his flesh." The sign enacts the reversal of judgment.
4.7: The restoration of the leprous hand — "like the rest of his flesh" (ke-besaro) — demonstrates both the afflicting and healing power of the same God. The sign is not primarily about Egypt but about YHWH's absolute sovereignty over bodily integrity, which will later be demonstrated in the plagues (afflicting Egypt) and the wilderness healing (restoring Israel).
4.8: Three signs in escalating weight: the first (staff/serpent) may be dismissed as magic; the second (leprous hand) is harder to replicate; the third (Nile water→blood) touches the sacred heart of Egyptian religion (the Nile as Hapy, the inundation deity). The conditional structure — "if not the first, then the second; if not both, then the third" — grants Israel a graduated level of evidence before requiring faith, consistent with YHWH's pattern of meeting people where they are.
4.9: Water from the Nile becomes blood on dry ground — a miniature preview of the first plague (Exod 7:20). Ye'or (H2975, the Nile) is the Egyptian loanword for the great river, used throughout Exodus specifically for the Nile rather than the generic Hebrew nahar (river). The sign deliberately targets Egypt's most sacred resource.
4.10: The fourth objection — "I have never been eloquent (lo ish devarim anokhi)" — is the most specific: Moses is literally "not a man of words/speech." Devarim (H1697) is the plural of davar (word/matter), the same term used for "the ten commandments" (Deut 10:4, "ten words"). The qualification "neither in the past nor since you have spoken to me" acknowledges that even this divine encounter has not changed his speech capacity — he is describing an ongoing condition, not a temporary case of nerves.
4.11: Who gave human beings their mouths? — YHWH's response is a series of rhetorical questions asserting his creative sovereignty over all speech capacities: "who makes them deaf (cheresh, H2795) or mute (illem, H483)? who gives sight or makes blind (iver, H5787)?" The answer is implicit: "Is it not I, YHWH?" The argument: the Creator of speech has more capacity to enable speech than the speaker has to claim incapacity. This is not a dismissal of Moses's limitation but a reframing of where the power lies.
4.12: "I will be with your mouth (im-pikha, literally with your mouth) and will teach you what to say." The divine commitment is specific: not to remove Moses's limitation but to accompany it and work through it. Yara (H3384, teach/instruct) — the same root as Torah; YHWH will continuously instruct Moses in real time what to say. This is not a prepared script but ongoing divine direction.
4.13: Please send someone else — the fifth and final objection, most direct: shelach na be-yad tishlach, "send by the hand of [whoever] you will send." Moses does not name a substitute but gestures at anyone other than himself. This provokes YHWH's anger (vayichar af YHWH, H2734 + H639) — the only time in this extended commissioning that YHWH expresses anger. The objections have moved from legitimate concerns to outright reluctance.
4.14: Aaron the Levite — the tribal identification "the Levite" is unusual when speaking of one's own brother; it may signal Aaron's priestly role before the Sinai legislation or distinguish him from other Aarons. "He can speak well" (daber yedaber, H1696 doubled — an infinitive absolute construction meaning "he can surely/fluently speak"); the intensive form emphasizes genuine eloquence. "He is already on his way to meet you" — Aaron's movement toward Moses is already in progress, a sign that YHWH prepared the solution before Moses raised the final objection.
4.15: YHWH promises to instruct both Moses and Aaron simultaneously: "I will help you both speak and will teach you what to do." The partnership of two speakers — one receiving the revelation, one articulating it — will become the model for the prophet-priest collaboration throughout Israel's history. Moses as the prophet who receives and transmits; Aaron as the priest who speaks to the assembly.
4.16: "He will be as your mouth, and you will be as God to him" — ve-hayah hu yih'yeh-lekha le-feh ve-atah tih'yeh-lo le-Elohim. "As God to him" (le-Elohim, H430) is a remarkable designation: Moses functions as the divine representative to Aaron, who functions as his mouth to the people. The structure is: YHWH → Moses (revelation) → Aaron (articulation) → people (reception). This is the first explicit description of prophetic mediation as a chain of communication.
4.17: "The staff of God" (matteh ha-Elohim) — the shepherd's staff has been transformed by the commission: ordinary wood is now the instrument of divine signs. The naming of the staff as "God's staff" is the first of several renamings throughout Exodus that mark how ordinary objects are consecrated by covenant use.
4.18: Moses returns to Jethro and asks permission to leave — a notable act of covenantal courtesy toward his father-in-law. He does not reveal the full content of his divine commission but uses the more generic "to see whether my relatives are still alive." Yeter (Jethro's alternative name, H3503) appears here; the name Yitro (H3499) is used elsewhere — two forms of the same name, perhaps indicating two traditions merged in the final text.
4.19: YHWH's reassurance: "all those who wanted to kill you are dead" — referring to the Egyptians who sought Moses after the killing of the overseer (Exod 2:15). This addresses an unspoken fear Moses had not articulated in his five objections: the personal danger of returning. The phrase "all who sought your life are dead" reappears almost verbatim in 1 Kgs 19:10, 14 (Elijah's complaint) and Matt 2:20 (Joseph's dream about returning from Egypt), marking it as a standard form of divine reassurance.
4.20: Moses takes his wife Zipporah and sons (banav, H1121, his sons — more than one, though only one son is named previously) and begins the return journey. "The staff of God (matteh ha-Elohim) in his hand" — the phrase closes the bracket opened in v.17; Moses departs equipped as the divine agent.
4.21: "I will harden Pharaoh's heart" — ani achazzeq et-lev Par'oh (H2388, to harden/strengthen + H3820, heart). This is the first mention of the heart-hardening theme. Three different verbs are used throughout the plague narratives: chazaq (to strengthen/make stubborn, here), kabed (to make heavy, as a burden), and qashah (to make hard/severe). In the first five plagues Pharaoh hardens his own heart; in the last five YHWH hardens it. The theological tension (human responsibility vs. divine sovereignty) is part of the text's design, not its inconsistency.
4.22: "Israel is my firstborn son" — beni vekori Yisrael (H1121 + H1060, my son my firstborn). The covenant sonship of Israel is here declared officially for the first time to Pharaoh. In the ancient world, the firstborn son holds the highest status in the family — rights, inheritance, authority. YHWH's designation of a nation of slaves as his firstborn son is the antithesis of their social status; covenantal reality inverts empirical standing.
4.23: The covenant logic of the tenth plague is stated before the plagues begin: "Let my son go... you refused... so I will kill your firstborn." The structure is covenantal: Pharaoh's refusal to release YHWH's firstborn will be repaid through the loss of Egypt's firstborn. This is not arbitrary retaliation but covenantal reciprocity — what is done to YHWH's son will be done to the oppressor's son.
4.24: The lodging-place attack — "the LORD met him and sought to kill him" (vayevaqesh hamito). One of the most syntactically compressed and theologically mysterious passages in the Pentateuch. The subject of "kill him" is ambiguous — it could refer to Moses, though the traditional reading (and what makes Zipporah's response intelligible) assumes it is Moses. The divine agent is understood as the "angel of YHWH" (cf. Gen 32:24-30, Jacob's night wrestling). The crisis appears to be circumcision: Moses had not circumcised his son born in Midian, thereby leaving the covenant mark incomplete.
4.25: Zipporah took a flint knife and cut off her son's foreskin — she acts as the covenant agent when Moses cannot. Tzur (H6697, flint) — stone knives were used for circumcision even when metal was available (Josh 5:2-3), preserving the archaic ritual. She "touched his feet (raglayv, H7272)" — in Hebrew, "feet" can be a euphemism for genitals (e.g., Ruth 3:7). The blood of circumcision (substituting for Moses's blood) averts the death threat. Bridegroom of blood (chatan damim, H2860 + H1818) — the Aramaic cognate of chatan (bridegroom) means "circumcised one"; the phrase may encode the covenant link between circumcision and marriage-covenant identity.
4.26: "The LORD let him alone" — the crisis passes with the circumcision completed. The parenthetical note "(she said 'bridegroom of blood' in reference to circumcision)" is the narrator's gloss explaining the cryptic phrase. The passage is preserved in its irreducible difficulty — an encounter with divine judgment that is averted not by explanation but by covenant action (blood).
4.27: YHWH instructs Aaron to go into the wilderness to meet Moses; Aaron meets him "at the mountain of God" — Horeb, where the bush appeared. The reunion is divinely staged: the two brothers meet at the very site of Moses's commissioning, so that Aaron enters the commission-context before beginning his ministry. Vayishaq lo (he kissed him) — the covenant greeting, as between Jacob and Esau (Gen 33:4) and the father and the prodigal son (Luke 15:20).
4.28: Moses "told Aaron everything YHWH had commanded him to say and all the signs he had commanded." The transfer of the commission from Moses to Aaron is total and exact — Aaron is fully briefed, including the miracle-signs. The chain of communication (YHWH → Moses → Aaron, as described in 4:16) now operates for the first time.
4.29: Moses and Aaron assemble all the elders of Israel — a formal national gathering. The elders (zeqenim, H2205) are the representative heads of Israel's clan-based governance structure; convening them is equivalent to convening Israel's leadership in total.
4.30: Aaron speaks all the words and performs all the signs — precisely as the communication chain of vv.15-16 prescribed. The text is careful to have Aaron do both the speaking and the demonstrating; Moses is in the background, the unseen source. This division of labor will characterize their relationship throughout the Exodus narrative.
4.31: They believed, and bowed their heads in worship — vaya'aminu (H539, they believed/trusted) and vayishtachavu (H7812, they bowed down/worshipped). The community's response combines cognitive trust (aman) and physical reverence (prostration). The trigger for the worship: "YHWH was concerned about them (paqad, H6485 — visited/attended to) and had seen their misery (ra'ah et-aniyam)." The knowledge that they had been seen and cared for — not forgotten — produces worship.

5.1: "Let my people go, so that they may hold a festival to me in the desert" — the first covenant demand to Pharaoh. Chag (H2282, festival/feast) — the pilgrimage feast requiring travel to the site of worship. The three annual pilgrimage feasts (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles) are all chagim; the demand is for the freedom of worship, not yet permanent liberation. The narrative begins with a religious-freedom request before the total-exodus demand escalates through the plagues.
5.2: "Who is YHWH (mi YHWH) that I should obey his voice?" — Pharaoh's response is the governing question of the entire book of Exodus. Mi YHWH (H4310 + H3068): the question challenges both the identity and the authority of Israel's God. "I do not know YHWH (lo yada'ti et-YHWH)" — the verb yada' (H3045) has the full covenantal sense of acknowledge/submit as well as merely know; Pharaoh refuses both intellectual acknowledgment and covenantal submission. The plagues will answer mi YHWH systematically.
5.3: "The God of the Hebrews" — Elohei ha-ivrim (H430 + H5680). Ivri (Hebrew) may connect to ever (crossing over), traditionally explaining "those who crossed the river" (Euphrates/Jordan), or to the Hapiru/Apiru, a social class of landless laborers documented in Egyptian records (the Amarna letters, 14th century BCE). The three-day journey request — derekh shloshet yamim — frames the worship as a practical, limited request, not yet a full departure from Egypt.
5.4: Pharaoh's accusation: "you are making them idle (vatishbetu, H7673 — rest/cease); root of shabbat)" — the irony is profound: the Israelites' desire to observe a religious festival is labeled Sabbath-keeping (laziness) by the tyrant. The vocabulary of Sabbath-rest is used as an accusation against the enslaved people who have no rest.
5.5: "Numerous" (rabbim, H7227) — the same word used in Exod 1:7, 9, 12 for the population growth that originally triggered Pharaoh's oppression. His fear of their numbers has not diminished; now he uses their numbers as an excuse to intensify rather than relax control.
5.6: Pharaoh issues the new decree on "that same day" — immediate administrative response, not delayed reaction. The swiftness of the retaliatory decree shows that Pharaoh interpreted Moses's request as a labor disruption, not a religious claim, and responded within his administrative framework (production quotas and slave oversight).
5.7: "No straw for bricks" — archaeological evidence from New Kingdom Egypt confirms that chopped straw was mixed into Nile mud for brick-making; the straw helped bind the clay and prevented cracking. The Papyrus Anastasi IV (13th century BCE) mentions administrators failing to meet brick quotas — an administrative structure consistent with Exodus's portrayal. Removing the straw while maintaining the quota is an economically precise cruelty: the physical material of production is removed while the productivity demand is unchanged.
5.8: "The same number of bricks as before — don't reduce the quota. They are lazy (nirpim, H7503 — to slacken/relax hands)" — rafah (slack hands) is precisely the opposite of chazaq (strong hands), the covenantal posture of readiness. Pharaoh's accusation of laziness redefines the workers' desire for worship as a character defect, not a legitimate religious claim.
5.9: "Let the work be heavier (tikhbad ha-avodah, H3513 + H5656)" — the verb kabad (to be heavy/honored) is the same root as kavod (glory). The work that should glorify YHWH (the Israelites building YHWH's dwelling rather than Pharaoh's cities) is instead made a crushing burden. The "lies" (divrei-shaqer) that the workers should not attend to are Moses's words of liberation — what YHWH calls truth, Pharaoh calls falsehood.
5.10: Pharaoh's slave drivers (nogesim, H5065) and overseers (shotrim, H7860) relay the decree. Nagash (to urge/press/exact) is the term for the oppressive taskmaster; shotrim are the Israelite record-keepers and overseers who occupy the middle tier between Egyptian masters and Hebrew workers.
5.11: "Go and get straw yourself, wherever you can find it" — the demand is practical humiliation: the workers must spend time and energy foraging for the raw material of their own oppression. The word kash (H7179, stubble/chaff) — not the full straw, just the remnants left after harvest — indicates that even the available material is inferior.
5.12: The people "scattered throughout the land" (vayafeitz ha-am... leqashot qash) — parats (H6327, scatter/disperse) echoes the threat of scattering as a covenant curse (Deut 28:64; Lev 26:33). The enforced dispersal for stubble-gathering mirrors the curse of exile in miniature.
5.13: The slave drivers "kept pressing them" (ha-nogesim atsim, H213, pressing/urging) — the continuous aspect of the participle emphasizes relentless pressure. "Complete your daily quota (davar yom be-yomo, the matter of a day in its day)" — the day-by-day demand allows no accumulation, no preparation, no rest. The phrase davar yom be-yomo recurs in the manna narrative (Exod 16:4) as YHWH's instruction for gathering — the same daily-sufficiency framework, but given as grace rather than coercion.
5.14: The Israelite overseers (shotrim) are beaten (yukku, from H5221 nakah) — the violence falls on the intermediate tier, the Israelite officials who occupy the impossible position between Egyptian power and Hebrew need. "Why did you not complete your quota (choq, H2706, your allotment/statute)" — the same word used for YHWH's covenant statutes; Pharaoh's quota-demand uses covenantal vocabulary for oppressive ends.
5.15: The Israelite overseers appeal directly to Pharaoh — a desperate move that bypasses the administrative hierarchy. The appeal to the throne was a standard mechanism for redress in ancient Near Eastern bureaucratic systems (documented in Egyptian petitions), but here it produces not relief but accusation.
5.16: "No straw is given to us, yet they say 'make bricks'" — the overseers articulate the logical impossibility of the demand. "The fault lies with your own people (chatat ammekha)" — attempting to redirect responsibility to the Egyptian supervisors. The phrase chatat (H2403, sin/fault) is the covenant word for transgression, used here in a secular complaint, indicating how deeply covenantal language shapes even non-religious speech.
5.17: "Lazy (nirpim), that is what you are — lazy!" — Pharaoh doubles down; his interpretation of the situation is the mirror-opposite of Moses's. Where Moses says "YHWH has spoken," Pharaoh says "you are idle." The fundamental conflict of the Exodus is interpretive: who defines what is happening — YHWH or Pharaoh? The plagues will settle the hermeneutical dispute by making YHWH's interpretation the only viable one.
5.18: "Your full quota of bricks" — matkonet levenah (H4971 + H3843, the exact measure of bricks) — precision vocabulary for brick-manufacturing output; the standardization of production quotas in the ancient Near East is well-attested in administrative papyri. Pharaoh's system operates by measurement, record-keeping, and enforcement; YHWH's coming response will overwhelm all these systems.
5.19: The overseers "realized they were in serious trouble" (be-ra vayu otam) — literally "they saw that they were in evil/trouble." This moment of recognition, when the intermediate authorities see that their position is untenable, prepares for their accusation against Moses in v.21.
5.20: The overseers find Moses and Aaron "stationed/waiting (nitzavim, H5324)" to meet them — Moses and Aaron are described with the same root used for YHWH's messengers "standing" in divine attendance (Gen 18:2; Dan 10:11). Even the posture of meeting carries theological resonance.
5.21: "May YHWH look on you and judge you" — the overseers' accusation against Moses and Aaron is framed as a covenant-lawsuit prayer: appealing to YHWH as judge (shaphat, H8199) against his own messengers. The irony is complete: the oppressed pray against their liberators. "You have made us a stench (hav'ashtem et-reichenu) in the eyes of Pharaoh" — ba'ash (H887, to stink/be abhorrent) uses the strongest social-disgrace language: to stink in the nostrils of power is to be marked for destruction.
5.22: Moses returned to YHWH and asks "Why? Is this why you sent me?" — the double lamah (H4100, why) is the structure of the biblical lament; cf. Ps 22:1 (the opening of the passion psalm). Moses's complaint has the same form as the Psalms of individual and communal lament: direct address, accusation, appeal for explanation. The complaint is not faithlessness but intimate engagement — Moses takes his confusion to YHWH rather than away from him.
5.23: "Ever since I went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has brought trouble on this people, and you have not rescued them at all." The Hebrew vehatsil lo hitzalta (H5337, and rescued not you-have-rescued) — the double negative with infinitive absolute: "you have absolutely not rescued them." Moses's distress is proportional to the gap between the commission ("I will deliver them") and the current reality (conditions have worsened). YHWH's answer will come in ch. 6.

6.1: "Now you will see" — YHWH's response to Moses's lament begins with a promise of witness: Moses will observe what YHWH will do. "By a strong hand (beyad chazaqah) he will let them go; by a strong hand he will drive them out" — the double use of yad chazaqah (strong hand) frames the Exodus as an act of divine force that overwhelms even Pharaoh's coercive power. The phrase becomes the theological shorthand for the Exodus throughout the OT (Deut 6:21; 26:8).
6.2: "I am YHWH" — the covenant self-identification formula that anchors everything that follows. The declaration ani YHWH appears seven times in the seven "I will" passage of vv.2-8, functioning as the name-signature at both the opening and closing of the covenant formula. Every "I will" statement is grounded in the unshakeable identity of the speaker.
6.3: "I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known (noda'ti) to them" — the key interpretive problem: the patriarchs used the name YHWH (Gen 12:8; 15:7), so in what sense was it "not known" to them? The key is the verb yada' (H3045): in Hebrew, to "know" involves not merely familiarity but experiential relationship — to know by encounter. The patriarchs heard the name; the Exodus generation will know its meaning through the redemptive events about to unfold. El Shaddai (H410 + H7706) — "God Almighty" (LXX/Vg: Pantokrator/Omnipotens); the etymology is disputed: shad (breast, a nurturing epithet?), shaddai from Akkadian shadu (mountain), or from shadad (to overwhelm). The patriarchal name encoded provision and power; the exodus name will encode redemption and covenant fidelity.
6.4: The covenant with the patriarchs is recalled: "I established my covenant (qumoti et-beriti, H6965 + H1285 — I caused my covenant to stand)" — the hiphil of qum emphasizes YHWH's active agency in maintaining the covenant. The land of Canaan as "the land of their sojournings (eretz megureihem)" — the patriarchs were gerim (sojourners) in the land that was promised but not yet possessed.
6.5: "I have also heard the groaning (ne'aqat, H5009) of the Israelites" — the same root as the "sighing and groaning" of Exod 2:24. Na'aq is a cry of oppression, closer to a groan than a prayer; it is not yet liturgical intercession but the raw sound of suffering. "I have remembered (zakar, H2142) my covenant" — divine memory is not cognitive retrieval but covenantal activation; to "remember" a covenant is to act upon it.
6.6: The seven "I will" declarations begin: (1) "I will bring you out (hotzeti, H3318)" — the Exodus verb yatza (to go out) in its causative form; (2) "I will free you from being slaves (hitzalti, H5337)" — natsal (to snatch/rescue); (3) "I will redeem you (ga'alti, H1350) with an outstretched arm" — ga'al is the kinsman-redeemer verb, the obligation of the nearest male relative to buy back enslaved kin (Lev 25:47-49; Ruth 3:13). YHWH applies to himself the legal role of the go'el — he has kinship obligations to Israel and will fulfill them even at cost. This is one of the most legally precise theological statements in the OT.
6.7: (4) "I will take you as my own people (laqachti etkem li le'am)" — the covenant-adoption formula; (5) "I will be your God (vehayiti lakhem le-Elohim)" — the bilateral covenant formula (cf. "you will be my people and I will be your God," Jer 30:22; Ezek 36:28). Then the epistemological purpose: "you will know (yeda'tem) that I am YHWH your God who brought you out from under the yokes of Egypt." The yada' that was denied to the patriarchs will now be granted to the Exodus generation through the redemptive events themselves.
6.8: (6) "I will bring you to the land" and (7) "I will give it to you as a possession (morashah, H4181, inheritance/possession)." Morashah appears only here and in Deut 33:4 ("the Torah is Moses's inheritance for the congregation of Jacob") in the Pentateuch; it is the legal term for heritable property. "I am YHWH" — the formula closes the seven-fold declaration, bracketing it with the divine name as its unshakeable foundation.
6.9: "They did not listen to Moses because of their discouragement (qotser ruach, H7114 + H7307 — shortness/exhaustion of spirit) and harsh labor (avodah qashah)." Qotser ruach is literally "shortness of breath" — the oppressed person who cannot breathe freely. The trauma of slavery impairs reception of hope: even the most liberating message cannot be received by a spirit that has been systematically crushed. This verse is a pastoral-theological observation: suffering can temporarily block the capacity for faith, without that block being a moral failure.
6.10: YHWH's instruction to Moses and the simple narrative marker "the LORD spoke to Moses, saying" — a formulaic introduction used repeatedly throughout Exodus and Leviticus. Its repetition creates the rhythm of continuous divine speech that structures the Pentateuch.
6.11: The commission is restated: "Go, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his country." The simplicity of the command contrasts with the elaborate commissioning of chs. 3-4; after Moses's lament and YHWH's response, the mission is re-launched in its most elemental form.
6.12: Moses raises the objection again: "if the Israelites will not listen to me, why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I speak with uncircumcised lips (aral sefatayim)?" Arel (H6189, uncircumcised) when applied to non-physical features in Hebrew means "blocked, stopped up, ritually unready" (cf. uncircumcised heart, Deut 10:16; uncircumcised ear, Jer 6:10). Moses's "uncircumcised lips" are lips that are blocked from effective speech — not in need of literal circumcision but of covenant opening. The repetition of this objection (first in 4:10) frames it as a persistent self-perception that YHWH will eventually correct not through argument but through experience.
6.13: YHWH's response to Moses's renewed objection is to commission both Moses and Aaron together. The "charging" (vaytsav, H6680) of them both — a term used for formal official orders — authorizes the joint mission with full divine authority.
6.14: The genealogical register begins: Reuben's sons — Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi. This interruption of the narrative just before the plagues serves to anchor Moses and Aaron in their full tribal lineage. The genealogy moves quickly through Reuben and Simeon to reach Levi, the tribe that matters for the commission context.
6.15: Simeon's sons listed, including "Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman" — the ethnically mixed ancestry is noted without comment, consistent with the text's honest inclusion of narratives that complicate the purity of covenant lineage (cf. Judah and Tamar, Gen 38).
6.16: Levi's three sons — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari — and his total lifespan of 137 years. The Levite genealogy is the point of the entire insert; Reuben and Simeon are listed to establish that the genealogy is ordered by birth sequence before arriving at Levi. Kohath (Moses's grandfather's line) gets the most detail.
6.17: Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei — the priestly clans that will serve in the Kohathite-Gershonite-Merarite division of Levitical service (Num 3-4).
6.18: Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years. Amram (H6019, "the people are exalted") — Moses and Aaron's father; his name connects the family to the collective identity of the people being liberated.
6.19: Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. The three clans of Levi are now established — Gershon, Kohath, Merari — each with their own sub-clans that will be assigned distinct roles in the tabernacle service.
6.20: Amram married Jochebed (Yocheved, H3115, "YHWH is her glory") his father's sister — a marriage that Lev 18:12 will later prohibit ("do not have sexual relations with your father's sister"). The genealogy records the union without apology: the Sinai legislation had not yet been given; the patriarchal and early Mosaic periods precede the codification of these prohibitions. Jochebed is described as bearing "Aaron and Moses" — Aaron is named first, consistent with his role as elder brother (Exod 7:7).
6.21: Izhar's sons: Korah, Nepheg, Zichri. Korah (H7141) — the rebel whose challenge to Moses and Aaron will be narrated in Num 16-17; the genealogy introduces him without comment here, establishing him as Moses's first cousin before his rebellion is recorded.
6.22: Uzziel's sons: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri. Elzaphan (Elizaphan) appears again in Num 3:30 as the leader of the Kohathite clan; his genealogical prominence here anticipates his later administrative role.
6.23: Aaron married Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon. Nahshon (H5177) was the prince of Judah (Num 1:7; 2:3) and a direct ancestor of David (Ruth 4:20) and of Jesus (Matt 1:4; Luke 3:32). Aaron's marriage connects the Levitical priesthood to the Davidic-Judahite line through the marriage of the first high priest.
6.24: The sons of Korah are listed — Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph. These become the "Korahite" Levites; Num 16 records Korah's rebellion, but the Korahite clan persists in priestly service (1 Chr 9:19; Pss 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88 are "Psalms of the Sons of Korah"). The genealogical record includes without erasing even the lineage that produces rebellion.
6.25: Eleazar son of Aaron married a daughter of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. Phinehas (H6372) will become the paradigm of covenant zeal (Num 25:7-11 — he acts decisively in the Baal-Peor incident and receives "the covenant of peace" and a "covenant of everlasting priesthood"). The genealogy is establishing the priestly lineage that will run through Eleazar and Phinehas, not through Nadab and Abihu who will die offering unauthorized fire (Lev 10:1-2).
6.26: "It was this same Aaron and Moses (hu Aaron u-Moshe) to whom the LORD said..." — the resumptive identification closes the genealogical bracket, re-anchoring the narrative to the two named protagonists. The genealogy has established their credentials; now the text can proceed. The reversal of order ("Aaron and Moses" instead of "Moses and Aaron") may signal Aaron's prominence as spokesman in the sequence that follows.
6.27: The same Moses and Aaron who appeared before Pharaoh are those who brought Israel out of Egypt — the genealogy has anchored them in the covenant lineage; now the narrative resumes with their commission confirmed and their identity established before the plague sequence begins.
6.28: The temporal marker "on the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt" — a resumptive temporal clause that re-anchors the narrative timeline after the genealogical digression. The formal date-formula is used in Leviticus and Numbers to introduce divine-speech units (Lev 1:1, "called to Moses and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying").
6.29: "I am YHWH — tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I tell you." The commission is restated in its simplest form: the entire content of the message is "everything I tell you" — total obedience to the divine word, not selective transmission. The formula ani YHWH again: the commission is grounded in identity, not argument.
6.30: "Since I speak with faltering lips, why would Pharaoh listen to me?" — Moses repeats the "uncircumcised lips" objection from v.12. The repetition (6:12, 6:30) creates a bracket around the genealogy: the same objection before and after the family credentials are established. Despite his genealogical legitimacy, Moses still distrusts his own speech. YHWH's response will not be to fix Moses's speech but to work through him anyway — the ultimate answer to the "faltering lips" objection is the plagues themselves.

13.1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: The standard prophetic address formula (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe le'mor) frames what follows not as legislation improvised after the event but as the divine interpretation of the Exodus itself. The consecration command is the theological corollary to the tenth plague.
13.2: Consecrate to me (qadeshu-li) every firstborn (kol-bechor): Qadash (to set apart as holy) is the priestly consecration verb; bechor derives from bakar (to be first, to bear firstfruit). Petter rechem (opener of the womb) — from pathar (to open/burst through) — specifies the firstborn as the one who ruptures the birth canal, the biological and legal first of the line. Both human and animal firstborn are addressed; the command grounds the Passover logic: Israel's firstborn were spared because a substitute died in their place.
13.3: Remember this day (zachor et-hayom hazeh): Zachor is the covenant-memory verb, used in Deuteronomy for sabbath (5:15), for the Exodus itself (16:3), and for the Amalekite attack (25:17). It means more than intellectual recall — it means to make present and act on. House of slaves (beit avadim): Egypt is defined not by geography but by its social structure; Israel is defined as the people freed from it.
13.4: Month of Abib (chodesh ha-aviv): Aviv = green/tender ears of grain (barley ripening); the month is named from agricultural phenology, not a number or a god. Later calendar reform (Babylonian influence) replaced aviv with Nisan, but Deuteronomy still uses aviv (16:1). The spring month marks both the agricultural new year and the redemptive new beginning.
13.5: When YHWH brings you (ki yeviacha): The causal-temporal ki + Hiphil imperfect of bo' anticipates the land-gift as already settled. Flowing with milk and honey (zavet chalav u'devash): The stock formula (appears ~20 times in the Pentateuch) describes agricultural abundance — chalav (milk from flocks) and devash (honey from date palms or wild bees). The five nations named are the standard catalog of Canaan's pre-Israelite inhabitants.
13.6: Seven days you shall eat matzot (unleavened bread): Seven is the week of completeness; the festival (chag) on the seventh day is a mirror of the Sabbath structure embedded in Passover observance. The temporal overlap of the week-festival and the annual commemoration links personal discipline (no leaven in the house) to communal celebration.
13.7: No leaven shall be seen with you (lo-yera'eh lecha chametz): The Niphal of ra'ah (to be seen) used as passive; chametz = leavened dough; se'or = sourdough starter or leaven. The prohibition covers not just eating but possession throughout all your territory (kol-gevulecha). Paul's midrash on this text (1 Cor 5:7-8) treats leaven as a metaphor for corruption spreading through a community.
13.8: You shall tell your son (v'higadeta l'vincha): The Hiphil of nagad (to declare/tell) is the catechetical verb for Passover explanation. B'avur zeh (because of this, on account of this) — the demonstrative this points to the physical matzot and bitter herbs currently before the narrator; memory is made tangible through objects. The Passover Haggadah (from this same root nagad) derives its name from this verse.
13.9: Sign (l'ot) on your hand... memorial (l'zikaron) between your eyes: Ot = sign (same term as circumcision in Gen 17:11, rainbow in Gen 9:12-13) — a physical marker of covenant status. Zikaron = memorial/reminder from zakar (the memory verb of v.3). The parallel is later literalized as tefillin (Deut 6:8 uses totafot): leather boxes with Torah portions worn on arm and forehead. Torah of YHWH in your mouth: The sign-on-body motif points to the ultimate goal — scripture internalized as speech.
13.10: Statute (chuqqah) at its appointed time (moed): Chuqqah = binding ordinance from chaqaq (to engrave/inscribe); it conveys permanence — the law engraved, not merely spoken. Moed = appointed time/meeting, the same root as ohel moed (tent of meeting); both the tabernacle entrance and the annual feasts are moadim — times of divine-human rendezvous.
13.11: When YHWH brings you to the land of the Canaanite: The promise-fulfillment frame (ki + Hiphil imperfect of bo') recurs from v.5, creating a sandwich structure around the Passover regulations. The firstborn law (vv.11-16) extends the Passover redemption logic into the future life of the nation in the land.
13.12: You shall cause to pass over (ha'avarta) to YHWH all that opens the womb: The Hiphil of avar (to cross/pass over) uses the Passover verb — peh samech chaf — the same root as Pesach; the firstborn are 'passed over' to YHWH just as death 'passed over' Israel's houses. Every firstborn male (zachar) of your cattle belongs to YHWH (laYHWH).
13.13: Firstborn donkey (petter chamor) redeemed (tifdeh) with a lamb (seh): The donkey (chamor) is the paradigm unclean animal — it cannot be sacrificed on the altar, so it must either be redeemed by substitution (padah = ransom by exchange) or have its neck broken (va'arafto — from oref, the nape of the neck). The lamb stands in for the donkey; the substitution logic is explicit. Padah (to redeem by payment/substitution) is distinct from ga'al (to redeem as kinsman); both appear in the Exodus redemption vocabulary. Every firstborn son also requires redemption — human sons and unclean animals share the same status before YHWH: too precious/dangerous to be unredeemed.
13.14: When your son asks in time to come (machar): Machar = tomorrow/future; the catechetical question-and-answer sequence (asked also in Exod 12:26; Deut 6:20; Josh 4:6) structures Passover transmission across generations. By strength of hand (bechozek yad) YHWH brought us out: The yad (hand) is the primary Exodus metaphor — the outstretched arm, the strong hand. The summary formula 'hand + Egypt + slaves' condenses the whole narrative into a sentence for children.
13.15: Pharaoh hardened himself (hikshaih): Here the root is qashah (to be hard/difficult), distinct from chazaq (strengthen) and kaved (be heavy) used elsewhere. The three hardening roots together form a theological composite: the heart can be made stubborn (qashah), strengthened in its resolve (chazaq), or weighted down in dullness (kaved). Pharaoh's hardening is both self-induced and divinely confirmed. YHWH killed all the firstborns: The theological consequence: because Pharaoh's firstborn died, Israel's firstborn must be consecrated — the covenant reciprocity of the tenth plague.
13.16: Totafot (frontlets) between your eyes: Totafot is a hapax legomenon in the Pentateuch (also Deut 6:8, 11:18); the etymology is uncertain — possibly Akkadian tutuptu (band), possibly a foreign loanword. The rabbis identified these as the phylacteries (tefillin), small leather boxes containing Torah portions, bound to the arm and forehead during morning prayer. Whether the original command was meant literally or metaphorically is debated; the practice is ancient and attested in Second Temple Judaism.
13.17: God did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines (derekh eretz-Pelishtim): The Philistines (Pelishtim) occupied the coastal plain (the Via Maris); archaeologically attested Egyptian military fortifications lined this route. Pen yinnachem (lest they regret/change their minds): Nacham = to be comforted/consoled, but also to change one's mind or regret — the divine rationale for the longer wilderness route reveals pastoral psychology: YHWH reads the people's fragility and accommodates it.
13.18: Yam Suph (Sea of Reeds): Suph = papyrus reeds/rushes (cf. 2:3 — the basket among the suph); the Greek translation 'Red Sea' (Erythra Thalassa) is a geographical overlay from later Hellenistic usage that identified the body of water with the Gulf of Suez. The Hebrew name points to a marshy reed-sea environment.
