Chamushim (armed/by fives?): The word appears twice in the OT (here and Josh 1:14); the root is debated — possibly chamesh (five, in military formation?) or chamush (loins/equipped). The LXX reads 'in the fifth generation' (fifth generation after the patriarchs?); most translations opt for 'armed for battle.'
13.19: Moses took the bones of Joseph (atzamot Yosef): Joseph had sworn Israel under oath (shevet hishbia = made them swear a solemn oath, Gen 50:25) to carry his bones out of Egypt. Atzamot = bones — the embodied continuity of the patriarchal promise; the living carry the dead through the wilderness into the land. The bones of Joseph, the greatest of Jacob's sons, travel with the redeemed nation as a sign that the covenant spans generations and crosses death.
13.20: Succoth... Etham: Succoth (Sukkot = booths/shelters) is probably Tell el-Maskhuta in the eastern Delta; Etham is at the edge of the wilderness (qetze hamidbar = end/border of the wilderness). The itinerary moves from Egyptian territory toward the desert frontier.
13.21: Pillar of cloud (ammud anan) by day... pillar of fire (ammud esh) by night: Ammud = column/pillar; anan = cloud; esh = fire. The double form — cloud (concealment + shelter from desert heat) and fire (warmth + light in darkness) — manifests both the hiddenness and accessibility of the divine presence. Lanchootam (to lead them on the way): Hiphil infinitive of nachah (to guide/lead) — the same verb for the divine leading in Ps 23:3 (yancheini b'ma'aglei tzedeq) and Isa 40:11. YHWH is Israel's shepherd-guide.
13.22: He did not remove (lo-yamish) the pillar: Yamish = Hiphil of mush (to remove/withdraw from a place); the negative verb emphasizes the constancy of the divine presence — day and night (yomam v'lailah) without interruption. The pillar is not intermittent but permanent throughout the wilderness journey, a sustained Shekinah-presence before the tabernacle formalizes the arrangement.

14.1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: The divine instruction frames the crossing not as an emergency improvisation but as a planned trap — YHWH directs the movement of Israel specifically to lure Pharaoh.
14.2: Pi-hahiroth... between Migdol and the sea... opposite Baal Tzephon: Baal Tzephon = Baal of the North; a Canaanite deity whose shrine stood on the coast. The place names are Egyptian/Semitic hybrid; the exact locations are debated. Strategically, the geography creates a cul-de-sac: the sea ahead, mountains on one side, Egyptian territory behind — the perfect setting for the trap.
14.3: Pharaoh will say... they are wandering in confusion (nevukhim) in the land: Navakh = to be confused/perplexed; the wilderness has closed in on them (sagar aleihem hamidbar). God deliberately creates a military situation that reads as Israelite incompetence, drawing Pharaoh's overconfidence. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is accomplished not only internally but by managing circumstances.
14.4: I will harden (achazzeq) Pharaoh's heart: Piel of chazaq (to make strong/hard); distinct from the earlier Qal forms where Pharaoh's own heart hardened. The divine initiative now takes over what Pharaoh had been doing to himself. I will get glory (e'kkaved) over Pharaoh: Niphal of kavad (to be honored/heavy); kavod (glory) derives from this same root meaning heaviness — what is weighty is glorious. The irony: Pharaoh's 'hardness' (chazaq) and YHWH's glory (kavod) operate from opposing directions; Egypt's heaviness becomes YHWH's glory.
14.5: The heart of Pharaoh and his servants was turned (nehehpak): Niphal of haphak (to overturn/reverse) — the same root as Sodom's overthrow (Gen 19:25) and the turning of the plague-blood (Exod 7:17). What have we done that we let Israel go from our service (me'avdenu)? Avad = to serve/work; avadim = servants/slaves; avodah = service/labor/worship. The question reveals that Egypt defined itself by Israel's service — the identity of the empire rested on the enslavement of God's son.
14.6: He made ready his chariot (wayyesor markavto): Asar = to harness/bind; markavah = chariot (from rakav, to ride); the six hundred chosen chariots (shesh-me'ot rekev bachar) — bachar = chosen/elite — represent Pharaoh's rapid-reaction force, his finest military asset.
14.7: Six hundred chosen chariots and all the chariots of Egypt: The number six hundred echoes the six hundred thousand adult males of Israel (12:37); the military equivalence frames the confrontation as a cosmic imbalance — all of Egypt's chariots against YHWH's unarmed escaping slaves.
14.8: YHWH hardened (vay'chazeq) the heart of Pharaoh: The divine hardening is now active and explicit; the phrase b'yad ramah (with a high/lifted hand) describes Israel's exit — ramah = to be high/exalted, the same root as the coming victory song's ga'oh ga'ah (he has triumphed gloriously). Israel marches openly, defiantly, under divine protection.
14.9: The Egyptians pursued (vayirdefu) them: Radaf = to pursue/persecute (the verb of the Psalms' enemies and of Pharaoh's army here and in v.23); horses (susim), chariots (rekev), horsemen (parashim), and army (cheil): the full military vocabulary catalogs the force bearing down on Israel encamped at the sea.
14.10: The people were very afraid (yire'u me'od) and cried out (yitz'aku) to YHWH: Yara = to fear/be in awe; za'aq = to cry out for help (the same verb as Israel's cry in 2:23 that moved YHWH to act — the cry of the oppressed). The wilderness complaint-cycle begins its first iteration here: fear → cry → complaint → Moses's reassurance → divine action.
14.11: Were there no graves in Egypt (ha'mibeli ein q'varim b'mitzrayim)? The bitter irony: Egypt is the world's greatest civilization of the dead — its landscape is dominated by tombs and mortuary monuments. The complaint turns Egypt's obsession with death against the exodus. You have brought us out (hotzetanu) — Hiphil of yatza (to bring out); the very verb of the Exodus creed becomes their accusation.
14.12: Is not this what we said to you in Egypt: 'Leave us alone (chadal mimenu) that we may serve (va'na'avod) the Egyptians'? Chadal = to cease/leave off/desist. The preference for Egyptian slavery over wilderness freedom is the recurring spiritual failure of the Exodus generation. Better to be well-fed slaves than hungry freedmen — the logic that Jesus addresses in John 8:33-36.
14.13: Do not fear (al-tira'u), stand firm (hitzyabu): Hithpael of yatzav (to station oneself, take a stand); the reflexive form emphasizes deliberate action — don't just stop shaking, plant yourself. See the salvation (yeshuah) of YHWH: Yeshuah from yasha (to save/deliver) — the same root as Joshua and Jesus. Moses's command is to watch YHWH do the fighting; the salvation belongs to YHWH, not to military resistance. You shall never see them again: The double negative (lo tosifun lir'otam) is absolute — the enemy that pursues you will be annihilated, not merely repelled.
14.14: YHWH will fight (yilachem) for you, and you only need to be silent (tacharishun): Lacham = to fight/do battle; charash = to be silent, still, to plow (the metaphor of a plowed field — quiet, open, ready to receive). This verse is the Exodus's greatest faith-statement: the battle posture called for is non-resistance. The same theology of divine warfare appears in 2 Chr 20:15-17 (Jehoshaphat) and Isa 30:15 ('in quietness and trust is your strength').
14.15: Why do you cry to me (mah titz'aq elai)? YHWH's surprising rebuke of Moses's prayer; the time for prayer is past — the time for action has come. The intersection of human prayer and divine command: YHWH has already determined to act; Moses's role is to execute, not to petition. Tell the people to go forward (yisa'u): Qal imperfect of nasa (to set out/journey); the same verb used for the camp's movement throughout the wilderness itinerary.
14.16: Lift up your staff (harem et-matte'cha) and stretch out your hand over the sea: The staff (mateh) has been the instrument of every plague sign; now it performs the ultimate sign. The gesture of outstretched hand (natah yad) over water is the signature action of Moses throughout chapters 7-14. The staff connects the Exodus miracles thematically — one instrument, many demonstrations.
14.17: I will harden (m'chazeq) the hearts of the Egyptians: Piel participle — the continuous divine action maintains Pharaoh's pursuit into the trap. And I will get glory (ve'ekkavedah) over Pharaoh and all his host: The kavod/kavad theme reaches its climax — all the weight of the Egyptian military will become the glory of YHWH when the sea swallows it.
14.18: The Egyptians shall know (yede'u) that I am YHWH (ani YHWH): The da'at-YHWH formula (to know YHWH experientially) is the purpose clause of the Exodus as a whole (3:19-20; 6:7; 7:5,17; 8:10; etc.). Egypt's knowledge is not salvific but judicial — they will know YHWH as the one who judges empire. When I have gotten glory (behikaveddi) over Pharaoh: The Niphal infinitive construct — when I am glorified, honored, made heavy with the weight of his destruction.
14.19: The angel of God (malach ha-Elohim) who was going before the host of Israel moved and went behind them: The mal'ach ha-Elohim (angel/messenger of God, compare to mal'ach YHWH earlier) = the divine representative, perhaps identical to the pillar. The tactical shift from vanguard to rearguard is a military repositioning — the divine escort becomes the divine shield, covering the retreat. The same cloud that guides Israel becomes the darkness that blinds Egypt.
14.20: Darkness and cloud to one, light and fire to the other: The same phenomenon serves opposite purposes — judgment and protection from the same divine presence. The pillar is not morally neutral; it discriminates according to the covenantal status of the parties. The wilderness darkness echoes the plague of darkness (10:21-23) where Egypt was in darkness while Israel had light.
14.21: A strong east wind (ruach qadim azzah) all night: Ruach = wind/breath/spirit; qadim = east (the desert wind); azzah = strong. The wind is the instrument but YHWH is the agent — the text carefully distinguishes the natural means (wind) from the supernatural cause (YHWH drove the sea back). Dry land (choravan): From charev (dry/desolate); the sea floor becomes desert. The creation echo: in Gen 1:2 the ruach Elohim (Spirit/wind of God) moves over the waters before dry land (yabbashah) appears; here the ruach YHWH parts the waters and dry land appears again — Exodus as new creation.
14.22: Israel walked on dry ground (yabbashah) through the sea: Yabbashah = dry land (Gen 1:9-10 — the term for dry land at creation). The creation-exodus typology is explicit: Israel passes through the parted waters to new life just as creation emerged when the waters were separated. Waters being a wall (chomah) to their right and left: Chomah = defensive city wall; the waters that threatened now protect, flanking Israel as a fortification.
14.23: The Egyptians pursued and went in after them into the midst of the sea: The Egyptians enter the creation-space Israel has been given — they trespass into YHWH's protected zone. Their confidence (the hardened heart) drives them into the trap.
14.24: In the morning watch (b'ashmuret haboqer) YHWH looked down (vayashkef) through the pillar of fire and cloud: Ashmuret = watch of the night; haboqer = morning (the period from roughly 2am to sunrise); shakaf = to look down from above. He threw into panic (vayaham): Hamam = to throw into confusion/panic (the same verb used for YHWH routing armies in Deut 7:23 and Josh 10:10); the divine gaze is itself a weapon.
14.25: He clogged/bound their chariot wheels (vayaser et-ofan markevotav): Asar = to bind/remove; ofan = wheel; the precise mechanism (wheels coming off or sinking) is less important than the theological point: Egypt's greatest technological advantage — the chariot — is disabled by YHWH. They drove heavily (bichvedut): From kaved (heavy) — the root of kavod (glory); Pharaoh's heavy heart now makes his army drive heavily. The Egyptians recognize their situation: 'YHWH fights for them against Egypt' — the enemy confesses the theology of the Exodus.
14.26: Stretch out your hand over the sea, that the water may come back: The gesture that parted the sea now reverses the division — Moses's hand mediates both the opening and the closing. The created order has been temporarily suspended; now it is restored, but not before its purpose is accomplished.
14.27: The sea returned (vayashov) to its normal flow when morning appeared: Shuv = to return; the sea 'returns' as YHWH intended — the temporary suspension of natural order served its redemptive purpose. YHWH shook out (vayena'er) the Egyptians into the sea: Na'ar = to shake out (as one shakes a garment to empty it); the image is of YHWH as one who shakes the enemies of Israel out of existence the way dust is shaken from a cloak (cf. Job 38:13 — the earth shaken so the wicked are shaken out of it).
14.28: Not one of them remained (lo-nishar bahem ad-echad): The total annihilation formula — every chariot, every horseman, every soldier of Pharaoh's elite force. The army that enslaved Israel for four hundred years is destroyed in a single night. The asymmetry between the means (an east wind, two outstretched hands) and the result (the total destruction of the Egyptian military) defines divine warfare.
14.29: The people of Israel walked on dry ground through the sea: The repetition from v.22 forms a frame around the Egyptian destruction, emphasizing the contrast: the same waters that were walls of protection for Israel were walls of destruction for Egypt.
14.30: Thus YHWH saved (vayyosha') Israel that day: The Hiphil of yasha — the salvation verb; yeshuah (salvation) in the abstract, yasha in the concrete act. Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore (al-sfat hayam): The body count on the beach is the visible evidence of the invisible battle; seeing the dead is the first step toward the climactic faith-statement of v.31.
14.31: Israel saw the great power (yad gedolah — great hand) that YHWH used against Egypt: The yad (hand) motif — the outstretched arm of YHWH — reaches its climax in this verse. They feared YHWH (vayire'u et-YHWH) and believed (vaya'aminu) in YHWH and in his servant Moses: The sequence — seeing → fearing → believing — is the Exodus paradigm for faith formation. Aman = to believe/trust, from the root meaning to be firm/reliable (the root of amen); faith is the response to evidence of YHWH's reliability. The Exodus functions as the founding demonstration of YHWH's trustworthiness, referenced throughout the Psalms and prophets as the ground of all future confidence.

15.1: Then Moses and the people of Israel sang (az yashir Moshe): Az = then (retrospective poetic formula; the perfect tense embedded in an imperfect form signals the performative, celebratory character of the ode; cf. Num 21:17, Josh 10:12). The Song of the Sea (Shirat HaYam) is among the oldest poetry in the Hebrew Bible, exhibiting archaic grammatical features. For he has triumphed gloriously (ki ga'oh ga'ah): The infinitive absolute + Qal perfect of ga'ah for intensification; ga'ah = to rise up, be exalted, triumph — the verb of majesty and pride used for divine victory. The horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea (ramah): Ramah = to throw/hurl; the conciseness of the image (horse + rider + sea) becomes the song's refrain.
15.2: YHWH is my strength and my song (ozi ve'zimrat Yah): The first occurrence of Yah — the short form of YHWH — embedded in Hebrew poetry here and in Ps 118:14 (cited in Isa 12:2). Oz = strength/might; zimrat = song/praise, from zamar (to sing/make music). The phrase is not 'he is my strength and I sing about him' but 'he is my strength and my song' — his power is itself what I sing. He has become my salvation (vayyehi-li lishuah): Yeshuah — the noun that names Joshua and Jesus. This is my God (zeh Eli): The demonstrative force of zeh — not an abstract theological proposition but a finger pointed at the one who just split the sea and drowned the army.
15.3: YHWH is a man of war (ish milchamah): The divine warrior title is explicit and unapologetic; milchamah = war/battle (from lacham = to fight). This is the only place in Scripture where YHWH is directly called a 'man of war'; the image is not domesticated. YHWH is his name (YHWH shemo): The self-identification formula — the victor's name is declared after the victory; the name is proven by the deed.
15.4: Pharaoh's chariots (markevot Par'oh) and his host (ve'cheilo) he cast (yaroh) into the sea: Yaroh = Qal perfect of yarah (to throw/hurl); the same root as Torah (instruction/direction, from the same throwing/pointing gesture). His chosen officers (mivchar shalishav) were sunk (tuba'u) in Yam Suph: Bacha = to choose/select; shalish = officer of the third rank (possibly chariot commander); taba = to sink/drown.
15.5: The floods (tehomot) covered them: Tehomot = deep waters, plural of tehom — the primordial deep of Gen 1:2 (tehom, the abyss over which the Spirit hovered); the waters of chaos/death that YHWH ordered at creation now serve as instruments of judgment on those who opposed the new creation of the Exodus. They went down into the depths (metzulot) like a stone: Metzulot = depths (from tzlul = to sink); the stone image becomes lead in v.10.
15.6: Your right hand (yemincha), O YHWH, glorious in power (ne'dari vakoach): Nedar = to be glorious/distinguished/majestic; the divine right hand (yamin) is the instrument of both creation (Ps 98:1) and destruction. Your right hand shatters (tir'atz) the enemy: Ratatz = to dash to pieces/shatter (the same verb as Ps 2:9 — ‘dash them to pieces like a potter’s vessel’).
15.7: In the greatness of your majesty (berov ga'oncha) you overthrow (teharos) your adversaries: Ga'on = majesty/grandeur, but also pride/arrogance — the word can be used for both divine majesty and human hubris; YHWH's ga'on is what Pharaoh's ga'on was trying to counterfeit. Haras = to pull down, tear down. You send out your fury (charoncha) and it consumes them like stubble (ke'qash): Charon = burning anger (from charah, to burn); the imagery of fire consuming chaff is judgment language used throughout the prophets (Isa 5:24; Nahum 1:10).
15.8: At the blast of your nostrils (beruach appecha): Ruach appecha = the wind/breath of your nostrils — the dual form apayim (nostrils) emphasizes the physical image of YHWH exhaling in anger; the same ruach that moved over the waters in Gen 1:2. The floods stood upright like a heap (netzvu ke'mo-ned): Natzav = to stand erect/upright (Niphal); ned = a heap or mound of water (also used in Josh 3:13,16 for the Jordan's walls at the crossing). The deep waters congealed (qafe'u tehomot): Qapha = to congeal/coagulate (of water freezing or thickening) — the tehom of Gen 1:2, now congealed into solid walls at divine command, anticipates the Jordan crossing and inverts the creation narrative: waters that were separated at creation are re-parted for redemption.
15.9: The enemy said, 'I will pursue (erdof), I will overtake (asig), I will divide the spoil': The enemies' first-person boast is quoted verbatim — five verbs of self-confident action (pursue, overtake, divide, fill, draw sword, destroy). The boast structure is a rhetorical device in ancient Near Eastern poetry; cf. the Assyrian king's boasts in Isa 10:8-14. My desire (nafshi) shall have its fill of them: Nefesh = soul/self/throat/desire; nafshi here = 'my appetite' — the enemy consuming Israel is now consumed by the sea.
15.10: You blew with your wind (nashafta b'ruacha), the sea covered them: Nashaf = to blow; the divine breath/wind that parted the sea now reverses and closes it. They sank like lead (ka'oferet) in the mighty waters (mayim addirim): Oferet = lead (the heaviest common metal); addirim = mighty/majestic (same root as adon = lord). The progression from stone (v.5) to lead (v.10) emphasizes the finality of the sinking.
15.11: Who is like you (mi-kamokha), O YHWH, among the gods (ba'elim)? The incomparability formula — the rhetorical question that functions as the song's theological climax. Ba'elim = among the mighty ones/gods; the song does not deny the existence of other powers but asserts YHWH's incomparable superiority. The name Micah (miykah = who is like YHWH?) derives from this verse. Awesome in glorious deeds (nora tehillot): Nora = awesome/fearsome (Niphal participle of yara = to fear); tehillot = praises/glorious deeds. Doing wonders (oseh-pele'): Pele' = wonder/miracle — the nomen of marvel that defies categorization.
15.12: You stretched out your right hand; the earth swallowed them (tivelam ha'aretz): Bala' = to swallow/engulf; the earth (aretz) now joins the sea as instrument of judgment — the text moves beyond the historical event to its cosmic significance. The victory over Pharaoh is a defeat of the anti-creation forces.
15.13: In your covenant love (bechasdecha) you have led (nachita) the people whom you have redeemed (ga'alta): Hesed = covenant faithfulness/steadfast love; nachah = to lead/guide (the shepherd verb); ga'al = to redeem as kinsman-redeemer (the same term as in 6:6 — YHWH as Israel's go'el). The Exodus is here framed as an act of kinsman-redemption: YHWH acting as the nearest relative who pays the ransom price to free enslaved kin. To your holy abode (neveh qodshecha): Neveh = pasture/abode (the shepherd's dwelling place — the cosmic mountain as YHWH's pasture).
15.14: The peoples have heard (shamu'u), they tremble (yirgazun): Ragaz = to tremble/shake (with both fear and excitement — the ambiguous trembling of theophany). The ripple effect: Egypt's defeat produces existential terror in all surrounding nations. Anguish has seized the inhabitants of Philistia (chil achaz yoshevei Peleshet): Chil = birth pangs/writhing; achaz = to seize/grip; the nations writhe as if in labor — a frequent prophetic metaphor for divine judgment (Isa 13:8; Jer 6:24).
15.15: The chiefs of Edom (alufei Edom) are dismayed (nibhalu); the leaders of Moab, trembling seizes them (yo'chuzan ra'ad): Aluf = chief/thousand-commander (from aleph = ox = strength); bahel = to be terrified/dismayed; ra'ad = trembling. Edom and Moab are the future enemies Israel will encounter on the way to Canaan — their paralysis is predicted here, to be confirmed in Num 22 (Balak of Moab's terror) and the Edom encounter in Num 20.
15.16: Terror and dread fall upon them (tippol aleihem emah va'pachad): Emah = terror (from the root of fear); pachad = dread/sudden fear. Because of the greatness of your arm (bigdol zero'echa) they are still as a stone: Zero'a = arm (the arm of YHWH is a major Exodus image alongside the hand/yad); the nations are petrified — literally stone-still. Till the people pass by whom you have purchased (qanita): Qanah = to acquire/create/possess — the creation verb (Gen 14:19 'Creator/Possessor of heaven and earth' uses qanah). Israel is YHWH's possession in the sense of being his created handiwork.
15.17: You will bring them in and plant them (tebitemo v'tita'emo) on your own mountain (behar nachalotecha): Neta' = to plant; nahalah = inheritance/allotment (the land-distribution term that will structure Joshua). The planting of Israel on the mountain is the agricultural image of settlement. The sanctuary (miqdash) which your hands have established (konenut yadecha): Miqdash = holy place; kun = to establish/prepare. The Song of the Sea anticipates both Sinai (the immediate destination) and Zion (the ultimate destination), with the tabernacle as the mobile version and the Temple as the fixed form.
15.18: YHWH will reign forever and ever (YHWH yimloch le'olam va'ed): Malak = to reign/be king (Qal imperfect — ongoing, permanent reign); olam = age/eternity; va'ed = and beyond/and further. The concluding declaration of the Song: the Exodus has demonstrated YHWH's eternal kingship. This verse is embedded in Jewish liturgy (Aleinu prayer) as the eschatological hope; Revelation's heavenly hymns draw on this declaration (Rev 11:15; 19:6).
15.19: For when the horses of Pharaoh came with his chariots and horsemen into the sea, YHWH brought back the waters of the sea over them: The prose summary (vv.19-21) provides the historical referent for the poetry of vv.1-18, framing the Song as the inspired response to a specific historical event. The prose restates the key facts without embellishment, grounding the poem in history.
15.20: Miriam the prophetess (Miriam hanevi'ah), the sister of Aaron: Miriam is identified as a prophetess (nevi'ah) — the feminine form of navi, one who speaks for God. She is named sister of Aaron (not of Moses), possibly reflecting a tradition that distinguishes her primary association. Took a tambourine (tof) in her hand: Tof = hand-drum/tambourine (the standard instrument of female celebration in ancient Israel — cf. Judg 11:34; 1 Sam 18:6); with dancing (b'machol): machol = round dance (from chul = to whirl/dance). The women's antiphonal response was a recognized form of celebration after military victory.
15.21: Miriam sang to them: 'Sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously': The Miriam Song (v.21) is possibly the oldest literary layer — a two-line refrain that Moses's longer song (vv.1-18) elaborates. The relationship between the two songs (which is earlier?) is debated; the antiphonal structure (Moses's song → Miriam's response) may reflect the liturgical performance tradition of Israel's victory celebrations. Both use the same opening (ki ga'oh ga'ah), suggesting either direct quotation or a shared liturgical formula.
15.22: Moses led Israel onward from Yam Suph (vayyasa' Moshe et-Yisra'el): The Hiphil of nasa' (to cause to journey) — Moses as the human shepherd/leader. Wilderness of Shur (midbar Shur): Shur = wall (possibly the Egyptian border wall, known archaeologically as the 'Wall of the Ruler' or 'Walls of Horus' — the defensive frontier). The wilderness of Shur is the Sinai Peninsula's western desert. Three days without finding water: The three-day journey that tests the new nation immediately after the Song — from triumph to thirst in three days.
15.23: Marah — they could not drink (lo yaklu lishhot) the water because it was bitter (ki marim hem): Marah means bitter; the place is named from the water's quality (aetiological etymology). Mar = bitter; the narrative names the place by the crisis it creates. The bitter-waters test follows immediately upon the crossing, establishing the wilderness complaint pattern: abundance and crisis alternate to develop trust.
15.24: The people grumbled (vayillonu) against Moses: 'What shall we drink?' Lun = to murmur/lodge/complain (the technical wilderness-complaint verb; also used in 16:2, 17:3; Num 14:2; etc.). The complaint is against Moses but addressed to YHWH — the people's anger at the human leader is always implicitly against the divine one who sent him. The question mah nishteh (what shall we drink?) is the first of the wilderness water-complaints.
15.25: He cried to YHWH (vayyitz'aq el-YHWH), and YHWH showed him a log (etz): Za'aq = to cry out (Moses uses the same verb the Israelites used in 2:23 — the leader intercedes using the vocabulary of oppression). Etz = tree/wood/timber — the material is deliberately unspecified; the tradition does not explain what species of tree or how the chemistry works, preserving the miraculous character of the sweetening. The etz thrown into bitter water to make it sweet is a type-pattern rich in later typology: the cross (the etz of crucifixion) makes the bitter waters of existence sweet. There he made for them a statute and a rule (choq umishpat) and there he tested them (nissahu): First occurrence of choq (statute/ordinance) and mishpat (judgment/rule) together — the pair that will structure Sinai legislation. Nissah = to test (the verb of Gen 22:1 — the Aqedah); the wilderness is the testing ground where faith formed at the sea is refined.
15.26: If you will diligently listen (shamo'a tishma') to the voice of YHWH: The doubled infinitive absolute + imperfect = the most emphatic form of the conditional — 'if you will truly/completely listen.' And do what is right in his eyes (hay'yashar b'einav): Yashar = upright/straight/right; the ethical standard is YHWH's sight, not human consensus. I will put none of the diseases (machalah) of Egypt upon you: The diseases of Egypt = the plagues (the ten plagues are now a threat to Israel if they disobey — the covenant has two sides). For I am YHWH your healer (YHWH rofecha): Rapha = to heal; YHWH-Rophe' = YHWH the Healer — the first compound divine name in the post-Exodus narrative. The name reveals YHWH's character through the act: the God who made bitter water sweet is also the God who heals the diseases of Egypt. Healing and obedience are structurally linked.
15.27: Twelve springs of water (shtem esreh einot mayim) and seventy palm trees (shiv'im temarim): Twelve springs (ayin = spring/eye) correspond to the twelve tribes — the abundant provision matches the structure of the community. Seventy palms (temarim = date palms, from tamar = upright/palm) correspond to the seventy souls who went down to Egypt (Gen 46:27) and the seventy elders of Israel. The numbers are too precise to be accidental; Elim is a type of the eschatological rest — twelve wells and seventy trees, enough for each tribe and each elder. They encamped there by the water (al-hamayim): After bitter Marah, abundant Elim — the rhythm of trial and provision that will structure the entire wilderness narrative.

10.1: kaved (kābēd, H3513): 'I have made heavy / hardened his heart.' The plague narrative employs three distinct Hebrew verbs for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart, each with a different shade of meaning: kaved (to be heavy, weighty — used of Pharaoh hardening his own heart in earlier plagues), chazaq (to be strong, firm — used most often when YHWH is the subject), and qashah (to be stiff, stubborn — rare, more extreme). The theological complexity is intentional: Pharaoh's hardness is simultaneously self-willed and divinely purposed. The purpose clause lemaan siti ototi eleh beqirbo ('that I may place these my signs in his midst') frames the entire plague cycle as a divine self-disclosure to Egypt, Israel, and all nations. Paul cites Exod 9:16 and ch. 4's pattern in Rom 9:17-18 to ground the doctrine of divine sovereignty in election.
10.2: ulemaan tesapper beoznei vincha u-ven bincha: 'so that you may tell in the ears of your son and your son's son.' The generational transmission of the Exodus memory is a structural motif in the Torah: the plagues are not only acts of judgment but acts of testimony, performed so that the story can be told. Sippur (telling) is the root of the Passover Seder's Haggadah (telling/narrating). The phrase hit'alalti (I have dealt harshly, or I have made sport) uses a Hithpael reflexive form — YHWH does to Egypt what Egypt did to Israel. The table-turning theme is fundamental to the Exodus narrative's justice logic.
10.3: anah lehathnot lifnai: 'refuse to humble yourself before me.' The Hiphil of ana (to humble, afflict) asks whether Pharaoh will submit to YHWH's sovereign authority. The same root (ana, H6031) is used for the affliction Israel suffered in Egypt (Exod 1:11-12) — YHWH now reverses the dynamic: the one who afflicted Israel is challenged to humble himself before YHWH. The refusal to humble oneself (anah) is the root sin of the Exodus: Pharaoh's ge'ut (pride) against YHWH's kavod (glory). James 4:10 and 1 Pet 5:6 ('Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand') draw on this vocabulary.
10.4: arbeh (ʾarbeh, H697): locusts — from the root rava (to multiply, be numerous). The name itself evokes the swarm's characteristic: countless multitudes. Locusts in the ANE were the most feared agricultural catastrophe — they could strip a region of all vegetation in hours. Egyptian reliefs and texts document locust plagues as national disasters. Joel 1-2 uses the locust army as the primary image for YHWH's eschatological army of judgment. The eighth plague is the longest and most verbally elaborate of the plague accounts, with extensive dialogue between Moses and Pharaoh's officials.
10.5: vichisa et-ein ha'aretz: 'they will cover the eye of the land' — a vivid idiom: ein (eye) here means the visible surface, the appearance. When the locusts cover the earth, the land's face cannot be seen. The same idiom appears in Num 22:5, 11, where the Israelites 'cover the eye of the land.' The completeness of the plague is emphasized: even what the hail (barad, plague 7) left behind will be devoured. The cumulative destruction of seven plagues on Egypt's agriculture is now being completed.
10.6: umale'u vatecha u-vatei kol-avadecha: 'they will fill your palace and the houses of all your officials.' The locust invasion invades even the private dwellings — the inner sanctum of Egyptian society is not protected. Melo (fullness, filling) echoes YHWH's claim in Ps 24:1: 'The earth is YHWH's and its fullness (umelo'ah)' — the locusts, as YHWH's instrument, fill what YHWH owns. The emphasis on something 'your fathers and their fathers never saw since the day they came to this ground' establishes the unprecedented severity: the plagues exceed all historical memory.
10.7: hayehi ha'ish moked lanu: 'Will this man be a snare/trap for us?' The noun moked (H4170) means a trap or snare, from the root meaning to ensnare. Pharaoh's own officials recognize the untenable situation: Egypt is being ruined (avad, perishing) for the sake of Pharaoh's stubbornness. The officials' intervention is the first crack in the Egyptian royal court's solidarity with Pharaoh. Their use of 'do you not yet know that Egypt is destroyed?' (terem teda ki avdah mitzrayim) employs a rhetorical question that functions as statement: Egypt is already ruined.
10.8: mi vaʾmi haholchim: 'who and who are going?' — Pharaoh's interrogative is a negotiating tactic to limit which Israelites will worship. He will allow the men but not the women, children, or property. The exchange exposes the false nature of his offer: the worship of YHWH ('we will hold a festival to YHWH') requires the whole community — uvizqenenu uvina'arenu bvanenu uvivnotenu uvetzoneinu uvivqarenu (with our old and young, sons and daughters, flocks and herds). The pattern of partial concessions (ch. 8, 10) followed by retraction is a literary device showing the progressive hardening.
10.9: bina'arenu u-vizqenenu nelech: 'with our young and old we will go.' Moses's response brooks no compromise: the entire community — every generation and all livestock — must participate in YHWH's festival. The totality requirement is theological: YHWH's worship is not a partial or partial-community activity. This prefigures the NT understanding that the gospel summons whole households (Acts 16:31-34), and that there is 'neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female' in Christ (Gal 3:28) — all are included.
10.10: yihyeh YHWH imachem: 'May YHWH be with you — if I let you and your little ones go!' Pharaoh's sarcastic blessing becomes ironic: YHWH will indeed be with them, precisely by overcoming Pharaoh's opposition. Ra lifneichem ('evil/harm is before you') — Pharaoh accuses Moses of having evil intentions. The word ra (evil, harm) is the same word used in the Tree of Knowledge of good and evil (tov vara) — Pharaoh positions himself as the arbiter of good (staying in Egypt) and evil (going to worship).
10.11: vayegaresh otam meim pnei pharoh: 'and they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence' — the Piel of garash (to drive out, expel) is used: a forcible expulsion from Pharaoh's court. Moses and Aaron are expelled — the same verb used of expulsion from the garden in Gen 3:24 (yigaresh) and used of Israel's later expulsion from Canaan by its enemies. The expulsion from Pharaoh's presence is a bitter irony: Pharaoh drives out the only mediators who could end the plague.
10.12: neteh yadcha al-eretz mitzrayim: 'stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt.' Moses's outstretched staff/hand is the recurring sign-gesture of YHWH's power acting through the human agent. Eight of the ten plagues involve Moses stretching out his staff or hand (exceptions: plagues 1, 5, 6). The gesture embodies the covenant relationship: YHWH acts through human agency, not apart from it. The raised hand of Moses in battle against Amalek (Exod 17:11) follows the same pattern — human gesture as conduit of divine power.
10.13: ruach qadim (east wind): the desert wind blowing from the east (Sinai, Arabia) — hot, dry, powerful, and in the ANE often understood as an ominous wind of divine judgment (cf. Hos 13:15: 'an east wind from YHWH will come'). The east wind drove the locusts overnight; when YHWH reversed it to a ruach yam (sea/west wind, v. 19), the locusts were swept into the Red Sea. YHWH controls both the natural forces and their direction — the plague and its removal are both divine acts.
10.14: lifanav lo hayah khen arbeh: 'before him there was no such locust swarm' — the superlative formula (before never, after never) applied to the eighth plague echoes the same formula in Joel 2:2 ('there has been nothing like it before, nor will be again after it'). Joel's eschatological locust army uses the Exodus eighth plague as its type. The quantity description — kaved meod (very heavy, very severe) — links the plague severity to the vocabulary of the hardening (kaved): both Pharaoh's heart and the plague are described by the same root, as if the natural disaster bears the imprint of the spiritual condition that caused it.
10.15: vayechashach ha'aretz: 'the land became dark' — the Hiphil of chashach (to be/become dark) is used; the locusts blackened the visible surface of the land. Lo nivkah kol-yerek be'etz u-veesev hasadeh ('nothing green remained on the trees or among the grasses of the field') — total agricultural devastation. Egypt's economy was agricultural; the Nile's annual flooding made Egypt the ancient world's breadbasket. Seven plagues targeting crops, livestock, and water systematically dismantled Egypt's food security.
10.16: chataʾti l'YHWH eloheichem v'lachem: 'I have sinned against YHWH your God and against you.' The first explicit verbal confession of sin by Pharaoh — using the standard term chata (to sin, miss the mark, H2398). The dual object (against YHWH and against you) acknowledges both the vertical and horizontal dimensions of wrong. Pharaoh makes similar confessions in 9:27-28 and 10:16, but each time reverts after the plague is removed. The confessions without genuine repentance are a biblical illustration of the difference between remorse and repentance (metanoia) — the same distinction Paul draws in 2 Cor 7:10: 'worldly grief produces death.'
10.17: sa na chataʾti: 'please forgive my sin just this once' — the Qal imperative of nasa (to lift up, carry, forgive) with na (please, particle of entreaty). The idiom nasa chet (lift/carry sin) is the standard Hebrew for forgiveness; YHWH is the one who “lifts up” sin (Exod 34:7; Num 14:18; Ps 32:5). Pharaoh's request uses exactly the right theological vocabulary — but the subsequent verse shows the hardened heart persisting after the plague's removal. Prayer for relief during judgment without transformation afterward is one of the plague narrative's defining themes.
10.18: vayetze meim pharoh vayitpallel el-YHWH: 'he went out from Pharaoh and prayed to YHWH.' Moses's intercession for Pharaoh — despite Pharaoh's persistent wickedness — is a remarkable act of prophetic intercession. Moses functions here as a type of the Christological intercessor: praying for oppressors who continue to resist (Luke 23:34: 'Father, forgive them'). The verb palal (Hithpael: to pray, intercede) becomes the standard term for intercessory prayer throughout the OT.
10.19: ruach yam chazaq meod: 'a very strong west wind' (ruach yam, literally sea-wind). YHWH reverses the wind direction to remove the locusts: what he sent by east wind he removes by west wind. The locusts are cast into the yam suf (Red Sea/Sea of Reeds) — the same body of water Israel will cross and Egypt will drown in. The sea that drowns the locusts will drown Pharaoh's army. YHWH's complete control over the natural world is the implicit theology: storm, wind, sea — all serve as instruments of his judgment and deliverance.
10.20: vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev paroh: 'YHWH strengthened Pharaoh's heart' — the Piel of chazaq (to strengthen, make firm, harden). This is the most common verb used when YHWH is the agent of the hardening. The three-verb hardening vocabulary of the plague narrative (kaved, chazaq, qashah) represents a progression: Pharaoh hardened his own heart first (chs. 7-9); YHWH then confirms and strengthens that hardening as judgment. Paul's use of Exod 9:16 in Rom 9:17 grounds the doctrine that divine sovereignty operates through human choices, not apart from them — God's hardening of Pharaoh is the judicial confirmation of Pharaoh's own self-hardening.
10.21: veyamesh choshech: 'a darkness that may be felt/groped' — a hapax phrase using mamash (to touch, feel). The darkness of the ninth plague is physically tangible: not merely absence of light but a positive, material quality. Exodus Rabbah connects this to the primordial tohu (formlessness) of Gen 1:2: 'the earth was formless and dark (choshech).' YHWH's darkness-plague reverses creation's order — three days of uncreation precede the death of Egypt's firstborn. The number three echoes the three days of creation's light-establishment (Gen 1:3-13). The darkness-to-light pattern is the foundation of Paul's new creation language: 'God who said “let light shine out of darkness” has shone in our hearts' (2 Cor 4:6).
10.22: vayehiy choshech-afelah bechol-eretz mitzrayim shloshet yamim: 'there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt for three days.' The double term choshech afelah (darkness of thick/pitchy darkness) intensifies beyond ordinary darkness into absolute deprivation of light. Egyptians worshiped Ra the sun-god as their supreme deity — three days of total darkness were a direct assault on the supreme Egyptian god. The ninth plague is among the most theologically pointed: YHWH claims dominion over the very light that Egypt considered divine. The parallel darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45: 'darkness over all the land from the sixth to the ninth hour') deliberately evokes this plague — judgment-darkness at the moment of the new covenant Passover.
10.23: ulekhol benei Yisrael hayah or bimoshvotam: 'but all the Israelites had light in their dwelling places.' The light/darkness contrast between Israel and Egypt is a pattern throughout the plagues: YHWH makes a distinction (hafla) between his people and Egypt. The dwelling places (moshvotam) of Israel are illuminated while Egypt gropes. This pattern appears in the Passover night itself (12:23: the destroyer does not enter Israel's blood-marked homes) and ultimately in Revelation's new Jerusalem: 'the city has no need of sun or moon, for the glory of God gives it light' (Rev 21:23).
10.24: rak tzoneichem u-vaqarchem yutztav: 'only your flocks and herds shall be left behind.' Pharaoh's fourth concession offer: go with your families, but leave your animals. Moses refuses because the animals are needed for the sacrifices — ki mimenu nikach la'avod et-YHWH eloheinu ('from them we will take to serve YHWH our God'). The animals belong to the worship of YHWH; to leave them in Egypt is to keep the means of sacrifice captive. Moses's insistence that lo tippalter parsa ('not a hoof shall be left') is a declaration that YHWH's worship requires total mobilization of resources — nothing can remain in bondage.
10.25: gam atah titen beyadenu zevachim ve'olot: 'you yourself must provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings to present to YHWH our God.' Moses turns the tables: not only will Israel take their own livestock, but Pharaoh must contribute to the sacrifices. The demand illustrates the comprehensive reversal: Egypt's wealth will fund Israel's worship of YHWH. This prefigures the spoiling of Egypt (Exod 12:35-36) and the NT pattern of earthly resources being redirected to the service of God's kingdom.
10.26: vegam miqnenenu yelech imanu lo tishar parsa: 'our livestock will also go with us; not a hoof will be left.' The idiom lo tishar parsa (not a hoof shall remain) became a proverbial expression for complete exit/departure. The explanation given — we don't know what YHWH will require until we arrive — is theologically important: worship cannot be planned in advance from a posture of compromise with slavery. Full exodus precedes authentic worship. This anti-negotiation principle runs through the NT: the gospel does not accommodate gradual partial repentance; it calls for complete reorientation.
10.27: vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev paroh: The tenth hardening reference in the plague narrative. The alternation between self-hardening (Pharaoh's own choice) and divine hardening (YHWH confirming the pattern) is now nearing its climax: the next plague will be decisive. The repeated hardening-and-reprieve cycle illustrates what Hebrews warns against: the danger of continued exposure to divine truth producing not softening but increased hardness (Heb 3:7-8: 'Today if you hear his voice do not harden your hearts'; 3:13: 'hardened through the deceitfulness of sin').
10.28: hishamer lecha al-tosef reot panai: 'guard yourself, do not see my face again.' The Niphal reflexive/passive of shamar (to guard, keep) used reflexively: 'guard yourself.' Pharaoh's threat carries irony: he is the one who should guard himself against YHWH. The prohibition of seeing Moses's face mirrors the earlier encounter where Moses could not see YHWH's face (Exod 33:20) — but the roles are reversed: a mortal potentate threatens the prophet of YHWH. Pharaoh's final rejection of Moses will be met by the final plague — the death of the firstborn.
10.29: ken dibarta: 'you have spoken correctly/rightly.' Moses affirms Pharaoh's statement — he will indeed never see Pharaoh's face again. The Hebrew ken (thus, rightly) is emphatic. Moses's departure is a prophetic symbolic act: the prophet's withdrawal from the king signals the withdrawal of divine grace and the inauguration of the final judgment. This scene closes the plague-cycle's dialogue phase; the tenth plague will require no further negotiation.

11.1: nega echad od: 'one plague more' — literally 'one stroke/blow still.' The word nega (H5061, plague, blow, stroke) is used throughout Leviticus for skin diseases but here describes the final divine blow. The phrase acharei ken ('after that') signals the definitive end: after this plague Pharaoh will not merely let Israel go but drive them out (garash) — the expulsion will be total and urgent. YHWH's control over the timing ('I will bring') is emphasized: the final plague waits on YHWH's initiative, not Pharaoh's decision.
11.2: veyishalu ish meyit reyahu veisha meyit reutah keli-keseph u-chelei zahav: 'each man shall ask of his neighbor and each woman of her neighbor articles of silver and gold.' The verb sha'al (H7592, to ask, borrow, request) is used — the same verb behind the Sheol wordplay in OT (sheol = place of asking/seeking?) and behind Samuel's name. Israel is instructed to ask/borrow the Egyptians' valuables. Exod 12:36 will describe the fulfillment: they “plundered” (vayenatzlu, Piel of natsal, to strip/plunder) Egypt. The plundering of Egypt was the original fulfillment of YHWH's promise to Abraham that Israel would leave with 'great possessions' (Gen 15:14).
11.3: vayiten YHWH et-chen ha'am beynei Mitzrayim: 'YHWH gave the people favor in the eyes of the Egyptians.' The noun chen (H2580, favor, grace, charm) — YHWH sovereignly disposed the Egyptians to view the Israelites favorably, so that they would willingly give their valuables. The same chen vocabulary describes Joseph's favor in Egypt (Gen 39:4), Moses himself before Pharaoh (Exod 11:3: gadol meod beynei avdei pharoh u-veynei ha'am), and ultimately God's favor on believers in the NT (Luke 1:30; Acts 2:47). Divine favor is not earned by the recipient but given by YHWH to accomplish his purposes.
11.4: kachatzot halaylah: 'about midnight' — the time of maximum darkness and vulnerability. The phrase kachatzot uses the preposition ke- (like, approximately) — either “about midnight” or a divine approximation. Rabbinic tradition debated whether this was exactly midnight (YHWH's clock is precise) or approximately (human calculation is imprecise). The midnight setting is theologically rich: the darkest hour of the darkest plague is YHWH's appointed time. The Passover night's midnight becomes the paradigm for the eschatological night-watch: 'the bridegroom comes at midnight' (Matt 25:6).
11.5: kol bekhor beeretz mitzrayim yamut: 'every firstborn in Egypt shall die.' The scope is total: from bekhor par'oh hayoshev al-kis'o (the firstborn of Pharaoh on his throne) to bekhor hashifchah asher achar harechayim (the firstborn of the slave-girl behind the millstones) to the firstborn of animals. The tenth plague targets the bekhor (firstborn, H1060) — the one who bears the legal primogeniture rights and the one who carries the family line. Egypt's future, from throne to stable, is struck. The logic: Egypt enslaved Israel, YHWH's firstborn son (Exod 4:22-23: “Israel is my son, my firstborn; let my son go”), so YHWH strikes Egypt's firstborn.
11.6: vehayetah tzeaqah gedolah bechol-eretz mitzrayim: 'there will be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt.' The word tzeaqah (H6818, cry, outcry) is used for the cry of the oppressed: it is the tzeaqat Sodom that rose to YHWH (Gen 18:20), and it is the cry of Israel under slavery that YHWH heard (Exod 3:7: vaeshma et-tzaakatam). The same cry Israel made under oppression is now reversed and falls on Egypt. The tzeaqah motif in the OT establishes that YHWH hears the cry of the oppressed — and his justice eventually turns oppressor and oppressed.
11.7: ul'chol benei Yisrael lo yecheratz kelev leshono: 'but to all the Israelites not even a dog shall move its tongue.' The idiom lo yecheratz kelev leshono ('no dog will sharpen/whet its tongue') describes complete silence and calm among Israel during the plague. The contrast is stark: Egypt's tzeaqah gedolah (great cry) vs. Israel's silence. Yafle YHWH ('YHWH will work wonders/distinctions') — the verb palah (H6395, to distinguish, separate, make wonderful) is the key term for YHWH's covenant distinction between Israel and Egypt throughout the plagues. The same distinction that protected Israel from the plagues now preserves them through the final judgment.
11.8: veyardu kol-avadecha eleh elai vehishtachavu-li: 'and all these servants of yours will come down to me and bow down to me.' Moses's prophecy is precise: Pharaoh himself will not bow, but his officials will come begging. Bechemah gedolah ('in great anger') — Moses's departure from Pharaoh's presence in great anger (af, H639, anger, nose) is the only place in the plague narrative where Moses's emotional state is described. The prophet's righteous anger at Pharaoh's persistent hardness mirrors divine anger (cf. Mark 3:5: Jesus looked at the Pharisees with anger at their hardness of heart).
11.9: lema'an revot moftai beEretz Mitzrayim: 'in order that my wonders may be multiplied in Egypt.' The purpose (lema'an) clause here provides the theological framework for the entire plague cycle: the plagues are not only punitive but revelatory. The multiplication (revot, Qal infinitive of ravah) of wonders serves divine self-disclosure. Moftim (H4159, wonders, signs) is paired with otot (signs) throughout the Exodus narrative (7:3; 11:9-10) to describe the plagues as the definitive divine testimony to YHWH's incomparable power. The NT uses the same semeia kai terata (signs and wonders) vocabulary for Jesus's miracles and the apostolic ministry (Acts 2:43; 5:12; Heb 2:4).
11.10: vaMoshe veAharon asu et-kol-hamoftim haeleh lifnei par'oh: 'Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh.' This verse summarizes the entire plague narrative (chs. 7-11) before the Passover account. The repeated summary also marks the transition: the moftim performed before Pharaoh are now complete; what follows is the pivotal final act. Vayechazzeq YHWH et-lev par'oh — the final hardening before the final plague. The summary formula has appeared ten times through the plague narrative; this last occurrence closes the loop and prepares for the Passover night.

7.1: netatikha elohim le-Paroh (nĕtattîḵā ʾĕlōhîm lĕpharʿōh): "I have made you like God to Pharaoh." Elohim here does not mean divine nature but authority-figure or judge — the same usage as Exod 21:6 ("bring him before elohim/judges") and Ps 82:1,6. Moses will function as the divine court's authorized representative before Pharaoh's court. Aaron is his navi (prophet) — the same relational structure as God-and-prophet.
7.2: dibber (dabbēr): imperative of davar. The chain of speech — LORD to Moses, Moses to Aaron, Aaron to Pharaoh — is the model of prophetic delegation. The ultimate source of the word is YHWH; the transmission is through two human intermediaries. "Speak everything I command you" emphasizes that no editorial selection is permitted; the word must reach Pharaoh unfiltered.
7.3: va'ani aqsheh et-lev Paroh (waʾănî ʾaqšeh ʾet-lēb parʿōh): "But I will harden Pharaoh's heart." The verb here is qashah (H7185) — to make stiff, stubborn (Deut 2:30; Isa 48:4). This is the first of three distinct hardening verbs: qashah (divine prospective declaration, 7:3), kaved (heavy, dull — Pharaoh's own tendency, 7:14; 8:15,32; 9:7,34), and chazaq (strong, firm — 7:13,22; explicitly God's action from 9:12). The MKT does not flatten these to a single English word.
7.4: tsivotai (ṣibĕʾōtay): "my hosts/armies." The exodus is framed militarily: Israel departs not as refugees but as an army (with tsava, military host — 12:41). Bi-yad ramah ("with an upraised hand," 14:8; Num 33:3) — the gesture of triumphant military procession. The plague sequence is the campaign; the sea crossing is the battle.
7.5: veyadu Mitsrayim ki ani YHWH: "Then the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD." The recognition formula — ki ani YHWH — is the theological purpose of the plague sequence. The plagues are not primarily punitive but revelatory: YHWH is revealing his identity to Egypt. The formula occurs throughout Ezekiel in the same function (Ezek 6:7,13; 7:4 etc.) as the purpose clause of divine action in history.
7.6: kaasher tsivah YHWH otam: "just as the LORD had commanded them." The compliance formula — used at 7:10,20; 12:28,50; 39:43; 40:19,21 etc. — emphasizes that the plagues are executed according to a precise divine script. Moses and Aaron's obedience is total and unvarying. The phrase functions as a refrain marking the faithful execution of divine commands.
7.7: ben-shemonim shanah Mosheh: "Moses was eighty years old." The age notice is unusual — ages at commission are rarely recorded. At 80, Moses is at a stage traditionally beyond active leadership in ANE culture. The advanced ages (80 and 83) emphasize that this calling is not about human vitality or natural aptitude; the power will be entirely YHWH's.
7.8: The section vv.8-13 introduces the staff-sign (mofet) before the plagues begin. The structure follows the plague narrative pattern: divine command → execution → Pharaoh's response → hardening statement. This pattern, repeated with variations across chapters 7-12, is the literary architecture of the plague sequence.
7.9: tenah lachem mofet: "Show us a wonder/portent." Mofet (H4159) — wonder, portent, prodigy — is distinct from ot (sign, H226). An ot is a symbolic marker; a mofet is a powerful demonstration. Together they form the technical term for the plague sequence (otot umofetim, "signs and wonders," 7:3). Pharaoh is demanding a demonstration of power, which he gets — but the snake-swallowing points to the ultimate swallowing of his own forces.
7.10: matteh (H4294): staff, rod — the instrument of divine power throughout the plague narrative and the sea crossing. The same word means "tribe" when used of Israel's divisions (Gen 49:28). Aaron's staff is used for most plague signs; Moses's staff is reserved for the decisive actions at the sea (14:16). The distinction may indicate that Aaron's priestly role encompasses the mediatorial plague-signs.
7.11: chakhamim umekhashfim: "wise men and sorcerers." Two terms: chakhamim (wise men, occult specialists) and mekhashfim (sorcerers, from kashaph, to practice witchcraft — Lev 20:27; Deut 18:10). 2 Tim 3:8 names two of them: Jannes and Jambres — names from Jewish tradition (cf. CD 5:17-19; Targum Ps-Jonathan) not in the Hebrew text. Their power is real but subordinate and limited.
7.12: vativel matteh Aharon et-matetam: "Aaron's staff swallowed (bala) up their staffs." Bala (H1104) — to swallow, engulf. The same verb is used at the sea when the deep swallows Pharaoh's army (15:12: "the earth swallowed them"). The preview is precise: the staff that swallows the Egyptian staffs here anticipates the sea that swallows the Egyptian army later. The instrument of YHWH's power consumes the instruments of Egyptian power.
7.13: vayechezak lev Paroh: "Pharaoh's heart was hardened (chazaq)." The first occurrence of chazaq (H2388) — to be/become strong, firm, resolute — in the hardening sequence. The passive construction ("was hardened") leaves the agent ambiguous. Contrast v.14 where kaved is used. The three verbs mark different aspects of the hardening: qashah = stubborn stiffening (God's declaration); kaved = heavy/dull internal state (Pharaoh's own); chazaq = strong resolve (ambiguous/divine from 9:12).
7.14: kaved lev Paroh: "Pharaoh's heart is heavy (kaved, H3515)." Kaved — heavy, dull, unresponsive; the same root as kavod (glory, weight). "Heaviness of heart" implies internal dullness and unresponsiveness rather than external compulsion. In the early plagues, kaved consistently describes Pharaoh's own heart-state (7:14; 8:15,32; 9:7,34) — Pharaoh is making himself heavy toward YHWH's word.
7.15: hinennu yotsee hamaymah: "Watch for him as he goes out to the river." Pharaoh's morning visit to the Nile is likely a ritual act — the Nile was sacred to Egypt (associated with the inundation deity Hapy). The confrontation at the sacred river is a deliberate choice: YHWH challenges Pharaoh in his own religious space. The "staff... turned into a snake" in Moses's hand is the same sign as 4:3 — the credentials remain the same.
7.16: shalach et-ami veyaavduni: "Let my people go, so that they may serve/worship me." The demand exploits a Hebrew wordplay: avad (H5647) means both to work/serve (as slaves) and to worship/serve (as devotees). The Israelites are currently ovdim for Pharaoh; the demand is that they become ovdim for YHWH. The entire exodus is a transfer of the object of avad — whose servants shall Israel be?
7.17: bezu tedah ki ani YHWH: "By this you will know that I am the LORD." The recognition formula (ki ani YHWH) introduces each plague-cycle: the plague is the sign whose purpose is knowledge of YHWH's identity. The Nile strike is a direct assault on Egyptian religious identity — the Nile is both the source of life and the focus of Egyptian worship. Striking it is striking Egypt's gods.
7.18: dam (H1818): blood. The Nile turning to "blood" is stated without qualification, though the magicians replicate it (v.22) and Egyptians can dig for seepage water (v.24), suggesting the water becomes undrinkable rather than literally becoming human blood. The Egyptian term for the Nile flood is Hapy, the red/fertile inundation — the wordplay on blood and the Nile's red flood season may be implicit in the sign.
7.19: al-mishneihem: "over all their waters." The command extends the blood-water to "rivers, canals, ponds, and all pools of water" — not just the Nile but every water body. "Vessels of wood and vessels of stone" — stored water is also affected. The total scope makes the plague comprehensive, though Exod 8:24 (where Egyptians can dig for water) suggests some sources remain accessible by effort.
7.20: leeinei Paroh veleeinei avadav: "in full view of Pharaoh and his officials." The public, witnessed nature of the plagues is theologically significant: these are not private miracles but performed before the maximum possible audience. The exodus narrative is constructed as a public theological drama witnessing YHWH's power to the entire Egyptian power structure.
7.21: vayivrash hanahar: "the river smelled." The death of fish and the stench of the Nile represent the undoing of Egypt's greatest natural blessing. The Nile's inability to sustain its fish (the most abundant protein source in Egypt) and the stench that follows are the signature of divine judgment against Egypt's central deity-resource.
7.22: belat'eihem: "by their secret arts." The acknowledgment that the Egyptian magicians possess real operative power is theologically careful: the text does not demythologize their abilities but demonstrates that YHWH's power exceeds them at every point until it doesn't (8:18-19). The graduated failure of the magicians — replicate blood, replicate frogs, fail at gnats — is the narrative's structured argument for YHWH's incomparable power.
7.23: velo sham libbo gam lezot: "paying no attention to this either." Literally "he did not set his heart on this" — lev (heart) is the organ of attention, decision, and will. Pharaoh's heart-failure is both described internally (heaviness, hardening) and externally (turning away, not attending). The biblical psychology of heart-orientation is being traced through the narrative.
7.24: vayachperu kol-Mitsrayim: "All the Egyptians dug." The practical response — digging for seepage water along the banks — demonstrates both the plague's reality (the Nile itself is compromised) and the resilience of Egyptian civilization. The irony: the nation whose slaves dug canals must now dig for their own drinking water, while their ruler refuses to relent.
7.25: shivat yamim: "Seven days passed." The seven-day interval marks a complete period before the next plague. Whether literally seven days or a rounded period, it emphasizes that YHWH does not accelerate the judgment beyond the pace he has set. The deliberateness of the plague sequence — each beginning after the previous is fully established — is part of the revelatory design.

8.1: bo el-Paroh: "Go to Pharaoh." The second plague cycle begins. The frog plague has Egyptian religious dimensions: frogs were associated with Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of fertility and midwifery. The plague inverts this: the emblem of fertility becomes an infestation of the most intimate spaces.
8.2: negof et-kol-gevulcha batsfardeiyim: "I will plague your whole country with frogs." Nagaf (H5062) — to strike, plague; related to nega (H5061, plague, stroke). The Hiphil participle of shalach and the noun makka (blow, plague, H4347) together are the plague narrative's vocabulary of divine striking. The frogs' reach is total: palace, bedroom, bed, kitchen, court.
8.3: uvemishtekha uvethanurekha: "your kneading bowls, your ovens." The invasion reaches the most intimate domestic spaces of food preparation. The inversion is complete: the Nile's fertility blessing, which made Egypt's agriculture possible, now desecrates the food supply itself. The frogs in the kneading bowls render food preparation impossible.
8.4: uvecha uvaamcha uvchol-avadecha: "over you, your people, and all your officials." The hierarchy addressed — king, people, officials — frames the plague as touching the complete social order. This is consistent across the plagues: the divine judgment is not class-specific but touches Egypt's entire social structure from the top down.
8.5: neteh et-yadcha bemateikha: "Stretch out your hand with your staff." The matteh (staff) and natah yad (stretch out the hand) form the plague-initiation formula. The same gesture initiates the sea crossing (14:16,21,26,27), creating a structural link between the plague-hand and the sea-crossing-hand. YHWH's outstretched hand is the consistent instrument of both judgment and salvation.
8.6: vataal hatsfardeiyah: "Frogs came up (alah)." Alah (H5927) — to go up, ascend. The frogs "go up" from the Nile over the land — using the verb for ascent that will later describe Israel's "going up" out of Egypt (13:18; Num 20:5). The frogs' ascending infestation is a counter-image to Israel's ascending departure.
8.7: vaya'asu khen hachartumaim belat'eihem: "The magicians did the same." The magicians replicate the plague by adding more frogs — but cannot remove the infestation they have added to. Their power extends to multiplication of the problem but not to its resolution. This limitation will be fully exposed at gnats (8:18): they can bring life but cannot uncreate what YHWH has made.
8.8: hatiru el-YHWH: "Pray to the LORD." Athar (H6279) — to pray, entreat; used for intercessory prayer (Gen 25:21; Num 21:7; Judg 13:8). Pharaoh's first request for intercession recognizes Moses's access to YHWH as something Pharaoh himself lacks. The intercessor who prays on behalf of the enemy who enslaved his people is the first of many such mediatorial paradoxes in Moses's career.
8.9: hitpa'er alai: "I leave to you the honor of setting the time." Literally "glorify yourself over me" — Moses offers Pharaoh the rhetorical advantage of choosing the schedule. The move is strategically significant: if Pharaoh sets the time and the frogs disappear precisely at that time, the timed fulfillment becomes an undeniable demonstration of YHWH's control. Moses is engineering an epistemological proof, not merely performing a service.
8.10: lemaan tedah ki-ein kYHWH Eloheinu: "so that you may know there is no one like the LORD our God." The incomparability formula — ein kamoni ("there is none like me," 9:14) / ein kYHWH ("none like the LORD") — is the theological claim the plagues are designed to demonstrate. Each plague is an argument; the argument is the uniqueness and incomparability of YHWH.
8.11: rak banehar tish'arna: "they will remain only in the Nile." The frogs' removal to the Nile — the source from which they came — is the reversal of their coming. The Nile, the focus of Egypt's worship and the origin of the plague, receives them back. The divine power that deploys from the Nile also retracts to the Nile.
8.12: vayitsa Mosheh... vayitsar: "Moses cried out (tsaaq, H6817) to the LORD." Tsaaq is the cry of distress and urgent appeal — the same verb used for Israel's cry under bondage (2:23; 3:7,9). Moses uses the language of oppressed Israel in his prayer on behalf of oppressive Egypt. The intercession that uses the vocabulary of the victim's cry is a profound irony.
8.13: vaya'as YHWH kidvar Mosheh: "The LORD did what Moses asked." The compliance formula applied to YHWH's response to Moses's prayer mirrors the compliance formula applied to Moses's response to YHWH's commands (7:6,10,20). YHWH and Moses are in a reciprocal relationship of command-and-compliance that defines the partnership of the plague narrative.
8.14: chamarim chamarim: "great heaps." Chomer (H2563) — heap, pile. The dead frogs piled into heaps recall the piled corpses of Pharaoh's army at the sea (14:30). "The land reeked" (tivash haarets) — the same root bo'esh as the Nile's stench in 7:18,21. The stench of each plague gives way to the stench of the next; Egypt accumulates evidence of its refusal.
8.15: hakhbeid et-libbo velo shama: "he hardened his heart (kaved) and would not listen." Here kaved (H3515) is used with Pharaoh explicitly as the subject: "he made his heart heavy." The MT's reading places the hardening initiative squarely with Pharaoh at the moment of relief. The pattern is: crisis → temporary compliance → relief → self-hardening → refusal. Pharaoh hardens when the pressure is off.
8.16: hach et-afar haarets: "Strike the dust of the ground." The third plague uses afar (dust, H6083) — the material of the earth, specifically of dry land. The plagues have moved from water (Nile blood) to water-plus-earth (frogs from the Nile) to earth itself (dust to gnats). The no-warning feature (unlike plagues 1 and 2) is the defining characteristic of each triad's third plague.
8.17: kinnim (H3654): the precise insect is disputed: gnats, mosquitoes, or lice (LXX skniphes, Josephus phtheires/lice). The attack on the dust-of-the-ground reverses the creation of humanity from dust (Gen 2:7): the dust that was the raw material of God's creation-blessing becomes the raw material of divine judgment on Egypt's civilization.
8.18: vaya'asu khen hachartumaim lehotsi et-hakinim veloh yakholu: "The magicians tried to produce gnats but they could not." The first absolute failure of Egyptian magic. The inability to produce gnats from dust — the very material of creation — marks the boundary of occult power. What YHWH makes from earth, no human art can replicate. The magicians' progressive failure: replicate (blood, 7:22), replicate (frogs, 8:7), fail (gnats, 8:18).
8.19: etsba Elohim hi: "This is the finger of God!" The magicians' theological interpretation is the pivot of the first six plagues. Etsba Elohim (H676 + H430) — finger of God. This is the same phrase Jesus uses in Luke 11:20 to interpret his exorcisms: "if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you." The Exodus phrase becomes the christological phrase for the in-breaking of God's kingdom through Jesus's ministry.
8.20: hashkem baboker: "Get up early in the morning." The morning confrontation at the water (cf. 7:15) suggests YHWH knows Pharaoh's routine. The fourth plague (flies, arob) opens the second triad (flies, livestock, boils), which introduces the Goshen distinction — escalating the theological drama from demonstrated power to selective protection.
8.21: arob (H6157): swarms, literally "mixture" — the exact insect is debated (LXX kynomuia, dog-flies; Ps 78:45 "swarms"). The comprehensive impact ("the houses of Egypt were ruined, and also the ground on which they stood") uses shachat (to ruin, corrupt, destroy) — the same verb as the flood narrative's "the earth was corrupt" (Gen 6:11,12). The plague-vocabulary deliberately echoes the primeval destruction.
8.22: vehafleti et-erets Goshen: "I will set apart (pala) the land of Goshen." Pala (H6395, Hiphil) — to make wonderful, to distinguish, to treat as exceptional. The rare verb (also 9:4; 11:7) conveys that YHWH's protection of Goshen is not a natural phenomenon but a miraculous differentiation. "By this you will know that I, the LORD, am in the midst of this land" — YHWH's presence is argued from the geographic precision of his protection.
8.23: pedut (H6304): distinction, separation — possibly from padah (to redeem, H6299). If the root is redemptive, the "distinction" YHWH places between his people and Pharaoh's people is simultaneously a redemptive act. The ambiguity may be deliberate: the "distinction" in the plagues anticipates the "redemption" at the sea (6:6: "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm").
8.24: kabed arob bayit Paroh: "Dense (kaved) swarms of flies poured into Pharaoh's palace." The plague itself is kaved (heavy) — the same adjective used of Pharaoh's heavy heart (7:14; 8:15,32; 9:7). The heavy-hearted Pharaoh receives a heavy plague; the descriptor mirrors the disposition.
8.25: lechu zivchu lEloheichem baarets: "Go, sacrifice to your God here in the land." Pharaoh's first compromise: in-country sacrifice. Moses rejects it: the problem is not merely distance but the nature of the sacrifice. Sacrificing cattle (sacred to Egypt) in Egyptian territory would provoke Egyptians to violence; the three-day journey into the wilderness is specifically required.
8.26: to'avat Mitsrayim: "detestable to the Egyptians." To'evah (H8441) — abomination, detestable thing. The same word used throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy for practices incompatible with Israel's covenantal identity. Here applied inversely: Israel's sacrifice is an abomination to Egypt. The clash of incompatible religious systems is the theological backdrop to the exodus's necessity.
8.27: derekh shloshes yamim: "three-day journey." The original demand (3:18; 5:3) maintained throughout the negotiations. Each of Pharaoh's compromises attempts to reduce the scope of the departure — in-country sacrifice (v.25), don't go too far (v.28) — without meeting YHWH's actual requirement. The total and unconditional departure is non-negotiable.
8.28: rak harbek lo tirchiku laleches: "but you must not go very far." Pharaoh's second compromise imposes a distance restriction on a departure he is ostensibly granting. The attempt to limit the scope of Israel's service to YHWH is the structural equivalent of every partial obedience — the departure without the departure.
8.29: hoel Paroh hotoel: "let Pharaoh not continue to deceive (tal tal)." The verb tal (H8591) — to mock, deceive, make a fool of — is Moses's first explicit accusation of Pharaoh's bad faith. Despite accepting Pharaoh's terms, Moses recognizes and names the pattern: Pharaoh is playing for temporary relief, not intending genuine compliance. The accusation proves accurate (v.32).
8.30: vayitaar el-YHWH: "prayed to the LORD." The intercessory movement — leave the human court, approach the divine court — is Moses's characteristic pattern throughout the plague narrative. His departure from Pharaoh's presence is the signal that intercession begins. The prayer's content is not recorded; the result speaks for it.
8.31: lo nishar echad: "not a single one remained." Total removal of the flies — complete, not partial. The comprehensive fulfillment of Moses's stated terms makes Pharaoh's subsequent hardening all the more stark: the proof was complete; the refusal that follows is therefore a fully informed rejection of YHWH's power.
8.32: vayakhbeid Paroh et-libbo gam bapa'am hazot: "Pharaoh hardened his heart even this time." Kaved (H3515) with Pharaoh as subject — the final such occurrence in the plague narrative. "Even this time" (gam bapa'am hazot) marks the cumulative character of each refusal: every pa'am (time, occasion) carries the weight of all previous occasions.

9.1: Elohi ha'Ivrim: "the God of the Hebrews." The full covenant title — "the LORD, the God of the Hebrews" — appears in each plague-introduction (7:16; 8:1,20; 9:1,13; 10:3). YHWH is not merely a deity but the God specifically of this people, whose identity is tied to their release. The covenant name establishes that the plague is not merely a power demonstration but a covenant action on behalf of a specific people.
9.2: veodkha mechazik bam: "and continue to hold them." Chazaq (H2388, Hiphil) with Pharaoh as subject: "if you continue making yourself strong/firm in holding them." The hardening vocabulary (chazaq) and the grip vocabulary (chazaq) are the same word: Pharaoh's hard heart is his hard grip on Israel. The theological connection is explicit in the Hebrew.
9.3: yad YHWH hovah: "the hand of the LORD will be." Yad YHWH — the hand of the LORD — is the central metaphor of Exodus: the mighty hand that delivers (13:9; 14:31; 15:6). Here applied as plague-instrument: the same divine hand that will deliver Israel through the sea first strikes Egypt's livestock. Dever (H1698, pestilence) — Hab 3:5 personifies it marching before the divine warrior.
9.4: veheflah YHWH bein miqneh Yisrael: "the LORD will distinguish (pala) between Israel's livestock and Egypt's." Pala (H6395) for the second time (cf. 8:22). The remarkable precision of YHWH's protection — biological and geographic specificity — is the strongest single evidence-claim the plagues make: if every Egyptian field animal dies while not one Israelite animal dies, the distinction is supernatural.
9.5: vayasem YHWH moed: "the LORD set a time (moed)." Moed (H4150) — appointed time, set meeting; the vocabulary of sacred assembly (Lev 23). The use of moed for the plague's appointed moment carries liturgical weight: even the judgment is ordered by divine appointment, as sacred in its timing as Israel's own festival calendar.
9.6: lo met mimikneh venei Yisrael echad: "not one of the Israelites' animals had died." Total fulfillment of the distinction: every Egyptian field animal dead, zero Israelite animals dead. The precision creates an empirically testable claim (Pharaoh sends inspectors, v.7). YHWH's word is verifiable by the evidence it generates.
9.7: vayikhbad lev Paroh: "Pharaoh's heart was heavy (kaved)." The inspectors' report confirms the miracle; Pharaoh receives verifiable proof and still hardens. The inverse relationship between evidence and responsiveness in Pharaoh — the more complete the proof, the heavier the heart — is the paradox the NT addresses in its hardening theology (John 12:40 quoting Isa 6:9-10; Rom 9:17-18).
9.8: piach kivshaim: "furnace soot (piach, H6368)." The kivshan (furnace, H3536) — the kiln used for brickmaking, the site of Israel's slave labor (cf. Gen 19:28 for kivshan; implied throughout Exod 1:14; 5). The sixth plague uses the physical material of Israelite oppression as the raw material of Egypt's judgment. The instrument of the slaves' suffering becomes the instrument of the slaveholders' affliction.
9.9: shechein poreiach avubot: "festering boils (avubot)." Shechein (H7822) — inflammation, boil; used of Job's affliction (Job 2:7), the Egyptian boils of Deut 28:27 as a covenant curse, and the plague of Rev 16:2. The sixth plague enters the human body itself — unlike the previous environmental and animal plagues. The escalation from external to bodily affliction marks the second triad's culmination.
9.10: vayiz'reihu Mosheh hashamayimah: "Moses threw it into the air (toward the sky)." The heavenward gesture — throwing soot upward, toward the divine domain — and the dust falling on all Egypt below creates a visual image of divine judgment descending from heaven. Plague descends from above; salvation (the pillar of cloud/fire) also descends from above. The vertical axis is the theological axis of Exodus.
9.11: velo yakhlu hachartumaim la'amod lifnei Mosheh: "The magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils." Their incapacitation by the very plague they helped multiply is ironic: they brought up more frogs (8:7) and more gnats (8:18, tried unsuccessfully) — now they cannot even present themselves in the contest. "Unable to stand" (amad) is the language of defeat in judicial or military contest. The Egyptian occult system is permanently incapacitated.
9.12: vayechazek YHWH et-lev Paroh: "But the LORD hardened (chazaq) Pharaoh's heart." The theological turning point: from 9:12 onward, YHWH is explicitly the subject of the hardening (9:12; 10:1,20,27; 11:10; 14:4,8). The shift in agency does not mean God begins overriding Pharaoh's free will arbitrarily; it means Pharaoh's pattern of self-hardening through repeated choices has reached the threshold where God ratifies and seals what Pharaoh has already determined to be. Romans 1:24,26,28 uses the same pattern: "God gave them over" — divine confirmation of human choice.
9.13: hashkem baboker vehityatsev lifnei Paroh: "Get up early in the morning and stand before Pharaoh." The seventh plague (hail) begins the third triad. "This is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says" — the covenant title occurs five times in the plague introductions. The hail is the most severe plague so far announced in advance; the announcement itself is part of the argument: YHWH announces precisely what he will do.
9.14: ki bapa'am hazot ani sholeiach et-kol-magefotai: "For this time I will send all my plagues against you personally." El-libkha — against your heart, i.e., against your very person.
