The escalation is explicit: previous plagues targeted the environment, animals, and servants; this one is directed at Pharaoh himself. "There is no one like me in all the earth" — the incomparability formula now claims universal scope, not merely supremacy over Egyptian deities.
9.15: ki atah shalakhti et-yadi vakek otekha: "For by now I could have stretched out my hand and struck you." The past potential — what God could have done already — establishes the restraint that has characterized the plague sequence. The plagues have been graduated, not maximal. YHWH's restraint is purposeful (v.16), not a limitation of power.
9.16: he'emadtikha (heʿĕmadtîḵā): "I have raised you up/maintained you." Hiphil of amad (H5975, to stand) — to cause to stand, to establish, to maintain. The LXX translates anestesa se ("I raised you up"), which Paul quotes verbatim in Romans 9:17: "For the Scripture says to Pharaoh: 'For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you, and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth.'" The hardening of Pharaoh is embedded within YHWH's purpose for the universal proclamation of his name — the exodus serves a missionary goal.
9.17: mistateil beami: "setting yourself against my people." Salal (H5549) — to cast up, pile up (a road or mound), here to erect oneself as an obstacle. The image is of Pharaoh constructing himself as a physical barrier against Israel's departure. The verb's construction-imagery suggests not passive resistance but active, engineered opposition.
9.18: kaved meod asher lo-hayah kamohuu mitsrayim: "the most severe... that has ever fallen on Egypt from the day it was founded." The superlative framing establishes the hail as a historical marker. Egypt's antiquity is acknowledged; this storm exceeds anything in its long history. The claim of unprecedented severity is the hail's claim on Pharaoh's attention.
9.19: shelach hoykl et-miqnekha: "Give the order to bring your livestock to shelter." The advance warning before the hail — absent in the third plague of each previous triad (gnats, 8:16; boils, 9:8) — is unusual mercy: YHWH gives Pharaoh's own officials the option of protecting their possessions. The warning creates a division within Egypt between those who respond to YHWH's word (v.20) and those who do not (v.21).
9.20: hayare et-dvar YHWH: "Those who feared (yare) the word of the LORD." Yirah (H3372) — the fear/reverence of YHWH — appears within Pharaoh's own court, among non-Israelite Egyptians. The plague creates a theological division within Egypt itself. This anticipates the "mixed multitude" (erev rav, 12:38) that departs with Israel — Egyptians who align with YHWH's purpose.
9.21: va'asher lo sam libbo el-dvar YHWH: "those who did not set their heart on the word of the LORD." The heart-orientation (sam libbo) toward YHWH's word is the distinguishing marker between those who are protected and those who suffer. The lev (heart) that is "set on" the divine word is the response YHWH requires — the same heart-orientation that Pharaoh consistently refuses is freely given by unnamed Egyptian officials.
9.22: neteh et-yadkha al-hashamayim: "Stretch out your hand toward the sky." The upward gesture — toward the heavens — marks the hail as descending from the divine domain above. The combination of thunder (qolot, "voices/thunders"), hail, and fire (lightning) recalls the Sinai theophany (19:16) and anticipates the cosmic disruptions of Revelation's trumpet and bowl judgments (Rev 8:7; 16:21).
9.23: qolot (H6963): "thunder/voices." The same word for the thunder at Sinai (19:16; 20:18) where YHWH speaks in qolot. The hailstorm's thunder is YHWH's voice, just as Sinai's thunder is YHWH's voice. Pharaoh will later acknowledge "voices of God" (qolot Elohim, v.28) — using the divine plural for superlative intensity. The storm is not merely meteorological; it is a theophany.
9.24: uvar uaish mitlaqachat betokh habarad: "lightning flashing through it." Mitlaqachat — flashing, self-intermingling — describes fire and hail as supernaturally interwoven. Ps 105:32-33 cites the hail plague: "He gave them hail for rain, and lightning throughout their land; he struck down their vines and their fig trees, and shattered the trees of their country." The Psalm's poetic rendering confirms the plague's status as paradigmatic divine judgment.
9.25: vayakh habarad bkhol-erets Mitsrayim: "The hail struck down everything in the fields throughout Egypt." The comprehensive destruction of the field environment — human laborers, animals, crops — is the culmination of the second triad's agricultural focus. The agricultural devastation of Egypt's labor-system (the one that enslaved Israel) is now being dismantled by the same God who commissioned it for his own purpose.
9.26: raq beerets Goshen: "Only in the land of Goshen." The third explicit Goshen exception (8:22; 9:4,26). The geographic precision of YHWH's protection — a named region, not merely individual exemptions — is the plague narrative's most powerful evidence-claim. The visible boundary between Goshen and Egypt is a spatial argument for YHWH's sovereign control of the natural order.
9.27: chata'ti hapaam YHWH hatsadiq: "This time I have sinned. The LORD is righteous (tsadiq)." Pharaoh's confession applies the judicial vocabulary: tsadiq (righteous, in the right) to YHWH and resha'im (wicked, in the wrong) to himself and his people. The judicial framework — who is in the right — is the framework in which Moses has been operating throughout. Pharaoh finally articulates it correctly. Moses's response (v.30: "I know you do not yet truly fear the LORD God") demonstrates that correct theological statement without genuine fear is insufficient.
9.28: qolot Elohim uvarad: "this deadly thunder (qolot Elohim)." Elohim used as a superlative intensifier (divine thunder = overwhelming/mighty thunder). The same construction appears in "mountains of God" (Ps 36:6), "cedars of God" (Ps 80:10). Pharaoh's language acknowledges the thunder as divinely overwhelming — he wants the divine voice to stop.
9.29: phros kapai el-YHWH: "I will spread out my hands to the LORD." The gesture of spreading palms upward (peres kaf) is the OT intercessory posture (1 Kgs 8:22,38,54; Isa 1:15; Ps 44:20). "The earth is the LORD's (laYHWH haarets)" — the creation-ownership formula grounds the prayer in YHWH's cosmic sovereignty. The one who owns the earth can stop its weather; the prayer is based on demonstrated ownership.
9.30: terem tiru mipnei YHWH Elohim: "you still do not truly fear the LORD God." Moses intercedes while knowing the confession is inadequate. Yirah (H3372) — the fear/reverence of YHWH — is the required disposition. Pharaoh acknowledges YHWH's righteousness (v.27) and power (v.28) without possessing genuine fear. The theological diagnosis: right information about YHWH without the right orientation toward him.
9.31: vehapishta vehase'orah nukah: "The flax and barley were ruined." The agronomic precision — flax and barley in bloom or heading, wheat and spelt not yet ripe — provides chronological specificity: the hail falls approximately in February (Egyptian calendar, month of Phamenoth). The detail authenticates the account as based on knowledge of Egyptian agricultural seasons and has been confirmed by Egyptologists' reconstructions of the Nile calendar.
9.32: afilot henah: "they ripen later." Afilot — late-ripening, from afel (dark, late). The wheat and spelt's survival means Egypt's food supply is not yet fully destroyed; the agricultural crisis is severe but not yet total. This restraint — even in the most destructive plague so far — is consistent with the graduated purpose (v.15-16): YHWH is demonstrating power, not annihilating Egypt prematurely.
9.33: vayifros kapav el-YHWH: "He spread out his hands toward the LORD." The intercessory gesture (cf. v.29) occurs outside the city — at a physical remove from Pharaoh's court, in the open field, facing the heavens. The spatial and social geography of the prayer matters: Moses does not pray in Pharaoh's presence but in YHWH's presence, turning from the human authority toward the divine.
9.34: vayosef lachato vayakhbeid libbo hu va'avadav: "he sinned again and hardened his heart, he and his officials." Kaved (H3515) — the final occurrence with Pharaoh as explicit subject. "He and his officials" — the hardening now encompasses the entire institutional court; it is not Pharaoh alone but the collective Egyptian ruling class that hardens together. The communal hardening prepares for communal judgment at the sea.
9.35: vayechezak lev Paroh velo shalach: "Chazaq — Pharaoh's heart was firm." The closing formula uses chazaq (the resolution-and-firmness verb) and ends with "just as the LORD had said through Moses" (kaasher diber YHWH beyad Mosheh). Every element of chapters 7-9 — prediction, execution, hardening — occurs "just as the LORD had said." The plague narrative is presented as the perfect fulfillment of YHWH's own announced plan.

19.1: bachodesh hashlishi letset bnei Yisrael me'eretz Mitsrayim bayom hazeh ba'u midbar Sinai: 'In the third month after the children of Israel left Egypt, on that day they came to the Sinai wilderness.' The arrival at Sinai is dated precisely — the third month after the Exodus, with bayom hazeh (on this very day) marking it as a kairos moment. The journey from Egypt to Sinai is the journey from redemption to revelation: deliverance comes first, then the covenant that shapes the delivered community. Torah is given to the redeemed, not as the means of redemption.
19.2: vayisu miRefidim vayavou midbar Sinai vayachanu bamidbar vayichan sham Yisrael neged hahar: 'They journeyed from Rephidim and came to the wilderness of Sinai and camped in the wilderness. Israel camped there before the mountain.' The precise itinerary — from Rephidim (where water was struck from the rock) to Sinai — frames the covenant-giving between provision (water) and revelation (Torah). The camp neged hahar (opposite/facing the mountain) positions Israel as the audience of the divine theophany that will fill the mountain.
19.3: uMosheh alah el-haElohim vayiqra elav YHWH min-hahar lemor ko tomar leveit Yaakov vetageid livnei Yisrael: 'Moses went up to God, and YHWH called to him from the mountain, saying: Thus shall you say to the house of Jacob, and announce to the children of Israel.' The double designation — beit Yaakov (house of Jacob) and bnei Yisrael (children of Israel) — addresses the community in both its domestic (house) and national (Israel) dimensions. Moses as covenant mediator ascends to receive what he will bring down: the pattern of the prophet who meets God on behalf of the people.
19.4: atem re'item asher asiti leMitsrayim va'essa etchem al-kanfei nesharim vabi etchem elay (ʾattem rĕʾîtem ʾăšer ʿāśîtî lĕmiṣrāyim wāʾeśśāʾ ʾetḵem ʿal-kanpê nĕšārîm wāʾābîʾ ʾetḵem ʾēlay): 'You have seen what I did to Egypt, how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.' Nesher (H5404, eagle): the eagle's training flight — hovering over the nest, carrying the young on its wings (Deut 32:11) — is YHWH's self-image for the Exodus: not rescue to a destination but rescue to a person. Vabi etchem elay (I brought you to myself): the Exodus goal is not the Promised Land but YHWH himself. The land is where YHWH will dwell with his people; the destination is personal, not geographic.
19.5: ve'atah eem shamoa tishme'u beqoli ushmartem et-beriti vihyitem li segullah mikol-haamim (wĕʿattāh ʾim-šāmôaʿ tišmĕʿû bĕqōlî ûšĕmartem ʾet-bĕrîtî wihyitem lî sĕgullāh mikol-hāʿammîm): 'Now if you truly obey my voice and keep my covenant, you shall be my treasured possession among all peoples.' Shamoa tishme'u: infinitive absolute + imperfect — truly, truly obey. Segullah (H5459, treasured possession/personal property): one of the most precious terms in the covenant vocabulary, used only 8 times in the OT (Deut 7:6; 14:2; 26:18; Ps 135:4; Eccl 2:8; Mal 3:17; 1 Chr 29:3). In Mesopotamian contexts, segullu refers to the king's personal treasury — the most prized possessions kept close. Israel among all nations is YHWH's personal treasure. Ki-li kol-ha'aretz (for all the earth is mine): the universality of YHWH's ownership makes the particularism of election more striking, not less — he chose Israel from what is entirely his to choose from.
19.6: vatem tihyu li mamlekhet kohanim vegoy qadosh (wĕʾattem tihyû-lî mamleket kōhănîm wĕgôy qādôš): 'You shall be for me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' Two covenant titles. Mamlekhet kohanim (kingdom of priests): every Israelite has the priestly vocation of mediating between YHWH and the world — Israel's existence is a priestly existence of access to God and representation before God. Goy qadosh (holy nation): qadosh (H6918, holy/set apart) — the national identity is consecration, not merely ethnicity. Peter applies these exact titles to the church in 1 Pet 2:9: you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession — the Sinai covenant's description of Israel is fulfilled and extended to the church in Christ.
19.7: vayavo Mosheh vayiqra leziqnei ha'am vayasem lifneihem et kol-hadevarim ha'eleh: 'Moses came and called the elders of the people and placed before them all these words.' The mediatorial role: Moses receives from YHWH (v.3-6) and presents to the elders (v.7-8) — the chain of revelation. Vayasem lifneihem (placed before them): the covenant proposal as a laying-out, a presentation for consideration — not an imposition but an invitation requiring response. The elders as representative leaders of the tribal community carry the covenant response.
19.8: vaya'anu kol-ha'am yachdav vayomeru kol asher diber YHWH na'aseh (wayyaʿănû ḵol-hāʿām yaḥdāw wayyōmĕrû kol ʾăšer-dibber Yhwh naʿăśeh): 'All the people answered together and said: All that YHWH has spoken we will do.' The covenant acceptance: yachdav (together/united) — the whole community in one voice. Kol asher diber YHWH na'aseh: all that YHWH has said we will do. The commitment precedes hearing the specific commandments — a promissory note on what has not yet been fully disclosed. Moses brings back the people's words to YHWH: covenant is bilateral, requiring the community's public engagement.
19.9: vehineni ba eleicha be'av he'anan ba'avur yishma ha'am bedabri imach vegam becha ya'aminu le'olam: 'Behold I am coming to you in a thick cloud, so that the people may hear when I speak with you and may also believe in you forever.' The thick cloud (av he'anan) serves a double purpose: YHWH's majesty is veiled (the cloud prevents the consuming fire from destroying Israel) while his voice is audible (so the people can hear). The Sinai theophany gives both audio access (the voice) and visual distance (the cloud). Paul in 2 Cor 3:14 uses the Moses-veil as the image of what Christ removes for the believer.
19.10: vekidashtam hayom umachar vechibesu simlotam: 'Consecrate them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments.' Kidash (H6942, consecrate/sanctify) — the active preparation for encountering the holy. The washing of garments is external preparation that signifies internal readiness: one approaches YHWH with intentional preparation, not casually. The two-day preparation period before the third day (v.11, 16) mirrors the preparation structures of other theophany narratives (Num 11:18; Josh 3:5 — consecrate yourselves for tomorrow YHWH will do wonders).
19.11: vehayu nechonim layom hashlishi ki bayom hashlishi yered YHWH le'einei kol-ha'am al-Har Sinai: 'And be ready for the third day, for on the third day YHWH will descend in the sight of all the people on Mount Sinai.' The third-day descent of YHWH — the same eschatological timing that structures Hosea's resurrection promise (Hos 6:2: after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up) and Christ's resurrection. The third day is the day of divine appearing after a period of waiting.
19.12: vehigbalta et-ha'am saviv lemor hishamru lachem alot bahar unegoa beqatzehu: 'And you shall set limits for the people on all sides, saying: Take care not to go up into the mountain or touch its edge.' The boundary at the holy mountain — the cordoning off of the holy space — is the spatial expression of the holy/common distinction. YHWH's presence makes the mountain holy; unauthorized approach means death. The Levitical laws of sanctuary access codify this spatial holiness into Israel's ongoing worship structure.
19.13: lo-tiga bo yad ki saqol yisaqel o yereh yiyareh im-behema im-ish lo yichyeh: 'No hand shall touch it; he shall be stoned or shot with arrows; whether beast or man, he shall not live.' The death-penalty for violating the boundary — the most severe possible consequence for unauthorized approach to the holy. The principle behind the boundary is not arbitrary exclusion but the protection of mortal life from what it cannot survive unaided. The incarnation is YHWH solving the access problem from the inside: the holy comes to humanity rather than requiring humanity to approach the holy.
19.14: vayered Mosheh min-hahar el-ha'am vayekadesh et-ha'am vayechabesu simlotam: 'Moses came down from the mountain to the people and consecrated the people, and they washed their garments.' The vertical movement of Moses — descending from the divine encounter to serve the human community — is the mediatorial pattern. Down and up, YHWH and Israel, divine and human: Moses traverses this axis continually. The washing of garments is the physical enactment of the moral-spiritual preparation for covenant encounter.
19.15: vayomer el-ha'am hyu nechonim lishloshet yamim al-tigashu el-isha: 'He said to the people: Be ready for the third day; do not approach a woman.' The abstention from sexual relations before the divine encounter is the preparation of total focus — not because sexuality is impure but because covenantal encounter with the holy claims the whole person's orientation. The sexual union creates its own deep intimacy; approaching the deepest covenant intimacy requires a focused reorientation.
19.16: vayhi vaboker hashhlishi vayhi qolot uveraqim ve'anan kaveid al-hahar veqol shofar chazaq me'od (wayhî babbōqer haššĕlîšî wayhî qōlôt ûbĕrāqîm wĕʿānān kābēd ʿal-hāhār wĕqōl šôpār ḥāzāq mĕʾōd): 'On the morning of the third day there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud on the mountain, and a very loud trumpet blast.' The classic theophany vocabulary: qolot (H6963, thunders/voices), beraqim (H1300, lightnings), anan kaveid (heavy/thick cloud), qol shofar (sound of the ram's horn). John sees the same phenomena around the throne in Revelation (Rev 4:5; 8:5; 11:19; 16:18) — the Sinai theophany language is transported into the eschatological throne-room. Vayecheradhu kol-ha'am asher bamachane: all the people in the camp trembled.
19.17: vayotse Mosheh et-ha'am liqrat haElohim min-hamachane vayatyatzevu betachtit hahar: 'Moses brought out the people from the camp to meet God, and they stood at the foot of the mountain.' Liqrat haElohim (to meet God): the covenant encounter is a meeting — a personal meeting requiring both parties to be present. Israel stands at the foot of the mountain — the lowest point, at the greatest distance that still constitutes approach. The vertical distance between foot and summit expresses the ontological distance between creature and Creator that the Sinai covenant bridges through revelation.
19.18: vehar Sinai ashan kulo mipnei asher yared alav YHWH ba'esh (wĕhar sînay ʿāšan kullô mippĕnê ʾăšer yārad ʿālāyw Yhwh bāʾēš): 'Mount Sinai was wrapped in smoke because YHWH descended on it in fire, and the smoke went up like the smoke of a kiln, and the whole mountain trembled greatly.' Fire as YHWH's medium — his holiness consuming what it touches. The kiln-smoke image links to the Abrahamic covenant ceremony (Gen 15:17 — a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch passed between the pieces). YHWH's fire is both destructive (to the covenant-breaker) and purifying (for the covenant-keeper).
19.19: vayhi qol hashofar holech vechazeq me'od Mosheh yedaber vehA'Elohim ya'anenu beqol: 'The sound of the trumpet grew louder and louder. Moses spoke, and God answered him in thunder/a voice.' The reciprocal dialogue at the mountain — Moses speaking, God answering — is the model of covenant communication. Ya'anenu beqol (answered him with a voice): YHWH's voice in response to Moses's voice. The Sinai dialogue establishes the principle of divine responsiveness: when the covenant mediator speaks, the covenant Lord answers.
19.20: vayered YHWH al-Har Sinai el-rosh hahar: 'YHWH descended to the top of Mount Sinai, to the summit of the mountain.' The descent of YHWH to the mountain's summit is the ultimate condescension in the OT — the infinite entering the finite, the divine touching the earthly. John 1:14 uses the same language of descent: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us — the same divine-descent pattern but now complete, personal, incarnational. The Sinai descent is the OT version of what Bethlehem fulfilled.
19.21: vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh red ha'ed ba'am pen-yihersu el-YHWH lir'ot: 'YHWH said to Moses: Go down and warn the people lest they break through to YHWH to look and many of them fall.' The danger of unauthorized access even from curiosity: the desire to see (lir'ot) YHWH directly is presented as potentially fatal for those not prepared. The holy requires mediation for those not able to sustain its direct weight. The veil Moses wears after Sinai (34:33-35) is the spatial extension of this protective mediation.
19.22: vegam hakohanim hanegishtim el-YHWH yitkadoshu: 'Even the priests who approach YHWH must consecrate themselves lest YHWH break out against them.' Yitkadoshu (Hitpael of qadash — consecrate themselves): the reflexive is important — the priests must perform their own consecration before approaching. The divine holiness is not simply a danger to the unprepared but an active force that breaks out against unauthorized approach (yifrots YHWH). The Levitical consecration system is the institutionalized form of this protective preparation.
19.23: vayomer Mosheh el-YHWH lo yuchal ha'am la'alot el-Har Sinai: 'Moses said to YHWH: the people cannot go up to Mount Sinai, for you yourself commanded us, Set limits around the mountain and sanctify it.' Moses restates the boundaries YHWH established — the covenant mediator understands and enforces the access rules. The boundaries are not YHWH's hostility but his protection: he established the limits precisely so Israel could survive near the mountain. Structure enables encounter; without limits, encounter destroys.
19.24: vayomer elav YHWH lech-red ve'alita atah ve'Aharon imach vehakohanim veha'am al-yihersu la'alot el-YHWH: 'YHWH said to him: Go down, and come up — you and Aaron with you — but do not let the priests and the people break through to come up to YHWH.' The graduated access structure at Sinai: Moses alone to the summit, Aaron allowed partway, priests below, people at the boundary. This vertical structure of covenantal access is the prototype of the tabernacle's and temple's spatial arrangement: most holy place (high priest), holy place (priests), outer court (Israelites), outer boundary (Gentiles).
19.25: vayered Mosheh el-ha'am vayomer aleihem: 'So Moses went down to the people and told them.' Moses's descent to speak the divine word to the people — the final movement before the Decalogue is spoken directly by God (20:1-17). The mediatorial descent prepares Israel for direct divine speech. When YHWH does speak directly from the mountain, the people's terror (20:18-19) demonstrates why mediation is necessary: the people need the mediator to survive the direct divine word.

21.1: ve'eleh hamishpatim asher tasim lifneihem (wĕʾēlleh hammiŝpāṭîm ʾăšer tāśîm lipnêhem): 'And these are the ordinances/judgments you shall set before them.' Mishpatim (H4941, judgments/ordinances): from shafat (H8199, judge/govern) — these are the legal applications of the Decalogue's principles. The Book of the Covenant (Exod 21-23) is the first legal code given to Israel, covering concrete cases. Tasim lifneihem (place before them): the same verb used for placing food before guests and for presenting arguments in court — the laws are set out for the community's engagement, not merely decreed from above.
21.2: ki tiqneh eved ivri shesh shanim ya'avod uvashvii yetse laChofshi chinam: 'When you buy a Hebrew slave, he shall serve six years, and in the seventh he shall go free, for nothing.' The debt-slavery law applies the Sabbath principle to servitude: six years of service; the seventh is freedom. The same seven-year structure (shemitah, the sabbatical year) governs land use (Lev 25:1-7) and debt release (Deut 15:1-11). The Sabbath pattern extends across all dimensions of Israelite life: work and rest, land and fallow, slavery and freedom.
21.3: im-begapo yavo begapo yetse im-ba'al-isha hu veyatsa ishto imo: 'If he came in single, he shall go out single; if he came in married, his wife shall go out with him.' The law preserves the integrity of pre-existing family bonds: the master who receives a slave with a wife cannot dissolve the marriage. The slave's domestic relationships are not extinguished by his servitude — a fundamental acknowledgment of the slave's personhood beyond his economic function.
21.4: im-adonav yiten lo isha veyalda lo banim o vanot ha'isha veyeladeha tihyeh la'adoneiha vehu yetse vegapo: 'If his master gives him a wife and she bears him sons or daughters, the wife and her children shall be her master's, and he shall go out alone.' The hard case: a master-arranged marriage produces children who belong to the master. The tension between the slave's freedom and his family creates the scenario of v.5-6 — the voluntary permanent slavery. The law acknowledges the painful cost of freedom: some bonds exceed the value of liberty.
21.5: ve'im-amor yomar ha'eved ahavti et-adoni et-ishti ve'et-banai lo etse chofshi: 'But if the slave plainly says: I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go free.' Ahavareti et-adoni (I love my master): love as the motive for voluntary permanent slavery — the unusual circumstance where a freed person chooses permanent servitude out of love. Psalm 40:6-8 (LXX: a body you prepared for me; in the scroll it is written of me — I delight to do your will, O God) applies this exact law typologically to Christ: the one who could go free (pre-incarnate) chose permanent embodied servitude out of love. Hebrews 10:5-7 cites the Psalm as the Servant's own words.
21.6: vehigio adonav el-ha'Elohim vehigio el-hadelet o el-hamezuzah vertza adonav et-ozno bamartze va'avado le'olam: 'His master shall bring him to God, and he shall bring him to the door or doorpost. His master shall pierce his ear with an awl, and he shall be his slave forever.' The ear pierced at the doorpost: the doorpost is the location of the Passover blood (Exod 12:7, 22-23) and of the mezuzah (Deut 6:9). The ear attached to the doorpost makes the body physically connected to the house — the voluntary permanent slave is bound by his own body to the place of covenant. Le'olam (forever/to the age): permanent belonging through love-chosen submission.
21.7: vechi-yimkor ish et-bito le'amah lo tetse ketset ha'avadim: 'When a man sells his daughter as a maidservant, she shall not go out as male servants do.' The special provisions for female debt-slaves acknowledge the additional vulnerability of women in servitude — her exit conditions are different from male slaves. The law is not a license for the sale of daughters but a regulation that limits exploitation within a practice already embedded in the ancient economy.
21.8: im-ra'ah be'einei adoneiha asher-lo yo'adah vefadah le'am nachri lo-yimshol limkoraha bibegdo-vah: 'If she does not please her master who designated her for himself, he shall let her be redeemed. He has no right to sell her to a foreign people, since he has dealt faithlessly with her.' The protection against exploitation: if a master fails to fulfill his obligations toward a designated slave-wife, he loses the right to sell her. Bigdo vah (dealing faithlessly with her) — the same root as beged (treachery/garment-deceit) in covenant language. The slave has rights; the master cannot simply dispose of her.
21.9: ve'im-livno yi'adennah kemishpat habanot ya'aseh-lah: 'If he designates her for his son, he shall treat her as a daughter.' The full daughter-status for the slave-wife given to the master's son — she is integrated into the family with full family rights. The law consistently moves toward humanization and integration rather than mere regulation of exploitation.
21.10: im-acheret yiqach-lo she'erah kesutah ve'onatah lo yigra: 'If he takes another wife to himself, he shall not diminish her food, her clothing, or her marital rights.' The three inviolable rights of the first wife when a second is taken: food, clothing, and conjugal rights (onah, H5772). Even within a polygynous arrangement, the law protects the first wife's basic claims. Marital obligation is a legal minimum that cannot be reduced by preference for a newer wife.
21.11: ve'im-shlosh-eleh lo ya'aseh-lah veyatsa chinam ein kasef: 'If he does not do these three things for her, she shall go out for nothing, without payment of money.' The slave-wife's exit right when the master defaults on his obligations: she is free, without cost to her. The law creates accountability for the master who makes covenantal commitments — failure to fulfill the three obligations transfers freedom back to the woman. Covenant obligations are enforceable even for the powerless.
21.12: makeh ish vamet mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes a man so that he dies shall be put to death.' The homicide law establishes capital punishment for intentional killing — grounded in Gen 9:6 (whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed). The death-penalty for murder is not a societal deterrent but a covenantal response to the violation of the image of God in the victim: the killer has assaulted the image of YHWH and must answer with his own life.
21.13: va'asher lo tzadah veha'Elohim inah leyado vesamti lecha makom asher yanus shamah: 'But if he did not lie in wait, God delivered him into his hand — I will appoint you a place where he may flee.' The unintentional manslayer law introduces the concept of divine causation in accidental death: ha'Elohim inah leyado (God caused it to come into his hand). Accidental death occurs within divine providence, not outside it. The cities of refuge (Num 35:6-34) implement this provision — a place of safety for those who kill without intent. The distinction between intent and accident is foundational to biblical jurisprudence.
21.14: ve'chi-yazid ish al-re'ehu lehargo vetormah mizbechi tikachenu lamut: 'But if a man willfully attacks another to kill him by cunning, you shall take him from my altar that he may die.' Betormah (with treachery/cunning): the premeditated murder that uses deception. Even the altar provides no sanctuary for the deliberate murderer — YHWH's house is not a loophole for covenant-breakers. The contrast with the accidental manslayer (who finds asylum) is sharp: motive determines the application of the law.
21.15: umakeh aviv ve'imo mot yumat: 'Whoever strikes his father or mother shall be put to death.' The capital extension of the fifth commandment's honor-principle: physical assault on parents is the extreme violation of the honor-commandment, treated as seriously as murder. The family is the foundational covenant unit; its destruction from within through violence against parents is equivalent to the destruction of the covenant community itself.
21.16: veganev ish umechrahu venimatze veyado mot yumat: 'Whoever steals a man and sells him, if he is found in his possession, shall be put to death.' The kidnapping-for-slavery law: the selling of a free person into slavery is a capital offense — the theft of a person, the most fundamental of thefts. Paul cites this law in 1 Tim 1:10 (enslavers/kidnappers as lawbreakers). The prohibition on person-theft places the biblical tradition in fundamental opposition to chattel slavery as a system.
21.17: umeqalel aviv ve'imo mot yumat: 'Whoever curses his father or mother shall be put to death.' Qalel (H7043, curse/treat with contempt/make light): the opposite of kabed (honor). To curse parents is to treat them as worthless — the maximum violation of the fifth commandment. The law creates a symmetry: the death-penalty for striking (v.15) and for cursing (v.17) parents brackets all forms of parental assault. Jesus cites this law in Mark 7:10 to expose the Pharisees' Corban loophole as a violation of the commandment.
21.18: vechi-yarivu anashim vehikah ish et-re'ehu be'even o ve'egrof velo yamut venafal lemishkav: 'When men quarrel and one strikes the other with a stone or with his fist and the man does not die but takes to his bed.' The personal injury law distinguishes between assault that kills and assault that incapacitates. The graduated liability structure — full compensation for lost time plus medical care (v.19) but not capital punishment — shows that the law tracks actual harm rather than applying blanket penalties.
21.19: im-yaqum vehithalek bachutz al-mishantoh veniqah hamakeh raq shivto yiten virappo yerap'e: 'If the victim rises and walks outdoors with his staff, the one who struck him shall be clear; only he shall pay for the loss of his time and shall have him thoroughly healed.' Verapo yerap'e (shall thoroughly heal): infinitive absolute — he shall see to complete healing. The assailant bears both the economic loss (lost wages) and the full medical cost. The double responsibility of economic and medical restitution is the legal embodiment of neighbor-love: the one who caused harm must bear its full cost.
21.20: vechi-yakeh ish et-avdo o et-amato baShevet vamet tachat yado naqom yinaqem: 'When a man strikes his slave or female slave with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he shall be avenged.' The master's capital liability for killing a slave: naqom yinaqem (he shall be avenged/punished). The precise punishment is debated, but the principle is clear — the slave's life has legal standing. The slave is not simply the master's property to kill at will; the slave's life creates covenantal accountability for the master.
21.21: ach im-yom o yomayim ya'amod lo yinaqem ki caspo hu: 'But if the slave survives a day or two, he is not to be avenged, for the slave is his money.' The provision for a slave who lingers after assault and then dies: the delay is taken as evidence that death was not the intent (Mesopotamian law codes contain similar provisions). The phrase ki caspo hu (for he is his money/property) is ethically uncomfortable but legally realistic: the master's economic self-interest presumably argues against murdering his own slave.
21.22: vechi-yinatzu anashim venagfu isha harah veyatze'u yeladeiha velo yihyeh ason: 'When men strive and strike a pregnant woman, and her children come out but there is no harm.' The case of miscarriage caused by bystander injury — establishing legal liability for injury to a pregnant woman. Velo yihyeh ason (no further harm/disaster): if the woman and child are not further harmed, a fine is imposed. The legal protection of the pregnant woman extends to the pregnancy itself.
21.23: ve'im ason yihyeh venatata nefesh tachat nafesh (wĕʾim-ʾāsôn yihyeh wĕnātattāh nāpeš taḥat nāpeš): 'But if there is harm, then you shall pay life for life.' Nefesh tachat nefesh (life for life): the lex talionis — the principle of proportional justice. The law is not a license for personal vengeance but a limit on it: the response must be proportional to the injury, no more. The lex talionis protects the accused from disproportionate punishment by legally capping the penalty at equivalence. Jesus's teaching on turning the other cheek (Matt 5:38-39) is not the abolition of just legal claims but the transformation of the personal response to injury.
21.24: ayin tachat ayin shen tachat shen yad tachat yad regel tachat ragel: 'Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.' The fourfold list of specific equivalences makes the proportionality principle concrete. In most ancient Near Eastern legal contexts, the lex talionis applied literally only in theory; in practice, monetary compensation was accepted. The Mishnah (Bava Qamma 8:1) interprets the law as requiring monetary compensation. The principle is justice, not physical retaliation for its own sake.
21.25: kevi'ah tachat kevi'ah petza tachat patza chaburah tachat chaburah: 'Burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.' The extension of the lex talionis to injuries less than loss of limb: burns, wounds, bruises. The principle of proportional equivalence covers the full spectrum of bodily injury. The comprehensive list — from life (v.23) to eye/tooth/hand/foot (v.24) to burns/wounds/stripes (v.25) — ensures that every level of harm has a proportional legal response.
21.26: vechi-yakeh ish et-ein avdo o et-ein amato veshichetah lachofshi yeshalachenu tachat eino: 'When a man strikes the eye of his slave, male or female, and destroys it, he shall let the slave go free because of his eye.' The eye-for-eye principle applied to slave-injury produces not physical equivalence but freedom: the master who permanently injures his slave's eye loses ownership. The lex talionis in the slave-injury context (v.26-27) functions as a liberation principle — injury to the eye or tooth of a slave purchases freedom.
21.27: ve'im-shen avdo o shen amato yapil lachofshi yeshalachenu tachat shino: 'If he knocks out the tooth of his slave, male or female, he shall let the slave go free for his tooth's sake.' The tooth-for-tooth principle applied to slaves: a knocked-out tooth purchases freedom. Even the smallest permanent injury to a slave (teeth are the smallest body part specifically mentioned in the lex talionis) triggers the freedom clause. The slave's bodily integrity creates a legal claim on freedom — the most radical expression of the Israelite slave-protection laws.
21.28: vechi-yigach shor et-ish o et-isha vamet saqol yisaqel hashor velo ye'achel et-besaro uva'al hashor naqui: 'When an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox shall be stoned, and its flesh shall not be eaten, but the owner of the ox shall be free of liability.' The ox-goring law: the ox is executed (stoning, the same death as for human murderers — reflecting the seriousness of life taken) but the owner bears no liability in the first instance. The law presupposes that an animal's behavior is not foreseeable without prior notice.
21.29: ve'im shor nagach hu mitmol shilshom vehu'ad bibe'alav velo yishmerenu vaheimit ish o isha hashor yisaqel vegam-be'alav yumat: 'But if the ox had a habit of goring in the past, and its owner has been warned but did not keep it in, and it kills a man or woman, the ox shall be stoned, and its owner also shall be put to death.' The ox with a known goring habit: the owner who receives warning and does not restrain his dangerous animal bears capital liability when the animal kills. Foreseeable harm creates culpability — the principle that establishes negligence as a legal category.
21.30: im-kofer yushat alav venat'a pidon nafsho kechol asher yushat alav: 'If a ransom is imposed on him, then he shall give for the redemption of his life whatever is imposed on him.' The ransom alternative to capital punishment for the negligent ox-owner: kofer (H3724, ransom/redemption price). The kofer is the substitutionary payment that redeems a forfeited life — the same root as the Day of Atonement's kippur (Lev 16) and the substitutionary animal sacrifice. The legal ransom principle illuminates the theological atonement vocabulary: a life at risk is redeemed by payment of a price.
21.31: o-ven yigach o-bat yigach kemishpat hazeh ye'aseh lo: 'Whether it gores a son or gores a daughter, the same rule applies to him.' The equality principle: the law applies uniformly regardless of the victim's age or gender. Legal protections are not differentiated by the social status of the victim — a daughter's life and a son's life are legally equivalent claims on the same standard of justice.
21.32: im-eved yigach hashor o amah kesef shloshim shaqalim yiten la'adonav vehashor yisaqel: 'If the ox gores a slave, male or female, the owner shall pay thirty shekels of silver to their master, and the ox shall be stoned.' Thirty shekels of silver: the legal value of a slave's life. This is the same amount paid to Judas for betraying Jesus (Matt 26:15; 27:9-10) — Zechariah's price of a slave (Zech 11:12-13). The number connecting the slave-valuation law to the betrayal price is the gospel's most concentrated use of the lex talionis: Christ's life valued as a slave's ransom precisely so that he might be the ransom for many (Mark 10:45).
21.33: vechi-yiftach ish bor o ki-yichre ish bor velo yechasennu venaphal shamah shor o chamor: 'When a man opens a pit, or when a man digs a pit and does not cover it, and an ox or donkey falls into it.' The pit-hazard law: liability for the uncovered pit extends to any animal that falls in. The law requires proactive hazard management — not merely avoiding direct injury but taking responsibility for dangerous conditions one creates. The duty of care extends beyond direct action to the foreseeable consequences of one's omissions.
21.34: ba'al habor yeshallem kesef yashiv livnehem vehamet yihyeh-lo: 'The owner of the pit shall make restitution; he shall give money to its owner, and the dead animal shall be his.' The restitution principle: full market value owed for the lost animal, with the owner receiving the carcass. The economic calculus of damage-and-restitution establishes that the party who created the hazard bears the full cost of the harm it causes.
21.35: vechi-yigof shor ish et-shor re'ehu vamet umakeru et-hashor hachai vechalelu et-kaspo vesat hachama hameit yechetzun: 'When one man's ox injures another's ox and it dies, they shall sell the live ox and share its price, and the dead animal they shall also share.' The civil liability for animal-on-animal harm: the loss is shared equally between the owner of the injuring animal and the owner of the injured animal — a default risk-sharing principle when neither owner is negligent.
21.36: o noda ki nagach hashor mitmol shilshom velo yishmerenu be'alav shalem yeshalem shor tachat hashor vehamet yihyeh-lo: 'Or if it is known that the ox had a habit of goring in the past and its owner did not keep it in, he shall repay ox for ox, and the dead ox shall be his.' The known-danger negligence case in animal injury: full liability when prior goring established foreseeability. The law closes with the principle that prior knowledge creates full responsibility — the same principle underlying the warnings about the consequences of covenant violation that YHWH gives throughout the Torah. Knowledge of the law creates accountability under it.

16.1: midbar Sin (midbār Sîn): the Wilderness of Sin — a geographical name, possibly connected to the Sinai peninsula. The narrator dates the arrival precisely: the fifteenth day of the second month, exactly one month after the Passover (Exod 12:6). The liturgical precision signals that the wilderness journey is being measured against YHWH's redemptive calendar.
16.2: yillônû (wayyillônû): 'they grumbled/murmured' — from the root lûn, the technical verb for Israel's wilderness complaint. It appears six times in ch16 alone (vv.2, 7, 8, 9, 12) and becomes the vocabulary of the wilderness rebellion pattern throughout Numbers. Grumbling is addressed to Moses and Aaron but YHWH hears it as directed at himself (v.8).
16.3: bĕyad YHWH ('by the hand of YHWH'): the wilderness generation prefers death in Egypt under YHWH's judgment to uncertain life in the wilderness. The phrase lĕpōt bāśār ('by pots of flesh') is nostalgic hyperbole. The irony is sharp: they want to die 'by the hand of YHWH' when YHWH's hand has just saved them at the sea (14:30-31). The covenant lawsuit begins here: the redeemed people question the goodness of their Redeemer.
16.4: māṭîr lākem leḥem min-haššāmayim: 'I will rain down bread from heaven for you.' The verb māṭar (to rain) is normally used of rain; here it is used of manna — a deliberate atmospheric metaphor establishing the bread as direct provision from YHWH's domain. The phrase leḥem min-haššāmayim (bread from heaven) is quoted verbatim in Ps 78:24. The second clause introduces the test motive: lĕmaʿan ʾănassennû hăyēlēk bĕtôrātî ʾim lōʾ — 'in order that I may test them whether they will walk in my Torah or not.' The manna is a structured test of covenant obedience from the beginning.
16.5: miśneh (mišneh): 'double/twice as much.' On the sixth day the gathering is doubled in anticipation of the seventh day's rest. The same root gives šēnî (second) and šānāh (to repeat). The doubling on the sixth day provides the material basis for Sabbath rest — YHWH's provision anticipates the need before the command is given explicitly.
16.6: The paired provision announcement: meat (śĕlāw, quail) in the evening and bread (man, manna) in the morning. The evening-morning sequence is the creation rhythm (Gen 1), and the paired provision demonstrates YHWH's complete provision across the full daily cycle. Moses and Aaron make clear that the murmuring is heard by YHWH, not merely directed at the two human leaders.
16.7: kĕbôd YHWH (kĕbôd Yahweh): 'the glory of YHWH.' The noun kābôd derives from kābed (to be heavy/weighty); glory is the weight or gravitas of God's self-revelation. YHWH's response to complaint is not judgment but demonstration of his character through provision — the provision is itself a glory-display.
16.8: lōʾ ʿālênû tĕlunnōtêkem kî ʿal YHWH: 'your grumblings are not against us but against YHWH.' The theological analysis of complaint: when Israel murmurs against Moses and Aaron, YHWH is the real target. This framing recurs in 1 Sam 8:7 and Luke 10:16 ('whoever rejects you rejects me'). Mediators bear the complaint of the people, but YHWH registers it as directed at himself.
16.9: qirĕbû lipnê YHWH: 'draw near before YHWH.' The verb qārab (to draw near, approach) is the central verb of the sacrificial system — worshipers draw near with offerings; here YHWH himself draws near with provision. The complaint creates the occasion for theophanic encounter.
16.10: hinnēh kĕbôd YHWH nirʾāh beʿānān: 'the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud.' The cloud (ʿānān) is the vehicle of divine presence throughout the wilderness narrative. Nirʾāh (Niphal of rāʾāh, to appear/be seen) indicates a controlled, mediated revelation — YHWH makes himself visible but in qualified form, not unmediated glory.
16.11: YHWH speaks to Moses: the divine word of promise precedes and guarantees the provision. The structure of vv.11-12 follows the established pattern: YHWH speaks to Moses, Moses relays to Israel, the promised event happens. The chain of mediation (YHWH → Moses → people → event) will be formalized at Sinai but is practiced here in the wilderness.
16.12: šāmaʿtî ʾet tĕlunnat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl: 'I have heard the murmuring of the children of Israel.' The phrase wîdaʿtem kî ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem ('you shall know that I am YHWH your God') is the recognition formula repeated throughout Ezekiel — the purpose of divine acts is theological knowledge. YHWH provides in order to be known, not merely to satisfy appetite.
16.13: śĕlāw (śĕlāw): quail — a migratory bird (Coturnix coturnix) that crosses Sinai twice yearly; exhausted migrating quail land in large numbers and are easily caught. The Hebrew word appears three times in the Pentateuch (here; Num 11:31-32; Ps 105:40). The provision is naturalistic but timed and organized supernaturally — the ordinary animal is the vehicle of extraordinary provision.
16.14: ḥasas dāq mĕḥuspar: 'fine flakes, like frost.' The manna is described in two paired adjectives: dāq (thin/fine) and mĕḥuspar (small/flake-like). The vocabulary of smallness — delicate, fine, thin — emphasizes the manna's otherness from ordinary grain. It descends with the dew, associated with the created abundance of morning moisture.
16.15: man hûʾ: This two-word phrase is the origin of the word 'manna' and is a studied ambiguity. Man in Aramaic/Hebrew means 'what?' — so man hûʾ is 'what is it?' The narrator preserves both meanings simultaneously: the word for the substance is the question about the substance. Moses's explanation ('it is the bread which YHWH has given you to eat') answers the question without removing the wonder. The name 'manna' is carried through Num 11:6-9, Ps 78:24, and the John 6 Bread of Life discourse.
16.16: ʿōmer (ʿōmer): a dry-measure unit, defined later (v36) as one-tenth of an ephah. This is its first occurrence in Scripture — the unit is only used in the manna narrative. Each person gathers one omer: legulgōlet ('per head,' literally 'per skull') is the counting unit, anticipating the census of Num 1. The provision is democratized — every person receives exactly enough, contrasting with normal food distribution that follows economic and social hierarchies.
16.17: The gathering proceeds as commanded but the amounts vary. The phenomenon anticipates the leveling of v18: despite differing quantities gathered, each has exactly what they need. The miracle is not identical individual effort but divinely calibrated distribution — the manna economy points toward 2 Cor 8:15.
16.18: hammarbeh lōʾ heydir wĕhammaʿʿîṭ lōʾ ḥiśîr: 'whoever gathered much had no excess, and whoever gathered little had no lack.' Heydir (surplus/excess) comes from yādar (to remain over); ḥiśîr (lack/deficit) from ḥāsar (to lack). Paul quotes this verse in 2 Cor 8:15 as the principle behind the Jerusalem collection: the Gentile abundance supplies the Jerusalem lack. The manna economy becomes the pattern for the covenant community's economic ethics.
16.19: ʾal yôter mimmennû ʾîš ʿad bōqer: 'let no one leave any of it until morning.' The command against hoarding tests trust: leave nothing overnight, gather fresh each day. YHWH trains his people away from anxiety-driven accumulation toward daily dependence — the structural teaching behind the Lord's Prayer's 'give us today our daily bread' (Matt 6:11).
16.20: wayyarūm tôlāʿîm wayyibʾaš: 'it bred worms and became foul.' Those who disobeyed and kept the manna overnight found it infested and rotting. The contrast with the Sabbath provision (v24) is pointed: what breeds worms on ordinary days remains pure on the Sabbath. The same physical substance behaves differently according to YHWH's covenant calendar — the natural order is subordinated to his decrees.
16.21: Daily gathering in the morning until the sun heats the ground, when the manna melts. The provision is neither storable nor indefinitely available — it must be sought each morning in its appointed time. The temporality of the provision enforces the daily rhythm of covenant dependence that YHWH is building into his people's habits.
16.22: miśneh leḥem: 'double bread' — on the sixth day the provision itself doubles before Moses announces the Sabbath principle (v23). YHWH's provision anticipates his own command. The assembly leaders come to Moses concerned; the explanation that follows is the first Sabbath announcement in the narrative Torah.
16.23: šabbat šabbātôn qōdeš laYHWH: 'a Sabbath of complete rest, holy to YHWH.' The phrase šabbat šabbātôn is the absolute-state construction with a cognate noun for intensification — 'Sabbath of Sabbaths' — marking superlative rest. This is the first time šabbāt (Sabbath) appears in the Torah narrative, preceding the Sinai legislation. Qōdeš laYHWH (holy to YHWH) indicates the Sabbath belongs to YHWH's sphere. The Sabbath is revealed in the context of manna provision, not first at Sinai.
16.24: The Sabbath provision is confirmed by miracle: the manna kept overnight for the Sabbath does not rot or breed worms (contrast v20). The physical preservation of the Sabbath portion is YHWH's material attestation of the day's holiness — empirical confirmation of the Sabbath command before it has been formally legislated at Sinai (20:8-11).
16.25: ʾĕkhuluhû hayyôm kî šabbāt hayyôm laYHWH: 'eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to YHWH.' The manna is the occasion for the first Sabbath teaching: the structure of the created week — six days of provision, rest on the seventh — is instantiated in daily wilderness life before the Ten Commandments are given. Creation theology (Gen 2:2-3) is embedded in covenant provision.
16.26: Six days of gathering; on the seventh, none is to be found. The provision respects the Sabbath by its absence — YHWH will not give manna on the day set apart for rest. The refusal to provide enforces the Sabbath rest structurally: you cannot gather on the Sabbath even if you try, because there is none to gather.
16.27: Some go out to gather on the seventh day and find nothing. YHWH's response (v28) — ʿad ʾānāh mēritem, 'how long do you refuse?' — uses the vocabulary of prophetic rebuke. ʿAd ʾānāh (how long?) is the language of Psalms and Prophets addressing chronic disobedience. The Sabbath command is enforced not only by command but by the absence of provision.
16.28: miṣwōtay wĕtôrōtāy: The paired terms: miṣwôt (commandments, specific directives) and tôrôt (plural of tôrāh, instructions/laws). The use of tôrôt (plural) suggests a broader instruction complex already in view, anticipating the full Sinai legislation. The manna narrative functions as a pre-Sinai probationary period — a training ground for covenant obedience before the law is formally given.
16.29: rĕʾû kî YHWH nātan lākem haššabbāt: 'See that YHWH has given you the Sabbath.' The Sabbath is framed as a gift (nātan = to give) before it is a law. The command to remain in one's place (šĕbû ʾîš taḥtāyw) on the Sabbath is the first formal Sabbath stipulation in the narrative, anticipating the later prohibitions against kindling fire (35:3) and carrying loads (Jer 17:21-22).
16.30: wayyišbĕtû hāʿām bayyôm haššĕbîʿî: 'the people rested on the seventh day.' The verb šāḇat (from which šabbāt derives) means 'to cease/desist/rest from activity.' The Sabbath rest is cessation modeled on YHWH's own rest after creation (Gen 2:2-3: wayyišbōt, 'and he rested'). The covenant community's Sabbath participates in the Creator's rest.
16.31: man: the name is confirmed. zera gad lāban (like coriander seed, white) and ṭaʿmô kĕṣappîḥît bidbāš (its taste like wafers in honey). Two similes: visual (white like coriander seed) and gustatory (sweet like honey). The honey simile evokes the promised land flowing with milk and honey — the manna is a foretaste of the land's abundance. Num 11:8 adds that grinding it produced an oil-cake taste.
16.32: ʾōmer hāmĕlēʾ: 'a full omer.' YHWH commands a jar of manna to be kept (hinnîaḥ) for generations to see ʾet-haleḥem ʾăšer heʾakaltî ʾetkem ('the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness'). The memorial function: the manna jar placed in the ark (Heb 9:4) connects this preservation command to the tabernacle's most holy space, making the manna a permanent witness to YHWH's wilderness provision.
16.33: ṣinnĕṣenet: a jar or pot — a hapax legomenon, the only occurrence of this word in the OT. The object's uniqueness mirrors the uniqueness of its contents. Aaron places it 'before YHWH' (lipnê YHWH), the phrase used for the sanctuary's inner presence. The manna jar's placement anticipates the ark not yet built — an anachronism the narrator resolves by noting (v34) the specific ark to which it refers.
16.34: Aaron places the manna before hāʿēdut (the Testimony) — a prospective act. The Testimony refers to the stone tablets to be placed in the ark (Exod 25:16; 40:20), not yet given at this point in the narrative. The narrator projects forward from the Sinai covenant to explain the placement. The manna jar sits in the ark alongside the covenant documents — provision paired with commandment, grace alongside law.
16.35: wĕbĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾākelû ʾet-hammān arbaʿîm šānāh: 'the children of Israel ate the manna forty years.' The manna provision spans the entire wilderness generation, ending only when they eat the produce of Canaan (Josh 5:12). The forty-year continuity establishes the manna as the wilderness-period bread in toto. ʿAd bôʾām ʾel-qĕṣēh ʾereṣ Kĕnaʿan ('until they came to the edge of the land of Canaan') — provision ends at the boundary of fulfillment.
16.36: wĕhāʿōmer ʿăśîrît hāʾêpāh hûʾ: 'and the omer is one-tenth of an ephah.' The parenthetical definition closes the manna chapter with a metrological note. An ephah is approximately 22 liters; an omer is thus about 2.2 liters. The precision serves the narrative's concern with exact provision: exactly enough, measured and calibrated, is YHWH's characteristic mode of grace in the wilderness.

17.1: ʿal pî YHWH: 'according to the mouth/command of YHWH' — the wilderness itinerary formula indicating each movement is divinely directed. The phrase recurs throughout Num 9:18-23 to describe the cloud's role in determining encampment and movement. Rĕpîdîm is the final station before Sinai; its place in the itinerary makes it the last test before the great covenant encounter.
17.2: wayyārēb hāʿām ʿim-mōšeh: 'the people quarreled with Moses.' The verb rîb is a legal term — it denotes filing a lawsuit, bringing a formal complaint. The same root underlies the covenant lawsuit (rîb) structure in the Prophets (Mic 6:2; Hos 4:1) where YHWH brings charges against Israel. Here Israel files a lawsuit against their deliverer. Moses's counter-question reframes the dispute: quarreling with Moses is testing (nāsāh) YHWH.
17.3: lāmāh zeh heʿĕlîtānû mimmîṣrāyim lĕhamît ʾōtî: 'why have you brought us up from Egypt to kill me?' The accusative ʾōtî (me) is singular — each individual speaker levels the complaint personally. The pattern of accusation — Exodus as a journey to death rather than life — repeats from 14:11 and 16:3. Each complaint ignores prior deliverance and focuses exclusively on present privation.
17.4: mā ʾeʿĕśeh lāʿām hazzeh: 'What shall I do with this people?' Moses's cry to YHWH is the mediator's lament caught between a rebellious people and the God they provoke. ʿÔd meʿaṭ ûsĕqālunî ('they are almost ready to stone me') — the hostility of the wilderness congregation threatens Moses personally. The mediator's vulnerability is structural: he bears the people's anger while interceding on their behalf.
17.5: ʿăbōr lipnê hāʿām: 'Pass on before the people.' YHWH directs Moses to take elders as witnesses — the provision of water is to occur publicly before credible witnesses. Taking the staff (maṭṭeh) used at the Nile connects this water-miracle to the Exodus plagues: the same instrument of judgment against Egypt becomes the instrument of provision for Israel.
17.6: hinnĕnî ʿōmēd lĕpānêkā šām ʿal-haṣṣûr bĕḥōrēb wĕhikkîtā baṣṣûr: 'I will stand before you there on the rock at Horeb, and you shall strike the rock.' The preposition ʿal (on/upon) with ʿāmad (to stand) means YHWH positions himself on the rock. The blow Moses strikes falls on the rock where YHWH stands — substitutionary solidarity. YHWH absorbs the stroke meant for covenant-breaking Israel. Ûyāṣĕʾû mimmennû mayim: 'and water shall come out of it' — not from the strike alone but from YHWH's presence on the rock. Paul's identification 'the Rock was Christ' (1 Cor 10:4) names the one who stood there to be struck so that water — life — flows for his people.
17.7: Massāh ûMĕrîbāh: Two place-names derived from the events: Massāh from nāsāh (to test/try) — a place of testing; Mĕrîbāh from rîb (to contend/quarrel) — a place of contention. The double name memorializes both the people's fault and YHWH's response. Ps 95:8 ('Do not harden your hearts as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness') is cited in Heb 3:8-11 as a warning against abandoning faith.
17.8: wayyābōʾ ʿĂmālēq wayyillāḥem ʿim Yiśrāʾēl bĕRĕpîdîm: 'Amalek came and fought against Israel at Rephidim.' The Amalekites make their first narrative appearance here; they will become Israel's archetypal enemy (Gen 36:12; Num 24:20; Deut 25:17-19). Their attack is particularly condemned in Deut 25:18 because they attacked 'from the rear, all who were faint and weary' — they preyed on the vulnerable.
17.9: Yĕhôšuaʿ (Joshua) — first narrative appearance by this name (his birth name was Hôšēaʿ, Num 13:16; Moses renamed him). Yĕhôšuaʿ = YHWH saves/delivers — a theophoric name. The military commander and the intercessor are paired — Joshua's sword and Moses's raised hands are not alternatives but complements in the theology of holy war.
17.10: Joshua implements Moses's command; the battle begins. The narrative shifts between the valley and the hilltop to show the connection between the earthly fight and the heavenly intercession. Hur — paired with Aaron — appears here and in 24:14 (holding authority during Moses's absence on Sinai). The three-man support structure mirrors the later pattern of multiple elders sustaining a single leader's ministry.
17.11: kaʾăšer yārîm mōšeh yādô wĕgābar Yiśrāʾēl: 'whenever Moses raised his hand Israel prevailed.' Moses's raised hands signal sustained prayer, not magical causation. The qal participle indicates continuous action. When his hands drop, Amalek prevails — the intercession must be unbroken. Raising hands (nāśāʾ yādayim) was the posture of oath and prayer (Gen 14:22; Ps 28:2; 134:2).
17.12: wîdê mōšeh kĕbēdîm: 'Moses's hands were heavy.' The word kābēd (heavy) is the same root as kābôd (glory) — glory is weight. The hands too heavy to raise enact the physical analogy of weighted prayer. Aaron and Hur hold up Moses's hands — the community sustains the intercessor. The support of Moses's arms is a picture of corporate intercession: the mediator does not pray alone but is upheld by the community he represents.
17.13: wayyaḥălōš Yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet ʿĂmālēq: 'Joshua subdued Amalek.' The verb ḥālaš (to weaken/prostrate) conveys decisive defeat. Lĕpî ḥāreb (literally 'to the mouth of the sword') is the standard idiom for total military defeat — the sword's edge consumes. The coordinated victory — Joshua's sword below, Moses's hands above — models holy war theology: YHWH fights from his throne while his people engage in the valley.
17.14: kĕtōb zōʾt zikkārôn basēper: 'Write this as a memorial in the book.' The first explicit command to write in the Torah. Zikkārôn (memorial record) from zākar (to remember) — the written record ensures preservation beyond the witnessing generation. Bāsēper (in the book) with a definite article suggests a specific document, perhaps the beginning of the Mosaic literary tradition. The command to remember and erase Amalek's name provides the framework for the Purim narrative (Esther) and eschatological battle themes.
17.15: YHWH Nissî: 'YHWH is my banner/standard.' Moses builds an altar and names it — the practice of naming altars to commemorate YHWH's acts recurs in the patriarchal narratives (Gen 33:20; 35:7). Nēs (banner, standard) is the military insignia around which troops rally; naming YHWH as the nēs declares him the standard under which Israel fights. The same word is used in Isa 11:12: YHWH will raise a banner (nēs) for the nations.
17.16: yād ʿal kēs YH milḥāmāh laYHWH baʿĂmālēq middōr dōr: 'A hand is on the throne of YH — war to YHWH against Amalek from generation to generation.' The phrase is compressed and debated. Kēs may be contracted from kissēʾ (throne). YH is the shortened divine name, perhaps because the full name is withheld while Amalek's name exists. The perpetual warfare (middōr dōr = from generation to generation) establishes the theological framework for Saul's failure in 1 Sam 15 and the eschatological defeat of evil's final representative.

18.1: Yitrô kōhēn Midyān ḥōtēn Mōšeh: 'Jethro the priest of Midian, Moses's father-in-law.' Jethro is introduced by his religious title (kōhēn = priest) and his familial relationship. As kōhēn of Midian, he is a non-Israelite religious official whose knowledge of YHWH (v.11) comes from prior revelation and now from hearing the news of the Exodus. The Gentile priest is the surprising vehicle of governance wisdom in this chapter.
18.2: ʾaḥar šillûḥêhā: 'after her sending away/dismissal.' This parenthetical note indicates Moses had sent Zipporah back to Jethro at some point. Šillûḥîm (dismissal, sending away) uses the same root as divorce terminology (Deut 24:1, 3), though here it simply means he sent her home. Jethro now returns her to Moses, restoring the family unit before the Sinai encounter.
18.3: Gēršōm: from gēr šām (a sojourner there) — Moses's naming theology embedded in his son's name. The gēr (resident alien/sojourner) is the central social category in the Exodus narrative: the law will repeatedly ground concern for the sojourner in Israel's own sojourn experience (22:21; 23:9; Lev 19:33-34). The name carries the Exodus's social ethic.
18.4: ʾĔlîʿezer: 'my God is help' — ʾĔlî (my God) + ʿezer (help), the same root as in ʿezer kĕnegdô (Gen 2:18). The specific rescue cited: wayyaṣṣilēnî mēḥereb parʿōh ('he delivered me from Pharaoh's sword'). The hiphil of nāṣal (to rescue/deliver) is key Exodus vocabulary for YHWH's saving action. The name commemorates personal deliverance that precedes and anticipates the national deliverance.
18.5: ʾel-hāhār ʾĕšer hûʾ ḥōneh šām: 'to the mountain where he was encamped.' The burning bush mountain (Horeb/Sinai, 3:1) is the destination toward which the whole wilderness journey has been oriented. Jethro comes to meet Moses at the mountain of God — the site of prior revelation is the site of this reunion. The movement brings the Gentile priest to the sacred center of YHWH's revelation to Israel.
18.6: Jethro sends word ahead of his coming — the proper protocol for approaching a leader. ʾĂnî ḥōtĕnĕkā Yitrô bāʾ ʾēlêkā: 'I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you.' The self-identification with relationship category (ḥōtēn = father-in-law) signals the domestic register of the visit — family reunion before political consultation.
18.7: wayyēṣēʾ mōšeh liqrāʾt ḥōtĕnô wayyištaḥû wayyiššaq lô: 'Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, bowed down, and kissed him.' Three verbs capture the full protocol of respectful greeting: going out to meet (liqrāʾt = a formal reception), prostrating (šāḥāh, the same verb used of worship), and kissing (nāšaq). The greeting is the full covenant-community hospitality gesture, parallel to Abraham meeting the three visitors (Gen 18:2).
18.8: wayyĕsapper Mōšeh lĕḥōtĕnô ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh YHWH: 'Moses told his father-in-law all that YHWH had done.' The verb sāpar (to count/recount/tell) is the same root as sēper (book/document) — telling is a form of inscription. The narrative covers: YHWH's acts against Pharaoh and Egypt; all the hardship (tĕlāʾāh) along the way; and how YHWH had delivered them. Moses's recounting is an act of covenant witness — he testifies to the Gentile priest.
18.9: wayyiḥad Yitrô: 'Jethro rejoiced.' The verb ḥādāh (to rejoice/be glad) is relatively rare; it appears in the Psalms and here. Jethro's joy is specified: ʿal kol-haṭṭôbāh (over all the good) that YHWH had done for Israel. The affective response to the news of redemption is joy — the appropriate and full response to the gospel of deliverance.
18.10: Bārûk YHWH ʾăšer hiṣṣîl ʾetkem: 'Blessed be YHWH who delivered you.' Jethro's benediction is a berakah formula — the standard blessing-formula that acknowledges YHWH as the source of good. The double miyyad (from the hand of) emphasizes comprehensive rescue: from Egyptian power as a system and from Pharaoh's personal power. The Gentile priest speaks the covenant community's liturgy of gratitude.
18.11: ʿattāh yādaʿtî kî gādôl YHWH mikkol-hāʾĕlōhîm: 'Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods.' Jethro's confession is the theological climax of the chapter. ʿAttāh yādaʿtî (now I know) — the experiential knowledge formula, knowledge through hearing testimony. The comparative gādôl mikkol-hāʾĕlōhîm (greater than all the gods) asserts YHWH's superiority. Kî baddābār ʾăšer zādû ʿălêhem ('for in the thing in which they acted arrogantly') specifies the basis: Egypt's gods sponsored Egyptian arrogance, and YHWH defeated them at every point. The Gentile priest confesses what Israel often forgets.
18.12: wayyiqqaḥ Yitrô ḥōtēn Mōšeh ʿōlāh ûzĕbāḥîm lēʾlōhîm: 'Jethro brought a burnt offering and sacrifices to God.' ʿÔlāh (burnt offering — wholly consumed, ascending to God) and zĕbāḥîm (peace/fellowship offerings — shared between God and worshippers) together constitute a complete worship act. Aaron and all the elders of Israel join the meal — Jethro's sacrifices are the occasion for a covenant meal that includes the leadership of Israel. The Gentile priest participates in Israel's liturgy.
18.13: wayyēšeb Mōšeh lišpōṭ ʾet-hāʿām ... min-habbōqer ʿad-hāʿāreb: 'Moses sat to judge the people ... from morning until evening.' The verb šāpaṭ (to judge, decide, govern) covers arbitration, rule-declaration, and case adjudication. The phrase min-habbōqer ʿad-hāʿāreb (from morning until evening) captures the unsustainability of one-person governance — Moses as sole judge creates a system that consumes its most gifted member.
18.14: wayyarʾ ḥōtēn Mōšeh ʾēt kol-ʾăšer hûʾ ʿōśeh lāʿām: 'Moses's father-in-law saw everything he was doing for the people.' Jethro's observation is the outsider's diagnostic perspective. Maddûaʿ ʾattāh yōšēb lĕbaddekā ('why do you sit alone?') — baddekā means 'by yourself alone.' The problem is systemic, not a matter of Moses's dedication; the system's architecture is defective.
18.15: kî-yābōʾ ʾēlay hāʿām lidrōš ʾĕlōhîm: 'because the people come to me to inquire of God.' The verb dāraš (to seek/inquire) is the technical term for consulting an oracle or judicial authority. Lidrōš ʾĕlōhîm (to inquire of God) — Moses's role is not merely legal arbiter but mediator of divine decision. Every case is framed as a consultation with God, which explains both the burden and the bottleneck.
18.16: kî yihyeh lāhem dābār bāʾ ʾēlay ûšāpaṭtî bên ʾîš ûbên rēʿēhû: 'when they have a dispute they come to me, and I judge between a man and his neighbor.' Moses also teaches the decrees and laws of God — the judge is simultaneously teacher and case-arbiter. The dual function (teaching and adjudicating) is the foundation of the Levitical-priestly and later rabbinic judicial role.
18.17: lōʾ-ṭôb haddābār ʾăšer ʾattāh ʿōśeh: 'the thing you are doing is not good.' Jethro's assessment uses the emphatic construction: nābōl tibbōl gam-ʾattāh gam-hāʿām hazzeh — 'you will surely wear out, both you and this people.' Nābōl tibbōl is the Qal infinitive absolute + imperfect: 'wearing out you will wear out.' The verb nābal (to wither/wilt/wear out) connects physical exhaustion with a kind of organizational foolishness.
18.18: nābōl tibbōl: the emphatic infinitive absolute confirms inevitability. Kî kābēd mimmĕkā haddābār ('the thing is too heavy for you') — kābēd (heavy) recurs from 17:12 (Moses's heavy hands). The same heaviness that required Aaron and Hur's support in battle now requires delegation in governance. Physical and organizational weight are parallel problems with parallel solutions: delegation and support structures.
18.19: ʾĕhyeh ʿimmāk lēʾlōhîm: 'be to God for the people.' Moses's role is defined as the people's representative before God. The phrase uses ʾĕhyeh (the I AM name) in a human application — Moses functions as the mediator of the I AM's presence. He stands between people and God: bringing their cases upward, bringing God's statutes and decisions downward. This mediatorial role is the OT pattern for the high priest and ultimately for Christ (Heb 9:24).
18.20: wĕhizahartāh ʾethem ʾet-haḥuqqîm wĕʾet-hattôrōt wĕhôdaʿtā lāhem ʾet-hadderek: Four vocabulary items: ḥuqqîm (statutes — fixed, often cultic decrees), tôrôt (plural of tôrāh — instructions, from yārāh to shoot/direct), hadderek (the way — the halakic pattern), and hammaʿăśeh (the work/deeds). The judicial role is inseparable from the teaching role: judges must first be teachers of the law they administer. Yārāh (the root of tôrāh) means to shoot/aim/direct — Torah is divine direction for the path of life.
18.21: ʾanšê ḥayil yirĕʾê ʾĕlōhîm ʾanšê ʾĕmet śōnĕʾê beṣaʿ: four qualifications for judges — 'capable men, God-fearing, men of truth, haters of unjust gain.' Ḥayil (capable/competent — the word for virtue in Ruth 3:11 and Prov 31:10) covers intellectual and practical competence. Yirĕʾê ʾĕlōhîm (God-fearing) is the foundational orientation — judicial wisdom begins with the fear of YHWH (Prov 1:7). ʾĔmet (truth/faithfulness — both cognitive and moral accuracy). Śōnĕʾê beṣaʿ (haters of dishonest gain/bribery) — incorruptibility. This fourfold profile becomes the standard for Israelite leadership (Deut 1:13-17; 16:18-20).
18.22: kol-haddābār haggādōl yabiʾû ʾēlêkā wĕkol-haddābār haqqāṭōn yišpĕṭû hēm: 'every great matter they shall bring to you, but every small matter they shall judge themselves.' The graduated judicial hierarchy: minor cases decided locally; significant cases escalated to Moses. Gādôl and qāṭōn become the standing terms for case-scale in Israelite jurisprudence (Deut 1:17). This architecture anticipates the Sanhedrin's three-tiered system (courts of 3, 23, and 71).
18.23: wĕṣiwwĕkā ʾĕlōhîm wĕyākaltā ʿămōd: 'and God so commands you, you will be able to stand.' Jethro conditions the entire plan on divine sanction — the reform is not his imposition but must be validated by covenant authority. Moses does not implement immediately; the chapter ends with Moses doing as Jethro said, but the assumption is that YHWH's approval is sought. Governance wisdom is subsumed under covenant authority.
18.24: wayyišmaʿ Mōšeh lĕqôl ḥōtĕnô wayyaʿas kōl ʾăšer ʾāmar: 'Moses listened to his father-in-law's voice and did everything he said.' The verb šāmaʿ (to hear/obey) carries both meanings. Moses's willingness to be instructed by a Gentile priest — taking counsel from outside the covenant community — demonstrates the humility described in Num 12:3 ('Moses was very meek'). Wisdom is not exclusive to the covenant community; YHWH's common grace distributes organizational insight broadly.
18.25: šārê ʾălāpîm šārê mēʾôt šārê ḥămiššîm wĕšārê ʿăśārōt: 'commanders of thousands, commanders of hundreds, commanders of fifties, commanders of tens.' The decimal hierarchy creates a scalable governance structure. The word śar (commander/prince/leader) is used for both military commanders and civil leaders. The decimal organization mirrors the census structure of Num 1 and the military organization of Deuteronomy. Governance and military command share the same hierarchical architecture.
18.26: ʾEt-haddābār haqqāšeh (the hard/difficult matter) — qāšeh (hard, stiff, difficult) is used of hard labor in Egypt (1:14) and the hardened heart of Pharaoh (7:3). The difficult cases require concentrated attention that the Exodus itself required. The gradation of difficulty structures legal access to Moses: only the genuinely complex reaches the covenant mediator.
18.27: wayyĕšallaḥ Mōšeh ʾet-ḥōtĕnô wayyēlek lô ʾel-ʾarṣô: 'Moses sent his father-in-law away, and he went to his own land.' Jethro departs before Sinai — he does not receive the covenant directly (though Num 10:29-32 suggests he or his son Hobab may have traveled with Israel). The Gentile priest who confessed YHWH's supremacy, offered sacrifice, participated in the covenant meal, and reformed Israel's judicial system returns home. His contribution is complete; the Sinai narrative awaits.

22.1: If a man steals (ganov yignov) an ox (shor) or a sheep (seh): The doubled infinitive absolute ganov yignov (steal he steals) emphasizes the definite act. The asymmetric restitution — five oxen for one stolen ox, four sheep for one stolen sheep — reflects their relative economic value in ancient Israel: an ox was a working animal critical for plowing and food production; a sheep was smaller capital. The fivefold restitution for ox is the highest in the Code; theft of an ox effectively deprives a family of its livelihood.
22.2: If a thief is found breaking in (bamachtereth) and is struck and dies: Machtereth = the act of tunneling through a wall (from chatar, to dig/burrow); breaking into a house at night through the wall rather than the door. The law permits lethal self-defense at night — the darkness makes the intruder's intent uncertain. There shall be no blood guilt (ein lo damim): Damim (bloods, plural) = blood guilt/liability; the homeowner is not guilty of murder because he acted in self-defense under conditions of uncertainty.
22.3: But if the sun has risen on him (im zarach hashemesh alav), there shall be blood guilt: Daylight changes the moral calculus — in the daytime the homeowner can see the intruder and assess the threat; lethal force is not presumptively necessary. The distinction is one of the oldest graduated lethality-in-self-defense principles in legal history. The thief shall make restitution (shalleim y'shalleim): Shalom = peace/wholeness; shilleim = to make whole/restore; the doubled form is the standard legal intensifier for binding obligation.
22.4: If the stolen animal is found alive in his possession: The living animal in hand is evidence; restitution is twofold (double, not fivefold) because the animal has not been slaughtered or sold — the theft was caught before its consequences were fully enacted. The principle: restitution scales with the harm actually done, not just the crime attempted.
22.5: If a man causes a field or vineyard to be grazed over (ba'er) by letting his livestock loose: Ba'ar = to consume by grazing or burning; the root covers both animals browsing and fire consuming. The principle of negligent damage: the owner is responsible for what his livestock does on others' land. He shall make restitution from the best of his own field: The quality standard — meitav (the best/finest) — ensures the offended party receives above-replacement value, incentivizing careful livestock management.
22.6: If fire breaks out and catches in thorns (qotzim): Qotzim = thorns/briers; the dry thorns of the Palestinian summer are classic fire fuel. The law addresses negligent fire spread — someone burning a field who allows the fire to escape into a neighbor's grain or standing crops. Responsibility follows the ignition source, regardless of wind direction.
22.7: If a man gives his neighbor money or goods to keep (lishmor): Shamar = to guard/keep watch; the safekeeping/bailment relationship (mishmeret = guardianship). The stolen goods from a trusted keeper entail a different liability than open theft; the keeper must prove innocence before elohim (the judges or the divine tribunal).
22.8: If the thief is not found, the owner of the house shall come near to God (ha-Elohim): Ha-Elohim in the judicial sense = divine tribunal or judges acting as divine representatives. The repeated el ha-Elohim in this section (vv.8,9) may refer to a formal oath-taking before God or before human judges who represent divine authority. Disputed property cases go to the divine tribunal — human courts operate under divine oversight.
22.9: For every breach of trust (kol-davar pesha') — for ox, donkey, sheep, garment, or any lost thing: Pesha' = transgression/rebellion (the strongest Hebrew word for sin — deliberate violation of relationship); here used for breach of trust/fiduciary duty. Both parties bring their case before ha-Elohim; the guilty party pays double. The doubling principle (mishneh) is the base penalty in disputed cases.
