22.10: If a man gives his neighbor a donkey or ox or sheep or any beast to keep: The bailment law continues; the keeper who watches an animal that dies or is injured without witnesses (ein re'eh) swears an oath (shevuat YHWH) that he did not lay his hand on it — the oath transfers the case from human to divine adjudication.
22.11: An oath by YHWH shall be between them both: Shevuat YHWH = YHWH's oath/oath-by-YHWH; the divine name functions as the ultimate guarantor of truthfulness. The owner accepts the oath and the keeper is absolved — no restitution. If the animal is stolen (after the oath), the keeper must make restitution; the oath covered negligence, not theft.
22.12: If it was stolen from him, he shall make restitution to its owner: The keeper who allows theft bears liability — the obligation of safekeeping includes protecting from theft. But if torn by wild animals (tarof yitaref), the keeper brings the evidence (the torn carcass) and is absolved; he swears he did not eat it (lo yeshalleim).
22.13: If a wild beast has torn it (tarof yitaref), let him bring it as evidence: The doubled tarof yitaref (torn it was torn) signals the severity and definiteness of the loss. The torn carcass (ha-terefa) is brought as legal evidence of force majeure — the keeper cannot be held liable for acts of predation he could not prevent.
22.14: If a man borrows (yish'al) anything from his neighbor and it is injured or dies: Sha'al = to ask/borrow/request; the loan relationship (she'elah) creates the strictest liability — the borrower who uses another's animal bears full responsibility if it is harmed while in his use. The owner not being with it, he shall make full restitution: The owner's absence is the key legal fact; if the owner was present (imo, with him), the liability is reduced because the owner participated in the use.
22.15: If the owner was with it, he shall not make restitution: The owner's presence (imo = with him) during the use means he bears joint risk and has no claim. If it was hired, it came for its hire: The hired animal's risk is priced into the rental fee (sekaro ba ba'avuro = its wage came in its wages) — the renter has already paid for the assumed risk.
22.16: If a man seduces (yefateh) a virgin who is not betrothed: Pathah = to entice/persuade/seduce (also used of prophetic inspiration gone wrong — Jer 20:7); the consensual seduction of an unbetrothed virgin. The man must pay the mohar (bride price) and marry her — the legal remedy prioritizes the woman's long-term social protection over punitive measures. If her father refuses the marriage, the man still pays the full mohar.
22.17: If her father utterly refuses to give her to him: The father's veto (refusal to give = me'en yema'en) is legally binding; the daughter cannot be forced to marry her seducer if her father objects. He shall pay money equal to the bride price for virgins (kemoharn'betulot): Mohar = bride price (the payment from groom to bride's family that transferred legal responsibility and provided the bride with economic security); betulot = virgins.
22.18: You shall not permit a sorceress (m'kashapha) to live: Kashaph = to practice sorcery/witchcraft; the participle form identifies the person by ongoing practice. The feminine form (m'kashapha) is notable — sorcery was predominantly associated with women in ancient Near Eastern contexts. The capital penalty reflects the seriousness of the offense: the sorcerer claims to mediate between the human and divine realms outside YHWH's authorization.
22.19: Whoever lies with an animal (shochev im behemah) shall be put to death: Shachav = to lie/sleep with (the standard sexual euphemism); behemah = beast/livestock. The death penalty covers both human and animal (Lev 20:15-16). The violation of the created order (Gen 1's separation of species) is the underlying rationale; sexual congress across species categories inverts the creation mandate.
22.20: Whoever sacrifices (zovach) to any god (la-elohim acherim) other than YHWH alone shall be devoted to destruction (yochoram): Zovach = to slaughter as sacrifice; cherem (related to yochoram) = the devoted ban — complete destruction as YHWH's property, the highest and most irrevocable sanction in Israel's law. Sacrificing to other gods is not merely religious deviance but treason against the suzerain of the covenant.
22.21: You shall not wrong (toneh) a sojourner (ger): Yanah = to oppress/wrong/maltreat; ger = resident alien/sojourner (from gur, to sojourn/live as a guest). The ger is the vulnerable outsider living within Israelite society without tribal protection. The grounding for the prohibition is covenantal memory: you were gerim in Egypt (ki gerim heyitem b'eretz mitzrayim). Israel's experience of oppression creates the ethical obligation to protect those in the same social position.
22.22: You shall not afflict (t'anun) any widow (almanah) or fatherless child (yatom): Anah = to afflict/humble/oppress; almanah = widow (a woman without a male protector); yatom = orphan (literally fatherless — without a father's economic protection). The almanah-yatom-ger triad recurs throughout the law codes and prophets as the paradigm of the vulnerable for whom YHWH himself acts as protector. Their mistreatment triggers direct divine response.
22.23: If you do afflict them and they cry out to me (veitz'aku elai): Za'aq = to cry out in distress (the same verb as 2:23 — the slaves' cry); YHWH responds to the cry of the powerless with wrath (charah appi). The consequence is in kind: their widows and orphaned children (almnoteihem yih'yu almanot, u'vneichem yetomim) mirror the oppressor's victims. The covenant law has a built-in retributive logic: do to others what was done to you in Egypt, and I will do to your families what Egypt did to you.
22.24: If you lend money (halveh) to any of my people who is poor (aniy): Lavah = to lend/borrow (the borrower is servant to the lender — Prov 22:7 uses the same root). You shall not be like a moneylender (k'nosheh) to him, and you shall not exact interest (neshek): Nosheh = one who drives/exacts; neshek = interest (literally a bite — from nashak, to bite; interest that bites into principal). The anti-usury law is rooted in covenantal solidarity: the poor Israelite is ach (brother), not a business opportunity.
22.25: If you ever take your neighbor's cloak (salmah) in pledge (chabol tachbelenu): Salmah = garment/cloak (the outer garment that doubled as a blanket); chabol = to take a pledge/hold as security (from chebl = cord/band). The poor man's only covering — his cloak — cannot be permanently retained as collateral. You shall return it to him before the sun goes down: The sunset deadline (ad-ba hashemesh) transforms property law into a humanitarian obligation; the cloak is not merely collateral but the man's bedding.
22.26: For that is his only covering (ki hi kesuto levadah): Levadah = alone/only; kesut = covering. It is his cloak for his body; in what else shall he sleep? The rhetorical question (b'mah yishkav?) is an appeal to empathy embedded in the law itself. And if he cries to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate (chanun ani): Chanun = gracious/compassionate (one of the divine attributes of Exod 34:6-7); the same word used of YHWH's character at Sinai. The covenant law is not just a social contract but a reflection of the divine character.
22.27: You shall not revile God (elohim lo teqallel) nor curse a ruler (nasi) of your people: Qalal = to make light, curse, hold in contempt; elohim here may refer to God directly or to judges/rulers as divine representatives (the word is ambiguous in this context; the LXX reads 'gods' = judges). Nasi = leader/prince (literally 'one lifted up' from nasa'). Both the divine order and human governance are protected from the same verb — profaning authority at any level dishonors the structure YHWH has established.
22.28: You shall not delay to offer from the fullness of your harvest (male'atcha) and from the outflow of your presses (dem'acha): Male'ah = fullness/abundance; dema' = the first pressing/overflow of pressed grapes or oil. The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me: The firstborn son (bechor) dedication principle (cf. 13:2); the male is given to YHWH — later redeemed by the Levites (Num 3) or by the five-shekel redemption price.
22.29: You shall do the same with your oxen and with your sheep: The parallel between firstborn sons and firstborn animals in vv.28-30 frames the entire created order as YHWH's — humans and animals both subject to the firstborn principle. Seven days it shall be with its mother (shiv'at yamim yihyeh im-imo): The seven-day nursing period before separation echoes the creation week; the animal is given time to bond before being dedicated — a compassionate interval even in the consecration law.
22.30: On the eighth day (bayom hash'mini) you shall give it to me: The eighth day = the day after the complete week, the day of new beginnings. Circumcision also occurs on the eighth day (Gen 17:12). The pattern of seven-plus-one (fullness + new beginning) structures both animal dedication and human covenant initiation.
22.31: You shall be consecrated people to me (anshei qodesh tih'yun li): Anshei qodesh = men of holiness/consecrated people; the dietary conclusion of chapter 22 is framed as a holiness identity-statement, not merely a health rule. Therefore you shall not eat any flesh that is torn by beasts in the field (treifah): Treifah (from taraf, to tear/rend) = an animal killed by a predator in the field; this is the first explicit kashrut (dietary law) category outside the Leviticus system. You shall throw it to the dogs (la-kelev tashlichun oto): The torn carcass goes to scavengers — Israel is holy; scavenger food belongs to scavengers.

23.1: You shall not spread a false report (lo tissa shav): Nasa' = to lift up/carry; shav = emptiness/falsehood/vanity (the same word as the third commandment's 'in vain' — lo tissa et-shem YHWH lashav). To carry a false report is to do with a neighbor's reputation what the commandment forbids doing with YHWH's name. You shall not join hands with a wicked man to be a malicious witness (ed chamas): Ed chamas = witness of violence/injustice; chamas = violence/wrongdoing (the same term for the corruption that preceded the Flood, Gen 6:11). False witness is an act of violence against the innocent.
23.2: You shall not follow a majority to do evil (lo-tih'yeh acharei rabim l'ra'ot): Acharei rabim = after the many/majority; the law against mob justice — the majority principle applies to voting majorities in court cases but not to mob action. Nor shall you be partial (tit'neh) to a poor man in his lawsuit: Natal = to incline/be partial; even the poor (dal = thin/weak) receive no automatic benefit of judicial partiality. Justice is not a redistribution mechanism but a truth-seeking process.
23.3: You shall not be partial to a poor man (dal) in his lawsuit: The prohibition of favoritism toward the poor in court — a surprising law that balances the many laws protecting the poor from oppression. Justice is impartial; poverty does not create automatic legal advantage. Cf. Lev 19:15: 'you shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great.'
23.4: If you meet your enemy's (oyivcha) ox or his donkey going astray, you shall bring it back to him: Oyev = enemy (personal adversary, not necessarily a foreign enemy). The return of the enemy's stray animal is the first formal enemy-love law in the Hebrew Bible — predating and forming the backdrop to Jesus's 'love your enemies' (Matt 5:44). The law does not require loving feelings but loving actions: the animal must be returned regardless of the relationship.
23.5: If you see the donkey of one who hates you (son'acha) lying down under its burden: Sone' = one who hates/is hostile to you; the law extends even to the enemy's animal. You shall refrain from leaving him with it; you shall rescue it with him (azov ta'azov imo): The doubled infinitive absolute (azov ta'azov) is emphatic — you shall certainly help. The phrasing 'with him' (imo) implies joint effort; the law creates a situation of forced cooperation between enemies. Working side by side to rescue an animal is itself a social reconciliation mechanism.
23.6: You shall not pervert the justice (mishpat) due to your poor (evyoncha) in his lawsuit: Evyon = destitute/poor (a stronger term than dal — the utterly impoverished, with no resources); mishpat = justice/judgment/rule. The legal system must serve the poor; its primary test is whether it protects the powerless. The poor man's case (rib = dispute/case/lawsuit) must be heard on its merits.
23.7: Keep far from a false charge (davar shav): Shav = emptiness/falsehood (same as v.1); Do not kill the innocent (naqi) and righteous (tzaddiq): Naqi = clean/innocent; tzaddiq = righteous/in the right. For I will not acquit the wicked (rasha'): YHWH's own justice is the model and guarantee for the human court; the divine standard backs the human legal order.
23.8: And you shall take no bribe (shochad): Shachad = a gift/bribe offered to pervert justice; shochad = the bribe. For a bribe blinds the clear-sighted (piqchim) and subverts the cause of those who are in the right (tzaddiqim): Piqeach = one who sees clearly/is sighted (the opposite of blind); the bribe reverses vision — the seeing become blind, the righteous become perverted. The bribery prohibition is the foundational anti-corruption clause of Israelite jurisprudence.
23.9: You shall not oppress (tilchatz) a sojourner (ger): Lachatz = to press/oppress/squeeze (related to the Egyptian lachatz in 3:9 — the oppression Israel experienced). You know the heart (nefesh) of a sojourner: The use of nefesh (soul/inner life) for 'the experience/feeling of a foreigner' is striking — Israel is commanded to empathize with the ger's inner life because they know it experientially. The ethical command is grounded in experiential memory, not abstract principle.
23.10: For six years (shesh shanim) you shall sow your land: The sabbath-year (shemittah) law opens with the positive command: six years of full cultivation. The Sabbath principle extends from the week to the year — creation rest is built into the agricultural calendar. Israel's economy must breathe.
23.11: But in the seventh year (hashevii't) you shall let it rest and lie fallow (tishmetennah u'netashtah): Shamat = to release/remit/let fall; natash = to let loose/abandon. The land gets rest — not merely a human agricultural technique but a theological statement: the land belongs to YHWH and must observe his rest (cf. Lev 25:2-4). That the poor of your people (aniyei ammecha) may eat: The fallow year's produce is freely available to the poor — the land's rest creates a welfare system for those who cannot purchase food. What the land produces without cultivation belongs to all.
23.12: Six days you shall do your work, but on the seventh day you shall rest (tishbot): Shavat = to cease/rest (the Sabbath verb from shabat); the Sabbath law restated in the social context of the Covenant Code — not just a creation ordinance but a social one. That your ox and your donkey may have rest, and the son of your servant woman and the alien (ger) may be refreshed (yinnafesh): Nafash = to take breath/be refreshed (from nefesh = breath/soul); even animals and resident aliens participate in the Sabbath rest. The Sabbath is the great equalizer: for one day, master and slave, citizen and alien, human and animal all rest together.
23.13: Pay attention to all that I have said to you, and make no mention of the names of other gods (shem elohim acherim): The law against invoking the names of foreign deities — shem (name) + zakar (to remember/mention). Names were not merely labels but invocations of power; to speak the name of a god was to invite its presence. The prohibition is reinforced: 'let it not be heard on your lips (lo yishama al-picha)' — the absolute exclusion of pagan invocation from Israelite speech.
23.14: Three times in the year (shalosh regalim) you shall keep a feast to me: Shalosh regalim = three foot-pilgrimages (regel = foot; the festivals were occasions for physical pilgrimage to the central sanctuary). The pilgrim-festival system is the organizing principle of Israel's annual liturgical calendar, integrating agricultural rhythms with redemptive history.
23.15: You shall keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread (chag hamatzot): The spring festival (month of Abib) commemorates the Exodus; seven days of matzot = the re-enacted experience of the departure. None shall appear before me empty-handed (lo-yeira'u fanai reqam): The Niphal of ra'ah (to be seen before) — pilgrimage to YHWH's presence requires bringing an offering; appearing empty-handed (reqam = empty) is not an option. The offering is the form of the encounter.
23.16: You shall keep the Feast of Harvest (chag hakatzir), of the firstfruits (bikkurim) of your labor: Katzir = harvest; bikkurim = firstfruits (from bakar, to be first); the late-spring grain festival, later called Shavuot (Weeks) or Pentecost (50 days from Passover). And the Feast of Ingathering (chag ha'asif) at the end of the year: Asif = gathering in; the autumn harvest festival (later called Sukkot = Booths), when all crops are brought in before the rains. The three festivals map the agricultural year (spring planting → early harvest → late harvest) onto redemptive commemoration.
23.17: Three times in the year all your males shall appear before the Lord YHWH (yira'eh kol-z'kurcha el-p'nei ha'adon YHWH): The triple pilgrimage requirement creates a national rhythm of covenant renewal; every adult male physically presents himself before YHWH's presence three times annually. The use of ha'adon (the Lord/Master) alongside YHWH is rare — the full divine title emphasizes the feudal-suzerain relationship of the Covenant.
23.18: You shall not offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened (chametz): Sacrificial blood and leavened bread cannot occupy the same space — the unleavened purity of the Passover extends to all blood-sacrifice. The fat (chelev) of my feast shall not remain until morning: Chelev = the richest fat portions dedicated to YHWH; they must be consumed the same night — no overnight storage of the dedicated portions.
23.19: The best (reshit) of the firstfruits (bikkurei) of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God: Reshit = beginning/best/firstling; bikkurim = firstfruits; the double emphasis (best + first) maximizes the offering. You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk (lo tevashel gedi bachalav imo): The foundational prohibition of kashrut meat/milk separation; it appears three times in the Torah (here, Exod 34:26, Deut 14:21). The exact rationale is debated: Canaanite ritual practice? Compassion for the maternal bond? A symbolic refusal to use the life-giving substance (milk) to destroy life (boiling)? The Talmud extends it to complete separation of meat and dairy.
23.20: Behold, I send an angel (malach) before you to guard you on the way and to bring you to the place that I have prepared: Malach = messenger/angel (from the root for mission/commission); this is the mal'ach ha-panim (angel of the presence) — the divine emissary who accompanies Israel through the wilderness. Guard you on the way (lishmorcha baderech) and to bring you to the place: The angel serves as both protector and guide, performing the same functions as the pillar of cloud and fire.
23.21: Pay careful attention to him and obey his voice; do not rebel (al-tammer) against him: Marar = to be bitter/rebellious; the cognate of marah (the bitter waters). For he will not pardon your transgression (lo yissa l'pish'achem): Nasa' = to carry/bear/forgive; the angel will not bear/forgive rebellion because YHWH's name is in him (ki shmi bo = for my name is in him). The angel's authority is derivative — his name is YHWH's name; rebellion against the angel is rebellion against YHWH.
23.22: But if you carefully obey his voice and do all that I say, then I will be an enemy to your enemies (v'ayavti et-oyavecha) and an adversary to your adversaries (v'tzarti et-tzorecha): The covenant protective formula — YHWH mirrors Israel's conflict relationships. Oyev/tzar (enemy/adversary) are both political and spiritual terms; the protection covers all who oppose Israel's covenant fidelity.
23.23: When my angel goes before you and brings you to the Amorites and the Hittites and the Perizzites and the Canaanites, the Hivites and the Jebusites, and I blot them out (v'hichadtiv): Six-nation formula (vs. the usual seven or ten — variations signal different sources or rhetorical contexts); hachadah = to cut off/destroy. The extermination of the Canaanite nations is here presented as the direct act of YHWH through his angel — not Israelite military prowess but divine warfare.
23.24: You shall not bow down to their gods (lo tishtacheveh l'eloheihem) or serve them (lo ta'avdem): Shachah = to prostrate/bow in worship; abad = to serve/work; the two verbs of religious service are prohibited together. You shall utterly overthrow them (ki haros tiharesem) and break their pillars (v'shabeir t'shabeir matzevotam) in pieces: Haras = to tear down; shabar = to break/shatter; matzevah = standing stone/sacred pillar (the Canaanite cultic monuments that YHWH requires destroyed).
23.25: You shall serve (avadtem) YHWH your God, and he will bless your bread and your water: The exclusive service formula: avad YHWH (to serve/work for YHWH) is the positive counterpart to the prohibition of serving foreign gods. I will take sickness (machalah) away from among you: Machalah = disease/sickness (cf. 15:26 — YHWH the healer); the covenant blessing of health is the positive form of the disease-threat (Deut 28's curse list). Obedience → blessing of provision; disobedience → curse of disease.
23.26: None shall miscarry (m'shakkelet) or be barren (aqarah) in your land: Shakol = to miscarry/lose young (the verb of Rachel's fear and Jacob's grief — Gen 31:38, 43:14); aqarah = barren (the term for Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — all whose barrenness precedes miraculous birth). The covenant blessing reverses the curse of infertility; covenant fidelity produces abundant offspring. I will fulfill the number of your days (et-mispar yamecha amaleh): Long life as covenant blessing — the years of one's life are in YHWH's hand to complete.
23.27: I will send my terror (emati) before you: Emah = terror/dread (the same word as the Song of the Sea, 15:16 — 'terror and dread fall upon them'); the divine terror-weapon precedes Israel's advance. I will throw into confusion (hamoti) all the people against whom you shall come: Hamam = to throw into confusion/panic (the same verb as 14:24 — YHWH throwing the Egyptian army into panic); divine warfare tactics: terror sent ahead, confusion at contact.
23.28: I will send hornets (tzirah) before you: Tzirah = hornet (an actual species, but also possibly a metaphor for terror-inducing panic or a supernatural agent); the word appears only here, Deut 7:20, and Josh 24:12. Whether literal insects or a metaphor for the demoralizing terror that drives nations into flight, the tzirah accomplishes what the human army alone cannot: pre-battlefield psychological collapse. Which shall drive out (v'gershah) the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you: Garash = to drive out/expel (the verb of expulsion from Eden — Gen 3:24; and of Pharaoh expelling Moses — Exod 10:11).
23.29: I will not drive them out from before you in one year, lest the land become desolate (shamamah) and the wild beasts (chayat hasadeh) multiply against you: Shamamah = desolation/waste; the theological realism of the conquest — a sudden total expulsion would leave the land uninhabitable. YHWH's timing serves both Israel's security and the land's ecological stability. The text acknowledges the complexity of settling a land that has been cultivated by others: vacated land becomes wild.
23.30: Little by little (meat meat) I will drive them out from before you, until you have increased and possess the land: Meat meat = a little a little (the repeated diminutive for gradual process); the same principle applies to spiritual formation — YHWH works incrementally, matching the pace to the people's readiness. Until you have increased (parita) and possess the land: Parah = to be fruitful/multiply (the creation blessing verb of Gen 1:28); Israel's growth justifies the expansion of settlement.
23.31: I will set your border from the Red Sea to the Sea of the Philistines (Yam Pelishtim), and from the wilderness to the Euphrates (Nahar): The maximal promised land dimensions — from Yam Suph (Gulf of Aqaba) to the Mediterranean (Yam Pelishtim), from the Arabian desert to the Euphrates. This is the territorial ideal, partially realized under David and Solomon (1 Kgs 4:21). For I will give the inhabitants of the land into your hand: The divine land-gift is the basis for human military action; the conquest is YHWH's work, not Israel's.
23.32: You shall make no covenant (lo tichrot lahem brit) with them or with their gods: Karat brit = to cut a covenant (the technical term for covenant-making, from the cutting of the covenant sacrifice — Gen 15:18); the prohibition of covenant-making with Canaanites is comprehensive: no political treaty, no religious accommodation. The exclusive loyalty demanded by the covenant brooks no competing alliance structure.
23.33: They shall not dwell in your land, lest they make you sin against me (yachatiu otcha li): Chata = to miss the mark/sin; the resident Canaanites would serve as a mokesh (snare/trap — from yaqash, to trap birds) — a mechanism drawing Israel into their religious practices. The logic of the prohibition: tolerated idolatry becomes contagious. The mokesh (snare) image recurs in Deut 7:16, Judg 2:3 — its anticipation here proves accurate in the subsequent history.

24.1: Then he said to Moses, 'Come up to YHWH (aleh el-YHWH), you and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel': The seventy elders (shiv'im) represent the full council of Israel (cf. Num 11:16-17; the seventy nations of Gen 10); Nadab and Abihu are Aaron's eldest sons who will later die for offering unauthorized fire (Lev 10). Their presence here at the covenant ratification makes their subsequent death more tragic — they witnessed the covenant but violated its worship standards. The number seventy = the fullness of the council.
24.2: Moses alone shall come near to YHWH (ve'nigash Moshe levaddo el-YHWH): Nagash = to draw near (the approach verb for both priests and worshippers); levaddo = alone, by himself. The hierarchy of access is explicit: Moses alone crosses the divine threshold; the elders and Aaron worship from a distance (min-rachok = from afar). The gradation anticipates the tabernacle's concentric zones of holiness.
24.3: Moses came and told the people all the words of YHWH and all the rules (kol-divrei YHWH ve'et kol-hamishpatim): Divrei YHWH = the words of YHWH (the Decalogue); hamishpatim = the rules/case laws (the Covenant Code of chs.21-23). And all the people answered with one voice (kol-echad): 'All the words that YHWH has spoken we will do (na'aseh).' The corporate consent — kol-echad (one voice) — is the ideal of covenant community. Na'aseh is the first of the famous na'aseh v'nishma of v.7.
24.4: And Moses wrote (vayyichtov Moshe) all the words of YHWH: The first explicit statement that Moses wrote the Torah (the words of YHWH); the covenant-writing that forms the basis of the sefer ha-brit (book of the covenant). Writing creates permanence and accountability; the spoken covenant becomes a legal document. He rose early in the morning (vayyashkem baboqer) and built an altar: Rising early (hashkem) is the verb of diligent, devoted action throughout the Pentateuch (Abraham in Gen 22:3 — the same urgency).
24.5: And he sent young men (na'are) of the people of Israel, who offered burnt offerings (olot) and sacrificed peace offerings (sh'lamim) of oxen: Na'ar = young man/attendant (not yet the Levitical priesthood — Aaron and his sons have not yet been ordained; these are lay offerers acting on Moses's behalf). Olah = the wholly-consumed burnt offering (from alah, to go up — the offering ascends entirely to YHWH); sh'lamim = peace/fellowship/completeness offerings (from shalom — the offering that restores wholeness and allows communion eating).
24.6: And Moses took half of the blood (chatzi hadam) and put it in basins, and half of the blood he threw (zaraq) against the altar: The bifurcation of the covenant blood — half on the altar (YHWH's side) and half to be sprinkled on the people (v.8) — is the structural act of blood-covenant: both parties are marked with the same blood. Zaraq = to throw/sprinkle (the Levitical term for sacrificial blood-manipulation at the altar). The altar represents YHWH; the people receive the corresponding sprinkle.
24.7: Then he took the Book of the Covenant (sefer ha-brit) and read it in the hearing of the people: Sefer ha-brit = Book of the Covenant (the first named Torah document — the written record of chs.20-23 that becomes the treaty text of the Sinai covenant). And they said, 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do, and we will be obedient (na'aseh v'nishma).' Na'aseh v'nishma = 'we will do and we will hear/obey' — the famous double assent. The order (doing before hearing) has fascinated rabbinic and Christian theology: the rabbis say Israel accepted the covenant before fully understanding it (trust before comprehension); Paul reads it as grace-response before law-understanding.
24.8: And Moses took the blood and threw it on the people (vayizroq al-ha'am) and said, 'Behold the blood of the covenant (hineh dam ha-brit) that YHWH has made with you in accordance with all these words': Dam ha-brit = blood of the covenant — the defining phrase that Jesus quotes verbatim at the Last Supper (Matt 26:28: 'this is my blood of the covenant'; cf. Mark 14:24; 1 Cor 11:25). The Sinai blood-covenant is the type; Jesus's blood-covenant is the antitype. Moses's blood thrown on the people physically marks them as covenant partners; Jesus's blood covers those who receive it in faith.
24.9: Then Moses and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel went up: The covenant ratification is followed immediately by covenant communion — the elders ascend to see God. The progression: proclamation (v.3) → writing (v.4) → sacrifice (v.5-6) → consent (v.7) → blood-marking (v.8) → vision (vv.9-11) is the complete covenant ratification sequence.
24.10: And they saw the God of Israel (vayir'u et Elohei Yisrael): The elders see God — the most remarkable statement in the pericope. There was under his feet as it were a pavement of sapphire stone (kivnat ha-sapir), like the very heaven for clearness: Livnat = pavement/brick (from levanah = white/brick; also the word for bricks Israel made in Egypt — the redeemed slaves now see the divine pavement that transcends all human construction). Sapir = sapphire (or lapis lazuli); the heavenly blue pavement recalls Ezek 1:26 and 10:1 (the sapphire firmament under the divine throne). The clarity (kaazem hashamayim latohar = like the very sky for purity) emphasizes the absolute transparency of the divine realm.
24.11: And he did not lay his hand (lo shalach yado) against the chief men of the people of Israel: The diplomatic understatement — YHWH had the power to destroy them for the audacity of seeing him, but restrained it (cf. Exod 33:20 — 'no man can see me and live'). The elders' survival is itself a revelation of grace. They beheld God, and ate and drank: The covenant meal eaten in YHWH's presence — the elders eat and drink before God in the same way that a suzerain's covenant vassals shared a table with their overlord. Covenant eating = covenant belonging.
24.12: YHWH said to Moses, 'Come up to me on the mountain and wait there, that I may give you the tablets of stone (luchot ha'even) with the law and the commandment (ha-Torah ve'ha-mitzvah):' Luchot = tablets/boards; even = stone; Torah = instruction/teaching (the comprehensive term); mitzvah = commandment (the specific directive). The stone tablets inscribed by YHWH (31:18 — 'written with the finger of God') are the permanent documentary form of the covenant. That you may teach them (l'horotam): The Hiphil of yarah (to teach/instruct) — the same root as Torah; YHWH gives Moses the Torah to teach Israel.
24.13: So Moses rose with his assistant Joshua (Yehoshua): This is Joshua's first appearance in Exodus as Moses's mesharet (minister/attendant — from sharath, to serve/minister; the same term for angels ministering before YHWH). Joshua will not go to the summit but accompanies Moses partway; his presence here anticipates his role as Moses's successor and the leader of the conquest.
24.14: He said to the elders, 'Wait here for us until we return to you. And behold, Aaron and Hur are with you. Whoever has a dispute (mi ba'al devarim), let him go to them.' Ba'al devarim = owner of words/matters (one who has a legal case or dispute); devarim = words/matters/things. Aaron and Hur are left as judicial deputies. Hur (mentioned only here and 17:10-12 at Rephidim) is traditionally identified as the husband of Miriam or Caleb's son; he disappears from the narrative after this, which has led to speculation about his fate at the golden calf incident.
24.15: Then Moses went up on the mountain, and the cloud (he'anan) covered the mountain: The cloud (anan) that led Israel through the wilderness now settles on Sinai as the medium of divine presence. The cloud that was a pillar of protection in transit becomes a static covering in theophany.
24.16: The glory of YHWH (kevod YHWH) dwelt (vayishkon) on Mount Sinai: Kavod = glory/weight; shakan = to dwell/tabernacle (the root of mishkan = tabernacle, and Shekinah = divine dwelling presence). The kavod shakan formula is the technical term for the divine presence settling in a place — the same formula will be used for the tabernacle's dedication (40:34-35) and the Temple's dedication (1 Kgs 8:11). And the cloud covered it six days: Six days of cloud-covered silence before the seventh — the Sabbath structure of Sinai theophany mirrors the creation week.
24.17: Now the appearance of the glory of YHWH was like a devouring fire (ke'ur esh ocheleth) on the top of the mountain: Ocheleth = consuming/devouring (from achal = to eat); the glory appears to the Israelites below as a consuming fire — the same image as Deut 4:24 ('for YHWH your God is a devouring fire') and Heb 12:29 ('our God is a consuming fire'). The fire of holiness consumes what is unholy — in the context of covenant it is simultaneously inviting and terrifying.
24.18: Moses entered the cloud (vayyavo Moshe betoch he'anan) and went up on the mountain: The climax of the approach: Moses enters the cloud — the divine presence itself. He is not destroyed because he goes at YHWH's invitation; the covenant mediator crosses the threshold of the divine holiness under divine commission. And Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights (arba'im yom v'arba'im lailah): The first of three forty-day periods at Sinai (here; 34:28 after the golden calf; cf. Deut 9:18,25). The forty-day pattern becomes the biblical number for a complete cycle of divine preparation: Moses, Elijah at Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8), Jesus in the wilderness (Matt 4:2). Forty = the complete period of testing, transformation, and formation.

28.1: veAta haqrev eleycha et-Aharon achicha: 'Bring near to yourself Aaron your brother.' The verb qarav (to bring near, approach) is the primary term for priestly access to YHWH — the priest is the one who is permitted to approach what is otherwise unapproachable. The appointment of Aaron and his four sons (Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar) from among the Israelites establishes the Aaronic priesthood as a subset of the people, appointed by divine sovereign choice — not hereditary nobility but divine calling. Hebrews 5:4 applies this principle to Christ: 'No one takes this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was.'
28.2: bigdei-kodesh (bigdê-qōdesh): 'sacred/holy garments' — dedicated garments that belong to the sphere of holiness (qodesh). The purpose stated: lecavod ul-tifaret ('for glory and for splendor/beauty') — the same pair used of YHWH's own nature in various doxologies. The high priest's vestments embody divine glory in visible, wearable form. The priest wearing bigdei kavod is clothed with the glory of the one he represents. Paul's 'clothe yourselves with Christ' (Gal 3:27; Rom 13:14) draws on the vestment-as-identity metaphor: believers wear Christ as the priest wore the sacred garments.
28.3: chakhmei-lev (ḥakhmê-lēb): 'wise of heart' — the craftsmen appointed to make the priestly garments are those to whom YHWH has given ruach chakhmah (spirit of wisdom, v. 3). The craftsmanship of the sacred garments requires divine wisdom, not merely technical skill. The same divine wisdom-giving appears in Bezalel's commissioning (31:3: 'I have filled him with the Spirit of God in wisdom'). The connection between the Spirit's presence and skillful sacred work is consistent throughout Exodus: the tabernacle is built under divine wisdom, not human ingenuity.
28.4: choshen ve-ephod ume'il vekhutonet tashbetz umitznefet ve'avnet: the six elements of the high priestly vestments — (1) choshen mishpat (breastpiece of judgment/decision); (2) ephod (the sacred apron/vestment); (3) me'il (the robe of the ephod, entirely blue); (4) kutonet tashbetz (woven/checkered tunic); (5) mitznefet (turban); (6) avnet (sash/girdle). Together these constitute the bigdei hakodesh — the full vestments that mark the high priest as the mediator between YHWH and Israel. Each element has both functional and symbolic significance, developed in the chapters following. Hebrews sees the entire Aaronic high-priestly role as a type of Christ's permanent and perfect high priesthood (Heb 7-10).
28.5: zahav utekhelet veargaman vetola'at shani veshesh: 'gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen' — the five materials of the priestly vestments, identical to the five materials of the tabernacle curtains (26:1). The color sequence is consistently ordered: blue (tekhelet), purple (argaman), scarlet (tola'at shani). Tekhelet is the blue from the Murex snail, used also for the tassels (tzitzit, Num 15:38) as a reminder of the commandments. Purple was the most expensive dye in the ANE — associated with royalty. The high priestly colors encode royal and priestly identity: the high priest is simultaneously priest and (symbolic) king, prefiguring the Melchizedekian pattern (Gen 14:18; Ps 110:4; Heb 7).
28.6: ephod (ʾēp̄ōd, H646): the sacred apron/vestment, the defining garment of priestly identity. The word's etymology is uncertain; the ephod appears in various contexts as both a priestly garment (here) and an oracular object (Judg 8:27; 17:5). The high priestly ephod is made of the finest materials including woven gold thread — an extraordinary luxury item. The ephod bears the choshen (breastpiece) and the avnei shoham (onyx stones) with the tribal names. David wearing a linen ephod before the ark (2 Sam 6:14) echoes the high priestly role — the Davidic king functioning in priestly capacity, anticipating the Melchizedekian priest-king of Ps 110.
28.7: shtei ktafot chovrot: 'two shoulder pieces joining it together' — the ephod is joined by two shoulder pieces (ktafot, shoulders). The shoulders in biblical imagery carry weight and responsibility: the government is 'on his shoulder' (Isa 9:6, of the Messiah); the lost sheep is carried on the shepherd's shoulders (Luke 15:5). The high priest's shoulders bear the names of Israel (v. 12) — he carries the whole nation on his shoulders before YHWH. Christ's high-priestly bearing of his people is visualized in this architectural detail.
28.8: cheshev epudato: 'the skilled waistband of the ephod' — the cheshev (artistic band/waistband) is woven from the same materials as the ephod and forms a single piece with it. The term cheshev (H2805) is from chashav (to think, plan, design) — the band is the work of skilled designing (machashevet). The unity of the waistband with the ephod (they are one piece) pictures the integrity of the priestly vestment: all elements form a coherent whole, nothing attached carelessly.
28.9: avnei shoham (onyx stones, H7718): two onyx stones engraved with the names of the twelve sons of Israel (six on each stone). The onyx is described as being set in mishbetzot zahav (gold filigree settings). The tribal names engraved on precious stones are a permanent inscription — the names are cut in, not painted over, and the stones are valuable. The priest carries Israel's identity in permanent, costly form on his shoulders. Compare Rev 21:12-14 where the twelve gates and twelve foundations of the new Jerusalem bear the names of the twelve tribes and twelve apostles — the priestly bearing of Israel's names finds its eschatological fulfillment in the new Jerusalem itself.
28.10: betoledotam: 'in the order of their birth' — the six names on each stone are ordered by birth sequence, not by tribal prominence or later canonical order. The historical order of birth (Reuben through Joseph and Benjamin) establishes the continuity between the patriarchal narratives and the tabernacle priesthood: the same twelve sons of Jacob whose story fills Genesis are now permanently inscribed on the high priest's garments. The priest carries not only the present community but the entire history of the covenant line.
28.11: pituchei chotam: 'engravings of a seal/signet' — the names are engraved on the stones the way a professional seal-engraver cuts a signet ring (chotam, H2368). The signet ring in the ANE was a person's legal identity — to press one's signet into clay was to commit oneself legally. The high priest bears Israel's names engraved as legal seals on his shoulders: he is the legal representative, the advocate (parakletos, cf. 1 John 2:1), bearing the people's identity before the divine court. Each stone is set in gold (mishbetzot zahav), the most honorable setting available.
28.12: avnei zikaron livenei Yisrael veNasa Aharon et-shemotam lifnei YHWH al-shtei ktefav lezikaron: 'stones of remembrance for the sons of Israel; Aaron shall bear their names before YHWH on his two shoulders as a memorial.' The threefold repetition — bear their names, before YHWH, memorial — establishes the theology: the high priest's primary function is representational intercession. He brings Israel's names into the divine presence. Zikaron (memorial) — YHWH “remembers” his covenant commitments; the memorial stones activate covenant memory. This is precisely what Christ does as our great high priest: he carries us before the Father permanently (Heb 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them').
28.13: mishbetzot zahav: gold filigree settings — the most technically refined goldsmithing, using twisted wire meshwork to hold precious stones. The word mishbetzot (H4865) suggests a latticed or checkered cage of gold wire. The settings connect the breastpiece chains to the shoulder pieces of the ephod. The entire construction uses gold as the primary connecting material — the most precious and imperishable metal — linking the people's names (on the shoulder stones) to the decision-making breastpiece at the heart.
28.14: sharsherot gabiot: 'twisted chains, rope-like' — pure gold chains braided like rope. The imagery of twisted gold chains connecting the breastpiece to the shoulder stones pictures the inseparable link between the priest's bearing of the people's names (on shoulders) and his intercession for their justice/decisions (at the heart). The chains are elaborately constructed (gabiot, twisted/braided) to be both beautiful and strong — the priestly intercession is both aesthetically glorious and structurally unbreakable.
28.15: choshen mishpat (ḥōšen mišpāṭ): 'breastpiece of judgment/decision' — worn over Aaron's heart (al-libo, v. 29-30). Mishpat (H4941) covers the semantic range of judgment, justice, legal decision, and ordinance. The breastpiece is the medium through which YHWH renders decisions for Israel: the Urim and Thummim (v. 30) are placed within it. The physical location — over the heart — is theologically significant: YHWH's decisions for Israel are rendered through the priest's heart, the seat of will and affection. The priest is not a detached functionary but a person whose heart is fully engaged with Israel's fate before YHWH.
28.16: zeret orko vezeret rochbo: 'a span long and a span wide' — the breastpiece is square, one zeret (span, H2239 — the distance from the thumb to the little finger when spread, approximately 9 inches) in each dimension, and folded double to form a pocket. The doubling creates a pouch that holds the Urim and Thummim (v. 30). The square form is significant: the holy of holies is cubic (1 Kings 6:20), the new Jerusalem is a cube (Rev 21:16), the altar's top is square (27:1) — squareness represents divine completeness and order.
28.17: ve'milata vo miluat even arba turim even: 'you shall fill it with stone settings, four rows of stones' — the breastpiece holds twelve stones in four rows of three, one for each tribe of Israel. The stones (even, H68) are precious gems. The first row: odem (carnelian/ruby), pitda (chrysolite/topaz), bareqet (emerald/malachite). The identification of the specific stones is uncertain as ancient gem nomenclature does not map precisely to modern terminology. What is certain is that twelve distinct precious stones, each unique, represent the twelve distinct tribes — diverse but equally valued before YHWH.
28.18: veHashor sheni nofech sapir veyahalom: 'the second row: turquoise, lapis lazuli, and moonstone/diamond.' Sapir (H5601) is typically translated sapphire or lapis lazuli — the deep blue stone associated with the divine throne (Exod 24:10: under YHWH's feet was like a pavement of sapir; Ezek 1:26: the throne-appearance like sapir). The presence of the sapphire/lapis lazuli in the breastpiece connects the priestly judgment-stones to the divine throne-imagery — the priest who wears the breastpiece stands before the same throne that sapphire/lapis underlies.
28.19: veHashor hashlishi leshem shevo ve'achlamah: 'the third row: jacinth/ligure, agate, and amethyst.' The stones of the breastpiece cover the full spectrum of precious gems known to the ancient world — red, blue, green, yellow, purple, white. The full spectrum encodes a theology: Israel's twelve tribes represent the full range of human diversity, all equally precious to YHWH, all equally requiring the priest's intercession. The twelve-stone breastpiece is the OT's most visual statement of YHWH's equal regard for the whole covenant community regardless of tribal rank.
28.20: veHashor harevi'i tarshish veshaoham veyashfeh meshubatzim zahav yihyu bemillu'otam: 'the fourth row: chrysolite, onyx, and jasper, set in gold filigree in their mounting.' Tarshish (H8658) is uncertain but may be beryl or chrysolite — associated with Tarshish in Spain or with the gleaming wheels of the divine chariot (Ezek 1:16: 'the appearance of the wheels was like tarshish'). Yashfeh (jasper) appears as the first stone of the new Jerusalem's foundation wall (Rev 21:19), and the appearance of the one on the throne is like jasper (Rev 4:3) — the high priestly breastpiece stones are distributed into the eschatological new Jerusalem's foundational structure.
28.21: veha'avanim tihyena al-shemot benei Yisrael: 'the stones shall be for the names of the sons of Israel' — explicitly: twelve stones, twelve names, twelve tribes. The one-to-one correspondence between tribe and gem is stated clearly, though the specific tribal assignments for each gem are not given here (a later tradition in Rev 21 assigns stones to the apostolic foundations). The breastpiece as a whole functions as a comprehensive intercession: the priest prays for the whole nation, not for favorites. Hebrews 7:25's 'he always lives to make intercession' has this all-comprehensive priestly inclusiveness in view.
28.22: veasita al-hachoshen sharshrot gabiot ma'aseh avot zahav tahor: 'for the breastpiece make twisted chains of pure gold, rope-work.' The breastpiece is attached to the ephod by matching chains and rings — the connections are multiple and reinforced. The entire construction of rings, chains, filigree, and cords creates a garment that cannot be accidentally displaced: the breastpiece is fixed over the heart by design. The permanence of the priest's intercession is encoded in the structural stability of the vestment: Aaron cannot minister before YHWH with the breastpiece separated from the ephod (v. 28).
28.23: vetasita al-hachoshen shtei taba'ot zahav: 'make two gold rings for the breastpiece.' The gold rings at the corners of the breastpiece form the attachment points for the chains. The doubling of rings (two at the top corners, two at the bottom corners) and the doubled cord (blue cord at the bottom, gold chains at the top) reflects the meticulous security of the construction. The high priestly vestment system is engineered so that nothing can fall off or come loose during the ritual service.
28.24: venatata et-shtei anefot hasharsherot: 'attach the two chains of gold to the two rings.' The chains connect the top corners of the breastpiece to the gold filigree settings on the shoulder pieces. The geometry of the connection ensures that when Aaron stands upright in the holy place, the breastpiece hangs naturally over his heart. The engineering of the vestment is inseparable from its theology: the breastpiece must lie over the heart, because the priest's intercession for Israel is a matter of the heart, not merely of duty.
28.25: vet-shtei qetzot shtei ha'anefot titen al-shtei hammishbetzot: 'the other ends of the chains attach to the two settings' on the shoulder pieces. The breastpiece-to-shoulder connection creates a structural integrity: the bearing of names on the shoulders (vv. 9-12) is mechanically linked to the bearing of names on the heart (v. 29). The same priestly act that lifts Israel's names on the shoulders of intercession also places those names over the heart of compassion: power and affection work together in priestly intercession.
28.26: vetasita shtei taba'ot zahav vesamta otam al-shtei qetzot hachoshen al-sfato: 'make two more gold rings and attach them to the two lower corners of the breastpiece on its inner edge.' The lower rings and the blue cord (v. 28) that connects the breastpiece to the ephod's waistband prevent the breastpiece from swinging outward. Multiple attachment points ensure stability. The combination of upper-chain and lower-cord attachments mirrors how effective intercession is held in place by both the dignity of the priest's authority (chains/shoulder) and the obedience of the priestly covenant (tekhelet/blue cord symbolizing commandment-keeping, Num 15:38).
28.27: vetasita shtei taba'ot zahav venatatah otam al-shtei ktafot ha'ephod: 'make two more gold rings and attach them to the bottom of the shoulder pieces on the front of the ephod, close to the seam above the ephod's waistband.' The precision of the lower attachment mirrors the precision of the upper: every connection is specified, every angle determined. The meticulous instructions for the vestments reflect the same principle as the meticulous tabernacle pattern: the earthly copy must match the heavenly original exactly (25:9). Careless reproduction produces a lesser mediator; the copy must be faithful to the original.
28.28: veyirtzu et-hachoshen mitaba'otav el-taba'ot ha'ephod bifitil tekhelet: 'the breastpiece shall be bound to the rings of the ephod with a blue cord.' The tekhelet (blue cord) that fastens the breastpiece to the ephod is the same blue used throughout the tabernacle system. Blue (tekhelet) in Israel's symbolism is the color of heaven and of the commandments (Num 15:38-40: blue cord on tassels to remind Israel of YHWH's commandments). The breastpiece is fastened to the ephod not by gold alone but by the blue of commandment-obedience: the priestly office is inseparable from Torah-faithful living. The cord specifically prevents the breastpiece from separating from the ephod — their unity is maintained by the blue of covenant fidelity.
28.29: veNasa Aharon et-shemot benei Yisrael bachoshen hamishpat al-libo bevaoo el-hakodesh lezikaron lifnei YHWH tamid: 'Aaron shall bear the names of the sons of Israel on the breastpiece of judgment over his heart when he goes into the holy place, as a continual memorial before YHWH.' Three key terms: (1) al-libo ('over his heart') — not on his shoulders only (v. 12) but over the heart, the seat of love and compassion; (2) lezikaron ('as a memorial') — activating YHWH's covenant memory; (3) tamid ('continually, always') — the intercession is perpetual, not occasional. Hebrews 7:25 applies all three: Christ as high priest 'always (pantote) lives to make intercession for them,' carrying their names before the Father perpetually and with the full engagement of his love.
28.30: Urim ve-Thummim (ûrîm wĕtummîm): 'Lights and Perfections' or possibly 'Curses and Completeness' — the translucent oracular stones placed in the fold of the breastpiece and used to determine YHWH's will in specific decisions (Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6). The exact mechanism is unknown: they may have been two lots that gave binary yes/no answers, or their luminosity may have indicated YHWH's response. What is clear: the Urim and Thummim were not divination tools (forbidden in Deuteronomy) but divinely authorized means of inquiry. Thummim is from the same root as tamim (perfect, without blemish) — 'Perfection.' In Christ, the Urim and Thummim find their fulfillment: he is both the Light (John 8:12) and the Perfection (Heb 7:28) who communicates the Father's will directly and completely.
28.31: me'il ha'ephod: 'the robe of the ephod' — a long robe worn under the ephod, made entirely of blue (kulo tekhelet). The robe of the ephod is the second layer of the high priestly vestment, beneath the ephod and above the tunic. Its solid blue color encodes the priestly calling to full alignment with heaven's order — not partially colored, not decorative, but entirely the blue of the commandments. The bell-and-pomegranate hem (vv. 33-35) adds acoustic and symbolic dimensions to the garment.
28.32: peh rosho betecho safah yihyeh lefiv saviv: 'there shall be a woven opening for the head, with a woven collar around the opening.' The reinforced collar (safah, binding/hem) prevents the robe from tearing when put on and taken off. The robe must not be torn: wearing a torn robe was a sign of mourning or disgrace. The Talmud (Yoma 72a) notes that a torn high priestly garment disqualifies the priest from service. The robe's integrity is essential — a torn robe means a compromised mediation. When the high priest Caiaphas tore his robes (Matt 26:65) at Jesus's trial, he was performing the very act that signaled the end of his legitimate high-priestly function — the true high priest was standing before him.
28.33: rimonim (pomegranates, H7416): along the hem of the robe, alternating with gold bells — pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn. The pomegranate (rimon) is one of the seven species of the promised land (Deut 8:8) and appears throughout the tabernacle and temple symbolism (1 Kings 7:18-20: 200 pomegranates on the temple pillars). Its many seeds make it a symbol of abundance and fruitfulness. The alternating bells and pomegranates (vv. 33-34) create a rhythmic liturgical pattern: sound-symbol, life-symbol, sound-symbol, life-symbol — the priestly ministry is simultaneously heard (bells) and fruitful (pomegranates).
28.34: paamon zahav verimmon paamon zahav verimmon al-shole hame'il saviv: 'a gold bell and a pomegranate, alternating, around the hem of the robe.' The alternating pattern is explicit: bell, pomegranate, bell, pomegranate — equal numbers, regular spacing. The complete regularity of the pattern mirrors the complete regularity of the priestly ministry: YHWH's service admits no improvisation, no careless approximation. The bells announce the priest; the pomegranates represent the fruit of the priestly ministry. The priest announces YHWH's presence (bells as proclamation) and produces life in the community (pomegranates as abundance).
28.35: veHayah al-Aharon lesharet venishma qolo bevo'o el-hakodesh lifnei YHWH uvetzeto velo yamut: 'Aaron shall wear it when he ministers; its sound shall be heard when he enters the holy place before YHWH, and when he comes out, so that he may not die.' The bells serve a protective liturgical function: the sound announces the high priest's movement and signals that a live, consecrated minister is present. The solemn warning velo yamut ('so that he shall not die') recurs in the context of approaching the holy (Lev 16:2, 13). Hebrews 10:19-22 draws out the contrast: Christ has opened the way into the holy place by his blood, so that we may 'draw near with confidence,' no longer under the threat of death that the bell-system protected against.
28.36: tzitz zahav tahor upatachta alav pituchei chotam KODESH L'YHWH: 'a pure gold plate, engrave on it like a seal engraving: HOLY TO YHWH.' The tzitz (H6731, flower/plate) is a pure gold frontlet worn on the high priest's turban (v. 37-38). The inscription qodesh l'YHWH (HOLY TO YHWH) is the theological summary of the entire high priestly ministry: the priest's highest identity is consecration to YHWH, set apart for his service exclusively. The inscription mirrors the first commandment's exclusive claim (Exod 20:3: 'no other gods before me'). Christ is himself qodesh l'YHWH — 'the Holy One of God' (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) — and his people are made holy in him (1 Cor 1:30: 'Christ Jesus ... became for us ... sanctification').
28.37: unatata oto al-ptil tekhelet vehayah al-hamitznafet el-mul penei hamitznefet yihyeh: 'attach it by a blue cord to the turban; it is to be on the front of the turban.' The gold inscription-plate is fastened to the front of the turban by a blue cord — it faces forward, always visible when the priest faces YHWH. The forehead position (v. 38) is significant: the mezuzah's totafot (frontlets) of Deut 6:8 are worn on the forehead as a constant reminder of YHWH's word. The priestly forehead-inscription makes the priest himself a living mezuzah: his identity proclaims YHWH's holiness wherever he goes.
28.38: veNasa Aharon et-avon hakodashim asher yaqdeshu benei Yisrael lechol-matanot qodsheihem: 'Aaron shall bear the guilt arising from the holy gifts that the Israelites consecrate for all their holy gifts.' The high priest's function is not only to carry Israel's names before YHWH (vv. 12, 29) but to nasa avon (bear the iniquity/guilt) of the people's imperfect offerings. Even the holiest acts of Israel's worship are contaminated by human sin; the high priest bears that contamination. Lifnei YHWH tamid ('before YHWH continually') — the guilt-bearing is also perpetual. This is the verse that most directly anticipates Christ's high-priestly guilt-bearing: 'he himself bore our sins in his body' (1 Pet 2:24), permanently and completely, so that our imperfect worship is accepted through him.
28.39: veshivatzta hakutonet shesh: 'weave the tunic of fine linen' — the innermost layer of the high priest's vestments. Shesh (fine white linen, H8336 — possibly Egyptian linen) is the purest, whitest fabric available. White linen throughout the tabernacle system represents purity and righteousness (Rev 19:8: 'the fine linen is the righteous deeds of the saints'). The turban (mitznefet) and sash (avnet) complete the high priest's ensemble. The layered vestments — linen tunic, blue robe, woven ephod, gold-adorned breastpiece, gold frontlet — build from inner purity outward to public glory, from private devotion to public representation.
28.40: ulevnei Aharon taase kutnot: 'for Aaron's sons make tunics' — the ordinary priests also receive sacred garments, though simpler than the high priest's. The same principle applies: lecavod ul-tifaret ('for glory and splendor'), reflecting the dignity of priestly service at any level. The democratization of priestly dignity (all priests, not only the high priest, receive vestments of glory) anticipates the NT's royal priesthood: all believers are priests (1 Pet 2:9) and all are clothed with Christ's righteousness (Gal 3:27).
28.41: umashacha otam umileta et-yadam vekidashta otam: 'anoint them, ordain them (fill their hands), and consecrate them.' Three actions constitute priestly commissioning: (1) meshachta (māšaḥtā, anoint) — pour oil on the head; the root of mashiach (anointed one, Messiah). (2) mileta et-yadam ('fill their hands') — the standard OT idiom for ordination; the hands filled with the portions of the offering symbolize the priest's right and responsibility to handle the sacred things. (3) kidashta otam (consecrate/sanctify them) — set them apart as holy. All three are applied to Christ: he is the Messiah (anointed, Acts 10:38), his hands are filled with permanent priestly work (Heb 7:24-25), and he is set apart as holy (Heb 7:26).
28.42: michnesai-bad lekhassot besar-ervah: 'linen undergarments to cover the nakedness of the flesh' — the priestly undergarments (michnesayim, trousers/shorts) protect modesty when the priest ascends the altar steps. The explicit concern is that no nakedness (ervah) be exposed in YHWH's presence. The priestly garments cover what must not be seen before God — they envelop the priest in holiness from head to toe. The theme of covering (Gen 3:21: YHWH clothed Adam and Eve) runs from Eden through the priesthood to Christ: he is himself the ultimate garment of righteousness that covers human nakedness before God.
28.43: vehayu al-Aharon veal-banav bevo'am el-ohel mo'ed o begishtam el-hamizbeach lesharet bakodesh velo yis'u avon umetu chuqat olam lo ulezar'o acharav: 'they shall be worn by Aaron and his sons when they enter the tent of meeting or approach the altar to minister in the holy place, so they will not incur guilt and die — a lasting ordinance for Aaron and his descendants.' The chapter closes with the warning that proper vestment is not optional: unrobed service in the holy place results in guilt and death. The entire system of vestments is a protective structure mediating between the holy God and the inadequate priest. Christ alone enters the divine presence without a protective covering because his own nature is the holiness the vestments symbolized — he is the High Priest who is 'holy, innocent, unstained' (Heb 7:26).

29.1: vezeh hadavar asher ta'aseh lahem leqadesh otam: 'this is what you shall do for them to consecrate them.' The verb qadash (H6942, to be holy, set apart, consecrate) is the root of qodesh (holiness) and miqdash (sanctuary). Consecration transfers a person or object from the common sphere to the sphere of divine holiness — a one-way, irreversible transfer. The consecration ceremony of Exod 29 is the most elaborate personal consecration ritual in the OT. The ingredients: one young bull (par ben-baqar echad) and two unblemished rams (aylim tamimim) — sin offering, first burnt offering, and ram of ordination.
29.2: lachmim matzot vegalot matzot belulot bashemen: 'unleavened bread, unleavened loaves mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers spread with oil' — made from fine wheat flour (solet), the highest grade of flour. The three forms of unleavened bread (loaves, thick cakes, wafers) represent different textures of the same basic offering material: the absence of leaven in all three forms signals the purity of the priestly consecration. All three forms appear in the NT's eucharistic symbolism: Christ as the living bread (John 6:35), the bread of life made without the leaven of sin (1 Cor 5:7-8).
29.3: vesamta otam al-sal echad vehigashta otam basal: 'place them in a basket and present them along with the bull and the two rams.' The basket (sal, H5536) holds the three bread types; the animals stand alongside. The combined presentation — animal blood sacrifices + grain offerings — represents the complete Levitical system: blood (atonement and consecration) + grain (thank-offering and dependence). The basket of bread reappears in Lev 8 (the actual ordination ceremony following these instructions) and in the ordination of the Nazirite (Num 6:15-17).
29.4: veRachatzta otam bamayim: 'wash them with water' — the first act of the consecration is ritual washing at the entrance of the tent of meeting. Rachatz (H7364, to wash, bathe) — full body immersion. The washing before investiture is the liturgical logic of baptism: you must be washed before you are clothed with the priestly garments. John's baptism in the Jordan and Christian baptism both participate in this ritual logic — washing precedes anointing, anointing precedes service. Hebrews 10:22 applies the vestment-washing to the believer's entrance before God: 'having our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water.'
29.5: velaqachta et-habegadim vehilbashta et-Aharon et-hakutonet ve'et-me'il ha'ephod: 'take the garments and dress Aaron with the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod, and the breastpiece, binding it with the artistic band of the ephod.' The investiture proceeds from the inside out: linen tunic → blue robe → ephod → breastpiece → artistic band. Each layer is donned in order; the sequence is not arbitrary. The layered investiture of the priest pictures the layered righteousness of the believer in Christ: each aspect of Christ's person (righteousness, intercession, mediation) is “put on” in the union with him. Gal 3:27 uses the investiture language: 'as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.'
29.6: vesamta hamitznefet al-rosho venatata et-nezer haqodesh al-hamitznefet: 'put the turban on his head and attach the holy crown/sacred emblem to the turban.' The nezer haqodesh (H5145, sacred crown/consecration) is the gold frontlet of 28:36-38 — KODESH L'YHWH. The term nezer is also used for the Nazirite's consecration and for the king's crown. The priest's turban-crown blends royal and sacred symbolism: the high priest wears a crown of dedication, not of political power. The same convergence of crown and consecration appears in Zechariah's vision of the high priest crowned as king-priest (Zech 6:11-13) — directly prophesying the Melchizedekian Christ who combines both offices.
29.7: veliaqachta et-shemen hamishchah veyatzaqta al-rosho umashachta oto: 'take the anointing oil and anoint him by pouring it on his head.' The pouring of the anointing oil on the head is the act that makes the priest a mashiach (anointed one). The word Messiah (Hebrew) and Christ (Greek Christos) both mean “the Anointed One.” Jesus's baptism by the Spirit (Matt 3:16; Acts 10:38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power') is the NT fulfillment of the high-priestly anointing: Jesus is consecrated to his high-priestly ministry by the Spirit descending on him like the oil poured on Aaron's head (Ps 133:2 applies the head-anointing to the unity of the covenant community). The Spirit as the divine anointing-oil is a consistent NT metaphor (1 John 2:20, 27: chrisma, anointing from the Holy One).
29.8: veet-banav taqriv vehilbashta otam kutnot: 'bring his sons and dress them in tunics.' The sons of Aaron receive investiture immediately after their father — the priestly order is transmitted from generation to generation. The dynastic structure of the Aaronic priesthood (each generation ordained by the previous one) provides temporary continuity but is structurally limited: mortal priests cannot serve permanently. Hebrews 7:23-25 contrasts: 'the former priests were many in number, because they were prevented by death from continuing in office, but he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.'
29.9: veyatzarta lahem mitznefot vesamta lahem avnetim: 'put the caps on them and tie sashes on them.' Vehayetah lahem kehunah lechuqat olam: 'the priesthood shall be theirs by a permanent statute' — chuqat olam (eternal statute). The Aaronic priesthood is declared a permanent, eternal covenant. But the NT reveals that “eternal” here means the principle endures forever, not the specific Aaronic line: Hebrews 7:12 explains that when the priesthood changes, the law necessarily changes too. The chuqat olam of the Aaronic priesthood is fulfilled and superseded in the eternal priesthood of Christ according to the order of Melchizedek (Ps 110:4; Heb 7:24).
29.10: veHigashta et-haPar lifnei Ohel Mo'ed vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh haPar: 'bring the bull before the tent of meeting and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on the bull's head.' The laying on of hands (samach yadav, H5564) is the act of identification and transference — the one placing hands on the animal's head transfers their identity, sin, or blessing to the animal. The sin offering's hand-laying makes the bull the legal representative of the offerers: it dies in their place. This is the OT's clearest enacted statement of substitutionary atonement. Hebrews 9:7 connects this: the high priest goes into the holy of holies with blood 'which he offers for himself and for the sins of the people committed in ignorance.'
29.11: veshachata et-haPar lifnei YHWH petach Ohel Mo'ed: 'slaughter the bull before YHWH at the entrance to the tent of meeting.' The slaughter at the entrance (petach) to the tent of meeting — not inside, not at a distance — places the death at the threshold between the common and the holy. The animal dies at the boundary where the holy and the common meet. Christ dies at the threshold of the age — the “entrance” between the old covenant world and the new creation — as the definitive sin offering for all humanity (Heb 9:26: 'he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself').
29.12: velaqachta middam haPar venatata al-qarnot hamizbeach be'etzba'echa: 'take some of the bull's blood and put it on the horns of the altar with your finger.' The horns of the altar (qarnot hamizbeach) are the four projecting corners of the altar — the places where a fugitive would grab for sanctuary (1 Kings 1:50: Adonijah grabbed the altar horns). Putting blood on the altar's horns applies the atonement to the very seat of divine power and mercy. The remainder of the blood is poured out (shafach) at the base of the altar — consecrating the foundation. The base-pouring of blood anticipates John 19:34: the blood and water poured out at the foot of the cross, at the very base of the ultimate altar.
29.13: velaqachta et-kol-hahelev hamekhaseh et-haqerev: 'take all the fat surrounding the inner organs, the lobe of the liver, and both kidneys with their fat, and burn them on the altar.' The burning of the fat and inner organs is the portion reserved for YHWH (Lev 3:16-17: 'all fat is YHWH's'). The fat (chelev) represents the richest, most vital part of the animal — the offering to YHWH is not the leftover but the best portion. The liver and kidneys in the ANE were understood to be the seat of the will and emotions (the same function assigned to the heart in the NT). The inner organs represent the deepest interior of the animal — its hidden inner life offered to God.
29.14: ve'et-besar haPar ve'et-oro ve'et-pirsho tisrof ba'esh michutz lamachaneh chatat hu: 'burn the bull's flesh, hide, and intestines outside the camp; it is a sin offering.' The sin offering's carcass must be burned outside the camp (michutz lamachaneh) — not on the altar, not inside the tabernacle precincts, but expelled to the unclean place. Hebrews 13:11-13 draws the explicit application: 'the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp.' The outside-the-camp burning of the sin offering points directly to Golgotha.
29.15: ve'et-ha'ayil ha'echad tiqach vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil: 'take one of the rams and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The first ram is the olah (burnt offering) — the whole-body offering entirely consumed by fire, nothing kept back. The laying of hands on the burnt offering is the same act of identification as with the sin offering: the offerer is fully identified with the sacrifice, not merely presenting a gift from outside. The olah is total consecration — the entire animal ascending to YHWH. Rom 12:1 applies the same language to the NT believer: 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice (thusian zosan), holy and acceptable to God.'
29.16: veshachata et-ha'ayil velaqachta et-damo vezaraqta al-hamizbeach saviv: 'slaughter the ram and take its blood and throw it against all sides of the altar.' The blood-throwing (zaraq, H2236 — to toss, scatter) covers all four sides of the altar — a comprehensive, 360-degree consecration. The blood of the olah consecrates the altar itself, not just the offerers. The altar must be purified and dedicated before it can serve as the perpetual meeting-place between Israel and YHWH. The priestly ordination consecrates not only the priests but the physical structure through which they minister.
29.17: ve'et-ha'ayil tenateach linetiachav vekibalta et-qirbo: 'cut the ram into pieces and wash the inner organs and legs, and put them with the rest of the pieces.' The dismembering of the sacrifice and the washing of the organs and legs ensures that the offering is complete, clean, and properly prepared. The washing removes the blood and waste products that belong to the earthly life of the animal; what is then burned is the clean body of the animal. The attention to cleanliness in the offering material is consistent: YHWH receives what is washed, clean, and wholly given.
29.18: vehiqtarta et-kol-ha'ayil hamizbeachah olah hu l'YHWH reyach nichoach isheh l'YHWH hu: 'burn the entire ram on the altar as a burnt offering to YHWH; it is a pleasing aroma, a food offering to YHWH.' Reyach nichoach (H5207, a soothing/pleasing aroma) — the standard formula for acceptable sacrifice in the Levitical system (Lev 1:9, 13, 17; 2:2, 9, etc.). Paul applies the same phrase to Christ's self-offering: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Eph 5:2 — Greek: osmēn euōdias, exact translation of reyach nichoach). The burnt offering's pleasing aroma is the OT's most vivid statement that sacrifice is not merely transaction but relationship — a gift that pleases God.
29.19: velaqachta et-ha'ayil hasheni vesamach Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil: 'take the second ram and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.' The second ram is the eil milluim (ram of ordination/filling) — a sacrifice specific to the ordination ceremony, with no equivalent in the regular sacrificial calendar. Milluim (H4394, ordination, from male, to fill) — the filling of the hands (v. 24) with the wave offering. The ram of ordination is the sacrifice whose portions are literally placed in the priests' hands as the defining act of priestly commissioning. The priests “receive” the sacrifice before they can offer it.
29.20: veNatata al-tenuch ozen Aharon hayemanit ve'al-bohen yad-yemino ve'al-bohen raglo heymanit: 'put some of its blood on the lobe of the right ear of Aaron, and on the thumb of his right hand, and on the big toe of his right foot.' The three blood applications — ear, hand, foot — consecrate the three primary means of priestly service: (1) right ear: consecrated hearing of YHWH's word — the priest must hear YHWH's instructions accurately before he can serve; (2) right thumb/hand: consecrated doing of priestly work — the hands handle the holy things; (3) right big toe/foot: consecrated walking — the priest's movement in holy space must be holy. The same three-point application appears in the cleansed leper's ritual (Lev 14:14-17) — total bodily consecration from hearing to acting to going. Christ consecrates believers entirely: 'he who believes in me ... streams of living water' — from hearing (ear) to acting (hand) to going (foot).
29.21: velaqachta middam asher al-hamizbeach umishemen hamishchah vezaraqta al-Aharon veal-begadav: 'take some of the blood on the altar and some of the anointing oil and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments.' The priestly vestments are themselves consecrated with the mixture of altar blood and anointing oil — the same combination that consecrates the priests. Altar-blood represents atonement; oil represents the anointing Spirit. The vestments, like the priests, are consecrated by the convergence of sacrifice and Spirit. Paul's 'Christ who is in you' (Col 1:27) and 'clothed with Christ' (Gal 3:27) apply both the vestment-language and the consecration-language to the believer: in Christ they are covered by his blood and anointed by his Spirit.
29.22: velaqachta min-ha'ayil hachelev vehaalyah: 'take from the ram the fat, the fat tail, the fat around the internal organs, the long lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat, and the right thigh — for it is the ram of ordination.' The ram of ordination yields a unique set of portions: the fat, the liver lobe, the kidneys (YHWH's portion in regular offerings) plus the right thigh (shok hayamin) — which is the priest's regular share — plus all three bread types from the basket. These portions are first placed in the priests' hands (milluim) and then burned on the altar, creating a unique layered offering where priest and altar share the same sacrifice before YHWH.
29.23: vekhikar lechem achat vechalat shemen achat verakiq echad misal hamatzot asher lifnei YHWH: 'from the basket of unleavened bread before YHWH take one loaf, one cake mixed with oil, and one wafer.' The three bread types (loaf, thick cake, wafer) are represented by one of each — a sample of the whole offering given to the ordination ceremony. The combination of animal sacrifice + bread in the wave offering anticipates the Last Supper's combination of the lamb (body) and bread: the priestly ordination meal is a type of the ultimate covenant meal where Christ is simultaneously priest, offering, and host.
29.24: vesamta et-hakol al-kapei Aharon ve'al-kapei vanav: 'put all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons' — this is the milu yad (filling of the hands) that gives ordination its name in Hebrew (milluim). The priests' hands are literally filled with the ordination sacrifice: they hold the offering before YHWH, and YHWH accepts it through Moses's burning it. The verb henif (to wave, H5130) describes the tenufah (wave offering): the priest holds the offering and waves it before YHWH, presenting it as an act of giving before it is taken for burning. The priest gives before God, then receives back his portion — the pattern of priestly ministry: total self-offering that results in received portion.
29.25: velaqachta otam miyedam veehiqtarta hamizbeachah al-ha'olah lireyach nichoach lifnei YHWH: 'take them from their hands and burn them on the altar on top of the burnt offering, as a pleasing aroma before YHWH.' The ordination wave offering is placed on top of the regular burnt offering (olah) — the ordination of the priests is built on the foundation of the regular sacrifice. The priest cannot be ordained apart from sacrifice; his entire ministry emerges from the sacrificial foundation.
