The whole of it was a single hammered piece (miqqshah echad) of pure gold: The most emphatic restatement of the menorah's unity: miqqshah echad (one hammered piece) stated twice in this verse. The entire menorah — base, shaft, branches, cups, calyxes, flowers — is one continuous piece of worked gold. This is the material embodiment of the 'one' that runs through the tabernacle construction: the mishkan is one (36:13), the mercy seat and cherubim are one piece (37:8), the menorah is one piece.
37.23: He made its seven lamps (neroteha) and its tongs (malqacheim) and its trays (machtoteha) of pure gold: Nerot = lamps (from ner = lamp/light); malqachayim = tongs/snuffers (for trimming the wicks); machtot = fire-pans/trays (for catching the trimmed wicks and ash). The maintenance tools are as pure as the menorah itself — the instruments of daily service share the holiness standard of the object they service.
37.24: He made it and all its utensils of a talent (kikkar) of pure gold: Kikkar = a talent (the largest unit of weight; approximately 75 pounds/34 kg of gold). The menorah required one full talent of gold — the largest single-piece gold object in the tabernacle. The investment of an entire talent of gold in a single hammered lamp is a statement: illuminating the presence of YHWH is worth the most precious material available.
37.25: He made the altar of incense (mizbeach hamiqtar qetoret) of acacia wood: Miqtar = a place of burning/smoking (from qatar = to make smoke); qetoret = incense/smoke-offering. The incense altar (also called mizbeach ha-zahav, the gold altar) is the fourth piece of furniture in the Holy Place, positioned directly before the parokhet. It is the last object encountered before the Most Holy Place — the piece of furniture that mediates between the priest's ministry in the Holy Place and the divine presence beyond the veil.
37.26: He overlaid it with pure gold, its top (gago), its sides (kirotav), and its horns (qanotav), and made a molding of gold around it: Gag = roof/top (also the word for a house roof); the golden crown-molding (zer) around the incense altar matches the ark and the table, marking it as one of the three furniture pieces requiring the crown. Its horns (qanotav): The incense altar has four horns like the burnt-offering altar, but smaller and of gold rather than bronze. Blood of atonement (on the Day of Atonement and other occasions) is applied to the horns of the incense altar, linking it structurally to the blood atonement system despite its primary incense function.
37.27: And he made two rings of gold for it under its molding, on two opposite sides of it, as holders for the poles to carry it: The incense altar has two rings (not four like the ark and table), one on each of its two sides. The smaller number of rings for a smaller piece — the incense altar is proportionally smaller than the table and ark (1 cubit × 1 cubit × 2 cubits high, vs. the ark's 2.5 × 1.5 × 1.5 cubits).
37.28: And he made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with gold: Same construction protocol as all other poles: acacia + gold overlay. The incense altar's poles, like all carrying implements, share the material dignity of the object they carry.
37.29: He made the holy anointing oil also, and the pure fragrant incense (q'toret sammim), blended as by a perfumer (ma'aseh roqeach): The chapter concludes with the two substances (oil and incense) that serve as sensory mediators of divine presence. Shemen mishchat-qodesh = holy anointing oil (used to consecrate every piece of furniture and every priest — the physical transfer of holy status through oil contact). Q'toret sammim = fragrant incense (the specific compound formula of Exod 30:34-38); ma'aseh roqeach = the work of a perfumer (from raqach = to compound/spice) — the same technical designation as the embroiderer and the jeweler; sacred manufacture requires professional expertise.

38.1: He made the altar of burnt offering (mizbeach ha-olah) of acacia wood: Mizbeach ha-olah = the altar of the whole burnt offering (olah = that which goes up; the completely-consumed sacrifice). This altar is the first object encountered after passing through the court entrance — before the Holy Place, before the laver, the worshipper passes the altar of sacrifice. Access to the divine presence begins with blood. The altar's position at the entry is the spatial theology of atonement.
38.2: He made its horns (qarnotav) on its four corners; its horns were of one piece with it (miqqshah), and he overlaid it with bronze: Qernot = horns (the projections at the four top corners of the altar; from qeren = horn/power/ray). The horns are structural and symbolic: blood is applied to the horns in the atonement rituals (Lev 4:7, 8:15); fleeing criminals grasped the altar horns for sanctuary (1 Kgs 1:50-51). Miqqshah again: the horns are not attached to the altar but hammered out of the same metal structure as the altar. Bronze (n'choshet) = the outer court metal; the transition from gold (inner furnishings) to bronze (outer court) is complete.
38.3: He made all the utensils of the altar, the pots (sirot), the shovels (ya'im), the basins (mizraqot), the forks (mizlagot), and the fire pans (machtot). He made all its utensils of bronze: The complete bronze service kit for the burnt-offering altar: sirot = cooking pots (for boiling portions); ya'im = shovels (for removing ashes); mizraqot = basins for blood (from zaraq = to sprinkle/throw; the same verb as Moses throwing covenant blood on the people in 24:8); mizlagot = flesh-hooks/forks (for handling the sacrificial portions); machtot = fire-pans (for carrying coals). Every piece of hardware is bronze — the altar's service set matches its material.
38.4: He made for the altar a grating (mikbar r'shet n'choshet), a network of bronze, under its ledge (karkav), extending halfway down: Mikbar = a grating/lattice (from a root meaning to make holes); r'shet = network/mesh; the bronze grating sits halfway down the altar, providing a shelf for the sacrifice and allowing air flow for combustion. Karkav = ledge/projection (a technical architectural term for a horizontal projection in a wall or structure). The grating's position halfway down the hollow altar creates a functional firebox — the bronze mesh holds the sacrifice while flames burn below.
38.5: He cast four rings on the four corners of the bronze grating as holders for the poles: The grating itself has four rings for poles — an inner carrying system for the grating inside the altar. Or the four rings are at the altar's corners at the grating level, serving as the carrying rings for the entire altar via poles inserted through the grating. The precise mechanics of assembly are described so that any future Levitical generation can reconstruct the altar correctly.
38.6: He made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with bronze: The altar poles follow the standard protocol: acacia wood + metal overlay. But the overlay here is bronze (not gold or silver) — the altar's poles match the altar's metal. The gradation of materials is maintained even for transport accessories.
38.7: And he put the poles through the rings on the sides of the altar to carry it with them. He made it hollow, with boards (n'vuv luchot asah oto): N'vuv = hollow (from navav = to hollow out/bore); luchot = boards/planks; the altar is hollow — not a solid block of acacia but a box made of boards, overlaid with bronze. The hollow construction serves two purposes: portability (solid acacia + bronze would be immovably heavy) and function (the hollow interior is filled with earth or stones at each campsite per 20:24-25, creating the sacrificial fire base on fresh ground).
38.8: He made the basin (kiyyor) of bronze and its stand (kano) of bronze, from the mirrors (mar'ot) of the ministering women who ministered (tzov'ot asher tzav'u) at the entrance of the tent of meeting: Kiyyor = basin/laver (used for washing); kano = its stand/pedestal. Mar'ot = mirrors (polished bronze mirrors — ancient mirrors were not glass but highly polished bronze or copper, which the women gave up for the laver); tzov'ot asher tzav'u = the ministering women who served (the same root tzava twice — they are army women, those who served in organized corps). These women appear nowhere else in the Pentateuch; they give up their personal mirrors — instruments of vanity and beauty — to make the vessel of priestly purification. The material transformation (beauty instrument → cleansing vessel) is itself a theological symbol: what was used for self-presentation becomes the means of purification for approach to God.
38.9: He made the court (chatzer). For the south side the hangings of the court were of fine twined linen (shesh moshzar), a hundred cubits: Chatzer = court/enclosure (also the word for a village or unwalled settlement — a defined but open space). The court is the outermost sacred space, accessible to lay Israelites who are not priests. The court hangings are fine linen but not the colored, embroidered linen of the interior — the court maintains holiness but not the same degree as the tabernacle itself. A hundred cubits = approximately 150 feet — the full length of the southern boundary.
38.10: Their twenty pillars and twenty bases were of bronze, but the hooks of the pillars and their fillets were of silver: The court pillars rest on bronze bases (the outer material) but their connecting hardware (hooks and fillets) is silver — the court is a transition zone between the fully bronze outer world and the silver-based inner structure. The material gradient: outside world (no metal) → court (bronze bases, silver hardware) → tabernacle walls (silver bases) → inner furnishings (gold).
38.11: For the north side there were hangings of a hundred cubits, their twenty pillars, twenty bases of bronze, with hooks and fillets of silver: The north wall mirrors the south wall exactly: same hanging length, same pillar count, same base material, same hardware material. The court's north and south walls are symmetrical — bilateral order in the sacred space.
38.12: For the west side there were hangings of fifty cubits, their ten pillars, and ten bases, with hooks and fillets of silver: The west end of the court: fifty cubits wide (the court is half as wide as it is long — 100 cubits by 50 cubits in total). Ten pillars at fifty cubits = five cubits per pillar spacing, consistent with the east and north/south spacing.
38.13: For the front to the east, fifty cubits. The court entrance faces east — the direction of sunrise, the direction from which the camp approaches. The east end is fifty cubits wide (the same as the west), divided into two screen panels flanking the entrance gate.
38.14: The hangings for one side of the gate were fifteen cubits, with their three pillars and three bases: The gate section: the overall fifty-cubit east end is divided as follows: 15 cubits of screen (south of the gate) + 20 cubits of gate opening + 15 cubits of screen (north of the gate) = 50 cubits. The gate opening (20 cubits wide) is the widest single opening in the tabernacle complex — welcoming, accessible, the point of entry for the whole congregation.
38.15: And so for the other side. On both sides of the gate of the court were hangings of fifteen cubits, with their three pillars and three bases: The symmetry of the gate flanks: 15 cubits + 3 pillars on each side. The gate is framed, not fenced off — its design creates a portal of invitation, not a barrier. The screen of the gate (masach sha'ar hechatzer) is the first of three thresholds: court gate → tabernacle entrance → inner veil.
38.16: All the hangings around the court were of fine twined linen: Despite using only one color and lacking the cherubim motif of the inner curtains, the court hangings are fine linen throughout — the entire court space is marked as sacred. Even the most accessible zone of the tabernacle complex uses premium material.
38.17: The bases for the pillars were of bronze, the hooks of the pillars and their fillets were of silver, and the overlaying of their capitals was of silver, and all the pillars of the court were filleted with silver: The systematic description of the court pillar hardware: bronze bases, silver hooks and bands, silver-overlaid capitals. The silver binding of all court pillars creates a visual cohesion — all the court pillars gleam with silver bands, visually unifying the sacred space.
38.18: The screen for the gate of the court was embroidered with needlework in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen (roqem): The entrance gate screen is the most elaborate element of the court — embroidered with all four colors (blue, purple, scarlet, linen), using the roqem technique. The gate is the most decorated element of the court, befitting its role as the threshold of transition from the profane world to the sacred space.
38.19: It was twenty cubits long and five cubits high in its breadth, corresponding to the hangings of the court: The gate screen is twenty cubits wide and five cubits high — the full width of the entrance opening and the height of the court walls. The gate screen fills the entire entrance portal.
38.20: And their four pillars and their four bases were of bronze; their hooks were of silver, and the overlaying of their capitals and their fillets were of silver: The gate screen's four pillars follow the court pattern: bronze bases, silver hardware. The gate's four pillars (vs. three for each flanking panel) mark the entrance as architecturally distinct — four is the number of completeness at the corners of a portal.
38.21: All the pegs (yetedot) for the tabernacle and for the court all around were of bronze: Yetedot = pegs/stakes (tent pegs that anchor the guy-ropes of the tabernacle and court hangings); the pegs are all bronze — the outermost attachment point of the structure uses the court material. The hierarchy bottoms out at bronze for the ground-level anchors.
38.22: These are the recorded accounts (p'quddei) of the tabernacle, the tabernacle of the testimony (mishkan ha'edut): Paquddei = the records/accounts/accounts (from paqad = to number/register/visit/oversee); the same root as the census (p'qudim = the numbered). Mishkan ha'edut = tabernacle of the testimony/covenant (edut = testimony, referring to the tablets of the testimony in the ark). The tabernacle is defined by what it houses: the testimony, the covenant record.
38.23: As Moses commanded, according to the service of the Levites, under the direction of Ithamar the son of Aaron the priest: Ithamar (the fourth son of Aaron) has supervisory responsibility for the tabernacle accounts — an early indication of his administrative role. The Levitical service (avodah) framework for tabernacle maintenance is established here; Numbers 4 will specify each Levitical clan's transport duties in detail.
38.24: Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that YHWH commanded Moses: The formal credit line: Bezalel is identified by father (Uri), grandfather (Hur), and tribe (Judah). Hur is the companion of Aaron at Rephidim who held Moses's arms during the battle (17:12); he may be Miriam's son. The Judah-tribe identification matters for later tradition: the artisan who built the divine dwelling is from the royal tribe.
38.25: And with him was Oholiab the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan, a craftsman and designer and embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen: Oholiab (Oholi-av = 'my father is my tent' or 'tent of the father') is from Dan — the northernmost tribe. The pairing of Bezalel (Judah, south) and Oholiab (Dan, north) represents the full geographic and tribal breadth of Israel contributing to the tabernacle construction. Together they represent the whole people.
38.26: All the gold that was used for the work, in all the construction of the sanctuary, the gold from the offering, was twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels, by the shekel of the sanctuary: The gold inventory: 29 talents + 730 shekels. A talent = approximately 75 pounds = approximately 34 kg; so approximately 2,175 pounds = nearly a ton of gold in the entire tabernacle. The accounting is exact: the gold total is tallied to the shekel (the shekel of the sanctuary = a standard weight kept at the tabernacle to prevent fraud — the sanctuary maintained the reference standard for commerce).
38.27: The silver from those of the congregation who were numbered was a hundred talents and 1,775 shekels, by the shekel of the sanctuary: The silver total of 100 talents + 1,775 shekels came entirely from the census half-shekels (v.26 = the ransom of those numbered). One hundred talents = 100 silver bases for the tabernacle frames and inner veil pillars; 1,775 shekels = the hooks, fillets, and overlays for the court pillars. Every ounce of structural silver in the tabernacle represents a numbered Israelite — the tabernacle's foundations literally consist of the registered lives of the people of God.
38.28: He made the hooks of the pillars and overlaid their capitals and filleted them with it: The 1,775 remaining shekels of silver (after making the 100 bases) went entirely to the court pillar hardware: the hooks, the capital overlays, and the connecting fillets. Nothing was wasted; the census surplus accounts were precisely expended.
38.29: The bronze of the offering was seventy talents and 2,400 shekels: The bronze total: 70 talents + 2,400 shekels — contributed as a freewill offering (not from the census; the census silver was the ransom payment). Bronze was contributed voluntarily; silver was a required payment. The material hierarchy corresponds to the spiritual hierarchy: gold (most valuable, inner furnishings), silver (census-ransom, structural foundations), bronze (freewill, outer court and practical implements).
38.30: With it he made the bases for the entrance of the tent of meeting, the bronze altar and the bronze grating for it, and all the utensils of the altar: The complete inventory of what bronze was used for: the entrance bases (5 for the entrance screen pillars), the altar and its grating, and all the altar's service utensils. The bronze goes first and primarily to the sacrificial apparatus — the altar, its tools, its support structure.
38.31: The bases of the court all around, and the bases of the gate of the court, and all the pegs of the tabernacle, and all the pegs of the court all around: The final bronze allocation: all the court bases (56 bases for the 56 court pillars), the gate screen bases (4), and every tent peg throughout the entire complex. The pegs (yetedot) that literally hold the tabernacle to the ground are bronze — the material that connects the holy structure to the earth is the most utilitarian metal, the one farthest from gold.

26.1: ha-mishkan (hah-mish-KAN): 'the tabernacle' — now the structure itself is described. yeriot (ye-ri-OT): 'curtains/sheets' (H3407). shesh moshzar (shesh mo-SHAR): 'twisted fine linen' (H8336 H7806 — braided/twisted). ma'aseh choshev (mah-ah-SEH kho-SHEV): 'work of a skilled craftsman/designer' (H4639 H2803 — from chashav = to think/devise/plan/weave). The highest grade of weaving — the curtains that form the inner skin of the tabernacle are the product of intentional design, skilled execution, and artistic thought. The same root chashav means to think or reckon — the tabernacle is a thought made fabric.
26.2: The dimensions of each inner curtain: 28 cubits long and 4 cubits wide — approximately 14m × 2m. All ten curtains identical in size: mida achat lechol hayeriot — one measure for all the curtains. The uniformity signals that there is no hierarchy among the curtains themselves; only their assembly into joined sets creates structure.
26.3: Five curtains joined to one another, and five joined to one another — two sets of five. The joining (chabar H2266 — to unite/couple) creates two large panels that are then clasped together to form the whole tabernacle ceiling. The number five appears repeatedly in the tabernacle — five curtains, five pillars at the entrance — and may echo the Pentateuch structure or the five fingers of the priestly hand.
26.4: lulot tekhelet (loo-LOT te-kheh-LET): 'loops of blue' (H3924 H8504). The loops are made along the edge of the last curtain in each set — fifty loops on each edge, positioned to align precisely with the loops on the opposite set. The blue (tekhelet) of the loops marks this joining edge as sacred — the same blue as the priests' garments and the tzitzit thread.
26.5: chamishim lulot (kha-mish-EEM loo-LOT): 'fifty loops' on each set — the total join has 50 pairs of loops. Fifty is significant: the Jubilee year is the 50th year (Lev 25:10), and the Feast of Weeks (Shavuot/Pentecost) is the 50th day after Passover. The tabernacle's internal structure carries the number of divine release and covenant renewal.
26.6: klasei zahav (klah-SEI zah-HAV): 'golden clasps' (H7165 H2091). Fifty gold clasps join the two halves of the inner tabernacle into one: vehayah hamishkan echad — 'and the tabernacle shall be one.' The architectural unity of the sanctuary reflects the oneness of its God. Fifty gold clasps, 50 bronze clasps for the outer covering — the same number unifies the sacred dwelling at both layers.
26.7: yeriot izim (ye-ri-OT iz-ZEEM): 'curtains of goat hair' (H3407 H5795). The second layer of covering — eleven curtains of goat hair over the inner linen curtains. Goat hair was used for tent fabric throughout the ancient Near East — waterproof and durable. The outer layers protect the inner; the sacred linen curtains with cherubim are never exposed to the elements.
26.8: The goat hair curtains are slightly larger than the inner linen ones: 30 cubits long (vs. 28) and 4 cubits wide. The extra length allows for overhang. Eleven curtains vs. the inner ten — the outer covering is more extensive, wrapping further around the sacred structure. The slight excess is architectural wisdom for desert conditions.
26.9: The sixth curtain is to be folded double at the front of the tent — the doubled curtain creates extra thickness at the entrance, where wear and exposure are greatest. The arrangement reveals practical engineering within the sacred commission: durability serves holiness. The doubled front also creates a visual marker of the entrance.
26.10: Fifty loops on the edge of the last outer curtain, aligned to match the inner curtains' loops. The same 50-loop structure — the outer goat hair covering mirrors the inner linen covering in its joining system. The tabernacle is built by correspondence: inner and outer, above and below, holy and most holy are all parallel structures.
26.11: klasei nechoshet (klah-SEI ne-KHO-shet): 'bronze clasps' (H7165 H5178). Bronze for the outer goat hair covering, gold for the inner linen curtains — the decreasing value of metals as one moves outward reflects the graduated holiness of the tabernacle zones: gold (most holy/holy places), silver (sockets/bases), bronze (outer court). Even the clasps encode the theology of access.
26.12: The half-curtain surplus from the odd eleventh curtain hangs down over the back of the tabernacle. Nothing is wasted in the divine design — the extra curtain creates additional back coverage. The instruction reveals that the outer covering extends beyond the inner curtains on all sides, protecting the sacred structure completely.
26.13: A cubit of extra length hangs down on each side of the tabernacle. The outer covering is longer than the inner by two cubits total — one cubit of overhang per side. The inner curtains are fully enclosed within the outer covering. This architectural detail reinforces the principle: holiness is surrounded, protected, and approached only through layers of mediation.
26.14: ore eilim meaddamim (oh-ROT ay-LEEM me-ad-dah-MEEM): 'rams' skins dyed red' (H5785 H352 H119, from adam = red/ruddy — same root as adam/human and edom/red). ore tachashim: tachash skins (H8476). The two outermost layers are utilitarian coverings — water-resistant animal skins. The innermost layer is gold and cherubim; the outermost is animal hide. The tabernacle is wrapped in mortality to enclose the divine presence — a structural parable of incarnation.
26.15: kerashim (ke-rah-SHEEM): 'planks/boards/frames' (H7175, singular keresh). The upright wooden framework of the tabernacle — 48 boards total forming the three solid walls. atzei shittim omedim: 'standing/upright acacia planks.' Omedim from amad (to stand H5975) — the same word for those who stand in divine service. The planks stand upright before YHWH.
26.16: Each board: eser ammot orech haqqeresh — ten cubits long (ca. 4.5m), and one and a half cubits wide (amah va-chatzi). The height of the boards (ten cubits) determines the interior height of the tabernacle — ten cubits of standing room for the worship of the Holy One. Ten is the number of the Decalogue; the house of the commandments stands ten cubits tall.
26.17: shetei yadot laqqeresh echad (she-TEI yah-DOT la-KE-resh eh-KHAD): 'two tenons/hands for each board' (H3027). Yadot is the plural of yad — hand. The mortise-tenon joints by which each board is inserted into its silver base are called hands. The boards are planted into the earth by their hands — anthropomorphic building language. Every board has two hands reaching down into the silver sockets that hold it. The architecture uses human body vocabulary: the tabernacle has hands and feet.
26.18: The south side (negev H5045 — same word as the Negev desert) receives twenty boards. The full south wall. lemiphat negev teymana: 'for the south side, southward' — the southern exposure of the desert tabernacle, facing the prevailing winds and maximum sun. Twenty boards for the longest walls.
26.19: arbaim adnei kesef (ar-bah-EEM ad-NEI KEH-sef): 'forty silver sockets/bases' (H134 H3701). Two sockets per board, under each of the two tenons. The silver sockets are the foundation of the entire tabernacle structure — the boards are not set into the ground but into cast silver. The number of silver sockets (96 total for the walls plus 4 for the veil = 100) connects to the 100 silver talents contributed as the silver redemption money counted in Ex 38:25-28. The foundation of the tabernacle literally rests on the ransom silver of the people.
26.20: The north side: twenty boards — matching the south side exactly. The tabernacle is symmetrical: north and south walls identical in board count, dimensions, and silver socket foundations. This bilateral symmetry reflects the orderliness of the divine dwelling — nothing is haphazard, everything corresponds.
26.21: Forty silver sockets for the north side — two per board, twenty boards. The total for the two long walls: 80 silver sockets, foundation of 40 boards. The silver foundation runs the full length of both long walls, unbroken. The most holy place rests on silver as surely as the outer court does — the entire sanctuary has a unified silver foundation.
26.22: ulirkete ha-mishkan yamah (oo-lir-ke-TEI hah-mish-KAN yah-MAH): 'and for the rear of the tabernacle westward' (yam = sea, the west — Israel orients by the Mediterranean Sea to the west). The west end gets six boards — the shorter back wall. The tabernacle faces east (entrance to the east, most holy place to the west) — oriented toward the rising sun while the divine presence dwells in the west.
26.23: ulirkete ha-mishkan shetei qerashot: 'and for the corners of the tabernacle at the rear — two corner boards.' miqqtso'ot: corner boards (H4742, from qatsa = corner/edge). The corner boards are a structural challenge — they must join two perpendicular walls while maintaining the uniform dimensions. The instruction that follows (v.24) explains how they are doubled and joined at the top.
26.24: veyihyu toa-mim mitachat veyachdav yihyu el rosho: 'they shall be twin from below, and together they shall be at its top, to the one ring.' The corner boards are doubled — each corner has two boards joined together, creating a structural column at each rear corner. They are joined at the top by one ring, binding the two corner boards into a single unit. This engineering solution for the corners reflects sophisticated desert carpentry within the divinely revealed design.
26.25: Eight boards for the west end (six boards + two doubled corner boards = eight total positions). Sixteen silver sockets — two per board. shisha qereshim velirkat: six boards for the back, plus two corner boards = eight total. The west wall closes the tabernacle, sealing the most holy place at the rear.
26.26: berikhim atzei shittim (be-ri-KHEEM ah-TZEI shit-TEEM): 'crossbars of acacia wood' (H1280 H6086 H7848). From barach — to flee through, to pass through (H1272). The horizontal crossbars run through rings in the boards, binding the vertical boards into a unified wall. Five bars for each side — five bars hold the south wall, five the north wall, five the west wall.
26.27: Five crossbars for the north wall, five for the west wall — the same structural system on all three sides. The crossbars ensure that the individual boards cannot topple or separate during the desert march and reassembly. They pass through gold rings in the boards, becoming part of the gold-overlay system — even the structural engineering is consecrated.
26.28: haberiach hatikkon betokh haqqereshim (hah-be-ri-AKH hah-ti-KHON be-TOKH hah-keh-rah-SHEEM): 'the middle crossbar in the middle of the boards' — tikkoni = middle/central (H8484). The central bar runs the full length of the wall from end to end. This central spine of the tabernacle — running through the middle of all the boards on each wall — is the structural backbone of the divine house. The middle bar is hidden within the boards, invisible once installed, like the spine within the body.
26.29: The boards are overlaid with gold (tetzapeh zahav); gold rings hold the crossbars; the crossbars themselves are overlaid with gold. The entire wooden structure — boards and bars alike — is encased in gold. The wood is the structural support; the gold is the sacred surface. The incorruptible acacia provides the skeleton; the pure gold provides the glory. Nothing of the wooden structure is visible inside the tabernacle — only gold.
26.30: kahaqem et ha-mishkan kemishpato (kah-HA-kem et hah-mish-KAN ke-mish-PA-to): 'you shall set up the tabernacle according to its ordinance/manner' — mishpat (H4941, ordinance, right manner) here means the proper procedure/design revealed to Moses. The third reference to the revealed pattern (cf. vv. 9, 40 in ch.25) — the assembly of the tabernacle is not improvisational. There is a right way, a divinely ordained order, for establishing the sacred dwelling.
26.31: parochet (pah-RO-khet): 'veil/curtain' (H6532, perhaps from parach = to separate/divide or from Akkadian baraku). The inner veil is the most theologically significant piece of fabric in the tabernacle. Same materials as the inner curtains (tekhelet/argaman/shani/shesh), with cherubim woven in by the most skilled craftsman (choshev). This veil separates the holy place from the holy of holies — the barrier between human worship and the divine throne. It is this veil that tears from top to bottom at the crucifixion of Jesus (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38), the torn flesh of Christ opening the way into the divine presence (Heb 10:19-20): a new and living way through the veil.
26.32: Four acacia pillars overlaid with gold, with gold hooks (vavihem zahav), set in four silver sockets (arba adnei kesef). The parochet hangs from the gold hooks on these four pillars. The pillars stand between the holy place and the most holy — the threshold of access. Four pillars: four is the number of the earth's corners, of completeness in the material realm. The silver bases again: the sacred structure rests on silver redemption.
26.33: vehivdilah haparochet lachem bayn haqdosh uvayn qodesh haqdoshim: 'and the veil shall divide for you between the holy place and the holy of holies.' The architectural division of the tabernacle is explicit: the parochet creates two zones — haqdosh (the holy place, outer room) and qodesh haqdoshim (the holy of holies, inner room). The veil is not merely decoration but a theological boundary: the most holy place is inaccessible to all but the high priest, once a year, on Yom Kippur. This spatial theology of graduated access is dissolved in Christ (Heb 9:8, 12).
26.34: The kapporet (atonement cover) is placed on the aron haedut (ark of the testimony) in the qodesh haqdoshim. The sequence: ark inside, kapporet on top, parochet surrounding — the most holy objects are the most enclosed. Three zones of access (outer court → holy place → holy of holies) correspond to three levels of approach: Israel, priests, high priest. The high priest alone enters the innermost room, only on one day of the year, only with blood.
26.35: The table of the bread of the presence is placed outside the veil on the north side; the menorah is placed opposite on the south side. These two furnishings face each other across the holy place, both outside the parochet — the bread of divine provision and the light of divine presence flanking the approach to the veil. All three furnishings of the holy place (altar of incense, table, menorah) are positioned with precise intentionality.
26.36: masak (mah-SAK): 'screen/curtain' (H4539, from nasak — to weave?). The screen for the entrance to the tent is woven in the same four colors as the parochet but without cherubim — the entrance has the colors of holiness but lacks the angelic guardians that mark the boundary of the most holy. The masak is an invitation; the parochet is a warning. Entry into the holy place is open to the priests; entry through the parochet is reserved for the high priest alone.
26.37: Five acacia pillars overlaid with gold, with gold hooks, for the entrance screen — but five bronze sockets (adnei nechoshet), not silver. The change from silver to bronze at the entrance marks the threshold between the holy place system and the outer court system: silver belongs to the inner sanctuary, bronze to the outer court. The entrance pillars stand at the exact boundary where the two material systems meet — a precise material theology of spatial holiness.

27.1: mizbeach (miz-BE-akh): 'altar' (H4196, from zabach H2076 — to slaughter/sacrifice). The altar is named from the act it enables: the slaughter of sacrifice. It is 5 cubits × 5 cubits × 3 cubits high — square, with equal sides. The square shape may symbolize completeness and equal access from all four directions. The altar of burnt offering stands at the entrance to the tabernacle courtyard — the first thing encountered when approaching the divine dwelling. There is no access to the sanctuary without first passing the altar of sacrifice.
27.2: qarayyim (kar-NAI-yim): 'horns' (H7161, from qeren — horn, also power/authority H7161). The four horns project from the four corners of the altar — one piece with the altar, not added on. The horns are where the blood of the sin offering is applied (Lev 4:7, 18), and where fugitives clung for asylum (1 Kings 1:50; 2:28). The horn is simultaneously the place of atonement and the place of refuge — power channeled into protection. nechoshet overlay: bronze, the material of the outer court.
27.3: The five altar utensils: sirotav (pots/buckets for removing ash H5518); ya'av (shovels H3257); mizraqotav (sprinkling bowls H4219 — for receiving and dashing the blood against the altar sides); mizlegotav (flesh hooks H4207 — for handling the sacrificial portions); machtotav (fire pans H4289 — for carrying coals). Every aspect of sacrificial service has its designated tool. The whole cultic system is equipped and ordered — nothing left to improvisation at the place of sacrifice.
27.4: mikbar nechoshet (mik-BAR ne-KHO-shet): 'bronze grating/lattice' (H4345 H5178). A bronze network or mesh is made for the altar — a latticework platform beneath the sacrificial fire. Bronze withstands heat (unlike gold or silver); the grating allows ash to fall while supporting the burning sacrifice above. The technical sophistication of this design — a suspended combustion platform — reflects that the revealed pattern encompasses engineering as well as theology.
27.5: The bronze grating is placed under the karkov (kar-KOV): 'ledge/rim/shelf' (H3764 — a projecting shelf around the altar exterior). The grating is suspended at the midpoint of the altar — not at the top or bottom but in the middle. This arrangement creates a lower chamber (for ash collection) and an upper chamber (for the fire and sacrifice), with the grating as the dividing floor between them.
27.6: Acacia wood poles (baddei shittim) overlaid with bronze for carrying the altar. The altar travels the same way as the ark and table — by poles through rings. But the altar's poles are bronze-clad (not gold), matching the outer court material system. Every object has poles; every sacred object is portable. The God of the desert tabernacle does not dwell in a fixed temple but travels with his pilgrim people.
27.7: The poles are inserted through the rings on the sides of the altar for carrying. The spatial arrangement ensures that the altar can be lifted and carried without the bearers touching the sacrificial surface. The altar is carried by priests — the Kohathites are assigned the altar in Num 4:13-14. What has received the blood of sacrifice requires careful handling in transport.
27.8: nevuv luchot taa'aseh oto (ne-VOOV loo-KHOT ta-ah-SEH oh-TO): 'hollow with boards you shall make it' — the altar is hollow, constructed of bronze-overlaid boards, not solid. ka'asher her'ah otkha bahar ken ya'asu: 'as he showed you on the mountain, so they shall make it.' The tabnit principle again — the altar design, like every other element, is revealed, not invented. The hollow construction makes it portable (lighter than solid stone or metal) while maintaining the sacred form revealed on the mountain.
27.9: chatzer ha-mishkan (khatz-AR hah-mish-KAN): 'the courtyard of the tabernacle' (H2691, from chatzar — to enclose/surround; also a village settlement in the wilderness). The courtyard is the outermost sacred zone — still sacred, but open to all Israel (not just priests). The south side is 100 cubits long — fine twisted linen hangings (qelai shesh moshzar) creating a white linen enclosure that marks off the sacred precincts from the camp.
27.10: Twenty pillars and twenty bronze sockets for the south side — one socket per pillar. vaveihem (vah-VEI-hem): 'their hooks' (H2053 — the letter vav itself means hook/nail, shaped like a hook). chashuqeihem (kha-shoo-KEI-hem): 'their bands/connecting rods' (H2838). Silver hooks and bands on the bronze-socketed pillars — silver connects the courtyard pillars to each other, creating a unified white linen enclosure. The courtyard is bounded by silver at the top of its pillars, bronze at the base.
27.11: The north side exactly mirrors the south: 100 cubits of linen hangings, twenty bronze-socketed pillars, silver hooks and bands. The bilateral symmetry of the courtyard (north = south) reflects the divine orderliness that pervades the entire tabernacle design. What is said of one wall is equally true of the other — no hierarchy between the lateral approaches to the divine dwelling.
27.12: The west end of the courtyard: 50 cubits wide, ten pillars with ten sockets. The courtyard is 100 cubits (east-west) by 50 cubits (north-south) — twice as long as it is wide. The proportional rectangle (2:1) mirrors the inner tabernacle structure, creating nested rectangular spaces of increasing holiness: outer courtyard (100×50), tabernacle itself (30×10), with the veil dividing the tabernacle into holy place (20×10) and holy of holies (10×10).
27.13: The east side (miqdma qedmah — toward the east) is also 50 cubits wide — matching the west end. The gate will occupy 20 of these 50 cubits (v.16), with 15-cubit screens on each side. The eastern orientation of the entrance mirrors Eden (Gen 3:24) and Ezekiel's vision (Ezek 43:1-4) — in Israelite sacred architecture, one enters from the east and moves westward toward the divine presence.
27.14: Fifteen cubits of linen hanging on one side of the gate, with three pillars and three sockets. The gate flanks create a framing for the entrance — the 20-cubit gate is proportionally centered within the 50-cubit east end, with 15 cubits of screening on each side. Approach to the divine dwelling is architecturally managed: the gate is wide (20 cubits) and well-lit (open to the east), but it is the only entry point.
27.15: Fifteen cubits on the other side of the gate — three pillars and three sockets. The symmetrical flanking of the entrance creates a formal gateway. The tabernacle has one gate facing east — one entrance to the sacred precincts. Jesus applies this spatial theology in John 10:9: 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved.' The exclusive entrance of the divine dwelling becomes a Christological claim.
27.16: masak sha'ar hechatzar (mah-SAK sha-AR heh-khatz-AR): 'screen/curtain for the gate of the courtyard' (H4539 H8179 H2691). The gate screen is 20 cubits wide, in the same four colors as the tabernacle entrance (tekhelet/argaman/shani/shesh) but with ma'aseh roqem (embroiderer's work H4639 H7551) rather than the most skilled weaving. Four pillars and four sockets for the 20-cubit gate. The outer gate is decorated but less elaborately than the inner — holiness increases as one moves inward.
27.17: kol ammudei hechatzar saviv meshhuqqim kesef: 'all the pillars of the courtyard all around with silver bands' — meshhuqqim (H2838 — banded/joined with silver). The courtyard pillars, regardless of which side, all share silver connecting rods and hooks. Silver unifies the entire courtyard at the top; bronze grounds every pillar at the bottom. The courtyard is literally ringed with silver — the redemption metal (cf. Ex 30:11-16 — silver as the ransom price of the census).
27.18: The full dimensions of the courtyard: orech hechatzar meah ba'amma uerochav chamishim bachameshin — length 100 cubits, width 50 cubits, height 5 cubits of twisted linen hangings. The courtyard height (5 cubits, ca. 2.3m) creates a privacy/separation zone without being so tall as to be oppressive — enough to mark off the sacred from the common while allowing air and light. The bronze sockets run beneath the entire perimeter.
27.19: kol kelei ha-mishkan bechol avodato: 'all the vessels/equipment of the tabernacle for all its service' — including all yetedotav (ye-teh-do-TAV): 'its tent pegs/stakes' (H3489). Even the tent pegs — the simplest, most utilitarian objects — are listed among the sacred equipment. Every peg that anchors the tabernacle to the Sinai desert is nechoshet (bronze), part of the consecrated complex. Nothing about the tabernacle is secular, not even the hardware buried in the ground.
27.20: shemen zayit zakh katit lammaor (sheh-MEN zay-IT zakh kah-TEET lah-mah-OR): 'pure beaten olive oil for the light' (H8081 H2132 H2134 H3795 H3974). Katit (H3795) means beaten/crushed — the oil is produced by pounding the olives, not pressing them under a stone. The finest grade of oil, from gently beaten olives. The word for olive press is gat shemen — precisely Gethsemane. The oil that feeds the eternal lamp is katit, crushed oil, from the garden whose name means crushing. ner tamid (NER tah-MEED): 'perpetual/continual lamp' (H5216 H8548). The lamp must not go out — it burns through the night before YHWH, the undying light of the covenant.
27.21: me'erev ad boqer (me-EH-rev ad BO-ker): 'from evening until morning' — the lamp burns through the night, the hours when darkness rules outside. Aaron and his sons tend the ner tamid in the tent of meeting before YHWH. chukkat olam ledorotam (khuk-KAT oh-LAM le-doh-roh-TAM): 'a perpetual statute throughout their generations' (H2708 H5769). The lampstand service is not temporary legislation but olam — the same word for the everlasting covenant. me'et benei Yisrael: the oil comes from Israel, supplied by the people, tended by the priests — the whole nation sustains the light that burns before the divine presence on their behalf.

33.1: After the golden calf catastrophe, YHWH commands Israel to depart for Canaan — but without his own presence: wĕšālaḥtî lĕpānêkā malʾāk ('I will send an angel before you'). The angel-substitute for divine presence is the catastrophic downgrade that generates the chapter's central crisis. The land can be inherited without YHWH's presence; that is not the question. Moses understands that a land without YHWH is not worth inheriting.
33.2: Kĕnaʿănî hāʾĕmōrî wĕhaḥittî: the standard six-nation list of Canaan's inhabitants. The promise of dispossession remains firm even as YHWH withdraws his direct presence. The theological distinction is between covenant guarantee (the land will be yours) and covenantal intimacy (I will dwell among you) — both are part of the Abrahamic promise, but they can be separated. Moses refuses to accept the land without the presence.
33.3: wĕlōʾ ʾeʿăleh bĕqirbĕkā: 'for I will not go up in your midst.' YHWH's refusal to dwell among Israel is not permanent abandonment but disciplinary withdrawal: pen ʾăkalkĕlĕkā badderek ('lest I destroy you on the way'). The withdrawal protects Israel — YHWH's holy presence among a stiff-necked people is consuming fire. The theology here: the presence of God is simultaneously the most desired gift and the most dangerous reality for sinful people.
33.4: wayyitʾabbĕlû: 'they mourned.' The people's response to the news of YHWH's withdrawal is lamentation. Wĕlōʾ-šāt ʾîš ʿādyô ʿālāw ('and no one put on his ornaments') — the removal of ornaments is the outward sign of mourning and penitence. The ornamental self-presentation of covenant celebration (the gold used for the calf) is now renounced. The same ornaments that became the calf are now removed as an act of repentance.
33.5: ʿam qĕšēh ʾōrep ʾattāh: 'you are a stiff-necked people.' The divine diagnosis is repeated from 32:9. Qĕšēh ʾōrep (stiff-necked/hard of neck) — the agricultural image of an ox refusing the yoke of the plowman. The conditional: 'if for a single moment I were to go up in your midst, I would destroy you.' The solution is Moses's intercession, which transforms the condition. The removal of ornaments (wĕhôrēd ʿĕdyô mēʿālāw) in response is the corporate penitential act.
33.6: wayyitnāṣṣĕlû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾet ʿĕdyām: 'the children of Israel stripped themselves of their ornaments.' The stripping is permanent from Mount Horeb onward — min har ḥōreb wāhālĕʾāh. The gold ornaments of Egypt (12:35-36) were the material of the golden calf; now permanently renounced, they become the material of the tabernacle donation (35:22). The same wealth that funded idolatry is redirected to YHWH's dwelling — the transformation of the people's material lives parallels the transformation of the covenant relationship.
33.7: wĕʾōhel nāṭāh lô miḥûṣ lammaḥăneh hārḥēq min-hammaḥăneh: 'he pitched a tent outside the camp, far from the camp.' The tent of meeting is relocated outside the camp in the aftermath of the golden calf — YHWH's withdrawal is geographically enacted. This is the proto-tabernacle, functioning before the construction of the actual tabernacle. Wĕhāyāh kol-mĕbaqqēš YHWH yēṣēʾ ʾel-ʾōhel môʿēd ʾăšer miḥûṣ lammaḥăneh: 'everyone who sought YHWH went out to the tent of meeting.' Seeking God requires going outside the camp — the outside positioning is not merely geographic but theological: holiness stands at a distance from the contaminated community.
33.8: When Moses went out to the tent, all the people rose and stood, each at his tent door, watching Moses until he entered. The communal watching (hibbîṭû ʾaḥărê mōšeh) is reverent observation of the mediator approaching the divine presence. The posture — everyone at their own tent door — simultaneously represents the community's solidarity with Moses and their inability to approach themselves. They can only watch the mediator enter what they cannot enter.
33.9: wĕhāyāh kĕbōʾ mōšeh hāʾōhĕlāh yērēd ʿammûd heʿānān wĕʿāmad petaḥ hāʾōhel: 'when Moses entered the tent, the pillar of cloud descended and stood at the entrance of the tent.' The cloud pillar — YHWH's theophanic presence — settles at the entrance: YHWH meets Moses at the threshold but does not enter in full. The contrast with 40:34 (when the cloud fills the completed tabernacle) is significant — here the presence is partial, transitional, anticipating the full Shekinah indwelling when the covenant is restored.
33.10: All the people saw the cloud pillar standing at the tent entrance and qāmû kol-hāʿām wĕhištaḥăwû ʾîš petaḥ ʾōhĕlô: 'all the people rose and bowed down, each at his own tent door.' The corporate worship from the distance of their tent doors mirrors the later arrangement of the court and sanctuary: the people worship from the outer court while the priest enters the inner sanctuary. The spatial arrangement of wilderness worship anticipates the tabernacle's graduated holiness.
33.11: wĕdibbēr YHWH ʾel-mōšeh pānîm ʾel pānîm kaʾăšer yĕdabbēr ʾîš ʾel rēʿēhû: 'YHWH spoke to Moses face to face as a man speaks to his friend.' The intimacy formula — face to face as a friend speaks — is the highest description of prophetic access in the Torah (Num 12:8: 'face to face I speak with him, plainly and not in riddles'). Yet v.20 declares Moses cannot see YHWH's face and live. The two statements are not contradictory: 'face to face' describes the relational directness and intimacy of the communication; the ban on seeing YHWH's face describes the impossibility of unmediated encounter with the full divine glory.
33.12: Moses argues with YHWH on the basis of personal knowledge: 'you have said to me, you know you by name, and you have found favor in my sight.' The verb yādaʿtîkā bĕšēm (knowing by name) indicates covenantal identification — YHWH knows Moses as an individual, not merely as a functionary. Moses leverages this personal knowledge in intercession: 'If I have found favor in your sight, please show me now your ways.' The intercession builds argument from already-granted grace.
33.13: wĕhôdîʿēnî nā ʾet-dĕrākêkā: 'please make me know your ways.' The request is epistemological: Moses wants to know YHWH's character and method of dealing — not merely his commands but his nature. The nā (please/I pray) is the particle of urgent request. The justification: lĕmaʿan ʾedāʿăkā lĕmaʿan ʾemṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênêkā — 'so that I may know you, so that I may find favor in your eyes.' Knowledge of YHWH and favor with YHWH are inseparable — to know him is to be known by him.
33.14: pānay yēlēkû wăhăniḥōtî lāk: 'My face will go, and I will give you rest.' YHWH's response is a renewed covenant of presence. Pānay (my face/my presence) is the theological term for YHWH's manifest presence accompanying his people — the same root as pānîm ʾel pānîm of v.11. The promise hăniḥōtî lāk ('I will give you rest') combines the land-promise (settled rest from enemies) with the presence-promise (YHWH accompanying and sheltering). Moses takes this as YHWH personally going with Israel, not sending a substitute.
33.15: ʾim ʾên pānêkā hōlēkîm ʾal taʿălēnû mizzeh: 'if your face/presence does not go with us, do not bring us up from here.' Moses's ultimatum: the land without YHWH's presence is not worth having. This is one of the OT's strongest statements of the theology of divine presence as the supreme covenant blessing. The land, wealth, and victory are secondary goods; YHWH himself is the primary good. Moses refuses the gift without the Giver.
33.16: ûbammeh yiwwādaʿ ʾēpôʾ kî-māṣātî ḥēn bĕʿênêkā ʾănî wĕʿammekā hălôʾ bĕlektĕkā ʿimmānû wĕnipalînu ʾănî wĕʿammĕkā mikkol-hāʿām ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh: 'how will it be known that I have found favor in your eyes, I and your people, if you do not go with us? It is by your going with us that we and your people will be distinct from every people on the face of the earth.' The distinguishing mark of Israel is not ethnicity, law, or land — it is the presence of YHWH. Wĕnipalînu (we will be set apart/distinguished, Niphal of pālāh) — the same root as in the plagues, where YHWH set Israel apart from Egypt. Divine presence is the identity-constituting reality for the covenant community.
33.17: YHWH grants Moses's intercession: gam ʾet-haddābār hazzeh ʾăšer dibbartā ʾeʿĕśeh — 'this very thing that you have spoken I will do.' The intercessor's prayer is answered in full. The basis: kî māṣātā ḥēn bĕʿênay wāʾēdāʿăkā bĕšēm — 'for you have found favor in my eyes and I know you by name.' The same arguments Moses used in intercession are now YHWH's stated reasons for granting the intercession. The prayer was effective precisely because it was grounded in YHWH's own prior grace.
33.18: harʾēnî nāʾ ʾet-kĕbōdekā: 'Please show me your glory.' Moses's request escalates: having secured YHWH's presence with Israel, he now asks for unmediated vision of the divine glory itself. Kābôd (glory/weightiness) is the full manifestation of YHWH's being — what appeared in the cloud and fire (16:10; 24:16-17) is what Moses now asks to see directly. The request tests the limits of human capacity for divine encounter — a test that v.20 will answer with the gracious accommodation of the cleft.
33.19: ʾănî ʾaʿăbîr kol-ṭûbî ʿal-pānêkā wĕqārāʾtî bĕšēm YHWH lĕpānêkā wĕḥannōtî ʾet-ʾăšer ʾāḥōn wĕriḥamtî ʾet-ʾăšer ʾăraḥēm: 'I will make all my goodness pass before your face, and I will proclaim the name of YHWH before you. And I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and I will show mercy on whom I will show mercy.' The double Qal imperfect of the cognate verb construction — ḥannōtî ʾet-ʾăšer ʾāḥōn (I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious) — declares YHWH's sovereign freedom in extending grace. Paul quotes this verse in Rom 9:15-16 to establish the doctrine of unconditional election: grace does not respond to prior human merit but to divine will. The ṭûb (goodness) of YHWH — his full moral character — is what will be displayed, not his unmediated face.
33.20: lōʾ tûkal lirʾōt ʾet-pānay kî lōʾ-yirʾanî hāʾādām waḥāy: 'You cannot see my face, for man cannot see me and live.' The ban on seeing YHWH's face (pānay) is absolute — no human being can survive the unmediated encounter with the divine glory. The word hāʾādām (man, the human) is generic: not Moses specifically but any human being. The limitation is not a judgment on Moses's character but a statement about the ontological gap between finite creature and infinite Creator. The cleft provision (v.22) is YHWH's gracious accommodation to this ontological limit.
33.21: hinnēh māqôm ʾittî wĕnīṣabtā ʿal-haṣṣûr: 'Behold, there is a place by me, and you shall stand on the rock.' The phrase māqôm ʾittî (a place by me/with me) is the spatial accommodation YHWH provides for Moses. The same word māqôm (place) will be applied to sacred sites throughout the OT (Gen 28:17: 'this is the gate of heaven'). The rock (haṣṣûr) is the hiding and protection place that mediates the encounter — related to the rock of 17:6 where YHWH stood to be struck.
33.22: wĕhāyāh baʿăbōr kĕbōdî wĕśamtîkā bĕniqrat haṣṣûr wĕśakkōtî kappî ʿālêkā ʿad ʿăbōrî: 'when my glory passes by, I will put you in a cleft of the rock, and I will cover you with my hand until I have passed by.' Three elements: the cleft (niqrat haṣṣûr — the hollow/cleft of the rock), the hand covering (kappî = my palm), and the passage (ʿad ʿăbōrî = until I pass). Moses is hidden, covered, and sheltered from the full blast of YHWH's glory. The rock cleft as the shelter for the divine encounter becomes a paradigm of the grace that protects the creature from consuming divine holiness.
33.23: wahăsirōtî ʾet kappî wĕrāʾîtā ʾet ʾăḥōrāy ûpānay lōʾ yērāʾû: 'Then I will take away my hand, and you shall see my back (ʾăḥōrāy), but my face shall not be seen.' The sequence: YHWH passes, the hand is removed, Moses sees ʾăḥōrāy (my back/afterglow/aftermath). The word ʾāḥôr (back/rear) may indicate the retreating glory — what remains after the direct encounter, the aftereffect of divine presence rather than its full face. This 'backglow' is sufficient to illuminate Moses's face (34:29). The contrast is maximal: no one can see the face; even this partial vision is sufficient to transform the prophet.

35.1: ʾēlleh hadĕbārîm ʾăšer-ṣiwwāh YHWH laʿăśōt ʾōtām: 'These are the words that YHWH has commanded you to do.' Moses assembles the whole congregation to receive the tabernacle instructions. The phrase kol-ʿădat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl (the whole congregation of the children of Israel) emphasizes the communal nature of the project — the tabernacle is Israel's collective act of worship, not Moses's or the craftsmen's alone.
35.2: šēšet yāmîm tēʿāśeh mĕlāʾkāh ûbayyôm haššĕbîʿî yihyeh lākem qōdeš šabbat šabbātôn laYHWH: 'Six days work shall be done, but the seventh day shall be for you holy, a Sabbath of complete rest to YHWH.' The Sabbath command is placed before the tabernacle construction instructions — even the holy work of building YHWH's dwelling is subordinate to the Sabbath. No project, however sacred, overrides the covenant rhythm of rest. The placement signals that the Sabbath is more fundamental than the sanctuary.
35.3: lōʾ tĕbaʿărû ʾēš bĕkōl mōšĕbōtêkem bĕyôm haššabbāt: 'You shall not kindle fire in any of your dwelling places on the Sabbath day.' The specific prohibition against fire on the Sabbath — appearing only here in the Torah — may address the smelting and casting involved in tabernacle metalwork. The Sabbath boundary holds even in the productive fire of construction. The Mishnah later derives 39 categories of prohibited Sabbath labor from the 39 types of work performed in building the tabernacle.
35.4: zeh hadĕbār ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH lēʾmōr: 'This is the thing that YHWH has commanded.' The transition from the Sabbath prohibition to the freewill offering collection. The connection is significant: the offering is described as divinely commanded, not self-initiated by the people — YHWH commands the freewill offering, which means the freedom and the command are not opposed. Commanded generosity is still real generosity.
35.5: qĕḥû mēʾittĕkem tĕrûmāh laYHWH kōl nĕdîb libbô yĕbiʾehā ʾēt tĕrûmat YHWH: 'Take from among you a contribution to YHWH. Whoever is of a generous heart (kol nĕdîb libbô), let him bring YHWH's contribution.' The contribution (tĕrûmāh, literally 'a lifting up/heave offering') is described by the quality of the giver: nĕdîb lēb (generous/willing heart). The motivation is internal disposition, not external compulsion. The freewill offering theology: the tabernacle is built not by taxation but by the willing generosity of those who have encountered YHWH's grace.
35.6: The materials list begins: gold, silver, bronze; blue, purple, and scarlet yarns; fine twisted linen; goats' hair. These are the same materials specified in Exod 25:3-7 — the instructions given on Sinai are now being gathered from the community. The list moves from precious metals to textiles, establishing the hierarchy of value that will be reflected in the graduated holiness of the tabernacle's zones.
35.7: The list continues: acacia wood, oil for the lamp, spices for the anointing oil and for fragrant incense, onyx stones, and stones for setting in the ephod and breastpiece. The comprehensive list covers everything needed for the completed tabernacle. The anointing oil and incense are specialized — requiring expert preparation (30:22-38). The gem-setting requires the craftsman's skill. The community's offering covers both raw materials and specialized items.
35.8: wĕkōl ḥăkam-lēb bākem yābōʾû wĕyaʿăśû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH: 'and every skillful person among you shall come and make all that YHWH has commanded.' The skill component: ḥăkam-lēb (wise/skilled of heart) — the craftsman's competence is a matter of the heart, not merely technical training. Wisdom in Hebrew encompasses practical skill as well as moral insight; the skilled craftsman exercises the same faculty as the wise ruler or the discerning judge.
35.9: The list of structures to be made: the tabernacle and its tent, covering, clasps, frames, bars, pillars, bases; the ark and poles and mercy seat; the table and its poles and utensils and bread of the Presence; the lampstand and its accessories and oil; the altar of incense and its poles; the anointing oil and fragrant incense; the screen for the entrance of the tent; the altar of burnt offering; the court and its equipment; the basin. The comprehensive scope of the tabernacle project is laid before the community: this is what their freewill offering will build.
35.10: wĕkōl-ḥăkam-lēb bākem yābōʾû wĕyaʿăśû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH: repeated call to skilled craftspeople. The repetition of the call emphasizes that the tabernacle requires both the generous givers and the skilled makers — both are acts of covenant service. The theological connection: spiritual gift and material offering are both responses to YHWH's grace.
35.11: The construction items: the tabernacle, its tent and covering, its clasps and frames, its bars, pillars, and bases. The technical vocabulary of construction begins here — the terms for the structural elements of the tabernacle will be repeated through chapters 36-40 as their execution is narrated. The repetition (instructions in 25-31, execution in 35-40) embodies the principle that obedient construction follows the divine pattern exactly.
35.12: The ark with its poles, the mercy seat, and the veil of the screen — the innermost furnishings of the most holy place. The mercy seat (kappōret) is mentioned first in this recapitulation because it is YHWH's throne, the locus of his presence, the theological center of the entire structure. The veil (pārōket) that separates the holy place from the most holy place is the structural boundary between accessible holiness and the unmediated divine presence.
35.13: The table with its poles, all its utensils, and the bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm, literally 'bread of faces' or 'bread of the presence'). The twelve loaves placed on the table every Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9) represent Israel's twelve tribes perpetually before YHWH's face. The bread of the presence is the enacted sign of Israel's covenant standing: YHWH hosts Israel at his table, sustaining the relationship through continuous provision.
35.14: The lampstand for light with its accessories and oil for the light. The mĕnôrāh (lampstand) with its seven branches — already specified in Exod 25:31-40 — is described as providing hammāʾôr (the light). The light of the lampstand in the holy place is the continuous illumination of YHWH's presence — not sunlight but the sanctuary's own light, symbolizing the light of divine revelation in the midst of the community.
35.15: The altar of incense with its poles, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance of the tent. The altar of incense (mizbaḥ hamiqṭār) is the innermost accessible altar — standing before the veil to the most holy place. The incense offering represents prayer ascending to YHWH (Ps 141:2: 'let my prayer be counted as incense before you'). The screen (māsāk) for the entrance separates the holy place from the outer court.
35.16: The altar of burnt offering with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand. The two altars — burnt offering (in the outer court) and incense (in the holy place) — frame the approach to YHWH's presence: first the sacrifice (burnt offering for atonement), then the worship (incense for prayer). The architectural movement inward through the tabernacle is a movement through purification toward intimacy.
35.17: The hangings of the court, its pillars, their bases, and the screen for the gate of the court — the outer boundary of the sacred space. The court's enclosure defined the perimeter of holiness in the wilderness camp. The gate screen (māsāk) marks the threshold between the camp (ordinary space) and the court (sacred space). The graduated holiness — court, holy place, most holy place — reflects the graduated access to YHWH: all Israel, priests, and high priest once a year.
35.18: The pegs of the tabernacle and the pegs of the court with their cords. The tent pegs (yĕtēdōt) and cords secure the tabernacle to the ground — the practical anchoring of the divine dwelling in the physical world. The detail is not peripheral: the tabernacle's portability (it can be dismantled and reassembled) is part of its theology — YHWH accompanies his people in their journey, not residing in a fixed location until the land is given.
35.19: ûgĕdê haśśĕrād laśśārēt baqōdeš bigdê haqqōdeš lĕʾahărōn hakkōhēn ûbigdê bānāyw lĕkahēn: 'the finely worked garments for ministering in the holy place, the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, for their service as priests.' The priestly vestments (bigdê šĕrād) are included in the list of items to be provided — the clothing of the mediator is as much a covenant object as the ark or altar. The vestments make the priest who he is in the sanctuary context; they are not mere uniforms but covenantal costume.
35.20: wayyēṣĕʾû kol-ʿădat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl millipnê mōšeh: 'the whole congregation of the children of Israel departed from Moses's presence.' The assembly disperses to respond individually and communally. The transition from public hearing to private response — from the congregation before Moses to the families at their tents — sets up the extraordinary outpouring of generosity narrated in vv.21-29.
35.21: wayyābōʾû kol-ʾîš ʾăšer-nĕśāʾô libbô wĕkōl ʾăšer nādĕbāh rûḥô: 'every man whose heart was lifted up came, and everyone whose spirit moved him.' The two expressions of motivation: nāśāʾ lēb (heart lifted up = motivated, enthusiastic) and nādĕbāh rûḥô (spirit moved = spontaneously generous). Both are interior movements, not external pressure. The offering for the tabernacle is the community's freewill response to YHWH's grace. The language anticipates Ezra 1:5 (the returning exiles whose spirits God stirred to rebuild the temple).
35.22: wayyābōʾû hāʾănāšîm ʿal-hannāšîm kōl nĕdîb lēb: 'both men and women came; all who were of a willing heart brought.' The inclusion of women as full participants in the offering is notable. The ornaments they bring — clasps, earrings, rings, and pendants, all objects of gold — are the personal jewelry of Egyptian origin (12:35-36). The same ornaments stripped off in penitence after the golden calf (33:6) are now given for the tabernacle. The redirected wealth of Egypt funds the dwelling of YHWH.
35.23: wĕkōl ʾîš ʾăšer nimṣāʾ ʾittô tĕkēlet wĕʾargāmān wĕtôlaʿat šānî ûšēš wĕʿizzîm ûʿōrōt ʾēlîm mĕʾoddāmîm wĕʿōrōt tĕḥāšîm ḥēbîʾû: the materials list continues with textiles and hides. The variety of contributors — those who had blue, purple, scarlet, linen, goats' hair, dyed ram skins, sea cow hides — suggests that the community's diverse wealth was comprehensively mobilized. No single type of material came from one source; the tabernacle is a collective offering.
35.24: kōl-mārîm tĕrûmat kesep ûnĕḥōšet ḥēbîʾû ʾēt tĕrûmat YHWH: 'everyone who made an offering of silver or bronze brought the LORD's contribution.' The silver and bronze offerings — the metals of the court furnishings and altar — come from those who happened to have them. The word mārîm (lifting up/contributing) is the verbal form of tĕrûmāh. Those who had acacia wood contributed it. The matching of resources to need is YHWH's provision through the community's diverse holdings.
35.25: wĕkōl-ʾiššāh ḥăkmat-lēb bĕyādêhā ṭāwû wayyābîʾû maṭweh ʾet-hattĕkēlet wĕʾet-hāʾargāmān ʾet-tôlaʿat haššānî wĕʾet-haššēš: 'all the women who were skillful of heart spun with their hands, and they brought what they had spun in blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine linen.' The skill-and-spirit combination appears in the women's spinning as well as the men's craftsmanship: ḥăkmat-lēb (skillful/wise of heart) is the quality of both Bezalel (v.31) and the spinning women. Wisdom and skill are not merely technical — they involve the heart's formation and disposition.
35.26: wĕkōl-hannāšîm ʾăšer nāśāʾ libbān ʾōtānāh bĕḥokmāh ṭāwû ʾet-hāʿizzîm: 'all the women whose hearts moved them with skill spun the goats' hair.' The goats' hair spinning is distinguished — it is a separate group of women whose hearts moved them with this specific skill. The precision of the distinction shows the narrator attending to the specific contributions of specific groups: not all the women did the same thing; each contributed what she was skilled to give. The community's diverse gifts are all needed and all honored.
35.27: The leaders (wĕhannĕśîʾîm) brought the onyx stones and the stones for setting in the ephod and breastpiece, and the spices and oil for the lamp, anointing oil, and fragrant incense. The leaders' distinctive contribution is the most valuable materials — the gems and spices that required wealth to obtain. Leadership is expressed here in generous material provision for the community's worship. The leaders' gift completes the comprehensive offering: precious stones and spices that no ordinary member could provide.
35.28: wĕhabbĕśem wĕhašāmen lĕmāʾôr ûlĕšemen hammišḥāh ûliqṭōret hassammîm: 'and the spice and the oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense.' The three specialized uses of oil and spice — lamp oil, anointing oil, incense — require different preparations. The leaders' provision of the raw materials enables the skilled craftsmen to prepare the specific formulations. The community's generosity covers both the materials and the expertise needed to use them correctly.
35.29: kōl-ʾîš wĕʾiššāh ʾăšer nādab libbām ʾōtām lĕhābîʾ lĕkol-hamĕlāʾkāh ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH laʿăśôt bĕyad mōšeh ḥēbîʾû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl nĕdābāh laYHWH: 'all men and women, the people of Israel, whose heart moved them to bring anything for the work that YHWH had commanded by Moses to be done brought it as a freewill offering to YHWH.' The summary of the offering: the whole community (kol-ʾîš wĕʾiššāh), motivated internally (nādab libbām), brought their contributions as a freewill offering (nĕdābāh) to YHWH. The word nĕdābāh (freewill offering, voluntary gift) is the technical term for an offering given without obligation — here applied to the entire tabernacle construction fund.
35.30: rĕʾû qārāʾ YHWH bĕšēm bĕṣalʾēl ben-ûrî ben-ḥûr lĕmaṭṭēh yĕhûdāh: 'See, YHWH has called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.' The divine calling by name — qārāʾ bĕšēm — is the language of prophetic appointment (Isa 45:4: 'I call you by your name'). Bezalel's genealogy — son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah — connects him to the leadership present at the Sinai events (Hur stood beside Aaron holding Moses's arms, 17:12). The appointment of the craftsman is as much a divine calling as the appointment of a prophet.
35.31: waymallēʾ ʾōtô rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm bĕḥokmāh bîtĕbûnāh ûbĕdaʿat ûbĕkōl mĕlāʾkāh: 'and he has filled him with the Spirit of God, with wisdom, understanding, and knowledge, and with all craftsmanship.' This is the first explicit charismatic endowment in Scripture — the Spirit of God filling a person for a specific task. The three gifts: ḥokmāh (wisdom — practical skill and discernment), tĕbûnāh (understanding — analytic insight), daʿat (knowledge — experiential and technical know-how). The same triad appears in Prov 3:19-20: 'YHWH by wisdom founded the earth, by understanding established the heavens; by his knowledge the deeps broke open.' The craftsman shares in the wisdom by which the world was made.
35.32: ûlĕḥašōb maḥăšābōt laʿăśôt bazzāhāb ûbakkesep ûbannĕḥōšet: 'to devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze.' The verb ḥāšab (to think/devise/plan) is the same root as the ḥōšēb craftsman whose intricate work is required for the tabernacle. Artistic design is a form of thought — ḥăšōb maḥăšābōt (thinking of thoughts) — and the craftsman's mind engaged in design participates in the same faculty that YHWH used in creation. Beauty is an intellectual as well as a manual achievement.
35.33: ûbĕḥărōšet ʾeben lĕmallōʾt ûbĕḥărōšet ʿēṣ laʿăśôt bĕkōl mĕleʾket maḥăšābet: 'in cutting stones for setting, and in carving wood, for work in every skilled craft.' The full range of Bezalel's mandate: gem-cutting, wood-carving, and every kind of skilled work. The Hebrew ḥărōšet (craftsmanship, artisanship) from ḥāraš (craftsman) covers the full range of skilled trades. The tabernacle requires all of them — there is no hierarchy among the crafts, only the coordinating direction of the Spirit-filled master craftsman.
35.34: ûlĕhôrōt nātan bĕlibbô hûʾ wĕʾohŏlîʾāb ben-ʾăḥîsāmāk lĕmaṭṭēh dān: 'and he has given him the ability to teach, both him and Oholiab the son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan.' The gift of teaching (lĕhôrōt — from the same root as tôrāh) is included among Bezalel's endowments. The Spirit-filled craftsman is also a teacher — the wisdom given to him is transmissible. Oholiab is introduced as the second master craftsman: from the tribe of Dan (north), pairing with Bezalel from Judah (south) — the collaboration of the two major tribes in building YHWH's dwelling.
35.35: mallēʾ ʾōtām ḥokmat-lēb laʿăśôt kōl-mĕleʾket ḥārāš wĕḥōšēb ûrōqēm bĕttĕkēlet ûbāʾargāmān bĕtôlaʿat haššānî ûbāššēš wĕʾōrēg ʿōśēh kōl-mĕlāʾkāh wĕḥōšĕbê maḥăšābōt: 'He has filled them with skill to do every sort of work done by an engraver or by a designer or by an embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, or by a weaver — by any sort of workman or skilled designer.' The comprehensive list of craft categories closes with the summary ḥōšĕbê maḥăšābōt (devisers of designs/skilled thinkers). The breadth of Spirit-given skills encompasses every art required for the tabernacle: engraving, designing, embroidering, weaving. The Spirit of God equips the whole spectrum of human artistic capacity in service of the covenant dwelling.

30.1: mizbeach miqtar qetoret (מִזְבַּח מִקְטַר קְטֹרֶת, H4196+H7004) — an altar specifically for burning incense. Mizbeach (altar) derives from zabach (to slaughter), yet this altar receives no blood: it is the altar of approach by fragrance, not sacrifice. The acacia-and-gold construction places it structurally in the outer holy place but functionally at the threshold of the Most Holy, standing before the veil. Hebrew distinguishes it consistently from mizbeach ha'olah (the burnt-offering altar in the court), so every occurrence of this noun carries the distinct register of priestly nearness rather than atonement.
30.2: amah orko ve'amah rochbo (a cubit long and a cubit wide) — the incense altar is the smallest altar: one cubit square, two cubits high, shaped like a tall column. The proportion — taller than wide — suits an altar whose purpose is vertical ascent of smoke rather than horizontal surface for offering. Qarnot (its horns) are integral, not added separately; they are mimmennu yihyeh (of one piece with it), echoing the same design language for the mercy seat (Exod 25:19).
30.3: zehav tahor (זָהָב טָהוֹר, pure/refined gold) — the incense altar is overlaid with the same pure gold as the ark and the mercy seat, not merely the table of showbread's ordinary gold. Tahor (H2889) carries connotations of ritual and moral purity as well as physical refinement; its application to the metal signals that this object belongs to the innermost sanctity-register. The zer zahav (golden crown/molding) running around its edge matches the ark and table (Exod 25:11,24), creating a visual unity among the holy vessels.
30.4: shetei tabba'ot zahav (two gold rings) — two rings on either side for carrying poles, the same portable design as the ark and table. The incense altar must be movable without being touched, maintaining holiness protocol. Its location al shetei tzala'otav (on its two sides) — the rings placed at the frame (lechatztzelo) — is a technical detail ensuring stable transport without profaning contact.
30.5: atzei shittim (acacia wood) — the same material used for every portable sacred object in the tabernacle. Acacia (shittah, H7848) is a dense, termite-resistant hardwood native to Sinai; its use throughout likely reflects both availability and the tradition that incorruptibility belongs to what is set apart for God. Gold overlay transforms this desert wood into the appearance of pure metal.
30.6: lifnei happarochet (before/in front of the veil) — the incense altar stands outside the veil but immediately before it, facing the ark and mercy seat within. The phrase lifnei hakaporet (before the mercy seat) makes its orientation explicit: Aaron burns incense before the presence, not merely before furniture. The spatial language establishes that priestly prayer, represented by the incense column, is directionally aimed at the locus of divine presence.
30.7: qetoret samim (קְטֹרֶת סַמִּים, fragrant/spiced incense) — Aaron burns this compound incense every morning when he tends the lamps (beheitivo et hanerot). Qetoret (H7004) derives from qatar (to go up in smoke, to make an offering by fire); the fragrance rising becomes the olfactory counterpart to visible fire. Samim (H5561) denotes aromatic spices in general; here specifying that only the prescribed compound (v34-38) is acceptable. The simultaneity of incense and lamp-tending frames the priest's morning service as a multi-sensory act of holy attention.
30.8: beha'alot Aharon et hanerot bein ha'arbayim (when Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight) — the incense is also burned at evening, making it qetoret tamid (perpetual/continual incense) before the LORD throughout generations. Bein ha'arbayim (between the evenings) is the standard phrase for the late-afternoon hour, used also for the Passover sacrifice timing (12:6). The word tamid (H8548, continual/perpetual) appears as the signature of a never-ceasing obligation — also describing the daily burnt offering, the showbread, and the lamp.
30.9: qetoret zarah (קְטֹרֶת זָרָה, strange/unauthorized/foreign incense) — zarah (H2114, strange, unauthorized, belonging to another) is the precise legal term for any offering not conforming to divine specification. This prohibition is violated in Lev 10:1 by Nadab and Abihu who offer esh zarah (strange fire), with fatal consequence. The altar's singularity of function enforces singularity of worship — the incense altar must do only one thing.
30.10: vekipper Aharon al-qarnot (Aaron shall make atonement on its horns) — once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the incense altar receives blood from the chattat (sin offering) applied to its horns. Kaphar (H3722) — the foundational atonement verb of Levitical theology — means to cover, to ransom, to make good a debt. The phrase qodesh qodashim hu laYHWH (it is most holy to the LORD) is the highest holiness designation in the tabernacle system, shared only with the ark-chamber and the sin-offering flesh.
30.11: A new speech-formula begins: vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor (the LORD spoke to Moses saying). The census section (vv11-16) is an independent divine speech inserted into the tabernacle instructions, connecting the counting of Israel to a redemption requirement. Its placement here — between altar design and laver instructions — keeps the practical and the theological inseparable throughout the tabernacle legislation.
30.12: kofer nafsho laYHWH (כֹּפֶר נַפְשׁוֹ לַיהוָה, a ransom for his life/soul to the LORD) — when counting Israel, each man over 20 must pay a ransom, or a plague will follow. Kofer (H3724) is a payment that substitutes for a life; it is cognate to kaphar (atonement). Nefesh (H5315, life/soul/person) here designates the individual as mortal and accountable before God.
